To ask the Minister of Education whether he will amend the Education Authority’s Pupil Allocation Number policy following the call from 46 pre-school leaders to reform the allocations. [R]
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for managing the pre-school admissions process and ensuring that sufficient places are available to accommodate all target age children whose parents want a place. In doing so, the EA takes account of all provision across statutory and non-statutory settings. My Department plays no role in this process.  The admissions process is still ongoing, but already 98.8% children have been offered a place in a setting of their parents’ preference, and 92.4% offered their first preference setting. I appreciate that settings are naturally keen to attract as many funded places as possible given the continuing decline in the birth rate and this must be managed fairly by the EA. The EA advises that, of the 46 settings referred to, only 25 were oversubscribed with applications at stage one of the admissions process for the 2026/27 academic year. Only 22 have requested at least one PAN increase, ten of which have had their requests wholly or partially approved. 11 settings have not had their requests approved, including three that were not oversubscribed and one that only received one first preference application at stage one.The PAN review carried out by the EA at my request last year included a range of measures to improve the process in both the short and longer term, many of which have already been implemented. Details of this are available on the EA website. I believe that there may be scope to further improve the manner in which the EA takes account of the preferences of parents, while still balancing its relevant statutory duties. The PAN review includes an action to consider alternative PAN allocation models which will enable a range of options to be fully explored with stakeholders and thoroughly tested to ensure effectiveness before any changes are made. My officials are supporting the EA in carrying out initial modelling to inform stakeholder workshops to discuss alternative PAN allocation models. This work is at an early stage but it is intended that the first workshop will be held before the end of this academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has progressed any of the recommendations relevant to her Department, contained within the Independent Review of Education, since the publication of the report.
answer_text: My Department continues to work collaboratively with the Department of Education on a number of relevant Independent Review of Education recommendations, particularly those relating to post16 provision, careers, and 14–19 pathways.There are now agreed structures in place to support more effective crossdepartmental collaboration and promote a more strategic and coherent approach to provision across the education system.As the Department of Education is the lead Department and ultimate owner of the Review and its recommendations, updates on overall progress should be provided by that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39575/22-27, to detail when the building contractor for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School’s new school build will be appointed.
answer_text: The procurement of a building contractor for the new school build is progressing well through the final stages.  The Business Case Addendum for the project is also being progressed in parallel and is in the final stages of review. The current programme estimates that contractor appointment will be in the first quarter of the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce a third-party rights of appeal in planning decisions.
answer_text: The current planning system includes measures to significantly front-load third party and community engagement in the development management and forward planning processes. Recent legislative amendments have also strengthened Pre Application Community Consultation provisions to increase early public participation and to allow developers to take into account representations from various stakeholders. My department is working in partnership with local government and key stakeholders, to advance the planning improvement agenda with a clear focus on enhancing the effectiveness, resilience, and long-term sustainability of the planning system in the North and I will continue to review the progress that has been made to date. At this time however, there are no current plans to introduce a third-party right of appeal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will amend the relevant legislation to increase the scope of neighbourhood notification of planning applications.
answer_text: While I am satisfied that current legislative requirements for notifying neighbours of planning applications as set out in the Planning Act (NI) 2011, and the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015 are adequate to deal with all types of application, I am committed to reviewing these notification requirements as part of a broader review of advertising and notifying the public about development which may impact them. Importantly, planning authorities are not restricted to the statutory minimum for neighbour notification purposes and, taking account of the nature of a proposed development, its scale and context, planning authorities can broaden notification to include other occupiers of properties likely to be impacted by a proposed development. Importantly, anyone can make a representation in relation to a planning application, whether or not they have received notification.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance his Department has issued to health practitioners across Health and Social Care Trusts on the diagnosis of juvenile idiopathic arthritis.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered the introduction of temporary speed limit zones around buses taking children to and from school.
answer_text: As part of my public consultation on a proposed legislative approach to improving safety around buses during boarding and alighting, the Department has considered a range of measures designed to create a temporary safety zone around stationary buses. The consultation opened on 26 March and will run until 21 May 2026. The consultation (Safer Journeys to School: Consultation on overtaking and passing buses) provides an opportunity for individuals, families, stakeholders and organisations to share views and evidence to help inform the programme’s next steps.My Department is also currently taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits, which is open for consultation. Further consideration on the use of both temporary and permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their office's Ending Violence Against Women and Girls programme facilitates specific community support in areas where a woman has been murdered.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of rheumatology specialists who can diagnose and support those living with juvenile idiopathic arthritis.
answer_text: The Trust has advised that there are currently 2 WTE substantive paediatric rheumatology consultants in post at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. In addition, 7 WTE adult rheumatology consultants provide care for patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis aged 16 and over.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has issued any guidance in relation to the combined use of a blood test and ultrasound to detect early-stage ovarian cancer, in Health and Social Care Trusts.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has provided any guidance to Translink regarding the appropriate use of surcharges on train tickets.
answer_text: Translink sets its fares within the framework of its public service contract and the wider faresetting powers available under public transport legislation. My Department has not issued any specific guidance to Translink regarding the use of surcharges on train tickets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is increasing the workforce of child specialist (i) physiotherapists; and (ii) therapists.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to fill the GP contract for Silverbirch Medical Practice, Bangor.
answer_text: I can confirm that the single-handed GP at Silverbirch Medical Practice has given notice to withdraw from his contract to deliver General Medical Services at the end of the notice period of 3 months.  The contract will end on 31 May 2026.  Officials have now commenced the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services. A number of different options are available to ensure patients are not left without a GP service. The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor, or grouping of GPs, to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process. In some circumstances, Health and Social Care Trusts can take over a GP contract as an interim solution.  Officials have written out to all practice patients notifying them of the situation and will continue to keep them informed throughout the process. I want to reassure patients that the existing Silverbirch Medical Practice will continue to retain the contract to deliver GP services until the 31 May 2026.  Patients at the practice do not need to take any action. They should continue to contact the practice as normal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the linkage between work on combating racism and prevention of other types of hate crime.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her Department’s contribution to the cross-Executive programme on ending violence against women and girls.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority in relation to reviewing Children's Court Services, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I wrote to the Chief Executive Officer of the Regulation and Inspection Authority (RQIA) on 15th January 2026 to request that RQIA undertakes an urgent review across the five Health and Social Care Trusts Children’s Court Services of practice in private law cases before the Family Courts. The review will consider cases where domestic abuse has been alleged or established and social services have had involvement in that context, specifically with regard to child contact issues and related child safeguarding issues. RQIA is currently finalising its terms of reference to undertake that review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust's bail-fostering pilot to other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (‘the Children Order’) places a statutory duty on Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to safeguard and promote the welfare of children in need, including those in care. As a Corporate Parent, the HSC Trust assumes the moral and legal responsibility for the wellbeing of a child in its care, including when a child becomes involved in the juvenile justice system.The Children Order also places specific duties on HSC Trusts to provide accommodation to children in care, eligible individuals who have left care and children for whom a responsible adult cannot provide accommodation. In fulfilling these duties, they must ensure that, when required, a child has somewhere safe to live while on bail, which meets the bail conditions.  For those children who are in care, this is often the child’s current placement e.g. foster care or residential home. However, as this cohort of children and young people often have highly complex needs / situations this is not always possible, and the Trust will need to identify an alternative placement which meets the needs of the child. For those children and young people not already in care, they may become looked after so that the relevant HSC Trust can provide them with suitable accommodation while on bail.  The existing bail fostering initiative which has been piloted in the Southern Trust area aims to provide short-term therapeutic foster placements for children remanded to Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre who could not meet their bail conditions due to lack of suitable accommodation being available. The pilot has been relatively successful, and discussions have taken place both within the Fostering Workstream of the Children’s Social Care Services Reform Programme, and between officials of my Department and the Department of Justice, regarding extending this pilot.The current initiative provides a very specialist placement, which requires skilled, knowledgeable, and experienced foster carers who have both the estate and ability to take on the role.  While both Departments of Health and Justice support expansion of the initiative it is acknowledged that it will take time to ensure that further delivery reflects the level of demand for placements and the availability of suitable carers. It is also intended that bail fostering will be included in the Fostering Strategic Plan currently being developed as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Reform Programme, which will inform any future expansion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 41100/22-27, whether the figure provided included unpaid rates on the Flagship Shopping Centre car park.
answer_text: The car park at the Flagship Shopping Centre constitutes a separate hereditament for rating purposes, and liability for its rates rests with a different company. As the previous question sought information specifically on the Shopping Centre and the units therein, the figure provided did not include any unpaid rates associated with the car park.Land & Property Services has advised that £34,797.09 remains overdue in respect of the car park, and recovery action is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when a funding decision for the TinyStart Programme from TinyLife will be made.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 have not yet been set by the Executive, therefore, I am not in a position to provide any commitments of funding for TinyLife beyond the current financial year. Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department has undertaken to ensure parental leave is as flexible and lengthy as possible, over the last five years.
answer_text: Following public consultation, my “Good Jobs” Employment Rights Bill will result in improved parental leave measures by introducing carer’s leave and neonatal care leave.  In addition, my Bill will enhance existing arrangements for paternity leave by enabling employees to qualify for this leave from the first day of employment, and by removing the current restriction on paternity leave being taken after a period of shared parental leave.Additionally, using existing powers, I will bring forward measures to permit paternity leave to be taken as two separate weekly blocks or a single block of two weeks at any time within the first 52 weeks following birth or adoption. The notice requirements for paternity leave related to birth and surrogacy will also reduce from 15 weeks to 28 days for each period of leave.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any analysis his Department has made of the Healthy Life Expectancy UK: between 2011 to 2013 and 2022 to 2024 statistical bulletin, published by the Office of National Statistics.
answer_text: Improving healthy life expectancy, along with reducing the health inequalities in this area, remains a particular challenge which my Department actively seeks to address through all its activity.The Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE) statistics released by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) continue to be of use to my Department in determining how the outcomes and recent trends in Northern Ireland compare with the rest of the UK.While these releases are welcomed, the official HLE estimates produced by my Department provide a greater scope for analysis and in supporting the development of policy and practice. While the ONS estimates source data from a general household survey, the official NI estimates are sourced from the Health Survey Northern Ireland (HSNI) and, as this is a health focused survey, the accompanying information that is collected allows for a much greater depth of analysis into the key drivers of inequality gaps and changes in HLE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps his Department took to communicate with parents, families and campaigners on the launch of his Department's consultation on restraint and seclusion in education; and (ii) any plans he has to promote and encourage parental engagement throughout the consultation's lifespan.
answer_text: (i) My Department undertook extensive steps to ensure that the launch of the consultation on restraint and seclusion in education was widely publicised and accessible to all interested parties, including parents, families and campaigners.A departmental press release was issued and the consultation was prominently highlighted on the Department’s website and social media channels. Information was disseminated to schools, education bodies and stakeholder organisations. Schools were requested to share the consultation with parents and carers through their usual communication systems. These measures were designed to ensure that all parents and members of the public had equal opportunity to be made aware of the consultation.In addition to these wide-reaching arrangements, the Department also contacted a small number of parent campaigners directly by email.  The department made contact with these parents as an exceptional step, reflecting their sustained engagement on the issue, in order to ensure that they were aware that the consultation had formally opened. This approach does not represent normal departmental protocol or practice in relation to the communications associated with the launch of a public consultation, which is to communicate with parents and the wider public through open and universally accessible channels. (ii) In relation to plans to promote and encourage parental engagement throughout the consultation’s lifespan, the Department is committed to ensuring that parents and carers feel able to contribute meaningfully to this process and officials remain open to engaging with all interested parties, including parents, families and campaigners who wish to discuss their experiences or provide feedback at any stage during the consultation.  Furthermore, the Department plans to hold a public consultation event which will be open to all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve healthy life expectancy in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) North Down, specifically.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. These policies also have a focus on health inequalities and are implemented on the ground by the Public Health Agency (PHA) throughout Northern Ireland. Within the Health Inequalities Team in PHA a range of interventions are commissioned across Health & Social Care Trusts, Councils and the Community & Voluntary sector to tackle health inequalities.The PHA fund a number of statutory and community & voluntary organisations within the North Down locality which have a specific focus on reducing health inequalities. These include:Peninsula Healthy Living Centre;North Down Community Network;Ards Community Network and Development Bureau; andA health & wellbeing project delivered by the South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust.These organisations deliver a wide range of health and wellbeing activities with a focus on improving physical activity, nutrition and mental health. The organisations use community development approaches in tackling health inequalities and reach a large number of people living in communities across Ards & North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be taking any action to address inconsistency in accessing ADHD support and medication through shared care arrangements between GPs and private healthcare providers following the February 2026 publication of the ADHD Needs Assessment Report.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department regulates the cost of court-ordered psychological assessments in family courts.
answer_text: My Department does not regulate the cost of court-ordered psychological assessments in family courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any processes that are available to children in care wishing to change their surname to that of their foster guardians without consent of their birth parent.
answer_text: Under the provisions of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, the name of a child in care cannot be changed by any person without the written consent of everyone who has parental responsibility for the child, or by leave of the courts.  Any child wishing to change their name without the permission of any birth parents who retain parental responsibility would need to apply to the courts to seek permission for the change.

answer_text: Children who are subject to a Care Order can for varying reasons be placed in the care of one or both biological parents. They can remain subject to the Care Order, and are deemed a child in care however they are not considered a child in foster care. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has specific guidance to ensure that children and young people with disabilities, including cerebral palsy, achieve recommended daily levels of physical activity.
answer_text: My Department does not have specific guidance to ensure that children and young people with disabilities, including cerebral palsy, achieve recommended daily levels of physical activity. This does not fall within the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure children and young people with neurological conditions have equitable access to therapeutic activity programmes, comparable to those available to their non-disabled peers.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for children with neurological conditions to access physiotherapy and related therapeutic interventions in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any provision his Department funds to ensure that children and young people with cerebral palsy have access to physical activity and therapeutic movement.
answer_text: All children with cerebral palsy in Northern Ireland are supported through existing commissioned paediatric and community services and receive individualised therapy programmes following specialist assessment delivered through a multidisciplinary team (MDT) including medical, nursing, allied health professional and psychology input.  Clinical psychology within the neurodisability service plays a key role in identifying barriers and supporting the person, family, and MDT to overcome them.  The assessments provided by the service are specifically used to guide the MDT care plans and interventions together with relevant adaptations consistent with the child’s cognitive and communication needs.The Regional Children’s Neurodisability Service in Belfast HSC Trust provides specialist oversight for children and young people with cerebral palsy and complex neurodevelopmental conditions, working in partnership with community paediatric teams and locally based services. A specialist nursing workforce supports children and families through the Regional Neurodisability Service and across community and school settings, contributing to co-ordinated care.Children and young people with cerebral palsy may also receive input from other specialist services, including paediatric neurology, orthopaedics and respiratory medicine, to support holistic management of co-morbidities and planned interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the early intensive therapy programmes there are for babies and children with cerebral palsy and other neurological conditions.
answer_text: All children with cerebral palsy in Northern Ireland are supported through existing commissioned paediatric and community services and receive individualised therapy programmes following specialist assessment delivered through a multidisciplinary team (MDT) including medical, nursing, allied health professional and psychology input.  Clinical psychology within the neurodisability service plays a key role in identifying barriers and supporting the person, family, and MDT to overcome them.  The assessments provided by the service are specifically used to guide the MDT care plans and interventions together with relevant adaptations consistent with the child’s cognitive and communication needs.The Regional Children’s Neurodisability Service in Belfast HSC Trust provides specialist oversight for children and young people with cerebral palsy and complex neurodevelopmental conditions, working in partnership with community paediatric teams and locally based services. A specialist nursing workforce supports children and families through the Regional Neurodisability Service and across community and school settings, contributing to co-ordinated care.Children and young people with cerebral palsy may also receive input from other specialist services, including paediatric neurology, orthopaedics and respiratory medicine, to support holistic management of co-morbidities and planned interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has provided any guidance to the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service in relation to electric vehicle fires.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service develops its procedures in line with National Fire Chiefs Council National Operational Guidance and is currently finalising dedicated guidance for managing electric vehicle related incidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department provides guidance to multi-storey car park providers, regarding (i) electric vehicle safety; and (ii) the installation of electric vehicle charging points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise on guidance in relation to petrol and diesel station forecourts.Multi storey car parks must comply with building regulations, which are governed by the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has put in place any guidance on the safe installation of electric vehicle charging points, in petrol and diesel station forecourts.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise on guidance in relation to petrol and diesel station forecourts.Multi storey car parks must comply with building regulations, which are governed by the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department regulates the safe manufacturing and make-up of electric vehicles in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Vehicle manufacturing standards including electric vehicles are governed by vehicle type approval.Type approval is a reserved matter. The British Secretary of State for Transport has primary responsibility for type approval. As a result of the Protocol, EU law continues to apply here for the purposes of type approval.Consequently, vehicles sold in the north must either carry EU or UKNI type approval certification.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement their Department has had with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology regarding the recommendations arising from the Creating a safer world: the challenge of regulating online pornography report, relevant to ending violence against women and girls.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Marine Protected Areas Strategy for the Northern Ireland Inshore Region 2025-2030 will support the conservation of vital habitats and improve biodiversity.
answer_text: My recently published Marine Protected Areas Strategy outlines the approach which will be taken for the next five years as the focus shifts from designation of marine protected areas to their management.  It prioritises key habitats and species, improving monitoring and evidence-led decision-making, and promotes nature-based solutions such as blue carbon habitats to enhance climate resilience and support biodiversity recovery. The strategy also recognises the valuable ecosystem services provided by MPAs and the long-term benefits they deliver for coastal communities, while enabling the sustainable use of the wider marine environment.There will be a small number of new designations required to fill gaps in the existing network and improve ecological coherence. This will be supported by management measures, where necessary, to ensure features are able to achieve favourable condition and contribute to Good Environmental Status under UK Marine Strategy. My Department is also developing a proposal to prohibit anchoring in Outer Belfast Lough Marine Conservation Zone to protect the designated features.  This will be subject to public consultation in due course. 		Building on these strong foundations and using actions set out in both the new MPA Strategy and the Northern Ireland Blue Carbon Action Plan, we will continue working in partnership with stakeholders to restore damaged habitats, enhance biodiversity and support a healthier, more resilient marine environment for the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme schemes for 2026-27, will be published.
answer_text: The NI Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) is administered by the Energy Saving Trust on behalf of the NI Utility Regulator.  Whilst it is not a DfE programme, I can report that the list of NISEP 2026/27 schemes will be published in early April 2026 and will run from 1st April 26 - 31 March 27.The List will be published on the Utility Regulator website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any works carried out; and (ii) the amount spent, on maintenance to the seawall at The Parade and Shore Street, Donaghadee, over the last 10 years.
answer_text: Over the past 10 years, my Department has carried out a number of interventions to the section of seawall along Parade, Donaghadee for which it is responsible. These have included routine upkeep and repairs following major weather events, such as those completed in January 2022 and January 2026 to address storm-related stone displacement and footway defects.More recently, in February 2026, my Department carried out further repairs to both the seawall and the adjoining footway following structural damage caused by Storm Chandra. The value of maintenance works recorded by the Department on this section of the seawall over the last 10 years is approximately £20,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how effectively the Managed Care Network’s Strategic Framework and three-year implementation plan will address the ambitions for eating disorders services improvements, as detailed in the Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: The Eating Disorder Managed Care Network’s Strategic Framework and three-year implementation plan provides a comprehensive and well coordinated foundation, for delivering the improvements to eating disorder services envisioned within the Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031. The Framework establishes clear regional governance through dedicated workstreams and leadership structures, ensuring consistent oversight and alignment across all Trusts. It strengthens the capability and confidence of the wider system by introducing structured training pathways and a coherent communication approach, both of which are essential to addressing variation in awareness, early identification and management of eating disorders. The plan’s focus on aligning and standardising care pathways across the region represents a significant step toward delivering equitable, NICE concordant treatment, while the development of regional supervision, reflective practice and training supports workforce sustainability and high-quality care. The Framework also enhances the system’s capacity for data driven decision making through the implementation of standardised outcome measures and improved use of baseline performance data, providing the evidence base required for future commissioning and service planning. The strong emphasis on coproduction ensures that lived experience informs the design, delivery and evaluation of services in a meaningful and continuous way. While the plan cannot independently deliver the full-service expansion, such as enhanced community and day treatment, without further funding and staffing investment, it effectively positions the region to implement these strategic enhancements efficiently and consistently, once resources become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any rates paid by (i) the Flagship Shopping Centre, Main Street, Bangor; and (ii) the units therein, since 2019.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer for AQW 41100/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any consideration being given to a Troubles memorialisation programme.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she intends to increase investment in speed detection measures, including fixed and mobile cameras, following the finding that one quarter of respondents identify speeding as their main concern in the Road Safety Issues in Northern Ireland, 2024-25 survey.
answer_text: I want to use this opportunity to express my condolences to the families of the people who have recently lost their lives on our roads. Lives changed in an instant. In 2026 15 people have not returned home following a road traffic collision, this is why road safety remains a top priority for me. The policy regarding any review of speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I would direct the member to contact the Partnership directly for this information. In light of the results of this Road Safety Issues survey, it is timely that my Department has recently launched an updated Road Safety Action plan. I continue to address the road safety issues that people are most concerned with and that are key in reducing road deaths.You will be aware that a Speed Limit review is currently underway and I would encourage everyone to take the time to respond. Other key interventions I am intending to introduce will include a new lower legal alcohol limit, examining options for increasing speeding fines, and the development of new scheme proposals on the Regional Strategic Transport Network, targeting those routes with highest collision rates where engineering-based solutions would mitigate risks.Alongside this, my Department continues to educate and promote safer road user behaviour, including the ‘Road Wise Up’ campaigns and ongoing ‘Share the Road to Zero’ initiatives, that remind us all of our personal responsibility on the road, and the things we can do to keep ourselves and other safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has engaged with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in Northern Ireland in relation to her December 2025 report, Totally Invisible, into the experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims and survivors and children engaging with family court processes.
answer_text: I met with the Commissioner Designate in relation to this research in October 2025 and spoke at the launch event. I have subsequently nominated a number of representatives from my Department to take part in a Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Working Group to examine what practical steps could be taken and the barriers that may need to be overcome, to make sure the family courts consistently protect victims of domestic abuse and their children from further traumatisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in Northern Ireland's Totally Invisible report, into the experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims and survivors, and children engaging with family court processes.
answer_text: I recognise the seriousness of the issues raised in the Totally Invisible report concerning the experiences of victims and survivors of domestic violence and abuse, and of children involved in private family law proceedings. The report highlights concerns about delays, consistency of practice, and a perceived “pro-contact” culture within parts of the family justice system. These are important matters that deserve full and careful consideration.My Department is responsible for the underlying substantive private family law, including the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995. Responsibility for court operations, judicial training, safeguarding practice, administration of contact services, or the wider family justice system, issues which the report highlights, are matters that fall to other Departments.I have noted those aspects of the report that relate to the legislative framework and I am committed to ensuring that framework remains appropriate and responsive. To support cross-departmental work, I have nominated an official from my Department to participate in the Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Group being established by the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has engaged with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in Northern Ireland regarding their Totally Invisible report into the experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims and survivors and children engaging with family court processes.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the issues raised in the Totally Invisible report published by the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, which highlights the experiences of domestic abuse victims, survivors and children who interact with the family courts. While many of the report’s findings relate to the operation of the family justice system, the concerns raised merit careful cross-departmental consideration. In relation to engagement, my Department has directly liaised with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime on this work. I have agreed that an official from the Department will participate in the Commissioner’s Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Group, which is being established to scope, design and cost a pilot model for private family law cases involving domestic abuse. My Department will continue to work collaboratively with other Departments to help ensure that the legislative framework remains appropriate and responsive. Through our participation in the Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Group and our membership of wider cross-departmental forums, we will play our part in supporting improvements across the family justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in Northern Ireland December 2025 report, Totally Invisible, into the experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims and survivors and children engaging with family court processes.
answer_text: I spoke at the launch of the “Totally Invisible” report on 3rd December 2025 and welcomed the research. I am concerned in relation to the findings of the research, that;There is a lack of understanding of domestic abuse dynamics among some professionals.There is inconsistent application of safeguarding measures, leaving victims and children at risk of being harmed.There are delays and complexity in proceedings, which can exacerbate trauma for victims and prolong exposure to risk.I have nominated representatives from my Department to participate in and contribute to a Family Court Pilot Task & Finish Working Group that the Commissioner for Victims of Crime, Geraldine Hanna, is convening to address findings of the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on waiting times for a gynaecology appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust. [R]
answer_text: The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Gynaecology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the Excel file which accompanies the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 31 December 2025 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-timesThe number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Gynaecology, Gynaecological Oncology, and Gynaecology - Urology specialties, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the Excel file which accompanies the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient And Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 31 December 2025 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-times

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his Department’s Equality Action Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) to detail how the plan will address gender discrimination in healthcare settings.
answer_text: The Department of Health published its 5-year Equality Action Plan (EAP) 2025-2030 on 19 December 2025. The first annual review of this plan will begin in April 2026. The EAP 2025-2030 identified four priority areas for action in relation to gender inequality. These include: women and girls being disproportionately affected by domestic and sexual abuse; barriers experienced by some women in accessing healthcare; men being disproportionately impacted in the justice system in relation to physical and mental health; andmen being disproportionately affected by substance misuse.While the actions linked to these priority areas are not designed as direct responses to individual cases of gender-based discrimination, they include relevant departmental frameworks, plans and strategies aimed at addressing systemic causes of gender inequality and improving outcomes across these areas. Where evidence of genderbased discrimination arises within healthcare settings, it can be considered by the relevant policy areas and provider organisations under existing governance and complaints structures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department engaged with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government on its 2025 consultation, Private parking code of practice.
answer_text: My department did not engage with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government on its 2025 consultation, Private Parking Code of Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether the British Parking Association’s Code of Practice adequately promotes meeting the needs and requirements of those with disabilities.
answer_text: My Department is leading on development of the cross-departmental Executive Disability Strategy which is currently out for public consultation.All issues identified as a result of the consultation, including an assessment of whether the British Parking Association’s Code of Practice adequately promotes meeting the needs and requirements of those with disabilities, will be looked at on a cross-departmental basis and subsequently by the Disability Forum which is to be established upon NI Executive approval of the finalised Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has engaged with the Department for Infrastructure regarding disability access within private parking infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department is leading on development of the cross-departmental Executive Disability Strategy which is currently out for public consultation.While the issue of disability access within private parking infrastructure has not yet been specifically discussed, all issues identified as a result of the consultation, including those related to disability access within private parking infrastructure, will be looked at on a cross-departmental basis and subsequently by the Disability Forum which is to be established upon NI Executive approval of the finalised Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the establishment of the Review of the Religious Education Core Syllabus drafting group.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of the Woman's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: I plan to publish the Women's Health Action Plan following completion of the public listening exercise.  My Department has helped to fund this important project by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast who have now completed a women’s health survey and focus groups with harder to reach women.  There will be a further round of engagement in partnership with stakeholders and representative organisations during February to share the findings and to identify the priorities and recommendations which will inform the final report to my Department. The Plan when published will describe the wide range of policy and service developments supporting women's health, what has been achieved to date and what is achievable in the years ahead within current resources. In addition to delivering against the set of actions, the Plan aims to encourage positive dialogue around women’s health and wellbeing within our health and social care system and in our society.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department will consult the original co-design partners. on the second delivery plan, for the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: The draft second Delivery Plan 2026-2028 has been informed by feedback from delivery partners which indicates that current delivery models, particularly around the Change Fund, are working well. We have also reflected any lessons learned from the first phase of delivery. The second Plan will continue to focus on community investment, collaboration and campaigns in line with the objectives of the Strategic Framework, with a focus on Prevention. This approach will allow us to build on momentum established through the first Delivery Plan, ensuring continuity, consolidating good practice and lessons learned and will allow for the scaling of initiatives that have proven effective.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps their Department will take to evaluate the 2024-2026 Delivery Plan associated with the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: Officials continue to evaluate delivery of the 2024–2026 Delivery Plan through a structured framework combining populationlevel indicators with quarterly actionlevel monitoring. Programmelevel activity is monitored through OutcomesBased Accountability (OBA) reporting, which assesses the performance of delivery actions across partners. Lessons learned are identified as part of this process and have been used to inform the development of the next Delivery Plan 2026-2028 which we intend to bring to the Executive for approval shortly.Official statistics and survey data, which are reported annually, are also evaluated to help measure the longterm progress toward the six strategic outcomes set out in the Strategic Framework. As well as the ongoing monitoring of outcomes, a mid-term review of the Strategic Framework will be taken forward which will include an evaluation of the 2024-2026 delivery plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has taken any preparatory steps, to create the second delivery plan, accompanying the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: Significant work has been undertaken in preparation for the next Delivery Plan including consideration of lessons learned from the 2024-2026 delivery plan, engagement with collaborative partners and key stakeholders as well as internal governance approvals to support the plan. Next steps include presentation of the draft plan which we have approved in principle to the wider Executive for their approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of applications to; and (ii) the total successful amount issued from, the DFI Claims Unit, in the North Down area, for each year, since 2023.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025.I would ask the member to be aware of the caveats relating to the information provided in the tables. Table 3 provides details of roads related public liability claims received in each of the last five full financial years, broken down by Section Office and claim type.  Table 7 provides details of claim expenditure for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office, claim type and expenditure type. Please note Section Offices broadly align with Council areas. The North Down area is covered by Ards and North Down Borough Council. This council area is covered by Ards and North Down Section Office.Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI. Please note that claims received in a financial year are not always concluded in the same financial year. Therefore, the compensation figure for concluded claims for a given financial year will usually include claims that were received in prior financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department has taken to repair the partially collapsed seawall at Parade, Donaghadee.
answer_text: Following a recent period of severe weather and tidal surges along the North Down coastline, a short section of wall under my Department’s ownership partially collapsed on Thursday 5th February. Officials closed an adjacent section of footway and nearby car parking in the interest of public safety until repairs could be assessed and undertaken. I am pleased to confirm that the repair work which commenced on 10 February has now been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will undertake a safety assessment of the remaining seawalls at Parade, Donaghadee, following a partial collapse.
answer_text: Following a recent period of severe weather and tidal surges along the North Down coastline, a short section of wall under my Department’s ownership partially collapsed on Thursday 5th February. Officials closed an adjacent section of footway and nearby car parking in the interest of public safety until repairs could be assessed and undertaken. I am pleased to confirm that the repair work which commenced on 10 February has now been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department has taken to adapt the infrastructure of coastal towns and villages, to mitigate the impacts of climate change.
answer_text: I am, along with the Minister for Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs, co-chair of the Coastal Forum. When the Forum last met, in November 2025, good progress had been and the coastal forum working group will produce a stocktake report on the current work programme and make recommendations for the 2026-2031 work programme. This will provide an opportunity to set priorities for the next five years and consider new work areas. My department also inspects and assesses the impact of coastal erosion on the public road network, including its sea walls and other coastal infrastructure where it meets the coastline and will make use of any relevant information from the LiDAR survey.Where coastal erosion or flooding impacts directly on key infrastructure such as roads, railways and the Department’s sea defences, these assets are managed through a programme of regular inspection and appropriate maintenance. The Department’s responsibilities for coastal management are limited to approximately 110 km of defences for coastal roads, 26 km of coastal sea defences, two tidal barriers and around 34 km of sea defences protecting the railway network, which are maintained as necessary to ensure safe operation and to mitigate, where practicable, the impacts of climate change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the work his Department is undertaking to tackle plastic pollution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Community-led Review on Rural Women and Girls as part of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024–2026.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason why St.Columbanus' College, Bangor, has been enrolled in the School Enhancement Programme, rather than the Major Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: The second call for projects to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) closed on 28 February 2017.  An application to SEP was made on behalf of St Columbanus’ College by the Catholic Council of Maintained Schools (CCMS).Following consideration of their options, the school confirmed that it was content to advance in design under SEP, rather than pursue a future application for a new-build project.  The school was announced to proceed in planning and design as a SEP project in January 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the business case for capital enhancement for Clifton School, Bangor.
answer_text: The business case in respect of the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at Clifton School was approved in November 2024 at a total cost of circa £5.2m.  The preferred option sought to address identified deficiencies through extension and refurbishment concentrating on provision of senior and transition years, nurture facilities and specialist classrooms.  Following my decision to set aside the capital threshold for SEP projects at special schools, the Education Authority has been working with my Department and the school to expand the scope of the project to better meet the needs of staff and pupils.  In addition to the originally approved option, the proposed enhanced project will deliver sports hall provision and four general classrooms as well as refurbishment of the school meals accommodation.  Due to the proposed change in scope and increased costs a business case addendum is now being developed. This will be subject to the necessary approvals.  A project board has been established to ensure that the school is kept informed of the progress of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the School Enhancement Programme proposals for St.Columbanus' College, Bangor.
answer_text: St Columbanus’ College was announced in January 2019 to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP). The project will provide a two-storey permanent teaching block for science, PE and drama, as well as a 3G multi use games area.  The project is currently at RIBA Stage 3 (Detailed Design).Funding is available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total staffing resource, allocated to her Department's Skills Initiative Branch, each year, since 2020-21.
answer_text: My Department’s Skills Initiatives Branch was established in 2021, details of the total staffing resource allocated are included on table 1. Year Skills Initiatives Branch staffing resource2021-2022£241,000       2022-2023£353,000       2023-2024£474,0002024-2025£639,0002025-2026£758,000Table 1. The Skills Initiatives Branch has delivered a range of strategically important programmes supported by multiple funding streams. In 2021, resources for programme delivery and staffing were provided by the Northern Ireland Office and the Executive’s Economic Recovery Action Plan.From 2022, the Branch assumed the partnership role for the Construction Industry Training Board, a Departmental arm’s length body. Its’ remit expanded further in 2023 to include the Multiply Programme, funded by the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities, and the Green Skills initiative linked to the Energy Strategy. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the procurement of a building contractor for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School's new school build.
answer_text: The procurement of a building contractor for the new school build is at an advanced stage, with appointment anticipated in the first quarter of 2026.  Construction works are expected to commence shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total end-year funding her Department has allocated towards encouraging uptake and growth of apprenticeships, for each year, since 2017-18.
answer_text: Total funding allocated per financial year since 2017-18 is as follows: 2017-18 - £172,8782018-19 - £256,7102019-20 - £225,6262020-21 - £187,0532021-22 - £247,3212022-23 - £166,5012023-24 - £201,9802024-25 - £650,062The above figures cover general promotion of apprenticeships, including twice yearly advertising campaigns, the NI Apprenticeship Awards and all associated design and printing costs.
