question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 35200/22-27, for an update on work to develop a tender for an operator of Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre and café.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) is the owner of Ballycopeland Windmill. DfC provided Construction and Procurement Delivery with a specification for an operator for the site and requested that a tender is issued for the contract. The procurement strategy and tender documentation are under development at present. It is envisaged the competition will be published on eTendersNI before 31 March 2026. Any further information in relation to this project should be directed to the Minister for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Department for Communities, to allow students to access universal credit and other forms of low income support.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with the Department for Communities to ensure that participation on an Assured Skills Academy would not have a detrimental impact on an individual’s award of Universal Credit.Additionally, a meeting was held between the two Departments on 4 October 2024 with regards to Further Education Student Support access, and whether those students from households claiming Universal Credit and other forms of low-income support were adversely impacted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of postgraduate maintenance grants and loans.
answer_text: Engagement is continuing on the development of draft Terms of Reference for a wider Review of Higher Education Funding.It is proposed that the review will include consideration of the financial support available to students, including whether current arrangements remain appropriate and whether support should be extended beyond its existing scope. The overall timeframe for the review will be determined once the Terms of Reference are finalised and subject to the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any funding her Department has allocated to universities and university colleges for student hardship support, for each year, over the last five years.
answer_text: The funding allocated in respect of financial hardship faced by students in local higher education institutions in each of the last five complete financial years, was as follows:Financial YearFunding2020/21£5.6m2021/22£10.3m2022/23£6.3m2023/24£3.8m2024/25£2.8mFinancial years 2020/21 to 2023/24 include additional targeted allocations in respect of the financial impact of Covid-19 and the subsequent cost of living crisis on students.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to alleviate student poverty, within further and higher education.
answer_text: Access to Further and Higher Education is key to addressing equality of opportunity and creating pathways to good jobs. This is particularly important for individuals who experience additional barriers to participation, such as people from a low-income background. A range of financial assistance is currently available for Further Education learners including Further Education Grants, the Hardship Fund, the Education Maintenance Allowance, free school meals and the Care to Learn childcare scheme.As a direct response to current student poverty, I have agreed additional funding this winter to continue the Further Education Sector Cost of Living/Winter Warmer scheme. This provides learners with Cost of Living information sessions including details of student support interventions and a warm meal.In the Higher Education sector, means-tested maintenance grants and loans are available to assist with living costs. Ordinarily, receipt of a maintenance grant reduces the level of loan available, however, for students eligible to claim means-tested benefits such as Housing Benefit or Universal Credit whilst studying, a Special Support Grant is available which does not impact on the amount of maintenance loan received.A range of targeted support for Higher Education students is available in the form of the Disabled Students’ Allowance, Childcare Grant, Parents’ Learning Allowance and Adult Dependants’ Grant. Additional support for students whose access to study might be inhibited by financial considerations, or who face financial difficulties in meeting their living costs, is available through Student Support Funds (also referred to as Hardship Funds), managed by the five local Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) on the Department’s behalf. My Department is currently undertaking a review of student support measures to ensure they continue to meet the needs of the most vulnerable in society to enable them to continue their education and training within further education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any circumstances in which (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time, students can access housing support, through the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the information requested from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:“We are unaware of any housing support directed specifically at or for students. All applicants seeking permanent rehousing under the Housing Selection Scheme (HSS) must meet the eligibility criteria set out in Chapter 2 of the HSS Guidance manual. These requirements areAgeConnection to Northern IrelandEligibility (Unacceptable Behaviour, Persons From Abroad)If the applicant satisfies these requirements, regardless of their educational status, they can access housing/homelessness support and will be assessed under the HSS based on their individual circumstances. As noted, for any full-time and part-time students, the nature of support provided will be based on their individual circumstances and Housing Advisors will ensure that all appropriate advice and support is provided. Housing BenefitHousing Benefit for working age customers is being replaced by the housing costs element of Universal Credit.  New Housing Benefit claims for working age customers are only possible if the customer lives in temporary or supported accommodation.For eligible students who become homeless, they can make a claim for Housing Benefit.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the funding in place beyond March 2026 for the delivery of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any progress made on the introduction of statutory paid leave for miscarriage, including the expected timetable for bringing forward the necessary regulations.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to facilitate rapid charging for older models of electric vehicles, that utilise the CHAdeMo plug.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the further plans within their wider networks. My Department engages regularly with the Electric Vehicle Association of Northern Ireland (EVANI) on issues affecting EV owners and the number of CCS chargers (Combined Charging System) being installed is beginning to increase. Drivers who use the CHAdeMO plug may wish to consider an adapter that enables access to other charger types. EVANI has previously published a review of one such adapter that can be found here: The CCS2 to CHAdeMO Adaptor Review – EVANIDepartment Officials will continue to highlight this issue in our ongoing stakeholder discussions with CPOs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any actions his Department is taking to address recruitment and retention challenges in the youth sector, including (a) support for workforce development; (b) pay and conditions; (c) training pathways; and (ii) how these actions align with the objectives of Priorities for Youth.
answer_text: Taking each of these matters in turn:(i) Any actions to address recruitment and retention challenges in the youth sector, including:(a) Support for workforce development In 2023 the Education Authority (EA)  implemented its Volunteer Development Strategy for 2023-2029 to develop and improve the recruitment and retention of volunteers in the youth work sector which has resulted in volunteer recruitment increasing from 18k in 2023 to 22k by 2025.Since 2022 the EA has provided grant aid assistance to the community and voluntary sector through Regional Strategic Funding to support Regional Voluntary Youth Organisations/Headquarter Bodies to provide workforce development support to their member organisations based on their needs. In addition,  the EA has provided bespoke grant aid assistance through Regional Development Funding to support Regional Voluntary Youth Organisations/Headquarter Bodies to provide accredited youth work training, including through the medium of Irish.The Education Authority Regional Youth Development Plan 2026-2029 is committing to working in partnership across the youth work sector to develop and implement a workforce development strategy as part of the next planning period. This strategy and subsequent delivery plan will consider actions to address recruitment and retention, workforce development, training pathways underpinned by a sectoral training needs analysis.(b) Pay and conditionsPay & conditions are nationally agreed (UK wide) with Trade union side (TUS) through the Joint Negotiating Council (JNC). The EA is contractually obliged to apply annual agreements once adopted by JNC.(c) Training pathwaysSince 2019 the EA has funded university fees through the ‘Pathways into a professional career in youth work’ programme. This provides paid employment in a youth work role whilst undertaking either an undergraduate or post-graduate study to obtain their Professional JNC Youth Work Qualification. (ii) How these actions align with the objectives of Priorities for Youth.Delivery of previous and future actions related to workforce development is aligned the current policy priority “Development of the Non-Formal Education Workforce”, informed by assessed need and actions identified in the Regional Youth Development Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the impact of criminal barristers’ withdrawal from legal aided Crown Court cases on victims and witnesses of crime.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on placements for children with special educational needs for the 2026/27 academic year.
answer_text: Ensuring every child with a statement of special educational needs has an appropriate school placement remains a primary focus for my department and the Education Authority.Since 2020, circa 6,350 additional SEN places have been created in total. 273 schools, approximately 26% of all schools in Northern Ireland, currently offer specialist provision. There are now over 700 specialist provision classes operating across a range of key stages. The EA's early planning for September 2026 identified a need for approximately 950 additional places. EA has been actively working with schools and has already initiated projects estimated to accommodate a significant number of additional placements across specialist provisions and special schools for 2026. The most recent update (as of 12 January 2026) is that there is a current shortfall of circa 365 places. The EA will continue to work with schools to expand SEN places and will continue to seek ways to encourage more schools to become involved. Reducing the cost and length of home to school transport, whilst enhancing positive integration across our school communities. Throughout November and December, the EA held engagement sessions both in person and online inviting over 300 schools in high-pressure areas to explore the development of specialist provisions within their school community, approximately 90 schools attended. EA is working with those schools that expressed an interest at the events to identify solutions.My department and the EA remain committed to the vision that every child with SEN has access to a placement, in their local community and with their peers.This will require a fundamental shift towards a system in which all schools offer specialist provision classes.In April 2024 I established the SEN capital investment programme to focus funding and resource with a view to proactively delivering additional SEN places and high-quality accommodation for pupils in both special schools and in specialist provision in mainstream schools.It is anticipated that investment of circa £93m will be required to deliver the level of accommodation required to meet the demand for SEN places in 2026/27, a significant increase on both of the previous years.  This funding currently comes from my Department’s allocated capital budget and, often at the expense of other, critical capital schemes across the school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the introduction of no fault divorce in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department held a public consultation to gather views on potential reforms to the law governing divorce and the dissolution of civil partnerships. The consultation ran from 23 June to 26 September 2025 and sought views on a range of possible changes.A total of 405 responses were received, comprising 384 online submissions and 21 written responses. A comprehensive analysis of the consultation has been finalised and will be available on the Departmental website shortly.The consultation has provided valuable insight into stakeholder views. I have now considered the findings in detail and prepared a paper setting out my proposals for reform of divorce and civil partnership dissolution. I have shared this paper with Ministerial colleagues for their consideration and comment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 35199/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35199/22-27 was answered on 8 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has installed any suicide prevention measures during the 2024/25 financial year.
answer_text: As part of my Department’s work in support of the Protect Life 2 Strategy, my officials continue to engage with stakeholders, including the Mental Health Champion, the Public Health Agency, local Councils and the Cross Departmental Coordinated Action Group, to explore options for progressing suicide prevention infrastructure. The Westlink, Belfast and Foyle Bridge, Derry have been identified as prevalent suicide spots where prevention and safety infrastructure could be installed. At the Westlink, my Department is working on a pilot to increase the height of the parapets on Clifton and Divis Street Bridges that has an estimated cost of £1.8m.  These parapets would be undertaken through the development of a green living wall instead of the normal metal railings that would be more pleasing to the eye, environmentally friendly and enhance the general appearance in the area.  I will be endeavouring to allocate funding in future years to try and get these schemes, that are so important for the local communities, pushed forward.  At Foyle Bridge, the proposed suicide deterrent measures include additional railings, however due to the complex nature of the structure, a detailed structural assessment must be undertaken to ensure that the bridge would be able to cope with the additional containment measures and increased wind resistance. I have now allocated funding to take forward the structural assessment of the Foyle Bridge and Departmental officials are now engaging with our consultant to progress this as soon as practicable.  I can confirm that my Department did not install any suicide prevention measures during the 2024/25 financial year but is continuing to work proactively with stakeholders to explore what we can do to support this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34236/22-27, to detail the reasons why the current infrastructure and inpatient accommodation Ward 10 North is not suitable to deliver CAR-T Therapy, including how it does not meet the British Society for Haematology and Royal College of Physicians established standards.
answer_text: The current infrastructure and inpatient accommodation available in Ward 10 North is unsuitable due to limitations in the number of isolation rooms, ensuite access and specialist protected (HEPA filtered with positive pressure) rooms. The environmental deficiencies of Ward 10 North, do not meet the British Society for Haematology and Royal College of Physicians established standards, which are endorsed by NICE. Furthermore, HTM (Health Technical Memoranda) 03-01 (2021); covers specialised ventilation for healthcare premises and recommends that rooms for haematology inpatients should have HEPA filtration and positive pressure for protective isolation. HBN (Health Building Note) 04-01 A (2013), covering adult-inpatient facilities, advises that it may be necessary to provide a higher level of isolation, particularly for those patients with airborne diseases or for immuno-suppressed patients who may be at risk of infection from others. In these cases, an isolation suite – which includes an entrance lobby, bedroom and en-suite sanitary facilities – will be required. HBN 04-01 Supplement 1 (2024): covers special ventilated isolation facilities for patients in acute settings. It sets out that among reasons to isolate in special ventilated isolation facilities are where a patient is known to be especially susceptible to infection or at risk of contamination from other sources, i.e. immunocompromised (protective isolation). It includes the following recommendations:•   Isolation rooms to be lobbied.•   Option for Positive pressure ventilated lobby (PPVL) isolation suite, can be used for source and protective isolation.•   Isolation room lobbies are recommended to be 6.5sqm •   The entire isolation suite envelope should be constructed to a 30-minute fire resisting.Currently, none of the 6 transplant rooms within Ward 10N BHSCT comply with the guidelines which pertain to isolation room requirements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether their Department (i) supported; and (ii) recognised, the 2025 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.
answer_text: The Executive Office fully supported and recognised the 16 Days of Activism against gender-based violence this year: reaffirming our unwavering commitment to ending violence against women and girls and supporting and engaging with partners. An Executive Statement was issued, and it made clear the determination of all Ministers to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm; and our social media channels were used to deliver targeted content to raise awareness and encourage action. This included six social media posts with accompanying images and one video. Ministers attended a number of events throughout the campaign period, including:‘Souls of Our Shoes’ exhibition in partnership with the Mothers’ Union, highlighting the lived experiences of survivors.Speakers’ event at Parliament Buildings, hosted with Women’s Aid Federation, marking International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women.Junior Ministers attended the launch of ‘Raise Your Game’ board game and a comprehensive 10-part toolkit - youth-designed resources developed by The Executive Office and the Education Authority. These resources are equipping young people to challenge and prevent violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department (i) supported; and (ii) recognised, the 2025 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.
answer_text: While the department did not run a specific campaign related to the 16 Day of Activism against Gender-Based Violence, it was both supported and recognised as part of the wider Northern Ireland Civil Service campaign, and the reaffirming of the Northern Ireland Executive commitment to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm, on 2 December.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether her Department (i) supported; and (ii) recognised the 2025 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.
answer_text: The 16 Days of Activism initiative has been coordinated by DoF on behalf of all Departments. Material was shared via emails to all staff and on the DfE intranet along with that of all other Departments. 		I welcomed the opportunity to speak at the ICTU Safe Leave Rally on 25 November around my commitment to introduce paid safe leave before the end of the Assembly mandate to enable workers who are victims and survivors of domestic abuse to access vital support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department (i) supported; and (ii) recognised, the 2025 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.
answer_text: My Department supported and recognised the 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence by publishing the wider Northern Ireland Civil Service campaign material during the period 20 November – 9 December.This series of intranet articles raised awareness of a range of issues and supports, as well as reaffirming the Northern Ireland Executive’s commitment to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether his Department (i) supported; and (ii) recognised, the 2025 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.

To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the community led review focusing on deaf and disabled women in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, as outlined in the 2024-2026 Delivery Plan.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) adopts an intersectional approach, recognising the specific barriers faced by deaf and disabled women and girls. As part of a series of community-led reviews, identified in the 2024-26 Delivery Plan, preparatory work is underway for the review focused on deaf and disabled women and girls. Initial engagement involving disability sector stakeholders is ongoing to ensure lived experience shapes the recommendations. This ongoing work will inform future actions and policy development under the EVAWG Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the creation of an ending violence against women and girls training portfolio for the Civil Service.
answer_text: Officials in NICSHR Learning and Development continue to support The Executive Office’s (TEO) Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Directorate with action 11 of their Delivery Plan 2024-2026. 		TEO has provided the draft content of an eLearning package for all NICS staff, focusing  on raising awareness of what EVAWG is and how to take action and respond. The intention is the training will be available to all NICS staff by April 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) an update on the administration of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund; and (ii) whether their Department plans to re-open applications in 2026.
answer_text: The Local Change Fund for 2025/26 supports grassroots organisations tackling violence against women and girls. All 11 Councils have established a Local Change Fund to provide grant funding directly to local community groups to understand what violence against women and girls is and take local action to tackle the issue. Currently 125 projects have been allocated funding under the Local Change Fund with a diverse range of activities and are expected to engage with over 48,000 participants. Additional funding has also been provided to Councils to address the oversubscription of Local Change Funds and support the implementation of local responses, raising awareness and building momentum. Engagement is ongoing with Councils to consider future delivery of the Local Change Fund under the next EVAWG Delivery Plan running from April 2026 to March 2028.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how their Department have reflected the programme requirements for the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls to the Department of Finance in the development of a multi-year budget.
answer_text: Programme requirements for the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls have been included within the recent 5 year spending review exercise commissioned by the Department of Finance. This includes details of potential future funding requirements to enable delivery of future delivery plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has engaged with informal education providers as part of his Department’s work to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Department is working to embed the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategy 2024-31 and the actions outlined within the associated delivery plan. As this work progresses, officials will engage with relevant stakeholders through the established EVAWG mechanisms.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on her Department’s Workplace working group to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Cross Sectoral Workplace Forum, established under the Executive’s Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG), brings together key stakeholders, including the Labour Relations Agency, Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, Diversity Mark, NIC Irish Congress of Trade Unions, and other partners, to support employers and employees in addressing violence against women and girls in the workplace.The Forum is actively engaged and are developing proposals which will align with the strategic aims of the EVAWG Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on her Department’s Safer Socialising working group to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Safer Socialising Steering Group was established under Action 8 of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan (2024-2026).  The group represents a broad cross section of stakeholders working together to improve safety for women and girls when socialising.We are committed to supporting and amplifying the work of partner organisations aligned in delivering EVAWG outcomes. The Safer Socialising Steering Group (SSSG) is one mechanism through which we are working to improve coordination and collaboration.  In recognition of this and of the importance of safe transportation in supporting the aims of the SSSG, we committed to providing £92,300 as part of a collaborative funding model to support the delivery of a pilot late night bus service in Belfast.  The cross sectoral group continues to work together to share expertise, align priorities and develop proposals to ensure women and girls feel safe and are safe everywhere, while socialising.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, for an update on the commissioning of their Department’s research on boys and men’s attitudes and behaviours in related to violence against women and girls.
answer_text: Research to identify evidence gaps in understanding attitudes and behaviours of men and boys related to violence against women and girls was commissioned on 19 May 2025 following a formal procurement process, with the contract awarded to Queen’s University Belfast. The research will focus on young men and boys aged 16-24. The research process is currently underway, including engagement with stakeholders, development of a scoping review, interviews with organisations who work with young men and boys, focus groups with young men and boys, and a survey. The results are expected to be published in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the staffing capacity of the Ending Violence and Girls division within their Department.
answer_text: There are currently 20.86 full time equivalent (FTE) staff in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Division.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department’s Tertiary Working Group to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: My Department recognises the critical importance of tackling violence against women and girls (VAWG) and is committed to supporting this Programme for Government priority. To this end, a Tertiary Working Group has been established to harness the collective expertise, resources, and influence of the Tertiary Education sector and its partners, enabling the sector to contribute strategically and collectively to tackling this issue.The Group is chaired by Ulster University and includes representatives from higher education institutions, further education colleges, student unions, the PSNI, health and wellbeing professionals, and other key stakeholders. The Group’s remit is to review, develop, and oversee the implementation of comprehensive interventions to prevent and respond to VAWG within our colleges and universities.The Group has met on several occasions since its establishment and has held a sector-wide workshop to identify priorities and share best practice. Sub-groups have been established to focus on policy, training, and reporting, with cross-institutional membership. A key milestone in the Group’s work was the delivery of a sector-wide Capability Building Day, held on 8 December 2025. This full-day event was designed to strengthen the capacity of members and sector leaders by providing expert insights into developing and embedding effective institutional policies, implementing best practice in prevention, including Bystander Intervention, and managing disclosures of sexual violence and supporting survivors.The Group continues to work collaboratively to identify gaps, map training provision, and develop shared actions to progress this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent by his Department on programmes to support mental health in North Down, in the last five years.
answer_text: Constituency boundaries are not co-terminus with HSC Trust areas.The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) have indicated that breaking down the amount spent for a single sector, i.e. North Down would involve a significant data extraction and a detailed financial review, therefore the amount spent on programmes to support Mental Health in North Down is not readily available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the length of the waiting list for cognitive behavioural therapy in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust does not hold a list of individuals waiting for Cognitive Behavioural Therapy. Individuals on waiting lists for Psychological Therapies will be assessed once they are seen and the resulting model of therapeutic provision agreed upon will be dependent on this assessment. Cognitive Behavioural Therapy is one of a range of Psychological Therapies which may be offered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether confirmation of address is the only mechanism for pharmacists to verify the identity of those collecting prescriptions.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance his Department have given to pharmacists on the process to verify the identity of those collecting prescriptions.
answer_text: For patient safety and governance issues my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) support community pharmacies in having a procedure in place to verify patient identity when collecting prescriptions. I can confirm the legacy Health and Social Care Board has issued guidance on this, notably as a campaign entitled “Are you the right person?” which featured counter-top stands and posters in community pharmacies. Advice was issued again in July 2020 via a formal learning letter, in response to reports of errors due to miscommunication in the environment of screens, face-masks and people collecting medications for others in the community. This recommended community pharmacies take steps to mitigate against errors, providing the example of asking patients or their representatives to state their address back to them. 	In addition, as part of the SPPG Community Pharmacy Assurance Framework, an assurance is asked that that there is a Standard Operating Procedure in place in all community pharmacies for the hand out of prescriptions. Whilst patient safety and verifying the prescription medications are being handed to the intended person is paramount, there is an expectation that patient confidentiality should be respected. This is outlined in the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland Professional Standards and Guidance for Patient Confidentiality which states all reasonable steps should be taken to ensure appropriate levels of privacy for patient consultations so that confidential information is not overheard or accessed by others.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that NI Water has paused works to increase capacity in wastewater treatment plants to prevent untreated pollution entering Belfast Lough, to outline any action she will take to ensure the necessary funding is provided to allow these essential works to proceed.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s work to develop a Barnahus model.
answer_text: The Barnahus Working Group, jointly chaired by the Department of Health and Justice, held a workshop in June 2025 to consider an options paper for a Barnahus model in Northern Ireland.  A programme of wider stakeholder engagement in relation to the options paper is currently ongoing and plans are in place to engage with people with lived experience.  Officials in both Departments are also currently working to determine the relevant high level costs involved.  I understand that a final options paper will be presented to both the Justice Minister and myself for consideration in due course.  Implementation of any model will be subject to appropriate funding and resources being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the future work programme of the Coastal Forum.
answer_text: During the recent meeting of the Coastal Forum on 19 November, the Coastal Forum agreed that the Coastal Forum Working Group would produce a stocktake report to include  recommendations for a future Work Programme covering 2026-2031. These recommendations will then be considered by the Coastal Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department is supporting the creation of entry level employment opportunities for young people in the hospitality sector.
answer_text: In January this year, my Department launched the Tourism Vision & Action Plan which sets out our ambition for growth over the next 10 years. One of the actions is to develop a bespoke skills action plan for the sector and my officials have established a Tourism Skills Delivery Group to ensure there is adequate skills training provision to meet the requirements of job roles at all levels, now and in the future.The Vision highlights that tourism, including the hospitality sector, has the potential to create substantial employment opportunities right across the North and within every section of our society. It provides a career path for school leavers into management and leadership roles that is rarely found across other sectors; a route to greater social mobility for many within our most deprived urban and rural communities; and the soft skills that young people are equipped with when working in the tourism and hospitality industry are highly transferable to a wide range of careers.I am committed to supporting young people into entry-level roles including those within the hospitality sector. My Department delivers a range of programmes that create clear pathways to employment. Skills for Life and Work (SfLW) and Traineeships provide a non-employed route to gaining Level 1–3 qualifications, helping participants build essential skills and confidence. For those ready to enter the workforce, ApprenticeshipsNI offers an employed route, enabling individuals to earn while they learn and progress within the sector.My Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan, published on 11 August 2025, directly supports the creation of more entry-level jobs for young people, including within the hospitality and tourism sector.  Together, these initiatives will ensure that people of all ages have flexible options to start and develop their careers in hospitality.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the topics discussed at the most recent meeting of the Coastal Forum.
answer_text: The most recent Coastal Forum, held on Wednesday 19th November, discussed the following topics:The Coastal Monitoring and Adaptation Planning project funded by PEACE PLUS. How the PEACE PLUS project will contribute to the Climate Change Committee’s recommendation on shoreline management plans. An update from the Coastal Forum Working Group, including progress with the Coastal Forum’s Work Programme and considerations for a future Work Programme.A review of the Coastal Forum’s Terms of Reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34238/22-27, for a breakdown of the number of (i) adults; and (ii) those under-18 who are registered with NHS dental care in North Down.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has engaged with the UK Government on their upcoming Strategy to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to identify areas for collaboration and sharing of best practice.
answer_text: Ending violence against women and girls is a global challenge, and we are aware that significant steps are being taken in each of our neighbouring jurisdictions.EVAWG officials regularly engage with counterparts through the Five Jurisdictions’ Forum, which brings together representatives from the NICS, Home Office, Scottish Government, Welsh Government, and the Government of Ireland. The forum was established to enable officials from each of those jurisdictions to look at national developments and provide the opportunity to share information, particularly regarding lessons learned and best practice. We also continue to use established structures, such as the British Irish Council, to collaborate on related EVAWG initiatives.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has any plans to review and update the Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The ‘Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework’ was published in August 2021 and updated in December 2023 following consultation with multi-agency partners.A review of the Framework will commence in early 2026.  It will be informed by lessons identified through recent responses, such as Storm Eowyn and Amy, along with findings from the COVID-19 Inquiry; and our recent exercising of the ‘Northern Ireland Central Crisis Management Arrangements’ during Exercise Pegasus.The review will aim to strengthen resilience and enhance coordination across the NICS and with partner agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the implementation of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Community Pharmacy Care Home Support Service, in North Down.
answer_text: The community pharmacy care home support service (CPCHSS) provided by participating community pharmacies provides support on the safe handling and administration of medicines to residents in nursing and residential care homes across NI. This service provides two scheduled visits between the care home and their paired community pharmacy each financial year. In 2023/24, 407 nursing or residential homes that are registered with RQIA in Northern Ireland benefited from support via the Community Pharmacy Care Home Support Service. Ninety-three of the homes were located in the South Eastern area. Data is not held which can identify how many were in the North Down area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support her Department have provided to Translink, to prepare public transport and the rail network, for adverse winter weather conditions.
answer_text: Translink has a comprehensive winter preparedness programme aimed at maintaining safe and reliable public transport and rail services during adverse weather conditions such as snow, ice, flooding, and high winds. For bus operations, measures include an annually reviewed Extreme Weather Procedure, winter driving safety bulletins, and real-time monitoring. Bus stations maintain salting and gritting arrangements with reserve stocks and contracted services to ensure safe access. For rail services, planning begins in early autumn and includes railhead treatment trains, traction gel applicators, and de-icing of critical infrastructure. Operational adjustments such as speed restrictions and winter safety briefings are applied as needed. Rail infrastructure resilience is supported through remote monitoring of signalling systems, heating mechanisms for level crossings and points, and backup power supplies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Shared Island Fund is facilitating cross-border cooperation within agriculture, environment and rural affairs.
answer_text: The Shared Island Fund is delivering practical benefits through enhanced cooperation between Northern Ireland and Ireland across agriculture, environment, and rural affairs. Key initiatives include:		The Shared Island Pollinator Plan: A €3.86 million programme, including €1.8 million Shared Island funding, to halt and reverse pollinator decline through coordinated monitoring and action across the island.			Peatlands and Natura Community Networks: Collaboration between NIEA and the Republic’s National Parks and Wildlife Service on peatland restoration and ecosystem recovery, supported by allocations under the Shared Island Initiative Peatland Programme and future Natura Communities Network funding.			An All-Island Biosecurity and Invasive Species Initiative: A five-year project to strengthen biosecurity and manage invasive species through joint risk assessments, awareness campaigns, and catchment-level measures.			A Bovine Tuberculosis Regionalisation Pilot: A cross-border project jointly developed by my department and the Department of Agriculture, Fisheries and the Marine to test a regionalised approach to bTB eradication across people, wildlife and cattle measures. The Irish Government has allocated €6.4 million to support this initiative from the Shared Island fund. These projects demonstrate how Shared Island funding is enabling knowledge exchange, resource pooling, and coordinated action to address shared challenges, support innovation, and build a sustainable future for communities across the island.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any guidance his Department provides to sports groups on the abuse of trust in youth sporting settings.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has produced Children and Adult Safeguarding Guidance for DfC sponsored Arms Length Bodies.  The guidance (available on the Department’s website) is intended to inform DfC sponsored ALBs and staff of their responsibilities under child protection and adults at risk legislation and point them to further sources of information and advice should they need it.As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the interim Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI has long supported the NSPCC campaign to change the law and close the loophole created when sports coaches were excluded from the initial criteria around “abuse of trust” positions and we celebrated and communicated the change in the law when it came. Sports organisations have been asked for years to ensure their codes of conduct reflected the breach of a position of trust as a reason to remove a coach/official before the law even changed. Since the law changed  in NI, Sport NI via our contract with the NSPCC’s Child Protection in Sport Unit (CPSU) have shared the following information with all National Governing Bodies. Preventing abuse in positions of trust in sport and activity: https://thecpsu.org.uk/resource-library/best-practice/preventing-abuse-in-positions-of-trust-in-sport-and-activity/ Briefing papers for both the sport and dance sector Positions of trust – FAQs about the changes:  https://thecpsu.org.uk/resource-library/best-practice/positions-of-trust-faqs-about-the-changes/ Sport NI have also communicated what is good practice via our Safeguarding Children and Young People in Sport workshop delivered to sports coaches across NI”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) location of, Changing Places toilets in hospitals in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust currently has one Changing Places toilet located at the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department has taken to improve the accessibility of railway stations in North Down, since 2020.
answer_text: In June 2025, Translink completed the access ramp at Marino station, that is compliant with the Disability Discrimination Act.I can also advise that Translink is undertaking a project to develop a strategy for improving accessibility across its rail network. This initiative will involve an assessment of accessibility features at all locations across the rail network. The project has included stakeholder engagement with Disabled People’s Organisations to ensure the work being undertaken reflects their needs and priorities. This project will help to inform future investment in the rail network, subject to the availability of future funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26399/22-27, to detail how his Department engages with (i) Northern Ireland Electricity; and (ii) British Oxygen Company, to ensure fair reimbursement for patients.
answer_text: There is no direct engagement between my Department and Northern Ireland Electricity. My Department manages the procured oxygen service, currently provided by BOC (British Oxygen Company) Homecare Service. As part of this service, BOC reimburses each patient fully for the electricity used by their home oxygen concentrator on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's work to address gender bias and discrimination in the healthcare system.
answer_text: Gender bias and discrimination is not measured or quantified across health and social care in Northern Ireland, however, I do accept there are systemic inequalities and certain groups of people face additional barriers to accessing healthcare.  I also understand from qualitative feedback received through engagement on the Women’s Health Action Plan, that many women report feeling dismissed or their pain normalised when seeking help, similar to the experiences reported last year in the publication from the UK Parliament Women and Equalities Committee on women’s reproductive health conditions.  My Department is currently considering how to – subject to resources - provide more targeted clinical education about conditions affecting women’s health and wellbeing and to improve awareness and understanding among the wider public to help remove the stigma associated with these health conditions.My Department will also be publishing an updated equality action plan for 2025-2030 which will outline work across the HSC to tackle inequality and discrimination in relation to all section 75 groups including gender.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the tender process to procure a contractor for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School's new build.
answer_text: The procurement of a building contractor for the new build school is underway.   Due to clarification requests from tenderers, it was necessary to extend the tender return deadline to the end of November 2025. It is still anticipated that construction will commence on site in the first quarter of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the timeline for the feasibility study for Grey Point Fort.
answer_text: Work on the feasibility study commenced in October 2025, it is scheduled to be completed by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29265/22-27, to detail whether all education settings are expected to have a bereavement policy, for both staff and pupils.
answer_text: Schools are not required to have a ‘bereavement policy’, however as part of the broader pastoral care arrangements they may have in place a clear structure and process to be followed if a bereavement happens. The Education Authority provides bereavement resources and information, including a sample bereavement policy for schools to adapt to meet their individual needs.The Department does not monitor completion of bereavement training in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether his Department will raise the minimum age for marriage to 18 years old, by the end of the2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: A Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill has been drafted and circulated to my Ministerial colleagues for comment. It includes provision to raise the minimum age for marriage and civil partnership from the present 16 to 18 and is on schedule for introduction in the 2025/26 session.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29265/22-27, to detail whether his Department monitors staff attainment of completed bereavement training, in schools.
answer_text: Schools are not required to have a ‘bereavement policy’, however as part of the broader pastoral care arrangements they may have in place a clear structure and process to be followed if a bereavement happens. The Education Authority provides bereavement resources and information, including a sample bereavement policy for schools to adapt to meet their individual needs.The Department does not monitor completion of bereavement training in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the tender process for the operation of Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre and café.
answer_text: The Centre of Procurement Excellence partner in the Department of Finance’s Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD), is currently carrying out work to develop a tender for an operator of this visitor centre and café.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29265/22-27, to detail the number of schools, and education providers, who can register with the Being Well Doing Well programme each year.
answer_text: 23/24(Cohort 1)24/25(Cohort 2)25/26(Cohort 3)Nursery Schools101110Primary Schools8794110Post primary Schools243421Special Schools31010EOTAS settings114TOTAL125150155In each academic year, around 130-150 schools register with the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29265/22-27, to detail any support on grief and bereavement available to education providers who are not registered with the Being Well Doing Well programme.
answer_text: ANSWERAll schools have access to the Education Authority (EA) Professional Learning in relation to bereavement.School Principals and Senior Management Team from educational settings not currently implementing the Being Well Doing Well programme can access the EA Professional Learning module A Whole School Approach to Bereavement and Critical Incident Management. This is a 2 day (3.5 hours per day) online professional learning event for staff. All educational settings can access bereavement support advice and resources from the EA Critical Incident Response Team (CIRT) Helpline which is available during office hours and out of hours. They will also be directed to the EA website which offers bereavement information and resources which can be downloaded. Schools provide Psychological First Aid and emotional support to staff and pupils after critical incidents involving the bereavement of a pupil or staff member, when needed.The Bereaved NI website is a useful resource for everyone to learn more about bereavement and grief, and signposting for specific support.Paul Givan MLAMinister of Education

answer_text: 23/24(Cohort 1)24/25(Cohort 2)25/26(Cohort 3)Nursery Schools101110Primary Schools8794110Post primary Schools243421Special Schools31010EOTAS settings114TOTAL125150155In each academic year, around 130-150 schools register with the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29265/22-27, to detail the elements of the Being Well Doing Well programme which focus specifically on (i) bereavement support; and (ii) grief education.
answer_text: The Being Well Doing Well programme provides schools with a framework to assess current policy and practice and implement actions to develop emotional health and wellbeing in their school. All educational settings that are implementing the Being Well Doing Well programme can avail of in school, ‘Bereavement and Loss’ Professional Learning for all staff (teaching and support staff) if it is identified as an action following their whole school baseline audit. The Learning Intentions aim to:Increase awareness of the ways in which children and young people grieve and an understanding of the grief processExplore effective strategies which support children and young people through bereavement Provide a Bereavement Policy FrameworkThe Being Well Doing Well team have also developed a range of Little Books including The Little Book of Grief for children and young people. This is accompanied by teacher guidance to assist them to approach this sensitive issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the regulatory framework concerning the sale and use of fireworks.
answer_text: The regulation of fireworks in Northern Ireland is among the most robust in these islands; the existing regulations provide a framework that seeks to ensure that fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use in both public and private settings.A retailer must be licensed by my department to store fireworks.  Members of the public and professional fireworks display operators must have a fireworks licence for the use of the fireworks.  A fireworks licence includes conditions on use, including specifying the types of fireworks that can be used and the time and location of the display.  A fireworks licence will only be issued if deemed safe to do so and additional conditions can be added to any licence as necessary, such a requirement to notify neighbours of the display.There is a balance to be struck between those who enjoy fireworks and those who do not which is why I encourage people using fireworks to be considerate as well as following the law.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks sale or use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.The power to restrict the decibel level of fireworks is a reserved matter; however, as part of the information available to the public, the availability of low and no noise options is included.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps her Department has taken to prepare for its winter service in North Down.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and salt stocks have been replenished. My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. While my Department targets the limited resources available for winter service on the busier main through routes, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.In addition, my Department has an arrangement with Ards and North Down Borough Council to salt city and town centre footways during times of prolonged ice and snow, and the council will be contacted to ensure continuity of this service for the incoming winter season should conditions dictate.My Department’s Winter Service Policy can be viewed via the following link : RSPPG E022, and details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via DFI Roads Salted Network.Our routine maintenance work to provide an annual clean to all DfI gullies in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area is also on-going alongside the routine cleaning of roadside channels and open drains and will continue throughout the winter season.In preparation for and during flood events local staff will utilise available resources to support community resilience groups in flood risk areas and respond, where possible, to requests for assistance from the public where property has suffered or is threatened by flooding. Departmental depots are fully stocked with sandbags for temporary flood protection which will be distributed directly when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the roads in North Down that have been prioritised in her Department's 2025-26 gritting network.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and salt stocks have been replenished. My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.In addition, my Department has an arrangement with Ards and North Down Borough Council  to salt city and town centre footways during times of prolonged ice and snow, and the council will be contacted to ensure continuity of this service for the incoming winter season should conditions dictate.My Department’s Winter Service Policy can be viewed via the following link : RSPPG E022, and details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via DFI Roads Salted Network

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget her Department has allocated for salt boxes for winter 2025-26, broken down by DfI Roads Division.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.There is no specific budget allocation for salt boxes, instead, when a salt box is requested, my officials will carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.I can confirm that our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department’s Stroke Action Plan sets out six priorities for stroke reform in Northern Ireland (NI). While funding and resource pressures have constrained the pace of implementation, important progress has been made in each priority area. Regarding priority one which focuses on prevention, progress includes the development of a regional model for a 7-day Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) or mini stroke assessment service and the development of action plans for implementation within each Trust. In line with priority two, an audit of thrombolysis has been undertaken which has informed the development of recommendations to help promote the achievement of a target rate of 16% for NI. The focus of priority three is on the thrombectomy service and progress includes the roll out of Artificial Intelligence software to all eight stroke receiving sites to assist in clinical decision making. In addition, it is anticipated that work on a Strategic Outline Case which will set out the resources required for a 24/7 thrombectomy service will be completed early in 2026. Priority four focuses on long term support and rehabilitation. Progress includes the development of regional models for both community stroke services and the management of spasticity.Priority five relates to the introduction of Hyperacute Stroke Care. A Hyperacute Stroke Project Board (HSPB) has been established to oversee a three-phase process to identify the optimum configuration for NI. Phase one of the process which used updated modelling to review measures to drive patient outcomes is now complete. Phase two of the process will involve an assessment of the sustainability of current services. This will be informed by an assessment of the workforce which is currently being progressed by the stroke workforce review under priority six. Priority six focuses on a stroke workforce review which is now underway. A Workforce Review Steering Group was established in June 2025 to oversee this work and an outline workplan was agreed.  The initial focus of the review is on an assessment of the hyperacute and acute workforce which will inform decision making on hyperacute services. It is anticipated that this phase of the review will conclude early in 2026.It is important to note, that the Stroke Action Plan priorities will continue to be progressed as far as possible within current resources. However, full implementation will continue to be impacted by funding constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timescale for the introduction for a 24/7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: The current thrombectomy service operates from 8am-5pm Monday to Sunday. It is estimated that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand to a 24/7 service.   Following approval of the final budget for 2025/26, my Department was left with a funding gap of over £600 million. In this context, and while I remain committed to the establishment of a 24/7 thrombectomy service in Northern Ireland, I am currently unable to allocate the required additional funding. Consequently, at this point, I am unable to commit to a timescale for a 24/7 service.In the interim, I am committed to maximising access to thrombectomy to ensure that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the publication of new guidelines on school uniforms.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how his Department is addressing poverty in North Down.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department engages with the UK Government to monitor and evaluate the effectiveness of the Agreement on the Mutual Recognition of Driving Disqualifications Treaty.
answer_text: Mutual recognition of driving disqualifications (MRDD) is an international agreement entered into by the British and Irish Governments, which came into force on 1 August 2017. My officials meet with Department of Transport (DfT) officials on a regular basis to discuss Driving Licence matters, including MRDD. Any issues raised are actioned and dealt with in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department engages with the Public Health Agency to monitor and evaluate the success of public awareness raising initiatives.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the public awareness campaigns the Public Health Agency (i) has delivered; and (ii) plans to deliver, in the 2025-26 financial year.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total budget allocated to the Public Health Agency for public awareness campaigns in each of the last five years.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total budget allocated to the Public Health Agency for public awareness campaigns in each of the last five years.DoH budget allocations to PHA for campaign promotional and advertising activity £m2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/251.21.41.50.50.4As part of a drive by all departments to reduce non-essential discretionary spend my department paused campaign advertising campaigns in 2023. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on his Department's Work Psychology Service’s relationship with Lifeline; and (ii) to detail the number of referrals made to Lifeline through the service, in each year since 2020.
answer_text: My Department’s Work Psychology Service has maintained a working relationship with Lifeline for some years for the purpose of enhancing awareness of suicide and self harm for our customer facing staff and to provide direct access to Lifeline’s crisis intervention service for our claimants. Whilst the Work Psychology Service does not make direct referrals, the Department for Communities has had a process in place to refer vulnerable customers at risk of suicide or self harm to Lifeline since 15 April 2024.  Referrals by year are as follows:5 April 2024 to 31 March 2025, 14 1 April 2025 to date, 7.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a pilot project for young women who have self-harmed or who are at risk of self-harm, per Theme Four of the Protect Life 2 Implementation Plan 2025-2027.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) has scheduled a programme of work to develop a pilot project for young women who have self-harmed or who are at risk of self-harm for the 2026/27 year, subject to funding.PHA currently fund the Self-Harm Intervention Programme (SHIP) which provides support for those aged 11 years and older who have self-harmed and have needs at Step 2 of the You in Mind care pathway. Health and Social Care Trusts provide services for those requiring support at Step 3 and Step 4.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a regional approach to protocols relating to domestic and sexual abuse, across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There are a number of regional guidance documents providing an operational framework for professionals responding to instances of domestic and sexual abuse;1. SBNI (Safeguarding Board NI) Regional Policy & Procedures2. Joint Protocol – Child Abuse Investigations3. PPANI (Public Protection Arrangements NI) Manual of Practice4. Joint Protocol – Adult Safeguarding Investigations5. MARAC (MultiAgency Risk Assessment Conference) GuidanceThese documents are applied consistently across the Health and Social Care Trusts and also provide a framework within which other professional stakeholders respond to reports of domestic and sexual abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department provides any funding to higher education institutions to support students seeking mental health support.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the October 2025 Voice Of Young People In Care report Show Us You Care: A Call to Action From Care-experienced Young People.
answer_text: The ‘Show Us You Care: A Call to Action from Care Experienced Young People’ Report was launched by Voice of Young People in Care (VOYPIC) in Parliament Buildings in October 2025, and several of my officials were in attendance to hear directly from young people who were speaking at the event.   The Report reflects their views that the challenges which led to the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services are still present, and sets out 12 ‘priority calls to actions’ for policy makers, commissioners, politicians, the media and service providers.  These include ensuring safe and stable homes, protecting sibling and wider family relationships, guaranteeing independent advocacy, providing better support for education and mental health, and creating a single children and families agency for Northern Ireland.My Department is making significant progress on addressing a number of the calls to action set out within the report, through a range of different approaches.The first call to action focuses on the establishment of a single agency for children and families, as recommended by Professor Ray Jones in his report on the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. VOYPIC played an important role in facilitating the views of young people in the Review, through supporting an ‘Experts by Experience’ group to engage with Professor Jones as he undertook his work.  The Justice Minister and I have agreed to work together to identify the steps necessary to establish a Regional Children and Families Authority incorporating children’s social care and youth justice services. The Executive has indicated support for the proposed approach and that work is now underway.As part of the Reform Programme established to address the challenges which led to the Review, there is a specific focus on enhancing capacity and capability of our residential care provision, in particular through ongoing work to develop a regional strategic plan for children’s residential care.  This will in turn ensure we have an estate that serves children across Northern Ireland well, and that every child has a safe place to call home.In respect of a right to independent advocacy, plans are already in place to introduce statutory independent advocacy services through the phased implementation of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.   The Act will also place care planning, and personal education plans, on a statutory basis, contributing to two further calls to action.  Finally, and in support of the call to action relating to transitioning from care, the Act will place on a statutory basis the ‘Going the Extra Mile (GEM)’ scheme which allows young people to remain in their foster home beyond their 18th birthday until they reach age 21. The Act will also introduce extended support to care leavers aged 21 to 25, and will introduce a new duty for HSC Trusts to publish information about services which are available to assist care leavers in preparing for adulthood a

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether the counselling provisions within schools in North Down meets demand.
answer_text: As noted in my previous answer to AQW 34129/22-27, the HOPE post-primary counselling and therapy service is available to all mainstream post-primary schools, EOTAS provisions and special schools that have post-primary aged pupils.The Department is not aware of any issues regarding access to this service within schools in North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how his proposed Tree Planting Action Plan will help address biodiversity decline and climate change.
answer_text: The management and expansion of our woodlands in Northern Ireland are vital for biodiversity, climate resilience, and community wellbeing. Sustainable forest management and tree planting are central to the Department’s commitment to the climate change and biodiversity challenges. 		Taking forward actions identified in the development of the Tree Planting Action Plan will contribute to meeting the objective of 12% forest cover by 2050 and provide greater opportunities to address biodiversity decline and mitigate climate change. The Tree Planting Action Plan will point to the forestry contributions needed to achieve Climate Action Plan targets. 		Tree planting, particularly with native tree species, helps to address biodiversity decline by restoring degraded habitats, riparian zones, and ancient woodlands. New diverse woodlands support a wide range of flora and fauna, including birds, insects, fungi, and mammals. In establishing new woodlands there is great opportunity to link fragmented habitats, allowing species to migrate, breed, and adapt to environmental changes.		Woodlands and trees sequester carbon and help to mitigate climate change. They contribute to increased climate resilience and provide a sustainable supply of timber, substituting more carbon-intensive products, for example in the housing and construction markets.I look forward to working with key stakeholders to co-design and develop the Plan and finding innovative ways to encourage and incentivise landowners appropriately to introduce woodland areas as part of their business activities, delivering the biodiversity and wider environmental values for society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why CAR T-cell therapy cannot be introduced until a new regional haematology ward based at Belfast City Hospital is opened.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that the current infrastructure and inpatient accommodation at Ward 10 North is not suitable to deliver CAR-T Therapy and this deficiency of environment does not meet the British Society for Haematology and Royal College of Physicians established standards, which are endorsed by NICE. The significant redesign and modernisation of the facility as described in the Outline Business Case for the new Regional Haematology Ward is required in order to remedy these issues and ensure that the BHSCT haematology clinical team is best supported to continue to provide safe, effective and appropriate care to patients requiring complex inpatient treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients in North Down who are registered to receive NHS dental care.
answer_text: The estimated population of the North Down Assembly constituency is 91,800 and with 36,851 registrations at 30 June 2025 (the most recent publicly available statics), the registration rate in the area is 40.1%, Under the current contract, dental practitioners have the right to register or not to register a patient as a Health Service patient. To register as a Health Service patient the patient must have a Health Care Number. Children under-18 must be examined as part of the registration process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of NHS dentists, in North Down.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to improve access to NHS dentists, in North Down.
answer_text: Access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present and I am aware that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist are finding it more difficult to obtain an appointment with a Health Service dentist. General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide, and under the current General Dental Service arrangements, the Department does not have a role in determining the location of dental practices. In an effort to improve access for all patients, an additional £7m is being invested in General Dental Services in 2025/26. The investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canal treatments, and a further £1.6m allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.In parallel, the Dental Access Scheme (DAS) continues to provide access for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing oral health need.  Ards and Down falls into the South Eastern Local Commissioning Group (LCG) area. The most recent data shows that 4,159 patients have been seen under DAS in this area up to 30 September 2025.Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.  While that work is progressed consideration is being given to other actions which could be taken to improve access to HS dental care for both registered and unregistered patients. This includes the commissioning of a cost-of-service review which will help inform the future direction of General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Ending the Harm campaign.
