answer_text: AQO 2662 22-27 - ANSWER ‘Ending the Harm’ is a series of public awareness campaigns that highlight the devastating impact of organised crime and paramilitary activity on victims, their families, communities and wider society.   The campaign is delivered by the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, for which I am lead Minister.   The current poster campaign specifically highlights a number of ways that paramilitary groups control, coerce and harm communities.    In December last year independent tracking research was undertaken which showed that this campaign is actively changing perceptions about the harms perpetrated by paramilitary gangs in Northern Ireland. Eighty-nine percent of respondents said that they fully supported what the campaign was saying and trying to do.    Last month a new iteration of the posters was launched which, for the first time, now explicitly references ‘Criminal Gangs’ as well as ‘Paramilitaries’, acknowledging the diverse perspectives on how such organisations are described. It continues to exceed expectations online, with the latest data showing campaign engagement is nearly five times higher than the industry average and delivering exceptional value for money. People are not only seeing the message, they’re clicking and watching the videos, showing that the campaign message continues to connect with the public.  The posters are being displayed across a wide range of outdoor advertising sites as well as featuring online and on social media.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support his Department gives to post-primary schools, on supporting children who return to school after juvenile detention.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides coordinated reintegration support for post-primary pupils transitioning back to mainstream education following a juvenile justice placement. This support includes multi-agency planning and may involve EA’s Special Educational Needs (SEN) Local Impact Teams (LIT) through a formal request for involvement. Pupils may also access placements in full-time or partnership Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) centres, alongside tailored transition programmes to support a positive and successful return to mainstream education.Summary of EA Support Services:Multi-Agency Collaborative Planning: A team of professionals, including external agencies, works together to support the pupil’s reintegration into their registered school.Risk Reduction Action Plan (RRAP): Advice and guidance on the development of a Risk Reduction Action Plan (RRAP) and other relevant education plans for the returning pupilRequest for Involvement: Guidance for school staff on the submission of a Request for Involvement for support from the pupil’s LIT, which may include transition support.   Parent/Carer Support: Signposting to relevant services and supports for families.HOPE Counselling Service: Available to all post-primary pupils in mainstream, special or EOTAS requiring emotional support on request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department offers any specialist counselling services to children in education settings, who have engaged with the justice system.
answer_text: The Department does not offer counselling provision specifically for those who have engaged with the justice system.The HOPE post-primary counselling and therapy service is available to all mainstream post-primary schools, EOTAS provisions and special schools that have post-primary aged pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether osteoporosis will be included as part of the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: I am aware that women are much more prone to osteoporosis than men, due to a combination of biological, hormonal, and lifestyle factors and particularly after menopause due to hormonal changes. NI Direct provides a useful information source for any person seeking information on osteoporosis at www.nidirect.gov.uk/conditions/osteoporosis which includes advice on prevention and treatment options.My officials are continuing to actively develop the Women’s Health Action Plan. The plan will not include actions on every health condition faced by women, but it will aim to ensure that women are informed about the risk factors and management of certain conditions throughout the course of their life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the capacity of contact centres is meeting demand, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust holds the contract responsibility for child contact centres within their locality. These contact centres are utilised to enable children and young people to share safe quality time with parents or significant people in their lives. Each HSCT has advised that their current contractual arrangements are meeting the demand, exemplified by minimal waiting lists across the region.On this basis it is considered that the contact centres currently available, do meet the demand within each Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what work his Department is undertaking to support teenagers in care, to transition to independent living.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any research his Department has completed on victims of domestic and sexual abuse experiencing self-harm and self-harm ideation.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy (2024-2031) acknowledges the profound impact of domestic and sexual abuse on victims. Its pillars are designed to tackle domestic and sexual abuse, ensuring victims are supported, perpetrators are held to account and society works together to make abuse unacceptable.A key priority area for the Strategy is to inform policy, legislation and service development with better quality data, research and shared learning. As we progress delivery of the Strategy, we will consider how we can improve data capture and analysis including what local research could be commissioned, to both gauge the effectiveness of our interventions and inform future policy and provision.The Lifeline 24 hour crisis helpline, which is available for anyone in distress or despair in Northern Ireland can also provide data where the main presenting issue recorded is related to domestic violence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has commissioned any research into the correlation between menopause and osteoporosis.
answer_text: The Department is aware of extensive existing research evidence around menopause and osteoporosis and related NICE recommendations.  The Health and Social Care (HSC) Research & Development (R&D) Division within the Public Health Agency are responsible for the administration of the Department’s budgetary allocation for Research & Development, including support for research offices in each of the acute Trusts.The HSC R&D have not specifically commissioned any research into the correlation between menopause and osteoporosis. Rather, they provide funding for infrastructure, including the NI Clinical Research Network (NICRN) and Clinical Research Facility (NICRF), which supports researchers to carry out work in various disease areas. In addition, my Department makes a financial contribution to the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR), which funds research into a wide range of conditions, including the menopause and osteoporosis.   NI researchers are eligible to apply to NIHR for funding to carry out research in this and other areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department monitors the number of pupils who are refused entry into year 13 and year 14 by their post-primary school, due to GCSE and AS Level results criteria.
answer_text: My Department does not hold or monitor this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department issues guidance to post-primary schools, in relation to readmittance into year 13 and year 14, on the basis of GCSE and AS Level results.
answer_text: Admission onto a course of post-16 study is a matter for Boards of Governors of individual schools and my Department does not provide guidance in respect of Year 13 admission.However, once pupils are enrolled on a two-year course of study, my Department expects schools to support sixth-form students at every stage of their learning journey.  In 2024 my Department wrote to schools to highlight the expectation that schools make every reasonable effort to ensure that pupils can wherever possible remain in their school for Year 14 and are supported to complete their studies over two years as originally planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timeline for his Department's review of the consultation responses on the Final Report of Regional Review of Neurology Services.
answer_text: My Department undertook a consultation on the report of the Regional Review of Neurology from 07 May 2025 to 06 August 2025. To support engagement and promote participation in the consultation, a series of in-person and online events were held. Now that the consultation has closed, my officials are currently reviewing both the written responses received and feedback obtained at the engagement events. I expect this work to be completed in the coming weeks. It is intended that this consultation analysis will then inform the development of an implementation plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department is supporting those living with multiple sclerosis.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Investments were made over a number of years and each of these priority objectives have been achieved. Colleagues from DoH work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. When I published the HSC Reset Plan in July 2025, the commitments in the plan were to Stabilise the HSC system,Drive forward our system Reform, and Increase and improve how we Deliver our servicesWhilst not specifically referred to in the plan, I acknowledge that the current new and review waiting times for neurology and wider support services are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed as part of the process to reset services, to addressing this, subject to available funding.As part of a process to consider the wider pressures on neurology services, the Department commissioned a Regional Review of Neurology Services. The review was tasked with considering the optimum service configuration for neurology services for the next 10-15 years. The final report of the Neurology Review was published as part of a consultation exercise in May 2025.A series of consultation events were held over the summer months and the Review Team are now in the process of considering the input received from the consultation events and from responses received on the report before considering next steps. Progress on taking forward the agreed priorities set out in the report will be subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on orthodontist provision in North Down.
answer_text: All general dental practitioners in Northern Ireland can provide orthodontic treatment under the General Dental Services contract without a specialist qualification.  A number of high street specialist orthodontic practices are located across Northern Ireland, with one such practice with four specialist orthodontic dentists located in Bangor, County Down, listed below.HSC Dentists at specialist orthodontic practices at 30 June 2025 in North Down Assembly AreaDental PracticePractice AddressDentist NameBANGOR ORTHODONTICS2 BINGHAM LANE, BANGOR, BT20 5DRMS J A FINLAYMR CONOR ARMSTRONGMR KARL GRIMESMR PETER TURNERAcross Northern Ireland there are an additional 24 specialist orthodontic practices.  Patients can be referred by their general dental practitioner to any of these specialist practices. Furthermore, there are Dentists with Enhanced Skills in Orthodontics who provide orthodontic treatment in non-specialist practices, however none of these have made arrangements with the SPPG to provide orthodontic treatments at any of the dental practices in the North Down Assembly Area.I am aware that there has been in increase in waiting times for orthodontic treatment through specialist practices due to increased demand for treatment and the small number of specialists who are working in practice. Sustainability of the orthodontic workforce is influenced by a number of factors including the available dental workforce. There are currently a number of Dental Core Trainees working within orthodontic specialty in the School of Dentistry, Belfast. As of September 2025, there are two orthodontic specialist trainees in post completing the consultant training pathway. In recent years the School of Dentistry has been unable to support orthodontic specialty trainees seeking to complete qualification for entry to the orthodontic Specialist List due to lack of capacity within the consultant team to support the programme. The Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency are actively exploring innovative solutions to support orthodontic provision in Northern Ireland. This includes the establishment of a Diploma in Orthodontic Therapy, which began in June 2025, for the first time in Northern Ireland. Orthodontic therapists carry out a range of orthodontic treatment, under the direction of an orthodontist or dentist, as part of skill mix team in specialist practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the re-commencement of the Home to School Transport Review.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy was initiated by DE in 2018; however, this process was paused by the then Minister due to the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequently suspended pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education.The transport policy is extensive in scope and affects a significant number of families, with over 90,000 pupils availing of free school transport each day. It underpins other departmental policies, including school attendance, and provides essential support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities. Any amendments to the policy criteria have the potential to substantially increase or decrease the number of pupils eligible for free transport. Therefore, any such proposals must be carefully considered within the framework of a full policy review to assess their impact on children and families and to establish the associated financial implications.I am committed to improving school transport and I want to carry out a review of the transport policy as I feel many of its outworkings can provide challenges and frustrations for parents. Currently, my officials are conducting a scoping exercise to examine key aspects of school transport where improvement is needed. The cost of delivering school transport is considerable, exceeding £120 million in the 2023/24 financial year and due to significant budgetary constraints currently facing the education sector, any policy proposals which may increase the numbers eligible for transport must be carefully evaluated for affordability. My decision on the timing of a comprehensive review of the DE transport policy will be made within the broader strategic and financial context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans to work with the Education Authority to review the exceptional circumstances policy regarding home to school transport.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for determining a pupil's eligibility for transport assistance in line with the Department of Education’s Home to School Transport Policy. The policy allows the EA to consider whether transport assistance should be provided in cases which fall outside the criteria where there are exceptional circumstances, but it states that such cases should be, by their very nature, exceptional.   The EA has published an Exceptional Circumstances Policy and it provides clear guidance for parents on its Transport Appeals process, which include examples of circumstances that may be considered exceptional and those which may not. Because exceptional circumstances are unique, the list of examples is not definitive and the EA must assess each case individually and consider the evidence provided by parents to determine if transport assistance should be provided.   The decisions made under the Transport Appeals process may be subject to legal scrutiny and the EA’s Exceptional Circumstances Policy and published guidance ensure that it is transparent and consistent in applying the process. I am content that the EA Transport Appeals process operates in line with the requirements of the current DE Home to School Transport Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale for the installation of the planned pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, near St Columbanus’ College, Bangor.
answer_text: Road safety continues to be a priority for my Department to ensure that all road users can travel safely and with confidence. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, especially near our schools. I can confirm a new controlled pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, has been programmed for this financial year and will be completed as soon as resources are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work his Department has undertaken to increase the safety of children on their journey to school, since the beginning of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: I consider the safety of pupils travelling to and from school of paramount importance. I am keen to explore any measures that my department or the Education Authority (EA) could introduce to improve the safety of school children and I met with the Minister for Infrastructure and the EA at the start of September to discuss this issue. We reviewed the current safety measures on school buses, such as special lighting that alerts other motorists to exercise caution. We discussed ways to refresh and reinforce this messaging and the EA has used its social media channels to draw attention to the ‘Jack and Jill’ lights on its buses and their purpose to alert drivers that children are getting on or off the bus. I am committed to working with partner agencies to further inform the development of policy and legislation surrounding transport of children to school and my Department participates at a senior level in the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) led Road Safety Strategic Forum. This forum brings different Departments and road safety partners together to consider actions and ensure the delivery of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy to 2030.  The EA operational responsibility for the delivery of school transport lies with the EA. It is also committed to working with partner agencies and it supports DfI and Translink’s road safety campaigns by encouraging schools to follow the DfI road safety guidance available online, offering additional Safe School Transport information on its website and it regularly shares safety information through its social media channels. The EA recently produced an educational video for pupils with special educational needs who travel on EA buses which highlights ways in which they can be safe on their journey to school and the importance of using seatbelts. Special Educational Needs (SEN) Transport Information and FAQ’s | Education Authority Northern IrelandRoad safety education provision within schools also has an important role in developing children and young people’s attitudes and behaviours to become safer road users  and the school curriculum provides opportunities for teachers to cover the issue of road safety at primary and post-primary level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils with a statement of special education needs in North Down who rely on taxi provision to get to school.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport and it has advised that according to its records, 196 pupils with a statement of special educational needs currently avail of home to school transport by taxi to schools in the Ards and North Down council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has engaged with Translink and the Education Authority, to ensure school transport aligns more accurately with the post-primary academic calendar.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport and it maintains regular contact with Translink regarding the alignment of school transport for the start of the new school year. All home to school transport services, including those operated by Translink, commence operation on 1st September each year. Individual schools are responsible for determining their own calendar, however the EA writes to each school in February/March to inform them of the non-operational dates for the following academic year and to remind them that home to school transport services do not commence until 1st September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline whether his Department has issued any advice to post-primary schools on voluntary contributions.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department monitors what post-primary schools, set as their voluntary contribution.
answer_text: Under current legislation, voluntary contributions are a matter for Boards of Governors.  For this reason, neither the Education Authority nor my Department monitors what schools set as their voluntary contributions recommended to parents. On 2 September 2025 my Department issued Circular 2025-10 Charges in grant aided schools to schools. This replaced circular 1991/21 and is consistent with the relevant legislation.  The circular draws attention to Chapter 18 of the Governors Guide which sets out the schools’ legal responsibilities with regards school fees, charges, remissions and voluntary contributions.While current legislation permits Boards of Governors to seek voluntary contributions from parents / guardians, any request must make it clear that: there is no obligation to contribute; and registered pupils at the school will not be treated differently according to whether or not their parents/guardians have made a contribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what support his Department can offer community organisations or council-owned spaces that suffer substantial property damage.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) is available to provide financial support to councils in the event of an emergency. SEFA flooding and non-flooding schemes may cover the cost of materials used for urgent repairs to council-owned buildings or amenities. However, they do not fund improvements beyond restoring the original condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why the Hardship Fund for community organisations is not being administered in this financial year.
answer_text: Due to my Department’s constrained financial position for 2024/25 and 2025/26, it has not been possible to continue this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government, regarding the introduction of the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: I refer the Member to answers provided to previous questions AQW 31249/22-27, AQO 2452/22-27, AQO 2458/22-27, AQW 32531/22-27 and AQW 33970/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail an update on any preparatory work his Department has undertaken, in anticipation of the closure of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) is delivered and managed directly by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) with no partnership role for the Executive. The SPF is scheduled to conclude on 31 March 2026, with no direct successor scheme confirmed. While the Local Growth Fund was initially, and as recently as July 2025, mooted by the British Government as a replacement for the SPF, subsequent engagement has clarified that this is no longer the British Government’s view. In March 2025, in anticipation of an announcement by the British Government at the Spending Review, I secured Executive agreement on an approach to the Local Growth Fund. This position set out high-level priorities for a future Fund, as well as lessons to be learnt in relation to the deficiencies of the SPF. It also recognised the need for urgent progress. The agreed Executive position was shared with officials and Ministers in Whitehall Departments, including MHCLG and the NIO, in March 2025.I, and my officials, have been pressing for the SPF to be succeeded by something, that both meets local needs, and avoids the repeated funding cliff-edges that our Community and Voluntary Sector have experienced in recent years. I have raised this repeatedly with successive British Government Ministers and will continue to press for a solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department engages with the British Parking Association; and (ii) for her assessment of whether private parking enforcement firms in Northern Ireland adhere to the British Parking Association's Code of Practice.
answer_text: My Department regularly engages with the British Parking Association (BPA) to discuss parking matters and potential new parking initiatives.My Department has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks, however, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association. Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department plans to regulate private parking firms, operating in Northern Ireland, before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement, it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any engagement he has had with children and young people in relation to his proposals for the future of CCEA qualifications.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
answer_text: We recognise the profound harm caused by clerical child abuse and extend our deepest sympathy and solidarity to all victims and survivors of this appalling abuse. Throughout this process the Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors to hear their stories and their views on what is needed. We are very grateful to all those who have participated in the process and shared their experiences.Work has now completed on three separate pieces of research: one examining records held by faith organisations and statutory agencies; one recording experiences and expectations of victims and survivors; and one examining the safeguarding policies and practices of faith groups. We have received the research reports and are giving them careful consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will publish the Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department will publish ‘Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2024-25’ on the 16 October 2025 at 9.30am.Department of Health statistical publication dates can be found on the Information & Analysis Directorate statistics calendar. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/doh-statistics-and-research.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Penalty Charge Notices issued by her Department on Market Street, Bangor, in the last three years.
answer_text: Market Street Bangor  currently receives scheduled enforcement, Monday to Friday between 8:00 and 18:00.  The table below show the number of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued on Market Street Bangor in the last three yearsYearsTotal PCN issued202322220242392025*262*1st Jan 2025 – 8th Oct 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether parking restrictions on school entrance markings are enforced by traffic attendants during periods of school holidays.
answer_text: Double Yellow Lines (DYLs) located at school entrances are always enforceable, including during school holidays. Some school entrance locations are included as part of Traffic attendants’ routine patrols and my Department can also respond to specific requests for enforcement should parking at a particular location cause a concern. The majority of “School Keep Clear” markings are advisory only and, as such, cannot be enforced by Traffic Attendants.However, where “School Keep Clear” markings have been legislated, these will be accompanied by signage advising of restriction dates and times, which can be enforced by Traffic Attendants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of recommendations relating to his Department included in the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.
answer_text: ANNEX ASummary of Progress Made on Accepted Review RecommendationsKeyGuiding principle for the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform ProgrammeAssigned to a workstream within the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform ProgrammeAssigned to the Department of Health to take forwardRecommendationCommentaryOctober 2025 Update1 - Northern Ireland is not that large compared to the rest of the UK and to the Republic of Ireland and this should be considered in how children’s services are organised and delivered.  Guiding PrincipleThis recommendation has been adopted as a guiding principle for the reform of children’s social care. The Children’s Social Care Services Reform Programme continues to work collaboratively across the region to progress reform.  One of the central approaches of the Programme to service improvement, highlighted at the Foundations for the Future conference in November 2024, is the identification of best practice in service delivery and consideration on how to scale up these approaches across the region.  3 - Action needs to be taken to address the children’s social care workforce crisis. Subject to funding availability – Assigned to Social Work Workforce Implementation Board (SWIBB) & the Workforce workstreamSocial CareWorking in collaboration with the equivalent workstream for adult social care on the Social Care Collaborative Forum, the workforce workstream of the Programme launched the Social Care Workforce Strategy in December 2024.  This strategy sets out a vision and actions to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next ten years.It includes 7 key priorities and 22 actions to develop career pathways, support the training and education of the workforce, and to raise the profile and recognition of the workforce as a skilled and competent social care workforce. A three-year implementation plan has been developed. The outline Band 5 job description for an enhanced support role for frontline teams: a ‘Children and Families Senior Support Officer’ has progressed through the Agenda for Change job evaluation process. As well as enhancing skills mix within children’s social care services, the new role has the potential to release social work time for tasks that require social work professional skills, reduce the numbers of unallocated cases and attendant risk and improve social worker retention.Subject to formal business case approval, the Department hopes to be in a position to recruit 60 Band 5 roles before the end of the financial year.Social WorkThe Department, in collaboration with the HSC Trusts, took the decision to cease the use of recruitment agency social workers in HSC services by end of June 2023. Up to until then, there were over 200 agency-employed social workers working in HSC Trusts – many within Family and Childcare Services. All of these agency social workers were offered contracts with HSC Trusts between January and June 2023, and the vast majority accepted. Ceasing the use of r

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding his Department has ring-fenced for the (i) production of; and (ii) fulfilment of the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: (i) There is no ringfenced funding currently available for the production of the Women's Health Action Plan.  The Department has, however, provided funding of £20k to Derry Well Women (DWW) and Queen’s University Belfast (QUB) to support their large-scale public listening exercise on women’s health.  This has delivered a NI Women’s Health Survey, which closed earlier this year, and focus groups are currently underway with hard to reach women and section 75 groups, thereby ensuring the findings are inclusive of all women.  A final report will be produced by DWW / QUB which will provide important feedback for inclusion in the Action Plan.    (ii) There is no ringfenced funding available for the fulfilment of the Women's Health Action Plan. The Action plan seeks to bring together the range of ongoing developments in women’s health such as tackling gynae waiting lists, improving maternity safety and the Live Better work which addresses the health inequalities between different groups of women.  Whilst some of this work is subject to available funding, there are ongoing initiatives that have funding attached, including for example £4.3m per annum for the delivery of enhanced contraception and abortion services and recent investment of £3.5m to expand gynaecology capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Women's Resource and Development Agency's September 2025 report Health Inequalities in Northern Ireland, Chapter Three: A Woman's Health Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Addressing health inequalities is a key priority for me and I acknowledge the work that the Women's Resource and Development Agency are doing in this area.  I have asked my officials to consider the findings of this report as part of their overall evidence base for the Woman's Health Action Plan, currently in development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps his Department has taken to consolidate all data and public information on women’s health to one digital, publicly available platform.
answer_text: There is a mandate on the NICS and its Arms-length Bodies to place key information and signposting to resources, on the publicly available NI Direct platform and there is a broad range of women's health information available via this platform.   I have acknowledged the need for better education and awareness on women’s health issues and the identification and management of risk factors as I am aware that a digital platform may not be accessible to all groups of women. My officials are considering how best to make progress within available resources as part of work to develop the Women's Health Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline whether his Department has considered introducing a women's health commissioner.
answer_text: I am aware that Women’s Health Ambassadors / Champions have been appointed in other jurisdictions and acknowledge the important role they play in raising the profile of women’s health.  Given the ongoing budget constraints faced by my Department there are no current plans to introduce a Women’s Health Commissioner for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what ring-fenced funding has been available for community-based action in relation to ending violence against women and girls, since September 2024.
answer_text: Since September 2024 up to 31 March 2026; £5.8m has been made available for community-based action through the EVAWG Change Fund. This consists of £4.2m from the allocation specifically ringfenced for EVAWG and £1.6m from TEO baseline budgets.Funding this year will be used to meet the objectives of the first Delivery Plan which includes proposed local and regional community-based actions. The additional funding will enable TEO to deliver on a larger scale and across a broader spectrum of platforms at both local and regional levels.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on securing contributions from religious orders and institutions towards the financial redress scheme and specialist support services for victims and survivors of historical institutional abuse.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the arrangements in place, between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, to ensure driver disqualifications are shared to apply in both jurisdictions.
answer_text: Arrangements are in place which allow for driving disqualifications for certain road traffic behaviours to be shared by the licensing authorities in each jurisdiction, subject to specified conditions.  Where a person is disqualified by order of a Court in one jurisdiction, and that person is either normally resident in the other jurisdiction or holds a driving licence issued by that other jurisdiction, the licensing authority in the state where the offence occurred may notify the licensing authority in the other jurisdiction.  Having received such a notification, that other licensing authority may impose the disqualification if all relevant conditions are met.Where a driver is normally resident in the south, holds a driving licence issued by the southern authorities, and is disqualified by a Court in the south, the Driver and Vehicle Agency would not be notified of such a disqualification.Regardless of mutual recognition arrangements, if a driver has been disqualified from driving by their own licensing authority, they no longer hold an entitlement to drive in any jurisdiction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to improve accessibility in older public buildings for people with disabilities.
answer_text: The capital budget position in my Department remains extremely challenging in the current financial year which has restricted the ability of this Department to take steps to improve accessibility in older public buildings. The capital budget for 2025/26 has been utilised to ensure that all inescapable capital and competing high priority requirements are met in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to strengthen legal protections for people with disabilities in terms of accessibility.
answer_text: We remain firmly committed to promoting equality and strengthening legal protections for people with disabilities, particularly in the area of accessibility.  This commitment is embedded in the Executive’s Programme for Government (PfG), which prioritises inclusive public services and equal opportunities for all.We will continue to work in partnership with stakeholders to ensure that accessibility and equality remain central to the Executive’s agenda.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they are taking to ensure hospitality businesses understand their legal duty to make reasonable adjustments under the Disability Discrimination Act 1995.
answer_text: The Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI) has responsibility for enforcing the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (DDA), as amended.  ECNI takes a range of steps to ensure service providers, including hospitality businesses, understand their legal duty to make reasonable adjustments under the DDA.  These steps include providing tailored guidance and advice, maintaining the Disability Code of Practice, supporting individuals who believe they’ve been discriminated against, and promoting the ‘Every Customer Counts’ initiative to help businesses improve accessibility.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the amount of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund has been spent on self-defence classes, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The following table shows the amount funded under the Local Change Fund for self-defense classes for 2025/26, broken down by Council area.CouncilAmountAntrim & NewtownabbeyNilArds & North DownNilArmagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonNilBelfast City £5,000Causeway Coast & Glens£5,137Derry City & StrabaneNilFermanagh & Omagh£500Lisburn & Castlereagh£2,000Mid & East AntrimNilMid UlsterNilNewry, Mourne & DownNil

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the amount of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund has been spent on information sessions, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Information and awareness raising activities provided through the Local Change Fund across Council areas are described in many formats such as educational sessions, preventative workshops, awareness sessions or training.There is no specific category to provide a single measure of expenditure on ‘information sessions’ by Council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 27883/22-27; and (ii) AQW 27935/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of actions arising from the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031 - Action Plan Years 1-3, relating to his Department.
answer_text: Work is progressing steadily on the implementation of the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy for which the Department of Health holds lead or joint responsibility for 24 actions within the initial three-year action plan.In addition to an initial focus on ensuring the establishment of robust governance structures, key developments include:the completion of a review to assess options and provide recommendations for a revised operating model for Local Domestic and Sexual Abuse Partnerships,in partnership with Justice, the launch of a Small Grants Scheme designed to support community and voluntary organisations in their essential work with victims of domestic and/or sexual abuse,the development of a Reflections Booklet for social workers, offering practical insights and guidance on best practice when responding to domestic and sexual abuse cases; andthe publication of the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland’s ‘Recognising the Impact of Domestic Abuse’ Learning and Development Framework. This framework is designed to standardise training across member agencies and make resources available to the wider community and voluntary sector. It will strengthen the capacity of professionals to understand and respond to the impact of domestic abuse on children.A comprehensive year 1 progress update on all actions is scheduled for publication in December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the services that his Department funds to support victims and survivors of child sexual abuse.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust holds statutory responsibility for providing a range and level of social services appropriate to Children in Need as defined under Article 17 of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995. Each Health and Social Care Trust utilises a range of services to support victims and survivors of child sexual abuse. My Department currently procures specific Therapeutic Support for Children and Young People who are potentially vulnerable to Child Sexual Exploitation through Barnardo’s.My Department also supports the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) through the Department of Health’s Core Grant. Although this Grant does not specifically procure a Therapeutic Service, it does support the NSPCC to deliver their services which includes Therapeutic Support. My Department also supports Nexus NI to provide counselling services to adult victims and survivors of sexual abuse and violence.  Following an initial assessment each individual will be offered up to 12 counselling sessions, based on presenting need. Referrals to the service will be from a range of agents, including GPs and HSC Trusts, and individuals who self-refer. This provision is currently specifically focussed on supporting individuals who have been victims of sexual abuse and are at the stage of their journey where therapeutic interventions are most likely to have the best outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with the Housing Executive on its Domestic Violence Action Plan since its publication in 2024; and (ii) whether his Department has contributed any ring-fenced funding to support its progression.
answer_text: My officials receive regular updates from the Housing Executive on progress against actions within its Domestic Violence Action Plan which contribute to the delivery of the Executive’s Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031.The Departments of Health and Justice are joint policy leads for Domestic and Sexual Abuse. The Department of Justice awarded the Housing Executive £73,000 via the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Fund to support delivery of its Domestic Abuse Action Plan in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the International Relations Strategy.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy is under development and will intersect with broader Executive and departmental strategies and actions. It will be important in setting out our long-term approach to global engagement. The Strategy requires Executive agreement; therefore, it will be subject to consultation and Executive colleagues’ consideration.We live in an increasingly interconnected world, where developments and decisions made elsewhere impact on us directly. If we are to drive prosperity and tackle disadvantage across all our communities, and deliver on our Programme for Government ambitions, we must engage with key international partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps her Department is taking to improve road safety on the A2 West Circular Road, Bangor.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a permanent memorial in Parliament Buildings to victims and survivors, as recommended by the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry report.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health to ensure special schools are getting the support they require from health professionals.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether his Department has had any engagement with the Department of Health and Social Care to share learning around the introduction of Right Care Right Person.
answer_text: The importance of learning from England and Wales to inform implementation of the Right Care Right Person (RCRP) model is widely recognised by all RCRP stakeholders and has formed a key consideration in the implementation planning in Northern Ireland.  This includes consideration of the joint Home Office and Department of Health and Social Care report evaluating the implementation of the Right Care Right Person approach in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what engagement his Department has had with the PSNI regarding the proposed introduction of Right Care Right Person.
answer_text: Planning for the implementation of the Right Care Right Person (RCRP) model in Northern Ireland is being taken forward in a partnership approach between key stakeholders across Health, Justice and the PSNI.  My Department is working closely with PSNI at all levels of the RCRP implementation. For example, PSNI colleagues are represented on the overarching RCRP Strategic Group which has been established to provide strategic support for the implementation.  The Silver Operational Group is co-chaired by senior officials in Health and the PSNI and membership consists of professionals from both.  A number of subgroups have also been established to deliver key strands of the RCRP implementation, reporting to the Silver Group, and are either co-led by PSNI staff or have PSNI staff involved as part of their membership.  All these groups continue to meet regularly to ensure a collaborative approach to delivery of the RCRP proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Improving Health within Criminal Justice Oversight Group.
answer_text: The Department of Health jointly with the Department of Justice recently published a Progress Report on the Improving Health within Criminal Justice Strategy and Action Plan (May 2025).  The Oversight Group approved the report prior to it being shared with the Health and Justice Ministers.  The Oversight Group will now consider and approve the recommended way forward with the Strategy, as it has reached its five years life span.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action plan remains in development with work ongoing to determine achievable actions based on the significant feedback we received from the stakeholder engagement events earlier this year.One of the common themes apparent throughout our stakeholder engagement is the need for better education and awareness on women’s health issues and the identification and management of risk factors.  I am aware and have acknowledged this.  Unfortunately, as I have also stated previously, ongoing budget pressures limit my ability to commit to specific Women’s Health public awareness campaigns at this time. Officials are considering how best to make progress in this area as the Action Plan develops.My Department is also continuing to support, and work alongside, Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast on their large scale public listening exercise with women in NI.  The first phase of this work, a Women’s Health Survey which was funded by DoH, has now concluded.  The second phase will take place over the coming months and will involve a series of focus groups targeted at “hard-to-reach” women.  The findings of the focus groups, alongside, the early analysis from Phase 1 of the listening exercise will provide additional evidence to underpin the Women’s Health Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will be running public awareness campaigns for women’s health issues during this financial year.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action plan remains in development with work ongoing to determine achievable actions based on the significant feedback we received from the stakeholder engagement events earlier this year.One of the common themes apparent throughout our stakeholder engagement is the need for better education and awareness on women’s health issues and the identification and management of risk factors.  I am aware and have acknowledged this.  Unfortunately, as I have also stated previously, ongoing budget pressures limit my ability to commit to specific Women’s Health public awareness campaigns at this time. Officials are considering how best to make progress in this area as the Action Plan develops.My Department is also continuing to support, and work alongside, Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast on their large scale public listening exercise with women in NI.  The first phase of this work, a Women’s Health Survey which was funded by DoH, has now concluded.  The second phase will take place over the coming months and will involve a series of focus groups targeted at “hard-to-reach” women.  The findings of the focus groups, alongside, the early analysis from Phase 1 of the listening exercise will provide additional evidence to underpin the Women’s Health Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on car parking provision at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The aim of the Department’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine to enable them to take into account local issues. The Ulster Hospital has approximately 2200 parking spaces of which of approximately 900 are public spaces and 1300 designated for permitted staff parking. Capacity is constrained by the geography of the site, planning restrictions and the number of entrances and exits serving the site.  On a typical day public car parking capacity at the Ulster Hospital meets the demands of vehicle users although some delays may be experienced at peak times.There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with an on-site car parking space, however the trust provides over 100 free off-site staff parking spaces at Dundonald Omniplex with a free shuttlebus operating every 15 minutes at peak times and every 30 minutes at all other times.  There are also cycle to work and car share schemes as well as evening and weekend passes for parking out of hours.The trust realises that car parking is a genuine concern for their staff and is working with government departments to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use and to increase off-site staff car parking capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the steps his Department has taken to develop global citizenship education for (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary pupils.
answer_text: Citizenship education is a statutory component of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, designed to equip young people with the knowledge, skills, and values needed to participate meaningfully in democratic life and contribute to inclusive, cohesive communities both locally and globally.At primary level, citizenship is embedded within Personal Development and Mutual Understanding, addressing themes such as diversity, cultural heritage, inclusion, human rights, and conflict resolution. At post-primary level, Local and Global Citizenship is delivered through Learning for Life and Work, enabling pupils to critically explore issues like prejudice, racism, and social justice.The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, published on 3 June 2025, provides a compelling case for comprehensive curriculum reform.  The report makes twenty-one recommendations and sets out the need for a new curriculum framework that is purpose-led; knowledge-rich; continuous and coherent; specific and focussed; and flexible and inclusive.  The Review recommends the rebranding and redesigning of LLW, as well as the need for a new Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development Programme.    I have announced my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce to lead the design and development of a new curriculum framework that embodies the principles recommended by the Review and meets the evolving needs of learners across Northern Ireland.  The Taskforce will be operational in Autumn 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps his Department have taken to maximise the role of formal and informal education settings in reducing youth violence.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Youth Service (EAYS), supported by funding from the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), has developed a multi-layered youth work model to address violence. This model combines early intervention, mentoring, education, and community engagement to reduce violence and build resilience among young people and includes:The ENGAGE programme - focuses on structured mentoring for young people at risk within their communities;CONNECT - supports young people who present at Hospital Emergency Departments;The FUSION project - embeds youth workers in schools to support vulnerable pupils;Early Intervention Programme - brings together youth workers and emergency services to engage young people involved in anti-community activities;The Mentors in Safety programme - trains young people to lead peer workshops on issues like mental health, bullying, and substance abuse, creating safer environments and stronger peer networks;The Y-BOX model - a targeted, evidence-informed intervention developed by Queen’s University Belfast and EA to address youth violence among young men.These projects and programmes are underpinned by the EA’s Social Unrest Model, which provides a strategic framework for youth work during social unrest. Youth services adopt a tailored response ranging from universal engagement and prevention to targeted interventions, protection during unrest, and post-event reflection and support.The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome. It requires all schools to have an Anti-Bullying policy and preventative measures which are kept under regular review.  Article 3 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 as amended by Article 19 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 requires a school to have a discipline policy which outlines the behaviour it expects from pupils and the sanctions it will impose for breaches in discipline. My department has produced "Pastoral Care in Schools: Promoting Positive Behaviour" guidance which sets out how a school should develop a discipline/positive behaviour policy using a whole school approach in consultation with pupils and parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children with special educational needs (SEN) do not have school place for the next academic year, in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:As of Friday 27 June, there are currently eight pupils with a statement of special educational needs and with an address in the North Down constituency area that do not currently have their school place for September 2025 confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made any complaints to the PSNI since March 2024, for the unauthorised erection of flags on street furniture.
answer_text: When a complaint about a flag erected on street furniture is received, my officials will always contact the PSNI for advice and to assess if removal is likely to increase community tensions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many flags her Department has removed from street furniture, since March 2024.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to improve the early screening and identification of (i) dyslexia; and (ii) dyscalculia, in school age children.
answer_text: Early actions from the SEN Reform Agenda will see the implementation of a Graduated Response Framework and roll out of Education Authority (EA) Local IMPACT Teams from September 2025. Both of these actions will see earlier identification and support for children and young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN).The EA Language and Communication Service has a range of resources and training to support schools in early identification, assessment and intervention in areas including developmental and expressive language and phonological development such as the EA Literacy Inclusion Toolkit, assistive technology software and training modules for schools supporting numeracy difficulties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any guidance his Department provides to schools on supporting pupils through bereavement and grief.
answer_text: Bereavement is one of many issues that our children and young people may face and demonstrates the importance of schools having a whole school approach to emotional health and wellbeing.The Education Authority led ‘Being Well Doing Well’ programme is a key part of helping schools develop and improve their approach, culture and ethos to ensure pupils are supported throughout their school years.  This programme includes bereavement as part of its learning and development programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 26073/22-27, to detail an update on the review of seasonal opening arrangements for Holywood Motte for summer 2025.
answer_text: Holywood Motte is open from noon until 4pm on Sundays; my officials are currently recruiting seasonal staff to extend July and August opening hours 10am to 4pm on Sundays and Mondays.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether a feasibility study for Grey Point Fort, Helen’s Bay, will be included in his Department's budget for 2025-26.
answer_text: I have allocated £60k in my 2025-26 budget for a feasibility study of Grey Point Fort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the timescale for a new building for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The contract for the construction of the new school building for Crawfordsburn Primary School was awarded to Bell Contracts Ltd on 14 May 2025. Construction is due to commence on site in July 2025 with completion programmed for November 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the role of the Children's Court Guardian Agency in (i) public law; and (ii) private law, legal proceedings.

To ask the Minister of Health how many child contact visits have been cancelled due to staffing availability in the (i) Bangor Child Contact Centre; and (ii) Ards Child Contact centre in the last three years.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust, which has a Service Level Agreement in place with both Contact Centres, has advised that no contact visits have been cancelled due to staffing availability at either location. The Centres are contracted for 48 sessions per annum, which have been delivered in each of the last 3 years with the exception of 2022/23 when a single session was cancelled due to the Bangor facility not being available.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a progress update on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024-2026.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail retention initiatives for social workers in the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care Trusts deliver a range of initiatives that focus on the retention of social workers. For newly qualified social workers this includes individual coaching, mentoring, and the provision of monthly support sessions. Early-career social workers are also supported by more senior staff to complete their mandatory Assessed Year in Employment requirements which allows them to progress in their HSC career journey. Trusts provide a range of opportunities designed to encourage the retention of all social work staff which includes:  in-service training,  University-accredited Professional in Practice programmes,events which showcase and celebrate best social work practice, andwellbeing and self-care initiatives.My Department has also been working collaboratively with Trusts to develop a range of initiatives designed to support the retention of social workers. These include: safer staffing guidance which will help to inform more manageable caseloads for social workers,a career pathway framework for HSC social work,producing a comprehensive 10-year workforce plan designed to attract, recruit and retain social workers, andtackling sickness and absence-from-work rates and sharing best practice on how to prevent and reduce staff absence rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 26402/22-27, when he will provide an update on timelines.
answer_text: I will issue an update on timelines for the Queen’s Parade project shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has approved the Outline Business Case for the Reawakening project at the Ulster Folk Museum.
answer_text: My Department has been closely engaging with National Museums NI on the Reawakening project at the Ulster Folk Museum and are continuing to work through the stages of the Department’s internal approval process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister if their Department stipulated a preference for Local Change Fund allocations going to groups based in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Letters of Offer between TEO and Councils required Councils to adhere to the ‘Core Components of Local Grant Schemes’ in the design and administration of local change funds. These core components defined “eligible applicants” as “CVS organisations formally constituted and legally able to operate in Northern Ireland, and whose primary operations and/or beneficiaries are within the specific local council area to which they are making an application.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department provides domestic abuse awareness training for public transport workers.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink has a Domestic Violence and Abuse Policy and a Child Protection Policy.  Awareness is raised through internal communications and at Corporate Induction.  The Domestic Violence and Abuse policy’s associated Management Guidelines provides access to a Domestic Abuse Awareness Raising Tool.In addition, all Translink front line staff receive training on the ‘Ask for Angela’ Womens Night Safety Charter NI as well as training on child protection responsibilities including reporting of any concerns. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has undertaken a mapping exercise of services relating to Ending Violence Against Women and Girls across the whole of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: As part of the development process for the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework, a comprehensive scoping exercise was undertaken to review strategies and approaches across other jurisdictions, including England, Scotland, Wales, and Ireland, as well as internationally.In 2024, we conducted a high-level mapping exercise to identify general services relating to violence against women and girls provided by Northern Ireland Departments. We continue to work closely with neighbouring regions to align our efforts and benefit from shared best practice and learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what work his Department is progressing to tackle misogyny and sexism in sport at all levels.
answer_text: In March 2022, my Department launched ‘Active Living’ the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland, which includes goals to ensure that sport and physical activity are inclusive, safe, diverse, shared, fun and offers equality of opportunity to participate for all. 		The participation of women and girls in sport and physical activity is fundamental to the Strategy, both from a participation and governance perspective. I am committed to ensuring that women and girls feel welcome, valued, supported and protected at every level in the sport sector, from grassroots participation to high performance and leadership. My Department and Sport NI are actively working to break down the barriers that women and girls face within the sector.		As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.		Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.		For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the tender process for the operation of Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre and café.
answer_text: The tender for an operator for Ballycopeland Windmill was published in February 2025, however no acceptable tenders were submitted.My officials are reviewing the tender specification and intend go back out to tender later in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of social workers in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of guidance on the management of anaphylaxis in schools being undertaken by the (i) Department of Education; and (ii) Education Authority.
answer_text: My department is progressing an update to the ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’ Guidance in collaboration with the Department of Health and the Education Authority.Department of Health guidance on the use of Adrenaline Auto-Injectors (AAIs) in schools appended to the departmental guidance was revised in October 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the commencement date for the Queen's Parade redevelopment project.
answer_text: My officials, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work very closely with the developer, Bangor Marine, in an effort to progress this scheme. Outstanding legal matters are almost finalised, which will enable a start on site.  These issues have proven more complex than anticipated and I hope to provide an update on timelines soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department provides any financial support to those with essential medical equipment at home in relation to energy costs.
answer_text: The only service area where payment is routinely made in respect of energy costs is Nephrology, where Home Haemodialysis (HHD) patients get £50 a month to go towards electricity costs.  This payment is made by BHSCT to all HHD patients regardless of which health trust their care is under.A reimbursement arrangement is also in place for patients using Peritoneal Dialysis (PD) machines. Usage is significantly less than the HHD machines and therefore payment reflects this.   The amount paid in respect of PD usage is £2.36 per week.There is a rebate scheme for reimbursement for patients in receipt of home oxygen. This is not administered by Trusts but NIE in conjunction with BOC (British Oxygen Company).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department has considered the needs of those with long-term medical conditions in the development of his Department's Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy is founded on an analysis of the risk factors for falling into poverty; the major impacts of poverty on people’s lives; and the factors that enable people to exit poverty. The evidence shows that living with a long-term medical condition is one of those risk factors as well as a potential impact of poverty. Based on this analysis, I have sought actions and commitments from across the Executive departments, including my own, aimed at improving the lives of all people experiencing and at risk of experiencing poverty across all communities in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work her Department has undertaken to reduce household energy bills for those with essential medical equipment in their homes since the beginning of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My Department along with the Utility Regulator (UR) and the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland (CCNI) continues to work closely to ensure that energy is affordable for all consumers. The Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy aims to move away from the imported fossil fuels that put us in the position of a “price-taker”.  One of the key objectives of the strategy is to ensure that all consumers, business and domestic, have access to essential and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible. Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE Networks) offer a medical customer care information service to customers who are dependent on life supporting equipment. If they use electrical equipment that is vital to their health, they can register with NIE Networks to receive up-to-date information during a power cut or interruption to your supply. They can register for this service on the NIE Networks website (Register for NIE's critical care service | Northern Ireland Electricity Networks) or by calling NIE Network’s customer helpline on 03457 643 643.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the review of Non-Domestic Vacant Rate exclusions.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to my answer to AQW 26076/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the review of the Small Business Rate Relief scheme.
answer_text: ANSWERMy officials have begun preparatory work on the planned Strategic Rating Reviews for 2025/26 and have commenced initial meetings with stakeholders to outline the intended approach. The Review process will formally commence after the Easter period with the Terms of Reference being published and provided to MLAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department’s plans to commission research on the cost of doing business.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 25646/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will extend the opening times for Holywood Motte.
answer_text: I am committed to making our State Care Monuments accessible to all where possible and I am aware of keen public interest in extending the opening hours of Holywood Motte. My officials in Historic Environment Division are currently reviewing their seasonal opening arrangements in advance of summer 2025.  I have instructed them to consider extending the opening hours at Holywood Motte as part of this exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the total number of children that applied through the Education Authority for a pre-school place in North Down for the 2025-26 academic year; (ii) the number of children that did not receive their first choice; and (iii) the number of children that did not receive any allocation in North Down.
answer_text: The EA has advised that at this stage in the pre-school admissions process, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and stage two remains ongoing at this time.  The EA has advised that there are currently sufficient funded places available to accommodate all children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. The current information in respect of the North Down parliamentary constituency area is set out in the table below.Total No’ of children that applied for a funded pre-school place in North Down parliamentary constituency area at Stage one792No’ of children that did not receive their first preference at Stage one96No’ of children that did not receive an offer in a preferred setting in North Down parliamentary constituency area at Stage one12No’ of funded pre-school places available at end of Stage one in North Down parliamentary constituency area78

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following a recommendation from the Marcella Domestic Homicide Review, how he will support the Education Authority to develop domestic abuse awareness training for school safeguarding teams.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that a training webinar on domestic abuse and the impact on children and young people is under development, in direct response to the ‘Marcella Domestic Homicide Review’. This will sit alongside the existing training for school safeguarding teams, which includes specific content in respect of domestic abuse.My department remains engaged in delivery of key actions as set out in the cross-departmental Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy: Action Plan 2024-2031.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will recall the High Street Task Force to implement the recommendations included in the Delivering a 21st Century High Street report.
answer_text: The High Street Task Force concluded its work when it presented its Report to Junior Ministers in March 2022. Since then, a number of the recommendations have been taken forward by Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance, and Infrastructure. Further detail can be sought from those departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will implement the recommendations arising from the Marcella Domestic Homicide Review for the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Department has noted the publication of the Marcella Domestic Homicide Review and will take time to consider the recommendations in full.The Housing Executive launched its Domestic Abuse Action Plan in December 2024 and a new training package has been developed to increase staff awareness of Domestic Abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is using its town centres first approach planning policy to benefit the North Down area.
answer_text: My Department’s regional planning policy contained in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) requires a town centre first approach for the location of new retail development and other main town centre uses.
