Councils must take account of the SPPS in the preparation of their Local Development Plans (LDPs), including Ards and North Down Borough Council who are continuing with their plan preparation for their draft Plan Strategy, which is anticipated later this year as indicated by the Council timetable. The provisions of the SPPS are also a material consideration in the determination of individual planning applications and appeals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department evaluates outcomes of the Go Succeed service.
answer_text: The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government as primary funders of the Go Succeed Service is responsible for evaluating its outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any training and educational resources his Department has made available to (i) primary; and (ii) secondary care professionals on cardiovascular disease in women.
answer_text: My Department does not directly or routinely provide training and educational resources to healthcare professionals. After completing undergraduate training, which will include up to date learning about cardiovascular disease, clinicians (Doctors, Nurses and other Allied Health Professionals) engage in continuing professional development (CPD) and will address their own learning needs through participation in online and in person courses, reading journal articles, implementing new clinical standards and guidelines, engaging in audit and quality improvement. Much of the ongoing learning and professional development is accessed through the various professional bodies. Within primary care, training can be accessed locally through GP Federations.  The content of training delivered by GP Federations is considered and chosen by members of the Federation in keeping with the training needs locally and regionally identified by our health care professionals.Currently, Northern Ireland Centre for Pharmacy Learning and Development (NICPLD), which is funded by my Department, offers over 32 CPD hours of cardiovascular education for pharmacists through online modules, live webinars, and face-to-face events. Primary care staff, including pharmacists, may also access a wide breadth of additional cardiovascular content through PrescQIPP, an NHS-funded not-for-profit organisation that promotes safe, evidence-based, and cost-effective prescribing, which is also funded by my Department. This education is designed to be comprehensive and inclusive, addressing cardiovascular care across the general population. While not condition-specific by gender, the content is clinically appropriate and applicable to all individuals, supporting pharmacists in the delivery of safe, effective, and equitable cardiovascular care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to review adherence to current guidelines from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence on cardiovascular disease and heart attacks, with a view to identify sex-specific disparities.
answer_text: National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) is considered to be best practice in Northern Ireland and my Department maintains a formal link with the Institute. NICE Guidelines are implemented within Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in line with Department of Health Circular HSC (SQSD) 13/22. My Department is monitoring the Trusts’ adherence to these guidelines. As the current NICE Guidelines pertaining to cardiovascular disease and heart attacks do not identify sex-specific disparities to the extent which would require an adherence review, my Department has no plans for such review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department has engaged the Public Health Agency to promote public awareness on the symptoms of heart disease in women.
answer_text: I recognise the need for improved education and information on a wide range of women’s health issues and risk factors, to support women and healthcare professionals in the identification, management and treatment of conditions including cardiovascular disease.While budget pressures continue to limit my Department's ability to improve public education and awareness on a range of important health matters, my officials have been engaging with the Public Health Agency to consider how best to do so within current resources and in a way that meets the specific needs of different groups of women and girls.  This will form part of the Women’s Health Action Plan which my Department is developing.The Public Health Agency also has an important role to play in promoting education and awareness in relation to public health initiatives which my Department is developing, which aim to tackle the health risks and behaviours that can lead to cardiovascular disease, such as consumption of alcohol, tobacco and vape products and obesity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of safety on the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. My officials are in the process of reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI for accident cluster sites, including the A2 Bangor to Belfast dual carriageway, to identify if there are any common causation factors which would benefit from the introduction of effective road safety measures. Any engineering measures which are proposed will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to available funding.In the meantime, I am pleased to advise that my officials have taken forward the cleaning of safety signage along the A2, as well as cutting back any overgrown vegetation. Action has also been taken to resolve reliability issues with the signalised junctions at Ballysallagh and Seahill. One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department supports uniformed youth groups.
answer_text: My department provides support to uniformed youth groups through the Education Authority’s Youth Service Grant aid assistance to local and regional uniformed organisations across Northern Ireland as part of the Funding Scheme for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations.Local uniformed organisations who are registered with the Education Authority can apply for “Generic/Non-Targeted” funding annually.  The amount funded is between £800-£3,000 per group, depending on the number of young people attending and hours of operation.Generic/Non-targeted funding is available to all registered voluntary youth organisations who deliver programmes on a volunteer staff basis and funding can be used to make a contribution to curriculum delivery, insurance and heat/light overheads.Representatives from uniformed groups are invited to attend Local Advisory Group meetings, which take place 6 times per year.   Regional Headquarter organisations can apply for Regional Strategic Funding (Up to £150k per annum), Regional Project Funding (Up to £50k per annum) and Regional Development Funding (funding dependent on the need). The Regional Strategic Funding provides uniformed headquarter bodies with resources to support their local units in the areas of governance; curriculum; representation and collaboration; workforce development; safeguarding and child protection; quality assurance; and youth work outcomes.In addition, the Regional Development Funding has been provided to uniformed headquarter organisations to support the recruitment, retention and development of their volunteers locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23015/22-27, to detail any guidance his Department has issued to Health and Social Care Trusts on hyperthyroidism.
answer_text: NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence) produce recommendations to help practitioners and commissioners get the best care to people fast, while ensuring value for money.  Whilst there is no specific Departmental guidance issued to practitioners on hyperthyroidism, my Department have shared the NICE guidelines on this condition.Guidance on the treatment of Thyroid conditions can be found on the NICE website: Overview | Thyroid disease: assessment and management | Guidance | NICE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department engages with the family courts system.
answer_text: My Department is represented on the Shadow Family Justice Board, which was established in response to the 2017 Review of Civil and Family Justice.  This is led by a Senior Family Judge and include representation from across the judiciary as well as other government/statutory organisations, including HSC Trusts.In addition, my officials engage with the judiciary on specific matters of mutual interest, as such matters arise.  Examples include presentations on looked after children in contact with the criminal justice system, and on a new memorandum of understanding between the Department and Probation Board for Northern Ireland relating to reports being provided to the judiciary on the impact on a child arising from a custodial sentence of a parent/carer.The approval of Departmental officials is also provided to the court as part of applications made by HSC Trusts under Article 44 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, where a secure accommodation order is being sought in respect of a child aged under 13 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the Go Succeed programme.
answer_text: Go Succeed is the Council led business start-up programme for the North of Ireland. It was launched in November 2023 and is majority funded through the British Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). Go Succeed plays a crucial role in helping entrepreneurs across the North start and grow their own businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 21118/22-27; and (ii) AQW 21119/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how he is implementing the recommendations included in the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report that are relevant to his Department.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 17411/22-27 which provides an outline of my Department’s response to the recommendations.In addition to this, the member will also wish to note that on 10 March 2025, following debates in the Assembly, I announced extensions to the both the Small Business Rate Relief scheme and the Back in Business scheme for the 2025/26 rating year.   On the recommendation for more frequent non-domestic revaluations to ensure fairness, equity and transparency in our taxation system my Department has demonstrated its commitment to this with Land & Property Services conducting non-domestic revaluations in 2015, 2020, 2023 and the next exercise due to be completed in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how she is implementing the recommendations included in the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report that are relevant to her Department.
answer_text: My Department’s regional planning policy contained in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) requires a town centre first approach for the location of new retail development and other main town centre uses.  Councils are required to take account of this in the preparation of their Local Development Plans.   I can advise that in October last year my Department responded to correspondence from the First Minister and deputy First Minister in relation to the proposed next steps on implementation of the report of the High Streets Task Force. A senior DfI official has been nominated to sit on any cross departmental group of the relevant departments, along with local councils to explore the opportunities for progressing the recommendations contained in the Task Force Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she is implementing the recommendations included in the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report that are relevant to her Department.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my Department on the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter will serve to support implementation of the recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he is implementing the recommendations included in the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report that are relevant to his Department.
answer_text: Of the 14 recommendations in the High Street Task Force Report, three could be identified where DfC would be seen as a significant lead department.  These are Recommendations 2, 5 and 12.  Both 5 and 12 require Executive endorsement and in the case of Recommendation 5, a significant Capital Budget to allow for implementation.Recommendation 2 endorses the Living High Streets Craft Kit and recommends that it is rolled out with the necessary support for implementation in each high street, village, town and city centre that is prioritised for action by the relevant district council.  This recommendation was recognised as one where DfC, given its relationship to the Ministerial Advisory Group on Architecture and the Built Environment (MAG), would have a leading role.On that basis, the Department undertook, subject to available resources, to carry out up to three Pilots of the “Living High Streets Craft Kit”.  The first pilot was conducted in Downpatrick and resulted in the launch of the Downpatrick Living High Streets Framework in September 2023.The second pilot project, to create the Shankill Living High Streets Framework commenced in September 2024.  To date it has delivered an intense series of community ‘Place’ workshops; seeking to understand the positive and negative aspects of the Greater Shankill area. This information has been collated and will now form the basis of an ‘Imagining’ event in April 2025 which will look how the area can be improved.  It is expected that the process will conclude in June 2025, when all findings will shape a Physical Development Framework and Action Plan.Recommendation 5 urges the Executive to prioritise the delivery of place-making by developing and implementing an investment programme for blue-green infrastructure, cleaner and greener public realm and sustainable transport.An Outline Business Case for a cross-departmental regeneration programme is currently in development by DfC in partnership with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). The vision is to implement a strategic and integrated approach to tackling the challenges facing our village, town and city centres. The proposals will be consulted upon in 2025 and the overall Programme will require Executive approval.Recommendation 12 proposes that the Executive should devolve further powers to local government, including those needed for regeneration.  It recommends that Ministers engage with elected representatives from local government and the Assembly to explore the benefits of extending powers in this way.Due to the significant period, which has passed since the introduction of the last Regeneration Bill (2014), and the experience of recent initiatives in response to Covid-19 e.g. the Covid-19 Recovery Revitalisation and Small Settlements Programmes in partnership DfI and DAERA, the cross-departmental nature of regeneration activities has been clearly demonstrated, confirming the ne

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: To ensure the smooth implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors.This group worked in conjunction with the Equality Commission and guidance for schools was published with the support of Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee. The Equality Commission delivering a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance. The target audience for the webinars was those school governors, principals, heads of departments involved in the recruitment and selection of school teachers. The Chair of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) management side wrote to all schools explaining the requirements for the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.The Education Authority is responsible for preparing and submitting the annual monitoring return to the Equality Commission for teachers in controlled schools and CCMS is responsible for carrying out this function for teachers in Catholic maintained schools. All other schools with over ten staff are required to submit monitoring returns to the Equality Commission.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act 2022 came into effect on 12th May 2024. It ensures that all teachers should be allowed to enjoy the same legislative provisions as other workers here, by being included in fair employment monitoring processes.In December 2024, The Fair Employment (Monitoring) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 came into operation. These transitional arrangements aligned reporting processes for schools, for fair employment monitoring purposes.The Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 (FETO) places responsibility for fair employment monitoring on the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland. The Commission will continue to monitor the employment returns from the education sector, including the information relating to teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance his Department has issued to Court Children's Officers relating to recommendations of contact arrangements for children and their parents or guardians.
answer_text: The Department has not issued any guidance specifically relating to recommendations of contact arrangements to Court Children's Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the March 2025 Violence against women and homelessness report by Heriot-Watt University and the University of Edinburgh, which was commissioned by the Community Foundation Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is committed to supporting the delivery of Executive priorities including the Ending of Violence Against Women and Girls and the Domestic and Sexual Abuse strategies and the report can help inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration his Department has given to initiating a partnership with a Financial Conduct Authority authorised Cycle to Work provider, in order to improve the Northern Ireland Civil Service cycle to work scheme.
answer_text: The existing Cycle to Work scheme is designed to promote staff health and wellbeing while delivering cost savings and supporting sustainability.  The last review of the scheme was in 2022.  The Department plans to look at the overall suite of benefits offered to civil servants over the next two years.The Department of Finance currently has no plans to partner with any organisations outside of the current Cycle to Work scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the roll-out of the 20 miles per hour speed limit outside schools.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the introduction of Lucy’s Law.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new school building for Priory Integrated College, Holywood.
answer_text: The plans for the new school build for Priory Integrated College are well advanced. The technical design and pre-tender estimate (RIBA Stage 4) report was lodged with the Department on 18 December 2024 and this is currently being reviewed by the Department’s technical advisers. Due to an increase in estimated project costs a Business Case Addendum (BCA) is required and this will be prepared by the Education Authority following the conclusion of the review of the Stage 4 report.However, following conclusion of this current stage, the project will unfortunately be unable to progress to the construction stage until there is sufficient budget available to do so.I will of course continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained capital funding for Education, to permit this and many other much needed school build projects to proceed to construction as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department plans to involve apprentices in the work her Department leads in to tackle violence against women and girls.
answer_text: My Department has supported the establishment of a sector led Tertiary Working Group on Ending Violence Against Women and Girls. This Group will play a vital role in fostering collaboration across our tertiary institutions, departments and other key stakeholders as we collectively seek to contribute to this priority as set out in the Programme for Government. The first meeting in March focused on establishing a clear Terms of Reference, sharing best practice and identifying key priorities for the Group. I am pleased to note that there is a student representative on this group and that engagement will take place as work progresses with students across all settings including those in further and higher education and those on an apprenticeship programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the establishment of an Ending Violence Against Women and Girls in further and higher education working group.
answer_text: My Department has supported the establishment of a sector led Tertiary Working Group on Ending Violence Against Women and Girls. This Group will play a vital role in fostering collaboration across our tertiary institutions, departments and other key stakeholders as we collectively seek to contribute to this priority as set out in the Programme for Government. The first meeting in March focused on establishing a clear Terms of Reference, sharing best practice and identifying key priorities for the Group. I am pleased to note that there is a student representative on this group and that engagement will take place as work progresses with students across all settings including those in further and higher education and those on an apprenticeship programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the creation of an Ending Violence Against Women and Girls training portfolio for the Northern Ireland Civil Service, as outlined in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan.
answer_text: ANSWEROfficials within NICSHR, a Directorate within my Department, are working in collaboration with The Executive Office to develop learning interventions to support ending violence against women and girls. This includes current work on an online learning product, which covers the Active Bystander Approach.  Additional development proposals include an online learning product to raise awareness generally of violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to include both further and higher education students in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls in further and higher education working group.
answer_text: My Department has supported the establishment of a sector led Tertiary Working Group on Ending Violence Against Women and Girls. This Group will play a vital role in fostering collaboration across our tertiary institutions, departments and other key stakeholders as we collectively seek to contribute to this priority as set out in the Programme for Government. The first meeting in March focused on establishing a clear Terms of Reference, sharing best practice and identifying key priorities for the Group. I am pleased to note that there is a student representative on this group and that engagement will take place as work progresses with students across all settings including those in further and higher education and those on an apprenticeship programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on (i) his Department's work on reviewing divorce, separation and dissolution legislation; and (ii) a timeline for when updated legislation will be brought forward.
answer_text: Work to review the law on divorce, separation and dissolution began in 2024. To date, Department of Finance officials have reviewed the relevant legislation in other jurisdictions and held a series of meetings with key local stakeholders to obtain their views on the current arrangements and on options for change. On foot of this, my officials prepared a scoping paper, which I have now considered, and I have asked them to prepare a public consultation exercise. I hope to launch that in the coming months. Consultation responses, once analysed, will then inform the development and drafting of detailed legislative proposals for the Assembly to consider, amend and approve. I cannot indicate at this stage when legislation will be passed, however, my aim is for this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals recorded with Hashimoto's disease in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: HSC Trusts do not collect this information as Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common form of primary hypothyroidism in Northern Ireland. Most people are diagnosed and treated in a primary care setting and are never referred to endocrine services in acute hospital. The prevalence rate in our community may be as high as 2-3%.  Given that the majority of people with this condition are never referred to secondary care, this number would not be reflective of the prevalence rate in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will provide any financial support to youth services organisations that had their premises damaged during Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My department administers allocations of capital funding to voluntary youth organisations and is unable to do so outside of a youth capital scheme call. There are not currently any youth capital schemes open for applications and there is no timeline at this stage for a future call.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department holds a record of the number of practitioners that deliver both private and public healthcare.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the number of GP practitioners who deliver private primary medical services in addition to services under the General Medical Services (GMS) contract.   General Practitioners in Northern Ireland are independent contractors. Under the General Medical Services (GMS) Contracts Regulations 2004, there is no restriction preventing a GP contractor who holds a GMS contract from also providing private primary medical services to individuals who are not registered patients with the practice.Where a practice adopts such a hybrid model, it is required to continue to provide GMS services to the practice’s registered patients and there should be no reduction in service provision for those patients. Under the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework, practices are required to provide assurance to the Department that funding provided by the Department is not used in the delivery of private services to non-registered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of individuals with hyperthyroidism, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any guidance his Department has issued to Health and Social Care Trusts on treatment.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the number of individuals with hyperthyroidism.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department has carried out to address patient ageism across the healthcare system.
answer_text: Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 requires that public authorities, in carrying out their functions relating to Northern Ireland, should have due regard to the need to promote equality of opportunity between persons of different age. The Department undertakes an Audit of Inequalities exercise to identify systemic inequalities across the nine Section 75 Categories to inform the development of its Equality Action Plan which is currently under review. Any issues or concerns in respect of the operational provision of Health & Social Care services should be addressed to the relevant service provider in the first instance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process of sharing patient medical history between jurisdictions across the United Kingdom; and (ii) any guidance his Department has issued to Health and Social Care Trusts to accomplish this.
answer_text: The process of sharing patient medical history between jurisdictions across the United Kingdom varies depending on the data sharing requirements, processes employed by care provider organisations involved and associated guidelines.   All information must be processed and shared in line with the UK General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Data Protection Act 2018. Guidance and Codes of Practice issued by the Information Commissioner Office (ICO) must also be followed. Information shared for secondary uses must also follow  UK data protection law, ICO guidance and the guidance set out in Code of Practice on Protecting the Confidentiality of Service User Information issued by the Department- https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/code-practice-protecting-confidentiality-service-user-information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recommendations from the January 2024 Getting It Right First Time Gynaecology Report.
answer_text: My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, to inform and support service improvement and investment decisions for gynaecology services across Northern Ireland– this will include considering the recommendations in the Getting It Right First Time report. Task and Finish Groups have been established as part of the Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum.  These groups will look at developing regional pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care.  The pathways will consider at what stages a woman can be managed in general practice with clear guidelines for management and skills transference, when required, and an enhanced triage process on referral ensuring that women receive the required assessment at each stage of their journey.  Referral into inpatient and day case care will be standardised and equitable for all patients regardless of where they live.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of endocrine specialists in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Each Trust has provided the following information in regard to the availability of endocrine specialists within their Trust.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed 13 endocrinology consultants. Northern Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed 3 permanent endocrine consultants and 2 locum consultants. These consultants are employed as General Medicine Consultants within the Endocrine-Diabetes specialism. Based on the question as asked, South Eastern Trust is unable to provide an exact number of specialists as there are many people working within wider endocrinology – including within diabetes teams, in surgery for endocrine cancers, and paediatrics.Southern Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed 3 substantive general medicine physicians with a specialist interest of diabetology and endocrinology. The Trust has also some locum provision contributing to the diabetes and endocrine service. Western Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed 4.89 whole time equivalent consultants, 1.0 whole time equivalent speciality doctor and 1.0 whole time equivalent specialist registrar.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what steps her Department is taking to improve active travel in North Down.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether her Department has considered extending student finance to those privately funding studies at the Institute of Professional Legal Studies, both in relation to (i) fees; and (ii) maintenance support.
answer_text: Postgraduate students, including those studying at the Institute of Professional Legal Studies at Queen’s University Belfast, are generally entitled to a tuition fee loan of up to £6,500 but do not have entitlement to maintenance grants or loans. My Department is committed to undertaking a review of higher education funding which, among other issues, will consider the merit and practicability of adjustments to the current package of student support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department works with the Department of Justice to fulfil the Children's Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is engaged in the Children’s Social Care Services Reform Programme, including through representation on the Reform Programme Board and Programme Workstreams. The Reform Programme was established to address a range of known service challenges within children’s social care and to progress recommendations made by the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.  27 of the 53 Review recommendations are being progressed by the Programme.  Separately, officials are working directly with Department of Justice officials to progress a number of other recommendations made by the Review that are directly relevant to the Department of Justice. Health and Justice Ministers have engaged in connection with one of the Review’s key recommendations to establish a Children and Families Arm’s Length Body. Like me, the Minister of Justice has publicly indicated her support for the creation of an ALB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department works with the Department of Finance to fulfil the Children's Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme.
answer_text: My Department submitted bids to the Department of Finance to inform the Executive’s consideration of Budget 2025-28. In addition to the bids to cover our inescapable pressures, we submitted bids for other key departmental priorities including Children's Social Care Reform.Unfortunately, the 2025-26 draft budget allocation to Health will not be sufficient to cover the significant increases in cost, which we will face as a result of pay and price inflation, increased National Insurance contributions for GPs, pharmacists and social care providers and rising demand. This means we are faced with a significant shortfall in funding and while I would like to be able to direct additional investment to areas such as children’s social care reform, I will need to consider all of the funding pressures my Department is facing before I make any final funding decisions. I also submitted an Executive paper to seek Ministers’ considerations on cross-cutting recommendations made by the Independent Review of Children Social Care Services that will require an Executive position to progress.  This included an ask to Ministers to consider the establishment of an Executive Family Wellbeing Fund in line with one of the recommendations from the independent review of children’s social care services. I have not yet received a response from the Minister of Finance on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department has taken to make (i) private tenancies; and (ii) mortgages more affordable.
answer_text: In order to help assist with affordability in accessing the private rented sector, my Department has already limited the amount of tenancy deposit that a landlord can request or retain to no more than one month’s rent.To assist with ongoing affordability challenges, I have instructed my officials to take forward work to commence a restriction on the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12-month period, with a 3-month written notice to be given.  I anticipate that the restriction will be in place from April.Commencing this legislation will assist low-income tenants by providing an assurance that they will not face multiple or unexpected rent increases over a short period of time.Supporting people into home ownership who are unable to do so due to affordability issues is another of my Department’s key housing priorities.This is demonstrated through the large commitment made to Co-Ownership in recent years, providing £158m in funding, primarily Financial Transactions Capital (FTC), to assist over 4,400 people into home ownership between 2020/21 and 2024/25 through its shared ownership product, Co-Own. Although the first-time buyer market here remains strong, I recognise that this group can face significant challenges to purchase a home, and the availability of a deposit is often as great a barrier to people as the price of the property. That is why it is so important that Co-Ownership customers require significantly lower, and indeed, sometimes no deposit to access their mortgage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of (i) current availability; and (ii) future sustainability of orthodontics in North Down.
answer_text: All general dental practitioners in Northern Ireland can provide orthodontic treatment under the General Dental Services contract without a specialist qualification.A number of high street specialist orthodontic practices are located across Northern Ireland, with one such practice located in Bangor, County Down.I am aware that there has been in increase in waiting times for orthodontic treatment through specialist practices due to increased demand for treatment and the small number of specialists who are working in practice.Sustainability of the orthodontic workforce is influenced by a number of factors including the available dental workforce. There are currently a number of Dental Core Trainees working within the orthodontic specialty in the School of Dentistry, Belfast. As of February 2025, there is one orthodontic specialist trainee in post completing the consultant training pathway. In recent years the School of Dentistry has been unable to support the training of orthodontic specialty trainees  seeking to complete qualification for entry to the orthodontic Specialist List due to a lack of capacity within the consultant team.The Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency are actively exploring innovative solutions to support orthodontic provision in Northern Ireland. This includes the establishment of a Diploma in Orthodontic Therapy, commencing in June 2025, for the first time in Northern Ireland. Orthodontic therapists are able to carry out a range of orthodontic treatment, under the direction of an orthodontist or dentist, as part of skill mix team in specialist practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what focus the Women's Health Action Plan will place on gynaecological cancers, particularly symptom awareness.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032 recognises the importance of raising awareness of signs and symptoms of all cancers to enable early diagnosis, treatment and support better outcomes for patients.  The Women's Health Action Plan will seek to place a greater focus on conditions which affect women's health and well-being, including gynaecological conditions and cancers. To help inform the development of the Action Plan, my Department recently hosted a number of engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals.  It was evident through this constructive dialogue that women want to see improvements in terms of public awareness, information and education on women’s health matters from an early age and across the life course, and I am fully supportive of making this one of the key objectives. Unfortunately, budget pressures have led to a pause across all campaign advertising currently, with detrimental impacts on my Department's ability to improve public education and awareness on a range of important public health matters including cancer and women's health.  However, in conjunction with the Public Health Agency, my officials are considering how best to educate and inform specific groups of women and girls through other approaches in a way that meets their needs as work on the Action Plan continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure appropriate training and support is in place to aid GPs in the timely diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.  All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including recognising ovarian cancer.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence) produce recommendations to help practitioners and commissioners get the best care to people fast, while ensuring value for money.  NICE guidelines are shared with GPs once they have been approved for application within Northern Ireland.  NICE have guidelines for the recognition and early management of ovarian cancer.The Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) is a strategic clinical network that brings together those who use, provide and commission services to make improvements in patient pathways using an integrated, whole system approach.  They provide GP Referral Guidance for Suspected Cancer which includes sections on suspected gynaecological cancers and suspected ovarian cancer.  My Department have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners. GPNI webinars have included a range of cancer related topics including ovarian cancerPractice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to shortening the diagnostic pathway for ovarian cancer.
answer_text: My Department’s has established a Regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Gynaecology Services Forum to oversee the implementation of service improvement recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report for Northern Ireland," published in January 2024.  GIRFT recommendations include streamlining patient pathways to improve diagnostic pathway and efficiency in patient care.  This incudes development of pathways that use a range of skills mix of nursing and allied health professionals to assist with triage and diagnostic procedures including colposcopy and hysteroscopy.  This should improve timescales in diagnosis for all gynaecological cancers including ovarian cancer. All Trusts adhere to NICE guidelines when triaging suspected cancer referrals and detecting ovarian cancer, (NICE CG122: Ovarian cancer: recognition and initial management).  To improve ovarian cancer survival, ensuring patients reach diagnostic pathways as early as possible is as important as the diagnostic pathways themselves.  The Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group (CRG) is currently working to update the Primary Care Guidance for Suspected Cancers for red flag gynaecological referral. This guidance will reinforce the need for GPs to test patients presenting with key ovarian cancer symptoms, using the CA125 blood test (to measure raised levels of a protein that can be elevated in women with ovarian or fallopian tube cancer) to refer them appropriately onto the suspect cancer red flag pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department undertakes with each Health and Social Care Trust to support (i) those with endometriosis; and (ii) those with endometriosis symptoms whilst on a waiting list for diagnosis.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting list for a laparoscopy to treat endometriosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Unfortunately, HSC Trusts are unable to distinguish which patients are requiring an endometriosis diagnosis as this information is not recorded on hospital systems. Patients also have laparoscopes for other conditions than endometriosis.To provide this information would be at disproportionate cost, this would require potentially trawling through hundreds of patient records on inpatient and day case waiting lists to ascertain if they are waiting for an investigative or treatment surgery for suspected endometriosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting list for a gynaecology appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Outpatient Waiting TimesThe number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Gynaecology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin’s associated Excel file. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-september-2024 Inpatient Waiting TimesThe number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Gynaecology, Gynaecological Oncology, and Gynaecology - Urology specialties, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient And Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin’s associated Excel file. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-waiting-times-september-2024

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of staffing levels for adult ADHD service provision in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD and therefore no staff has been specifically recruited to provide this service.  Although there is no commissioned Adult ADHD service Belfast, Northern and Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts provide a level of provision from existing staff complement. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) in response to demand, has progressed a small unfunded service for those patients who have transferred with an existing diagnosis and who are prescribed medication and require monitoring as per NICE Guidelines. SEHSCT have advised that the number of staff sessions available for this service varies as it is unfunded and funded work must take priority.For patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.SEHSCT receives referrals for Adult Mental Health Services via Mental Health Assessment Centres located in Newtownards, Lisburn and Downpatrick.  Individuals, including those with a potential/confirmed co-morbid ADHD diagnosis are offered a mental health assessment and are managed via the adult mental health core care pathway, where appropriate.Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address waiting lists for adult ADHD services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD and therefore no staff has been specifically recruited to provide this service.  Although there is no commissioned Adult ADHD service Belfast, Northern and Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts provide a level of provision from existing staff complement. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) in response to demand, has progressed a small unfunded service for those patients who have transferred with an existing diagnosis and who are prescribed medication and require monitoring as per NICE Guidelines. SEHSCT have advised that the number of staff sessions available for this service varies as it is unfunded and funded work must take priority.For patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.SEHSCT receives referrals for Adult Mental Health Services via Mental Health Assessment Centres located in Newtownards, Lisburn and Downpatrick.  Individuals, including those with a potential/confirmed co-morbid ADHD diagnosis are offered a mental health assessment and are managed via the adult mental health core care pathway, where appropriate.Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department’s rationale for undertaking a mass redundancy exercise in further education colleges in 2024, only to begin recruitment for permanent listings in 2025.
answer_text: Any recruitment is not to fill vacancies for staff who left under the voluntary exit scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number reports of spiking recorded in each of the last five years, broken down by Emergency Department in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the Trust is unable to confirm the number of patients who have attended ED as a result of “spiking”.  This information is not routinely recorded and the cost of providing this information, which would require a manual trawl through hundreds of patient’s notes, could only be completed at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support his Department coordinates across the Health and Social Care Trusts for those with adverse childhood experiences.
answer_text: The Department has been involved in awareness raising and research into the impact of childhood trauma for many years.  More specifically, the Department supports the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) in its role as lead for Trauma Informed Practice and Trauma Informed Care.The SBNI works with Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) to embed Trauma Informed Approach (TIA) to address Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs). The SBNI has delivered Trauma Informed Organisational Toolkit sessions attended by HSCTs and the HSCTs are represented on the SBNI Trauma Informed Practice Committee. SBNI also provides free access to all HSC staff to regarding raising awareness of the impact of adversities and trauma via online training modules on HSC Learn, with 879 certificates at Level 1 and 512 certificates at Level 2 awarded in 2024. In addition to this, the SBNI offer free access to further training for HSC staff via the Solihull Approach modules (Understanding Attachment, Brain development and Trauma).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether provisions to protect victims of domestic abuse who are seeking legal separation from their spouse will be included in the Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill.
answer_text: The Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill, on which my Department consulted in 2021-22, focuses on belief marriage and increasing the current minimum age for marriage and civil partnership from 16 to 18. Work is separately in progress to review the substantive law on divorce, separation and dissolution. In this our aim is to ensure that the relevant legislation better meets the needs of those who wish to end their marriage or partnership, including people who have experienced domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions that are taken by Health and Social Care Trusts following the assignment of a (i) Public Protection flag; and (ii) Public Protection / Sharing to Safeguard flag on encompass.
answer_text: Public Protection flags are assigned to an individual’s record in line with Regional Public Protection Arrangements NI (PPANI) Risk Assessment and Management.  PPANI will review the individual’s record depending on their category of risk, every 16 weeks or annually.  PPANI staff will review existing flags and amend or remove to reflect any change in the Risk Management Plan.  Flags will have an expiry date added where applicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) Public Protection flags; and (ii) Public Protection / Sharing to Safeguard flags that have been issued since the encompass system launched, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This table below the volume of Public Protection and Safeguarding/Public Protection FYI flags currently assigned to patients broken down by each patient's Trust of Residence on encompass. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the resource allocation for the South Eastern Domestic Violence Partnership in the last five years.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Violence Partnerships are not formally constituted and therefore not directly resourced by the Department of Health. Each Health and Social Care Trust provides the Partnership within their geographical area with funding for a co-ordinator post, with Partnerships otherwise reliant on funding from other sources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has access to, and monitors, records to ascertain whether a Public Protection flag on encompass has been assigned to a member of staff that works within a health and social care setting.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of the recently activated Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was spent in the North Down constituency; and (ii) a breakdown of the spend.
answer_text: I have ring-fenced up to £1m to alleviate the impact of Storm Éowyn.  As the scheme is still live and costs are re-imbursed to council retrospectively, it is not possible to provide a more detailed breakdown at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has had any discussions with the banking sector in relation to setting up a Banking Hub in Bangor.
answer_text: In her engagements with the banking sector, my predecessor, Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA, pressed on banks to protect their remaining branch network in the North and to maintain access to cash and vital banking services especially for areas impacted by bank closures, including the timely roll out of banking hubs in impacted areas.I am therefore disappointed to learn that Halifax has announced that its branch in Bangor is due to close on 29 May 2025.  I am also aware that LINK has recently completed an assessment of cash access in Bangor and recommended that no additional services, such as a banking hub, are needed at this time.I plan to visit the Warrenpoint Banking Hub next month, where I will meet with Cash Access UK and press on the need for appropriate in-person access to banking services for those areas losing bank branches, including Bangor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve in-person access to banking facilities in Bangor city centre.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 21733/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of requests for traffic calming measures in Donaghadee his Department has received in the last three years.
answer_text: Traffic calming schemes are funded through my Department’s Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) budget which covers a range of measures aimed at providing a safer road transport system. Requests are considered in line with current policies and guidance and any works will be subject to prioritisation with all viable proposals competing for limited resource.My Department prepares priority lists of traffic calming measures and annual planned programmes of work are presented to respective Councils when budgets have been confirmed.As traffic calming measures covers a wide range of requests, any attempt to provide you with the information requested would require an extensive exercise to determine the number related to Donaghadee for a 3 year period. This may only produce data with a limited degree of exactitude and would very likely involve disproportionate costs.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work his Department has undertaken to ensure private tenancies are affordable for students.
answer_text: Private rented legislation does not distinguish on the basis of the characteristics of tenants (including whether or not a student lives in the tenancy).  My balanced programme of reform will therefore ensure that the Private Rented Sector is a good housing option for all tenants. In order to help relieve affordability issues, my Department will restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12-month period, with a 3-month written notice to be given.  I have instructed my officials to take forward work to commence this power and I anticipate that the restriction will be in place from April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of recipients of Education Maintenance Allowance in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Responsibility for the administration of Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA), including the assessment of eligibility and payment of support, has been delegated to the Student Loans Company (SLC). As such, information on recipients is held by the SLC rather than by my Department. The SLC does not currently hold an analysis of EMA recipients broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether improving accessibility of the Sydenham bypass footbridge will be included in the ongoing works to the road.
answer_text: The current resurfacing scheme on the A2 Sydenham Bypass is focused on essential carriageway maintenance and structural improvements to ensure the long-term integrity of the road surface and does not include works to modify the accessibility of the footbridge.Any future works to the footbridge will be considered in line with broader infrastructure priorities and available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to improve rail access to the George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), examined how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the Review. In support of this objective, the Review recommends developing new or improving existing rail-airport connections at both Belfast International Airport and George Best Belfast City Airport. Translink are undertaking a Feasibility Study for options to improve the accessibility and connectivity to George Best Belfast City Airport. The study is examining options for potential connections to the airport including the projected costs for each identified option. The feasibility study is due to be completed by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the uniform policy for Speech and Language Therapists based in education settings in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement she has had with civil service Trade Unions on the revised Northern Ireland Civil Service Dignity at Work policy.
answer_text: ANSWERMy department has extensively engaged with Central Trade Union side (CTUS) throughout the review of the Dignity at Work policy. Formal consultation with CTUS has commenced through the established Whitley procedures with a view to implementing a revised policy before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will provide any financial assistance to Ards and North Down Borough Council for repairs to Bangor Aurora Aquatic and Leisure Complex for damage which occurred during Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: In response to Storm Éowyn, I activated and extended the scope of the Non-flooding Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) to provide financial assistance to councils. I have ring-fenced funding of up to £1m for outreach services, delivered in partnership with councils and the community and voluntary sector, to ensure the right supports are in place in the right way for those most in need.  The costs of repairs to buildings are not eligible for funding under this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has had any communication with Ulster Rugby in relation to any incidents of (i) harassment; (ii) bullying; (iii) hazing; and (iv) child safeguarding issues during the Ulster Schools' Cup.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (“the Act”) states that the Board of Governors (BoG) must determine the measures to be taken at the school with a view to preventing bullying involving a registered pupil at the school. This includes while the pupil is in the lawful control or charge of a member of the staff of the school or while the pupil is receiving educational provision arranged on behalf of the school and provided elsewhere than on the premises of the school. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority (EA) provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT has a focus on preventative education to embed cultures that eradicate bullying. The team also provides children, young people and parents/carers, with support to deal with specific incidents of bullying. While the EA do not offer any specific service or support directly to school sport teams regarding hazing, and ABSIT do not refer to “hazing” specifically in guidance and advice, behaviours such as this would be addressed through the wider definitions of bullying. The use of the Addressing Bullying Type Behaviour Policy and use of Bullying Concern Assessment Form (BCAF) may be appropriate in this incident. A school’s BoG has the statutory remit to oversee the implementation of the Act in their setting, within the context of the pastoral suite of Positive Behaviour, Safeguarding and other associated policies. Article 3 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 as amended by Article 19 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 requires a school to have a discipline policy which outlines the behaviour it expects from pupils and the sanctions it will impose for breaches in discipline. The Order does not dictate any fixed format for the discipline policy. Responsibility for discipline within schools’ rests with the school Principal and its BoG and they should promote positive behaviour among pupils attending the school to safeguard their welfare and facilitate their educational progress at school.  The Department’s guidance, "Pastoral Care in Schools: Promoting Positive Behaviour" sets out how a school should develop a discipline/positive behaviour policy using a whole school approach and in consultation with pupils and parents.In addition, the BoG has a statutory duty to safeguard pupils (Articles 17, 18 and 19 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 refer).Neither the Department nor the Education Authority has had any communication with Ulster Rugby incidents arising during the Ulster Schools' Cup.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether the current Educational Visits guidance for post-primary schools provides adequate measures in relation to the safeguarding of young people.
answer_text: Responsibility for oversight of the Educational Visits guidance resides with the Education Authority (EA) and it also considers making any updates as may be necessary to the guidance. This would include co-ordination and the collation of advice and guidance from a range of EA services in relation to such visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will update the guidance on Educational Visits to include incidents of hazing.
answer_text: Responsibility for oversight of the Educational Visits guidance resides with the Education Authority (EA) and it also considers making any updates as may be necessary to the guidance. This would include co-ordination and the collation of advice and guidance from a range of EA services in relation to such visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the roll-out of the Sexual Offences Legal Advisers scheme.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on actions being taken to improve animal welfare.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
