question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust delivers appropriate support for women who are experiencing menopause alongside other health conditions.
answer_text: I acknowledge the current waiting lists for many services are longer than we would like them to be, and that includes gynaecology services. However, there is currently considerable work underway across the Health and Care System, including South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, to try to reduce waiting lists, although the current financial position does make progress difficult. You will acknowledge you supported the 2024/25 budget settlement.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum will oversee ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate and secondary care level for local and regional populations, as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. This work will include the development of a pathway for Menopause to ensure equitable access to services for women across Northern Ireland.Alongside this, I understand that my officials are also establishing a Regional Engagement Forum in the coming months, this will allow my department to garner expert user feedback from stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the sites her Department has responsibility for in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: My Department has one owned and three leased buildings in the North Down constituency for the use of civil servants. These are:Rathgael House, Bangor (Owned)Crown Buildings, East Street, Newtownards (Leased)Crown Buildings, Hamilton Road, Bangor (Leased)Sketrick House, Newtownards (Leased)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will publish a consultation report following the Domestic Abuse Safe Leave regulations consultation which closed in September 2024.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing the consultation responses in respect of Safe Leave.  My officials are due to brief the Economy Committee on the responses received on 5th February 2025.  Following this, the consultation response will be finalised and published in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the waiting times for a child assessment for autism, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) for his assessment of these waiting times.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the waiting times for an adult assessment for autism, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) for his assessment of these waiting times.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) financial resourcing; and (ii) staff support his Department has made available in each Health and Social Care Trust for those experiencing menopause.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the delivery to date of the proposed projects outlined in the Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2024-2025.
answer_text: My Department endeavours to deliver all projects listed within each of the Annual Roads Reports to Council including those within the Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council.  However, there can be external factors resulting in some projects not being delivered within the planned financial year, for example, lack of contractor capacity, severe weather conditions etc. Any project that is not completed as planned will be prioritised for completion in the following year, subject to funding availability and competing priorities, and included in the subsequent Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve sporting facilities in North Down.
answer_text: In accordance with Article 10 of the Recreation and Youth Service (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, the statutory responsibility for the provision of facilities for recreational, sport, physical and cultural activities lies with the relevant local authority for each area.I understand that Sport NI, an arm’s length body of the Department, is undertaking an infrastructure review of sporting and physical activity facilities across Northern Ireland. When completed it is anticipated the results of this survey will be shared with Councils and the sports sector and may help identify areas where provision of facilities can be improved.Finally, subject to funding, I hope to announce the reopening of the Your School Your Club initiative for 2025-2026 in the next few months. The purpose of the initiative is to promote community use of school sports facilities outside of normal school hours. Investment is disbursed to revitalise existing school sports facilities or to develop new and additional provision on school sites whilst ensuring access and use for local community users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The purpose of the Women’s Health Action Plan, which my Department is currently developing, is to highlight ongoing policy and service development initiatives, and to outline the actions and improvements that can be delivered over the next three years to improve health outcomes for women. The focus to date has been on mapping the work that is already under way across the health and social care system (including, for example improving maternity, gynae and menopause services, cervical screening and breast cancer services, and planning for a mother and baby unit) and identifying areas where further action is needed (for example, improving public health awareness around women’s health, providing more training and education for healthcare professionals on these issues, promoting healthy lifestyles and tackling health inequality). My Department is currently hosting a number of engagement events to listen and share insights & priorities with a diverse range of women and health professionals to further inform the development of the Action Plan.  The pace and extent of delivery will be determined by our available capacity and resources, which remain under extreme pressure. You may also be aware that my Department part-funded the Women’s Health Survey for Northern Ireland which launched on 11 November 2024.  The survey is being carried out by the School of Nursing & Midwifery at Queen’s University, Belfast, in partnership with Derry Well Women.  Its purpose is to hear directly from women about their specific health needs and experiences to help shape planning for women’s health services.  The survey will remain open into February 2025 and I would be grateful for your support in sharing the survey and encouraging as many women as possible to take this opportunity to share their views.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has given any consideration to establishing overdose prevention facilities.
answer_text: My Department leads on the Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery which recognises the need to target those most at risk, including those who are homeless, people who inject drugs and those who are in contact with the justice system. This strategy aims to directly reduce the harm for these groups through actions that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures for people who use alcohol and other drugs. There are no proposals in the strategy on Overdose Prevention Facilities. The issue of criminal sanctions for those found in possession of drugs is an issue that is frequently raised with this type of facility, hence any such intervention would also require a wider assessment of the legal position and be cross-cutting in nature. However, I am aware of the pilot sites that have just opened in Glasgow and Dublin, and my Department will closely watch the development and evaluation of these pilots to assess their potential effectiveness.  In the meantime, it is important to note that there is currently a full range of services available for those who require help and support with substance use issues, and for their families. A directory of services available in each Trust area across Northern Ireland is available online at: https://drugsandalcoholni.info/self-help-resources/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is supporting the Reawakening project at the Ulster Folk Museum.
answer_text: My Department has been closely engaging with National Museums NI on the Reawakening project at the Ulster Folk Museum. National Museums NI are in the process of providing my Department with an updated Outline Business Case for the project. Once this has been received, it will be considered in line with the Department’s internal approval process.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the future plans for the recently demolished Rathgael House site on Balloo Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 16521/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding that has been available to facilitate nursing home care in North Down in the last five years.
answer_text: North Down is situated in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT). SEHSCT representatives have advised that it is not possible to provide a value solely relating to nursing care homes based in the North Down locality. SEHSCT representatives have advised that their trust’s total payments to Nursing Homes, in the financial year 2023/24 was £56,358,400 and in the financial year 2024/25 is £52,317,629.00 to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of salt box (i) requests; and (ii) installations, in North Down in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the requested information on a constituency basis, but it is held by Council area. I can confirm that there have been 717 salt box requests with 30 new installations in the last three years within Ards and North Down Council area.  The member should note that many of the 717 requests can represent duplicate bids at the same location and the number of installations does not directly correlate to the number of requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide detail on (i) the outstanding legal issues which were recently announced as delaying construction of Queen’s Parade, Bangor; and (ii) the action his Department will take to resolve them.
answer_text: The outstanding issues relate primarily to the Developer’s proposed approach to developing the Marine Gardens site, and the associated legal agreements which are required with the Crown Estate. My officials continue to meet regularly with all parties to support the resolution of the remaining outstanding issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the current staffing levels of Neighbourhood District Nursing in North Down; and (ii) for his assessment of the current staffing levels.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust advise that they have funding to support one Neighbourhood District Nursing (NDN) team to operate within North Down. The remaining 8 District Nursing teams in North Down have not implemented the NDN model at this time and therefore staffing levels across North Down do not fully reflect the NDN Model.The current staffing levels within the one NDN team in North Down is 6.0 whole time equivalent (WTE) staff within the service. This is reflective of additional funding through DoH Delivering Care investment that supported the recruitment of 2 additional staff to support the 24/7 model. I am pleased to note that due to my Department’s Delivering Care investment there has been a significant increase across the entire District Nursing workforce across all Trusts in terms of staff in post when compared to March 2023. Vacancy levels have reduced from 277.65 WTE in March 2023 to 103.3 WTE in August 2024, a reduction of 174.30 WTE (62.8%).  The regional average District Nursing staff in post per 10,000 population was 6.44 in 2024 compared with 5.42 in 2023, which reflects those reduced vacancy levels. My Department continues to work with Trusts to identify appropriate means by which this improvement can be continued.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 will be implemented.
To ask the Minister for Communities when the Marine Gardens Public Realm Project, Bangor, will begin construction.
answer_text: My officials, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work closely with the developer, Bangor Marine, to finalise some outstanding legal matters. Construction is expected to commence in the near future and an announcement will be made closer to the time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children in North Down that received free school meals (i) during the 2023-24 academic year; and (ii) between September 2024 and December 2024.
answer_text: (i) There were 2,976 pupils entitled to free school meals enrolled in schools in the North Down constituency.  This figure relates to census day in October 2023, it is not indicative of the whole academic year.(ii) The Department does not hold this information.  Information on the number of pupils entitled to free school meals on census day for the 2024/25 academic year will be published at the end of February.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to modernise the technology used to process prescriptions.
answer_text: My Departments Strategic Planning and Performance Group leads on the ePharmacy programme which includes a project to replace the current written prescription system with an electronic prescribing system. Whilst there is policy support for this programme my Department continues to operate under significant budgetary and financial constraints including in our Capital budget and timelines have not yet been confirmed.  I remain hopeful that the implementation of this project will be able to proceed once capital funding has been prioritised for this programme.There are four projects within the ePharmacy programme. 		Go Pharmacy NI (to support clinical services delivery in Community Services);		Electronic Transmission of Prescriptions;		Electronic Prescribing Eligibility System (EPES) replacement; and		NI Drug Tariff Masterfile.A business case is currently in development for capital funding, however due to pressures on the departmental capital budget, this is not likely to be prioritised until 2026/27 at the earliest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will conduct a traffic flow survey for Donaghadee.
answer_text: My Department usually undertake traffic surveys for specific traffic management issues, such as for the provision of pedestrian crossings, setting speed limits, traffic calming assessments etc.  I can advise that my officials have undertaken numerous traffic data assessments on various routes within the Donaghadee area over several years to inform consideration of a range of traffic related matters.Traffic data is gathered, via a permanent counter, from A2 Millisle Road, between Donaghadee and Millisle, and published in the annual report within the Traffic and travel information section of the Departmental website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/traffic-and-travel-information-incorporating-annual-traffic-census-and-variations-traffic-flow. The collection of traffic data (obtained from sites such as the A2 Millisle Road) contributes to the effective management of the road network by providing information to assist in the allocation of departmental resources, the development of future proposals for the network and Winter Service provision.At present, my officials have no plans to undertake further traffic flow surveys within Donaghadee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the effectiveness of the Northern Ireland Civil Service Dignity at Work policy.
answer_text: The Civil Service is committed to creating and sustaining an environment where everyone is treated with dignity and respect. The Dignity at Work policy effectively supports this aim by making staff aware of the types of behaviour that might cause offence, highlighting the sources of information and assistance available and the informal and formal procedures for dealing with inappropriate behaviour.The effectiveness of the Dignity at Work policy is evidenced by: the relatively low number of civil servants who have raised formal dignity at work complaints in each of the past three financial years when set against total staff numbers of 24,000, and the success of the in-house mediation service which enabled informal resolution in 95% of mediations and avoided cases progressing to formal dignity at work complaints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what progress he has made to produce a comprehensive strategy on rejuvenating high streets and town centres, further to the debate on the Private Members' Motion on 4 November 2024.
answer_text: Question 10 – AQO 1386/22-27High streets are crucial in energising our local economies and are woven into the fabric of our local communities.  Whilst the Department for Communities has a remit for town centre renewal, it is not for one Department to develop a comprehensive strategy. In recognition of the cross-departmental approach required to effect meaningful change, TEO commissioned a High Street Task Force which delivered its report and recommendations in 2022.I have identified that the High Street Task Force Recommendation 8: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’ sits within the direct remit of my department. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my priority on Good Jobs, and my department is already actively working on initiatives that will support its implementation. Rejuvenating our high streets and town centres requires a response from across the Executive and I remain committed to cooperating with my Executive colleagues to find solutions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what support his Department has made available to charities and community groups in North Down during winter 2024-2025.
answer_text: My Department provides financial support to a number of charities and community organisations in the North Down constituency, however this funding is provided on an annual basis and is not specific to the winter period. Within the North Down constituency, funding of approximately £513,000 was provided to Voluntary & Community organisation projects in the 2024/2025 financial year.In addition, £31.6k was allocated to Ards & North Down Borough Council through the Department’s Community Festivals Fund, which provides grants to community-based festivals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria his Department has in place for someone to become approved to give emergency respite to those caring for someone with additional needs.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of emergency respite/ Short Break services, aligned to assessed needs.Emergency respite care is a temporary care solution provided in critical or unforeseen situations when primary caregivers unexpectedly need time off, such as illness or emergencies, or there maybe a safeguarding concern which might mean care needs provided outside of the family home or by someone else.The type of emergency care required is identified through an assessment of need. During office hours this assessment is completed by a named worker or through the duty system. Emergency care can be accessed out of hours through the regional emergency social work service. In the first instance, an increase in the person’s support system including family/ carers and existing service provisions would be explored. This would include any additional support available through existing respite services that the person may already have access to.  In Children Services, in an emergency if no family are able to provide increased support or if it is deemed unsuitable for the child to remain in the current situation, the Trust would source Emergency Foster Carers. Foster Carers are registered to provide care on a short-term emergency basis. A robust regional registration and approval system is in place for Foster Carers in Northern Ireland. To become a Foster Carer, individuals must undertake ongoing learning and development and also engage with social workers and other parties involved in the child or young person’s life, included educational and health professionals. Rigorous assessments and checks are completed. Together, the five Trusts’ fostering services are supported by HSC NI Adoption and Foster Care.Within Adult Services if family/ carers are unable to provide additional support, an increase to any domiciliary care package already in place would be considered.  Additional respite/ Short Breaks would also be considered including any service already in place through the Shared Lives Scheme. Shared Lives NI host carers are people who open their homes to support eligible adults with support needs. These carers are registered through a robust process within each Health and Social Care Trust, who are responsible for the delivery of local schemes.  If there are not appropriate existing services that can be increased, emergency respite/short break services are primarily provided in statutory and independent sector care homes. The Trusts have contractual arrangements in place to enable emergency package of care to be implemented or emergency admissions to care facilities.  Contracts are awarded through regional procurement and tendering processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding made available for community groups who deliver care and activity for those with severe learning disabilities in North Down in each financial year since 2019-20.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for the provision of services and may commission community groups to deliver services, in accordance with assessed needs and available funding. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have outlined the following approximate levels of funding made available from Children and Adult Services to community groups who deliver care and activity, for those with severe learning disabilities in North Down in each year. 2019/2020: £2,383,6702020/2021: £2,440,0882021/2022: £3,111,2582022/2023: £2,199,8272023/2024: £2,400,9612024/2025: £2,527,152

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any ongoing work by his Department to support parents and guardians whose children have been diagnosed with (i) autism spectrum disorder; (ii) learning difficulties; and (iii) attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: In October 2024, I was pleased to announce additional regional funding for the Children with Disability Service. It is intended that this funding will assist with increasing short breaks capacity, and expand family, therapeutic and behavioural supports. The challenges faced by those caring for children who have additional needs is fully recognised, and the positive impact that breaks from caring can have on mental, physical and emotional wellbeing.  DoH has received proposed Short-Term Actions to assist with alleviating the pressures experienced within the Children with Disability Service from all Trusts. These proposals will provide increased residential and short break capacity across the Trusts for Children with Disability, in addition to expanding family, therapeutic & behavioural support.  The Children’s Social Care Reform Programme are reviewing models of short breaks for children which are working well, to understand how they could be replicated and expanded across Northern Ireland.  This includes additional residential capacity as well as specialist short term foster care.  There is also a plan to change legislation so that, where it is appropriate to do so, some children can receive short break care without becoming ‘looked after’. It is important to recognise that overnight residential Short Breaks are only a part of the continuum of services needed for children with disabilities, and the opportunities to expand support services that can be provided in the community are being actively considered. Other supports include Self-Directed Support (SDS) options. Direct payments, available through Self-Directed Support increases choice and promotes independence providing for a more flexible response to meeting the needs of service users. Managed budgets as a way for people to manage and pay for their care and support needs.  In a managed budget, a Trust holds the budget, but the individual has control over how it is spent. It is recognised that further work is needed to ensure that people supported by HSC can avail of managed budgets, subject to assessed needs. Carers Assessments are also offered and completed, when appropriate.Parents and guardians of children diagnosed with ASD, ADHD or LD will have access to all of the supports available through HSC Trusts including access to specialist advice and signposting. Advice and signposting for Autism ADHD and Learning Disability is available through the Family Support NI website. FamilySupportNI - Family support and childcare services across Northern IrelandSearch Results in Disability - Learning Disability, Autism, ADHD CategoryHelp and support is specific to individual families and there is specific ASD supports available in each Trust at the web links below. BHSCT - Pre-diagnostic Resources - 1Paediatric ASD Resources - Northern Health and Social Care TrustAutism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Co-ordinator (South Eastern Trust) - Directory ListingAutism Services - 1  (SHSCT)Children and Young Peopl

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department actively incorporates the views of (i) patients; and (ii) patients’ families into the decisions of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is committed to its statutory duty with regards to Involvement and Consultation as set out in the Health and Social Care Reform Act (NI) 2009. The involvement of service users, carers and other key stakeholders is ‘at the heart’ of effective planning, delivery and evaluation of our health and social care services. The Trust receives feedback from service users and carers through a range of methods, including: - Online User Feedback System (Care Opinion) - 10,000 More Voices - Patient & Client Experience Standards Programme - Bespoke Survey Programmes - Compliments - Complaints- Public Consultations Feedback is regularly reviewed by teams and changes are planned and evaluated based on service user and carer experience.The Trust has established a Service User Network of service users and carers who are interested in supporting Trust services through involvement opportunities. The Network membership is continuing to grow with an expanding range of opportunities available. The Trust has a central Involvement and Experience team, the role of which is to promote and support the embedding of service user and carer Involvement and Experience across the Trust at all levels.The Trust has a clear leadership and governance structure in place to ensure service user and carer Involvement and Experience is embedded into policy and practice. The Trust Involvement and Experience Leadership Committee leads the strategic development of the service user and carer Involvement and Experience agenda across the Trust to ensure that Involvement and Experience principles, programmes and initiatives are embedded at service level. Committee membership consists of Trust staff, health and social care partners and service user and carer representatives. Assurances are given at Trust Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the availability of overnight respite care provision for both adults and children in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is adequate.
answer_text: 1). The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust advise that there is no one on the waiting list for adult short breaks.  There are 63 children on the waiting list for overnight short breaks in the South Eastern Trust.  2).  The availability of overnight short breaks is adequate for adult services.  This is reflected in the fact that there is no one on the waiting list for adult short breaks.  The Minister recognises that there are children on the waiting list for overnight short breaks and acknowledges the impact of this on children and their carers.   There has been an increasing regional demand for access to short breaks & respite services.  It is of note that emergency respite care is available for children and adults.   It is provided in critical or unforeseen situations when primary caregivers are unexpectedly unavailable, through illness or emergencies, or there may be a safeguarding concern which may mean that care needs provided outside of the family home.  3).  The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust were contacted by Department of Health officials in relation to overnight respite care providers available to applicants who live in North Down.   The Trust advises that with regards to adult services they commission the Cedar Foundation to provide two facilities for overnight respite in the North Down area – Croft and Twisel Lodge.   Both facilities can provide respite for those who have a brain injury, physical and sensory disability and autism.   The Trust can also spot purchase respite from other independent providers as required to meet the needs of adults depending on the assessment of need.In relation to children overnight short breaks they are offered in the Greenhill short break facility in Newcastle.On 23rd October 2024, the Minister was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the children with disability service. It is intended that this funding will assist with increasing short breaks capacity, and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports.  Trusts are actively engaging with Department of Health officials to finalise their proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department or a Health and Social Care Trust has made a referral to an overnight respite care facility for (i) adults; and (ii) children, in (a) Scotland; (b) England; (c) Wales; and (d) the Republic of Ireland, in place of a space in Northern Ireland due to lack of availability, since 2022.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/Short Break services, aligned to assessed needs. Within both Children’s and Adult Services respite/ Short Breaks are provided within the person’s own home, through the provision of enhanced domiciliary care packages or within the homes of Foster Carers or Shared Lives Carers. Alternatively respite/ Short Breaks can be provided within  statutory and independent sector care homes.  My Department is not aware of any Health and Social Care Trust referring an adult or child to Scotland, England, Wales or the Republic of Ireland for overnight respite care, due to lack of availability in Northern Ireland. My Department is currently finalising the Children with Disability Framework and the Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. Both aim to respond to increased challenges faced by families of children and adults with disability and to support HSCTS in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a Regional perspective and locally in each of the HSCTs is required, including the provision of respite/Short Break Services. On 17 October 2024, I approved the allocation of £2 million in year for children with disabilities, with a recurrent impact of £13.1 million. My Department has ensured this funding has been ringfenced to directly improve short breaks capacity, while rapidly expanding a range of family, therapeutic and behavioural supports. In addition, my Department is leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream and the Maximising Capacity workstream will support best use of current respite/ Short Break provision and expansion of service provision within care home settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applicants on a waiting list in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to avail of (i) adult; and (ii) child overnight respite care.
answer_text: 1). The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust advise that there is no one on the waiting list for adult short breaks.  There are 63 children on the waiting list for overnight short breaks in the South Eastern Trust.  2).  The availability of overnight short breaks is adequate for adult services.  This is reflected in the fact that there is no one on the waiting list for adult short breaks.  The Minister recognises that there are children on the waiting list for overnight short breaks and acknowledges the impact of this on children and their carers.   There has been an increasing regional demand for access to short breaks & respite services.  It is of note that emergency respite care is available for children and adults.   It is provided in critical or unforeseen situations when primary caregivers are unexpectedly unavailable, through illness or emergencies, or there may be a safeguarding concern which may mean that care needs provided outside of the family home.  3).  The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust were contacted by Department of Health officials in relation to overnight respite care providers available to applicants who live in North Down.   The Trust advises that with regards to adult services they commission the Cedar Foundation to provide two facilities for overnight respite in the North Down area – Croft and Twisel Lodge.   Both facilities can provide respite for those who have a brain injury, physical and sensory disability and autism.   The Trust can also spot purchase respite from other independent providers as required to meet the needs of adults depending on the assessment of need.In relation to children overnight short breaks they are offered in the Greenhill short break facility in Newcastle.On 23rd October 2024, the Minister was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the children with disability service. It is intended that this funding will assist with increasing short breaks capacity, and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports.  Trusts are actively engaging with Department of Health officials to finalise their proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is regulating day centres for those with additional needs.
answer_text: Day centres are regulated as adult day care settings and offer services to older people and those who have mental health issues, physical disabilities and/or learning disabilities. Settings are defined through legislation as a place where persons in need of prescribed services may attend for the purposes of assessment, rehabilitation or counselling but where they are not provided with board or accommodation.Day centres are subject to registration and inspection by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Inspection Authority.Minimum standards can be found at:https://www.rqia.org.uk/RQIA/media/RQIA/Resources/Standards/adult_day_care_standards_-_final_version_-_january_2012.pdf Community-based day opportunities, typically provided by the independent sector, are not regulated in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is considering introducing a further energy payment for grassroots organisations impacted by rising costs.
answer_text: On 5 June 2024 I announced that, despite the significant shortfall in both the Resource and Capital budget allocations for my department, I had decided to protect the level of resource funding provided to Voluntary and Community Sector (VCS) groups through the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme.In addition to that, I have provided an additional 5% of resource (£1.45m) of resource funding to organisations funded through my departments, Community Empowerment Division (CED) and Voluntary and Community Division (VCD) programmes.This additional resource element is intended, as part of my ongoing commitment to supporting the sector, to make a contribution towards easing the pressures VCS groups face with rising costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of a new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School, including a timescale for completion.
answer_text: I announced Bangor Central Integrated Primary School to proceed to construction on 28 May 2024. Following a review of the proposed project a reduction in the total enrolment numbers at the school in recent years was identified, which prompted my officials to engage with school representatives and the school’s Managing Authority regarding the implications of this reduction in enrolment.  I am happy to report that agreement regarding the size of the school and provision of Enhanced Learning Support has now been reached between all parties and the Integrated Consultant Team have updated the final design accordingly. The process to procure an Integrated Supply Team is imminent and the current programme estimates construction commencement in October 2025 with completion in April 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following publication of the review of the part-time 20mph speed limits at schools initiative, whether he will consider introducing a 20mph speed limit as standard in the vicinity of all schools, as is the case in Scotland.
answer_text: One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes, particularly to speed when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. As part of my consideration of the recently published review of the part-time 20mph speed limit at schools initiative I asked my officials to prepare me a paper on 20mph speed limits in general, which is in progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) criteria; and (ii) application process for receipt of the energy payment provided for grassroots organisations impacted by rising costs in 2022.
answer_text: Since 2022, on three separate occasions my Department has provided payments to support Voluntary & Community Sector (VCS) organisations to meet the pressures associated with the cost-of-living crisis. On each occasion payments were limited to those VCS organisations who had a funding relationship with the Department’s Voluntary & Community Division and Community Empowerment Division. In May 2022 a payment of £1,000 was offered, with the additional criteria that the organisation’s funding included support for running costs;In January 2023 a payment of up to £1,500 was offered with no additional criteria; andIn March 2024 a payment of £1,500 was provided also with no additional criteria.For each payment the application process consisted of eligible organisations indicating that they wished to receive the support offered and completing a declaration to that effect.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the progress of actions in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024 – 2026.
answer_text: The Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework 2024-31 and first Delivery Plan 2024-26 were launched on 16 September 2024. Since then, progress has been made across multiple areas, reflecting consistent momentum toward achieving the six outcomes within the Strategic Framework.£3.2 million has been allocated to the EVAWG Change Fund. We launched the EVAWG Local Change Fund on the 8 January 2025 and will open across 11 Councils for applications between January and March 2025, with delivery commencing from April 2025. Momentum Funding of £600k is also being provided across all 11 Councils in 24/25 to enable them to implement emergency responses, raise awareness and build on the ground momentum.We also announced £1.2m investment under the EVAWG Regional Change Fund on 21 January 2025. This funding will be available until March 2026 and will support and partner with eight Community and Voluntary Sector expert organisations who are already leading the way regionally in preventing violence against women and girls. They will raise awareness of violence against women and girls, increase understanding of the issue, and build capacity to take action, and equip people to have safe and healthy relationships. Following an open competition, six suppliers have been selected to develop a range of Phase 1 proofs of concept, as part of the Small Business Research Initiative [SBRI] Challenge Fund.Work is also progressing in collaboration with partners to advance other elements of the Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his assessment of the impact of the recent employer National Insurance announcement on dental practices; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to support dental practices in relation to this.
answer_text: I recognise that the changes arising from the Autumn Budget will have a significant impact on future budget and costs. However, it is not possible to provide a definitive assessment on our future funding position until a budget settlement for future years has been reached. Despite the positive news about the 2024/25 budget, it is still likely we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.I acknowledge there are a number of healthcare providers that will be affected by the rise in employer National Insurance contributions announced in the Budget and I understand that as well as General Dental Practitioners, GPs, care homes and hospices have all voiced concerns about the impact this will have on them.  Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to improve waiting times for occupational therapy appointments in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: PHA continue to work with SPPG and Trusts to ensure Allied Health Profession (AHP) services including Occupational Therapy deliver a range of support that reflects the needs of the population of NI and is aligned to evidence based practice. Work is underway between SPPG and PHA to separate data on waiting times for under and over 18-year olds across elective AHP waiting times including Occupational Therapy.  This will help provide a better understanding of the specific waiting times across CYP and adult elective AHP services to help identify demands and trends across each of the Trusts including SET.Strategies being used by South Eastern Health and Social Care trust to help reduce waiting times are:waiting list validationmonitoring capacity and demand of complex casesproactive recruitmentutilising bank and agency staff to cover long tern vacancies or absences as appropriatereviewing new ways of working and assuring appropriate skill mix to maximise efficiency

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his work to develop a business case to improve access to the Gender Identity Service in tandem with the Gender Identity Review Group and Gender Identity Liaison Panel.
answer_text: My Department has been working together with the Belfast HSC Trust to address the issues of waiting times, resources, and to develop a new model of care for Gender Identity Services for transgender people living in NI.  As part of this process, a Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group was established and Chaired by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), with membership on the group including, the Public Health Agency, the Patient Client Council, the Royal College of General Practitioners and policy officials from my Department. The work of the Pathway Review Group began in September 2019.  There was significant engagement undertaken to help inform the review, notwithstanding the obvious challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sits under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the review. On completion of the review, my officials have continued to work closely and collaboratively with the Belfast HSC Trust, the Gender Identity Review Group and Gender Identity Liaison Group to develop a business case for the first phase of service expansion, which identifies the extra resources required to enhance the capacity of the services to meet the growing demand and includes additionality to address the implications of the Cass Review report. Funding of around £825k will be required recurrently to fund the service development.  The business case details the extra medical and nursing resources required, including additional endocrinology provision. General Practitioner shared care arrangements are currently being explored for Phase 2 of this service development. The business case was submitted for consideration on 4th November 2024. SPPG officials are currently working with the Belfast Trust to clarify  a number of issues  as part of the approval process. The business case will then be subject to my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the 2024-2025 pay award for Northern Ireland Civil Service staff.
answer_text: I met recognised Civil Service Trade Unions immediately following the October Monitoring Round.  At that meeting on Tuesday 12 November, I reiterated my commitment to working constructively with unions on Civil Service pay.Officials began negotiations with unions on the 2024 pay award last.  It would not be appropriate for me to make further comment while those negotiations are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he is taking to ensure victims of domestic violence are prioritised in the points system for housing allocations.
answer_text: My Department led a review to improve how social homes are allocated in Northern Ireland and is now working closely with The Housing Executive on the implementation of the proposals as part of the Fundamental Review of Allocations. My officials are working alongside the Housing Executive to consider independently commissioned research investigating future provision for victims of violence and those at risk or under threat of violence, including victims of domestic abuse. This will be used to inform any changes I may make to the Housing Selection Scheme. I can assure you that I am taking a very close interest in this work and I expect to make a decision on this matter soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reasons for the delay of Marine Gardens Public Realm Project, Bangor, until January 2025.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made over recent weeks and months, and the final planning approval has been granted by Ards and North Down Borough Council’s Planning Committee, at its meeting on 5 November 2024. Completion of the necessary legal agreements is anticipated in the coming weeks which will pave the way for construction of Phase 1 to commence in January 2025. This has been delayed to minimise the impact to trade in the city centre during the busy holiday period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime programme will continue on current funding levels beyond the end of March 2025.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 September, the Executive approved the extension of its Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime to 31 March 2027, with a continued investment of £8m per year for the final two years of the Programme. In the Chancellor of the Exchequer’s budget on 31 October, the UK Government (UKG) also confirmed funding of £8m for the Programme for the financial year 2025-26; UKG funding for 2026-27 – the final year of the Programme - will be considered as part of its Spending Review planned for 2025. The operating assumption is, therefore, that there will be an overall Programme budget of £16m for 2025-26.  Executive funding of £8m is earmarked for 2026-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the budget allocation to their Department to promote and encourage civic engagement with the draft Programme for Government consultation.
answer_text: In the eight weeks of public consultation, over 70 events were held and 1,375 responses received. 17 events were held for members of the public, 10 of which were online. A total of 194 people attended in-person events in Belfast, Lisburn, Ballymena, Newry, Derry/Londonderry, Enniskillen and Cookstown. A total of 205 people attended the 10 online events.A budget of £27,500 was allocated to this work of which £17,500 was spent on all aspects of the consultation including venue hire, facilitation, and advertising, with £5,300 of this money being spent on civic engagement in the form of venue hire and facilitation. Despite us holding more events and driving a greater number of responses, this has been delivered at a lower cost, in real terms, to previous Programme for Government consultations, and we would like to thank all organisations who supported this work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all public engagement events that are taking place on the draft Programme for Government consultation.
answer_text: The public consultation launched on 9th September and ran until 4th November. During this period, we held 7 in-person public events and 10 online public events which over 500 people attended. A list of all public events that have taken place are detailed below for your information.In addition to these events, we held over 70 events and meetings with stakeholders. In-Person Events:BelfastGirdwood Hub, 10 Girdwood Avenue, Belfast, BT14 6EGTuesday, 1 October 2024 - 6:30pm to 8:30pmLisburnISLAND Arts Centre, Lagan Valley Island, the Island, BT27 4RLMonday, 7th October 2024, 6:30pm - 8:30pmBallymenaThe Braid Theatre, 1-29 Bridge St, Ballymena, BT43 5EJTuesday, 8 October 2024 - 6:30pm to 8:30pmNewryCanal Court Hotel, Merchants Quay, Newry, BT35 8HFThursday, 10 October 2024 - 6:30pm to 8:30pmDerry/LondonderryDestined, Northwest Disability Learning Centre, 1 Foyle Road, Derry, BT48 6SQTuesday, 15th October 2024 - 6:30pm to 8:30pmEnniskillenFermanagh House, Broadmeadow Pl, Enniskillen, BT74 7HRThursday, 17 October 2024 - 6:30pm to 8:30pmCookstownThe Burnavon, Burn Road, BT80 8DNTuesday, 22nd October 2024, 6:30pm - 8:30pmOnline Events:Wednesday, 25 September 2024 - 12:00pm to 1:00pmMonday, 30 September 2024 - 7:15pm to 8:15pmThursday, 3 October 2024 - 12:00pm to 1:00pmFriday, 4 October 2024 – 12:00pm to 1:00pmWednesday, 9 October – 12:00pm to 1:00pmMonday, 14 October 2024 - 7:15pm to 8:15pmMonday, 21 October 2024 – 12:00pm to 1:00pmWednesday, 23 October 2024 - 12:00pm to 1:00pmTuesday, 29 October 2024 - 7:15pm to 8:15pmThursday, 31 October 2024- 12:00pm to 1:00pm

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) guidance; and (ii) financial support his Department has in place to support international and cultural exchanges for students in further and higher education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Study USA is my Department’s flagship international mobility programme, giving participants distinct academic and personal benefits, as well as equipping them with the skills to compete in a global economy. Eligible students studying at one of our local higher education institutions or further education colleges can apply for the opportunity to avail of a full academic year placement at a US college. Successful applicants benefit from free tuition fees, accommodation, food and travel. I have recently approved an expansion of Study USA, committing £1m of funding per annum, to enable 60 students to participate each year. In addition, I have waived participation fees and approved a bursary of £1,000 to help reduce the financial barriers for students from lower income households.  	Whilst not funded directly by my Department, eligible students may also be able to avail of The Turing Scheme, which replaced Erasmus+ and is the British government’s programme to provide funding for students to undertake periods of mobility in another country. My Department does provide eligible Turing Scheme participants with financial support in the form of the Disabled Students' Allowance and universities with funding to allow them to waive tuition fees for any Turing Scheme student undertaking a full year study or work placement as part of their undergraduate degree course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) guidance; and (ii) financial support his Department has in place to support international and cultural exchanges by youth and community groups in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not directly support international and cultural exchanges by youth and community groups in Northern Ireland. This issue is an operational matter for the Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies (ALB) as support for such activities is channelled through them.  Making direct contact with the relevant ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.Details of the Department’s ALBs can be found on the Department’s website www.communities-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) guidance; and (ii) financial support his Department has in place to support international and cultural exchanges led by post-primary schools in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The guidance relating to the arrangements for educational visits involving registered pupils at grant-aided schools is set out in Chapter 14 of the Governor Guide.  In addition, the Education Authority has published Educational Visits Guidance for schools on its website.All grant-aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating the Aggregated Schools’ Budget (ASB) to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  A school’s CFF allocation is expected to meet a range of items of expenditure, as detailed in Annex A1 of the CFS, including but not limited to education visits and journeys.  The CFF does not provide any specific funding to post-primary schools to support international and cultural exchanges.  In addition, my Department does not have any other funding in place for this particular purpose outside the ASB (e.g. earmarked funds).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline whether he is considering a direct subsidy scheme to improve pay for early years and childcare workers to prevent people from leaving the sector.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding allocated to maintenance of State Care Monuments in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department allocated £1,392,214 to the maintenance of State Care Monuments in 2019/20.  £1,369,355 in 2020/21 and £1,560,834 in 2021/22.  In 2022-23 this rose to £1,867,418 and further increased to £1,906,652 in 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has submitted a bid to Treasury to cover the costs of an increase to the public sector pay award.
answer_text: I have consistently made the case on the need for increased funding for public sector pay awards both in meetings with the Treasury and Chancellor. The Chief Secretary to the Treasury has confirmed that the 2024-25 budgets for Whitehall department will be substantively settled at the Autumn Budget on 30 October. Under the Barnett formula, the Executive will receive a population based share of any changes to Whitehall departments’ budgets announced on the 30 October. Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning they do not have to be spent for the purpose which gave rise to the consequential.  Following the Autumn Budget announcement, the Executive will decide how to allocate the available funding to address total departmental overcommitments and pay awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timeline for finalising a public sector pay deal for 2024-25.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided for AQW 17363-22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the cause of the sewage spill in Castle Park, Bangor, on 22 October 2024; and (ii) the steps his Department will take to address it.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that:NI Water was made aware of a sewage leak in Castle Park, Bangor on Tuesday 22nd October 2024. A thorough investigation took place immediately and a major blockage was discovered on a sewer which had been caused by a considerable amount of inappropriate materials and resulted in out of sewer flooding surcharging from a manhole out to Valentines playing fields. NI Water teams were deployed immediately to clear the blockage on the 22 October and remained on site on 23 October 2024 with a jetvac, mini excavator and three field crews to clean up the extensive area affected. NI Water also instructed its contractor to attend with a digger to help with the clean-up, including scraping the area.NI Water will respond to blockages as required.  On this occasion non sewer related debris has entered the system and caused the problem. No amount of investment in our sewers will stop out of sewer flooding if blockages caused by inappropriate materials occur. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the organisations that were part of the co-design process for the Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034.
answer_text: We are delighted to have agreed the new Victims and Survivors Strategy. The Strategy was developed in three phases, managed by an overarching Collaborative Co-Design planning group. The Executive Office (TEO), Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) and the Commission for Victims and Survivors (CVS) worked together as a Collaborative Co-Design team, holding regular meetings to share information and cross reference findings.Throughout the development of the Strategy there has been extensive engagement with a range of stakeholders. VSS funded groups, members of the Victims and Survivors Forum and individuals with lived experience were invited to attend co-design events and get involved in working groups to inform the drafting of the new Strategy. The outputs from these events and working groups helped co-design the outcomes and actions which reflect the many complex, varied and changing needs of victims and survivors.The following VSS funded Organisations were invited to Co-design Sessions:Action Mental Health (New Life Counselling Service)Aisling Centre Antrim Voluntary Welfare GroupAshton Community TrustAurora CounsellingBarnardo’s (NOVA Project)CALMSCentre for Health and WellbeingCounty Armagh PhoenixCunamhDecorum NIDerry Well WomanEly CentreFAIRFamilies Beyond ConflictFamilies Moving OnHoly Trinity CentreKoram CentreLarne Voluntary Welfare GroupLenadoon Counselling ServicesMASTMilitary and Police Support of West Tyrone (MAPS)MUVE ProjectNewry and Mourne Local Voluntary Welfare GroupOmagh Police WivesOut of the ShadowsPaper TrailPat Finucane CentreREACTRelatives for JusticeSouth and East Tyrone Welfare Support (SETWS)South Armagh Rural Women’s NetworkSouth East Fermanagh Foundation (SEFF)South Tyrone Voluntary Welfare GroupSpringhill Community HouseStrule AssociationSurvivors of TraumaTara CentreTullyvallen Family SupportUDR ColeraineUDR CastledergUDR EnniskillenUDR LisnaskeaUlster Human Rights WatchVASTWAVE Trauma Centre

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of The Story of Belfast Lough A Document to Inform Engagement between LWWP Partners; and (ii) to detail any actions his Department will take following publication of the document.
answer_text: The ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ was produced by NI Water and published on its website.   The key water quality issues in Belfast Lough, referenced in the document, have been known for some time and are identified in my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan, which was published in November 2021 following public consultation and Executive endorsement.The NI Water ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ document outlines some of the further work carried out through the Living With Water Programme since 2021. This includes integrated environmental modelling, which was funded by my Department and has been beneficial in identifying the sources of the pollution in Belfast Lough and informing the future design standards for the upgrades needed to the wastewater treatment works that discharge into Belfast Lough. It has been accepted that delivery of my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan is the optimum solution to addressing the city’s drainage and wastewater infrastructure issues, which will help protect against flooding, enhance the water environment and grow the economy. It was always acknowledged that additional funding would be required to deliver all aspects of the Belfast Plan within the original 12-year timeframe.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, published in January 2020, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding, however, this was not received. In 2023, NI Water also increased the cost estimate of its elements of the Belfast Plan by £700m, pushing the overall costs from £1.4 to £2.1 billion.  An affordability review conducted by my Department in response to this increase has concluded that the need for the Belfast Plan continues to exist, however, without the necessary budget, its original timescale is no longer achievable. Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan will therefore be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets.  In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding, however, I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether a delivery plan will be developed for the Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034.
answer_text: We are pleased that the new Victims and Survivors Strategy has been endorsed by the Executive. Our officials have been working to develop action plans for the implementation of the commitments in the Strategy and they have engaged with the Victims and Survivors Service and the Commission for Victims and Survivors on the co-design of new funding programmes to align with the new Strategy. It is vital that the action plans and funding programmes are co-designed with stakeholders including VSS, CVS, and community and voluntary groups, to ensure that plans will meet the needs as laid out in the Strategy.When the Strategy was published we wrote to the Chair of the TEO Committee, as we were aware that the Committee has not yet had the opportunity to hear from officials about the new Strategy.  Officials would welcome an opportunity to brief the Committee and seek Members’ views on the development of action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work his Department is currently undertaking to address gender inequality in the workplace.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has responsibility for the Gender Equality Strategy to which my officials have provided input.Work being carried out by my Department to address gender inequality in the workplace includes:-Assessing financial childcare support for those wishing to participate in our skills and education programmes.  Funding a number of female-specific training programmes via the Skills Fund. These provide upskilling opportunities in leadership and management for those currently employed and work placement opportunities to enable access for those not in employment. Currently 250 places are available to eligible participants across the North. A "Men into Healthcare" programme is being developed by North West Regional College to support more males to enter the health and social care workforce.The Women Returners Access Programme (WRAP) within Step Up has been created to bring women back into the workforce by refreshing skills and building confidence through the provision of a range of flexible interventions, creating progression pathways including employment.My Department is also providing support to a small number of young males considering employment opportunities within education.Invest NI is planning  the next competitive call for the Ambition to Grow Supporting Women Programme. This Programme was shaped in partnership with Women in Business NI  (WIBNI) and aims to offer up to £30k of support to up to 20 innovative export focused women-led businesses. The support will include grant monies for market development, technical, trade and job creation and will be complimented by a series of Leadership Peer Networks. Invest NI are working alongside Enterprise Ireland and in partnership with Intertrade Ireland to deliver an All-Island Entrepreneurship Programme of support. This will be funded by the Government of Ireland Shared Island monies. WIBNI will be able to apply for funding under this initiative towards their Grow It Programmes for 2024/2025.The Labour Relations Agency (LRA) has produced information, advice and guidance within the Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) sphere as many social issues with a gender dimension now feature heavily as workplace issues and to this extent the LRA has, in partnership with bodies such as NIC ICTU, the Equality Commission NI, CIPDNI and others, produced guidance on:sexual harassment in the workplace, domestic violence as a workplace issue, respectful conversations, and other gender-related material on issues such as menopause in the workplace and inclusive recruitment via flexible work practice advice working with bodies such as Timely Careers.The LRA also facilitates the Workplace Subgroup Forum for the TEO strategy group on ending violence against women and girls (EVAWAG) and this group met for the first time last week specifically to address strategic issues around what can be done via workplaces to eliminate violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will take forward the action arising from the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2023-2030 in relation to the appointment of a Non-Executive Director on each Health and Social Care Trust Board for tackling and preventing domestic and sexual abuse and violence against women and girls.
answer_text: My Department, along with the Department of Justice, published the new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024 – 2031, Performance Framework and multi-year Action Plan on 25 September 2024.  The Strategy is built around 5 pillars: Partnership; Prevention; Children and Young People; Support and Provision and Justice. A key priority area under the Prevention pillar is “Building capacity among people working in frontline and public-facing roles to identify and respond effectively to domestic and/or sexual abuse, including in relation to children and young people, at the earliest opportunity.”My Department will work with the Health and Social Care Trusts to identify opportunities to ensure that staff are adequately trained to recognise and respond to domestic and sexual abuse. As part of that work, we intend exploring the possible appointment of a Non-Executive Director to each Health and Social Care Board for tackling and preventing domestic and sexual abuse and violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Domestic Abuse Safe Leave regulations.
answer_text: The consultation on the introduction of paid safe leave from work for victims of domestic abuse closed on 27 September 2024.  		This consultation invited views on the issues that need to be included in the regulations that will implement the provisions of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (NI) 2022.  		The responses to the consultation are being analysed and will inform next steps and the detail of the regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is engaging with those with lived experience as part of delivering the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2023-2030.
answer_text: The development of the new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024 – 2031, through the Call for Views in 2022 and the Consultation in 2023, was extensively informed by those with lived experience and the voluntary and community organisations that represent them.My Department and the Department of Justice are aware that victims and survivors of domestic and sexual abuse are also key to the Strategy’s delivery and are committed to ensuring that their lived experience continues to inform our response. The intention is to both listen and respond, including taking further action where this is considered necessary.In implementing the strategy, we will engage with those with lived experience, including with the networks that have been established to support work to end violence against women and girls. We will also make available an ongoing survey mechanism through which victims can anonymously provide feedback on the support and services that they have received. Finally, we propose to make best use of the feedback and monitoring mechanisms that service providers already have in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a time frame on preparing detailed guidance for schools on Preventative Education, as outlined in the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy Action Plan.
answer_text: Guidance is being prepared following extensive engagement with a range of stakeholders, which I will consider in due course.  I have not yet set a specific time frame for issuing this guidance to schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of actions arising from the Online Safety Strategy relating to his Department.
answer_text: Substantial work continues to be carried out under the Executive’s Online Safety Strategy 3 year Action Plan and it is largely proceeding as planned. The work is led by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) online safety coordinator. Implementation of actions is overseen by a cross-departmental implementation group (CDIG), chaired by my Department.The SBNI’s Online Safety Hub, which was launched in April 2024, delivers on several actions within the Action Plan. It is a central repository of information and support relating to online safety for young people, carers and professionals; content is kept under review. The Online Safety Hub has been shortlisted in the Best Public or Third Sector IT Project category of the 2024 Belfast Telegraph IT Awards; with the winner being announced at an awards ceremony on 15 November 2024. In delivery of strategic action 14, draft sample online safety policy templates have been designed by online safety experts in NSPCC in conjunction with members and partners and been reviewed by members of the CDIG.  This action is expected to complete in Autumn this year; finalised templates will be published on the Hub. Between January and March 2024, the SBNI and PSNI ran a social media campaign for young people and adults about online dangers and reporting pathways. Focusing on sextortion, online grooming and the risks around sharing nude imagery, the campaign was well supported and secured extensive news coverage on Safer Internet Day. This campaign recently won the Best Social Media Campaign for Public Sector at the Northern Ireland Social Media Awards.In April 2024, the SBNI collaborated with the National Crime Agency to coordinate an education alert about financially motivated sexual extortion (sextortion). The alert was published on the Hub and issued to all education and youth settings through the Education Authority. It featured information for professionals and a letter for parents/carers with advice on how to talk to their child about sextortion, and how to support them if they become a victim, signposting to the PSNI and Online Safety Hub for further support. The alert secured strong news and social media coverage, supported by the Departments of Health, Justice and Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the time frame for the production of the Reflections booklet for Social Workers on Domestic and Sexual Abuse; and (ii) the stakeholders that have been consulted in relation to this booklet.
answer_text: As outlined in my Written Statement of 25 July 2024, Public Health Wales (PHW) was commissioned by WHSCT, in conjunction with the Public Health Agency (PHA), to provide a clinical opinion on the small number of cytology slides associated with the 12 cases identified with a category 3 audit outcome. On receipt of the clinical opinions from PHW, information in relation to the 12 cases (with a category 3 audit outcome from the Trust’s invasive cervical cancer audit process and notified as SAIs) was triangulated and assessed by the WHSCT and the PHA. This assessment concluded that the number of category 3 outcomes notified by WHSCT, occurred as a result of the Trust applying a different threshold when categorising cervical cancer audits with an ‘unsatisfactory’ outcome. I have been advised that the WHSCT did not deviate from the 2019 ‘Framework for the Audit of Invasive Cervical Cancers and Disclosure of Findings,’ but instead applied a more rigorous interpretation to the guidance of when an audit outcome is considered to be ‘unsatisfactory’.Additional guidance has now been issued by the PHA, using a more recent toolkit published in England, to promote a more standardised approach by all Trusts in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of current legislative protections for those living in park homes, compared to other jurisdictions across the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: I consider the current legislative provisions contained within the Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 continue to be robust in providing protection for those living on residential caravan parks here and compare favourably overall with those provided for in other jurisdictions.  		Section 4 of the 2011 Act places a statutory obligation on my Department to review the legislative provisions every five years to assess the legislation remains fit for purpose.  The last review in 2021, and published in March 2023, found that the issues or concerns raised by respondents living in residential or holiday caravans already had a pathway for resolution under existing legislation or alternative mechanisms were in place.  The follow-on action for my Department from the 2021 Review was to produce factsheets as a quick reference guide to inform both residential occupiers and site owners of their responsibilities under the legislation and provide signposting on the relevant pathway of recourse should difficulties arise. This work is nearing completion and has included engagement with representatives of those living in residential caravan park homes here to ensure the information provided is pitched to suit their needs. 	One issue which was not raised during the review was that the 2011 Act references that a residential pitch fee shall increase or decrease by a percentage aligned to the Retail Price Index (RPI).  My Department is proposing an amendment to the legislation to change this to the Consumer Price Index (CPI). Although there is no evidence to date to suggest that the legislation as it currently stands is proving to be problematic for those living in residential caravans, the amendment will bring NI in line with other jurisdictions.  Whether or not such an amendment is possible within this mandate depends on securing a suitable legislative vehicle.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how their Department plans to collaborate and cooperate with local government to deliver relevant actions in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework recognises the need for a whole of government and whole of society response. Local Government is represented within the EVAWG governance structures and network arrangements are in place to take forward actions within each Council to support the delivery of the Strategic Framework and first Delivery Plan. A key action in the EVAWG Delivery Plan is to deliver a Change Fund to support community groups to prevent and respond to violence against women and girls. We are now engaging with all local councils to work in partnership with us to deliver the Change Fund.  We also hope that the Change Fund will be a catalyst for councils to amplify and extend actions to end violence against women and girls in their plans and programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has plans to increase protections for those living in park homes in the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has indicated within the DfC legislative programme that it is seeking to amend Part 1 of the Schedule to the Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to align residential pitch fee percentage increases/decreases with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rather than the Retail Price Index (RPI).  The amendment is being sought due to CPI being classified by the Office of National Statistics (ONS) as a more appropriate measure of inflation than RPI.  That will bring this jurisdiction in line the Mobile Homes (Pitch Fees) Act which received Royal Assent on 2 May 2023 which applies to England and Wales.
