Scotland is currently in the process of seeking a similar amendment by way of the Housing (Scotland) Bill.Whether or not such an amendment is possible here within this mandate depends on whether a suitable legislative vehicle can be secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to legislate on introducing a fit and proper persons test requirement for (i) new; and (ii) existing caravan owners.
answer_text: At present my Department has no plans to bring forward legislation in respect of introducing a fit and proper person test for a site owner.  I note that the issue did not arise as a major concern during the 2021 Review of the Caravan Act (NI) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an updated timeline of the Budget 2025-26 process following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: The funding envelope for 2025-26 was announced on 30 October with the settlement for further years to be announced in spring 2025. As a result, the Executive will only be able to set a single year Budget for 2025-26 before the start of the financial year in April. It is my intention, subject to Executive agreement, to publish a draft Budget by early December to allow time for a full 12 week consultation before a final Budget is agreed by the Executive prior to the start of the new financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why Glen Road and Station Road, Craigavad, have not been adopted by his Department; and (ii) whether his Department will adopt these roads.
answer_text: My officials have confirmed that Station Road, Craigavad, from below the railway bridge, and the entire length of the Glen Road, Craigavad, are private roads and not maintained by my Department.In circumstances where a road has not been adopted by my Department as part of the street planning functions then Article 9 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 can apply. In such cases the owners or occupiers of the land need to write to the Department requesting that the street become a public road.I have been advised by my officials that they have not received any formal request for adoption from the land owners or occupiers of Station Road, Craigavad or Glen Road, Craigavad nor any request for an assessment of work required to bring these roads to the required standard for adoption into the public road network.It is important to note that the responsibility for carrying out any such remedial works and the associated costs rest solely with the owner / occupiers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) all dates and times the Sydenham bypass will be closed to facilitate the recently announced road improvement scheme; and (ii) how he will ensure the closures are communicated to the public.
answer_text: The £3.8 million road resurfacing scheme on the city bound section of the Sydenham Bypass, Belfast is progressing with overnight and weekend closures in place until Monday 25 November. Weekend closures are taking place over the next seven consecutive weekends, commencing at 10pm Friday 11 October until 6am on Monday 14 October, between the Tillysburn junction and the start of the M3. Weeknight overnight closures are in operation for the duration of the scheme. Some weekend and overnight lane closures will also be required on the country bound lanes to ensure the safety of road workers and the travelling public. The majority of the scheme is expected to be completed ahead of the Christmas period during which the road will remain open. However, further closures may be required in the New Year.Electronic message signs have been in place on the A2 Sydenham Bypass for the last four weeks to advise road users in advance of the works.  In addition, information relating to associated road closures and lane restrictions can be found on www.trafficwatchni.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the measures his Department will take to provide any support for businesses who have been impacted by suspected fraudulent activity on the Just Eat platform.
answer_text: Businesses that are impacted should consider reporting any alleged fraud toAction Fraud   - https://www.psni.police.uk/safety-and-support/keeping-safe/protecting-yourself/scams-and-fraud/reporting-fraud. My Department’s Trading Standards Service also provides support and assistance to businesses affected by scams. Traders seeking further advice should contact Consumerline on Freephone 0300 123 6262 or by e mail to Consumerline@economy-ni.gov.uk.Additionally, Just Eat appear to be aware of attempts by scammers to defraud businesses by gaining access to customer accounts and have provided the following advice to their partners :https://partner.just-eat.co.uk/knowledge-centre/update-on-scammers/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is supporting the creation of new Business Improvement Districts.
answer_text: My Department has policy and legislation responsibility for Business Improvement Districts (BIDs) and works closely with local business groups, district councils and other stakeholders where there is interest in establishing a BID. The cost of setting up a new BID is usually shared between the Department and the relevant district council; however, any support is dependent on budget availability at the time. My officials have written to councils to gauge the level of interest in establishing new BIDs and will provide support and guidance as necessary.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 12386/22-27, for their assessment of whether the funding provided to the Northern Ireland Bureau China represents value for money.
answer_text: Through our draft Programme for Government, we have set out an ambitious programme of change, including job creation and growing a sustainable economy that works for all. The Northern Ireland Bureau in China, alongside our other overseas offices and the Invest NI network, will play a key role in this regard.Our draft International Relations Strategy currently under development will set out our ambition to build on the goodwill and strong levels of interest that the Bureau has established in developing our relations and pursuing opportunities both in China and across the wider Asia-Pacific region.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 13394/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13394/22-27 was answered on 22 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that the closure of the Great Victoria Street station in May 2024 has had on the number of rail journeys on the Belfast to Bangor line.
answer_text: Passenger journeys on the Bangor Corridor between May and September 2024 were down 14% on the same period in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of social workers in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The availability of social workers continues to be a challenge across all Health and Social Care Trusts due to recruitment, retention and sickness absence issues. The South Eastern Trust have reported that as of 31st August 2024 it  employed 950 social workers. The Trust currently has approximately 30 Band 6 social work posts vacant, and 72 social workers absent due to sickness or maternity leave. This impacts on social work availability in some services, resulting in the use of waiting lists/unallocated cases to manage referrals. The Trust has implemented a review and monitoring process to ensure the safe management of cases awaiting allocation within Children’s Services. My department continues to work collaboratively with all five Health and Social Care Trusts to address vacancy and absence levels in social work services.  As with other Trusts, in recent years, the South Eastern Trust have participated in a number of Department-led regional initiatives in an effort to stabilise the HSC social work workforce, including: Ceasing the use of recruitment agency social workers in June 2023. The fast-track recruitment of newly qualifying social workers into vacant posts.Participating in regional recruitment exercises for Band 6 social work staff.However, despite this activity there continues to be an inadequate supply of social workers to meet service demands, and my department annually reviews and seeks to increase the number of commissioned training places to meet these demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12130/22-27, for an update on the review of safety on the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: I would confirm that, as per my response to AQW12130/22-27, road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. My officials continue to review sites where there are clusters of collisions on the A2 to identify if there are any common causation factors for these collisions which would permit the introduction of an effective road safety measure. Once the review is completed, and any engineering measures are proposed, they will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to available funding.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will bring forward legislation on managing coastal change and designate responsibility to a Department to oversee coastal risk management from climate change, as recommended by the Climate Change Committee report Adapting to climate change – Progress in Northern Ireland April 2023.
answer_text: The Climate Change Committee report ‘Adapting to climate change – Progress in Northern Ireland April 2023’ allocated a target to the Northern Ireland Executive, and not The Executive Office (TEO), to consider bringing forward legislation on managing coastal change and designate responsibility to a NICS department to oversee coastal risk management from climate change.The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) co-chair the Coastal Erosion Forum which is working collaboratively to address the challenges associated with coastal change. We have no plan to change this approach at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Coastal Forum meetings his Department has attended in the last three years, including the dates of the meetings.
answer_text: Since 2021, the Coastal Forum has met on two occasions on 24 June 2021 and 14 March 2022.  DfI officials attended both meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) why the Northern Ireland Civil Service cycle to work scheme is limited to £1,000 total cost, when the UK Government Civil Service have set theirs at £4,000; and (ii) whether she has any plans to increase the limit.
answer_text: The £1,000 limit on cycle to work salary sacrifice is based on a requirement that, if any employer exceeds this limit, the employer becomes subject to regulation by the Financial Conduct Authority which is an administrative burden that is not resourced. The Civil Service will keep this issue under review as part of its work on developing the range of benefits available to civil servants

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale for the launch of the Change Fund, as outlined in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024 – 2026.
answer_text: The Change Fund will build on and complement prevention work, supporting grassroots groups and community and voluntary sector organisations to do more, further build capacity and be better equipped to address the significant issues in their area. An initial amount of £3 million is earmarked for the Change Fund over the 2024-2025 and 2025-2026 financial years, subject to budget.  Work is continuing at pace to put in place the necessary supporting arrangements and further details will be announced in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how she will ensure that any proposed changes to non-domestic rates will protect and encourage the growth of small, local businesses in the current economic climate.
answer_text: This rating year my Department is providing support to almost 30,000 businesses through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme which provides rate relief of up to 50%. In addition, 2,459 businesses with a valuation under the Small Businesses Rate Relief threshold of £15,000 receive Industrial Derating, providing rate support of 70%. In May I reintroduced the ‘Back in Business’ scheme to help and encourage new and expanding businesses by providing 50% rates support to new businesses in their vital first two years in business. I also reintroduced the Rural Automated Teller Machine exemption to assist cashflow for businesses in rural areas.In terms of augmenting that work, following the recent rating consultation I will be bringing forward short, medium and long-term rating policy proposals for the Executive’s consideration. These proposals will include a more strategic longer-term policy approach.I will also continue my engagement with business bodies, organisations and wider society to work together to build a progressive rates system which grows our tax base and stimulates our economy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the timescale for their Department to commission reports to identify evidence gaps in Northern Ireland, as outlined in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024 – 2026.
answer_text: Data and evidence are essential for understanding the problem of violence against women and girls, measuring our progress towards ending it, and informing funding, service design and delivery decisions.  Preliminary work at a high level has already commenced on the gap analysis of front-line general service provision for victims and survivors of violence against women and girls.Work is also ongoing to commission research on men and boys’ attitudes and behaviours related to violence against women and girls. It is anticipated that this research will commence within this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will deliver on actions attributed to his Department in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2024 – 2026.
answer_text: Addressing violence against women and girls is an issue for all areas of society and education has a role in supporting that. The Delivery Plan brings into sharp focus my Department's responsibilities, and we will consider how the curriculum can support the wider strategy, with schools having an important role to play in addressing violence against women and girls. My Department will contribute to the Delivery Plan and is establishing an Oversight Group to decide how the actions it is required to take forward will be delivered together with key delivery partners including the Executive Office.  This will include engagement with stakeholders.  Officials will bring forward recommendations for my consideration in due course. In turn I will want to consider whether any agreed actions should to be brought to the Executive. Our curriculum is already designed to provide opportunities to educate young people to develop healthy relationships.  Pupils have the opportunity to explore their management of a range of feelings and emotions and those of others; initiating, developing and sustaining mutually satisfying relationships, explore the qualities of a loving, respectful relationship and develop coping strategies to deal with challenging relationship scenarios.Schools are supported to deliver the curriculum by the development, by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment, of teaching materials including materials in respect of Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse, Healthy, Positive Sexual Expression and Relationships and Consent all of which are relevant to the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of applicants; and (ii) the total amount of funding provided through the Help with Health Costs scheme to residents in North Down, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme provides individuals, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for Health and Social Care (HSC) treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The Department does not provide a specific funding allocation for the HwHC scheme. The Business Services Organisation (BSO), usually through individual dental and optical practices, administer the HwHC scheme on behalf of the Department of Health for the purposes of dental treatment, eyesight tests and glasses or contact lens vouchers. The number of exempt patients (and thus provided help with their health care costs) residing in the North Down Assembly Area, and the associated fees/costs covered by the health service, is shown in the tables below. Figures are provided for the last three financial years, including figures up to 2023-24, the latest publicly available annual Accredited Official Statistics. Please note: Financial year relates to when claim was paid, this is not necessarily the same year as when the activity took place.Table 1: Number of Exempt Dental Patients Seen and Total Item of Service (IoS) Fees of Exempt Patients residing in North Down Assembly Area by Financial YearFinancial Year2021-222022-232023-24Patients Seen113,52315,53615,295Total IoS Fees2£885,000£1,056,000£1,043,000The total number of patients to have received treatment under any of the exemption categories listed as eligible for financial support. If a patient appears in multiple exemption categories or more than once in an exemption category they will only be counted once in this total. Based on paid claims and information supplied by dentist making the claim. Excludes all private work, and secondary care activity. Total fees are rounded to the nearest thousand pounds.The total IoS fees for patients represent a sum of the codes of all treatments that were undergone by patients claiming exemption. Excludes registration fees paid in relation to children and adults. For patients aged under 18, a number of minor treatments (e.g. examinations, x-rays, scale and polish) are included in the capitation fee – these patients will be included but only claimable treatments will be included in the IoS fees.  Table 2: Number of Exempt Ophthalmic Patients provided with a Sight Test and Total Sight Test Fees of Exempt Patients residing in North Down Assembly Area by Financial YearFinancial Year2021-222022-232023-24Total Patients1,323,30224,00025,753Total Sight Test Fees3£558,000£596,000£671,000The total number of patients to have received treatment under any of the exemption categories listed as eligible for financial support. If a patient appears in multiple exemption categories or more than once in an exemption category they will only be counted once in this total. Based on paid claims and information supplied by optician making the claim. Excludes all private work, prison activity and secondary care

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the allocation of funding spent on weed control in North Down over the last three years.
answer_text: The Department delivers a programme of weed control aimed at ensuring the safety of road users and preventing the deterioration of the road pavement. Expenditure on weed control by constituency area is not held. However, records of expenditure on Grass Cutting, Weed control and Tree Maintenance for Southern Division is maintained and set out in Table 1 below for the past three completed financial years from 2021-22 to 2023-24: Table 1Grass Cutting, Weed Control and Tree Maintenance2021-22£m2022-23£m2023-24£m1.71.42.0Southern Division expenditure covers Ards and North Down, Newry, Mourne and Down and Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses in North Down that have availed of the Small Business Rate Relief Scheme in the last three years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services does not hold data at Parliamentary Constituency level, therefore figures have been provided in the table below for Ards and North Down District Council at 31st March for each of the three rating years.Rating YearNo. of occupancies2021/222,2362022/232,3002023/242,257

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time it has taken DFI Roads Division to respond to a report over the last two years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain statistics in the format requested.Following reported pothole defects from the public it may be necessary for my officials to carry out a site investigation to reach a decision on whether the pothole meets our intervention criteria and the creation of an instructed surface defect record.  My Department receives a high volume of surface defect related enquiries, and regrettably it is not possible to investigate all enquiries immediately. It is likely that some faults will not be investigated until the next routine inspection of the area.If, after investigation, it is found that the reported pothole requires action, our current standards specify a range of response times for the repair of defects, depending on factors such as their severity and the volume of traffic on the road. They range from one calendar day for the most serious defects, to periods of five working days and four weeks for less serious defects. The least serious defects are only required to be repaired in the next work programme for that route.If it becomes apparent that the relevant response time cannot be met, then my Department has the option of installing signs to warn road users of a possible danger.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the International Relations Strategy will be published.
answer_text: Officials have undertaken extensive preparatory work and engagement and are now in the process of preparing a draft International Relations Strategy which will form the basis for further engagement and a public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department’s consultation on the proposed Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any ongoing work to maximize the skills pipeline in North Down in conjunction with the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: My Department is working with stakeholders across business, education, and skills to deliver a skills system across the region which will support both individuals and businesses to reach their full potential. 		My Department funds delivery of the Level 2 Traineeship which is a full-time vocational education and training programme open to anyone over 16 years of age who is unemployed.  		In 2022/3 there were 60 trainees undertaking the programme in the North Down Parliamentary constituency area. 		Latest official statistics indicate that there are just over 400 apprentices from the North Down constituency area participating on the Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes.		In addition, as part of the Belfast Region City Deal the SHED 1.0 project is currently in the early stages of development. This project, intended as part of the Ards and North Down Innovation Hub, is designed to provide space for technologists, local SMEs and communities to pursue innovation projects at small to medium scale, providing growth in skills, employment and the economy. 		The project will specifically focus on creative and digital skills, with Holywood chosen as the most appropriate site due to the number and quality of this type of employment in the town and its connections to the industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Gender Equality Strategy.
answer_text: Details of the proposed timetable for the next steps in developing the Gender Equality Strategy will issue in the coming months. While the Department for Communities is leading on the four Executive Social Inclusion Strategies (Anti-Poverty; Disability; Gender Equality; Sexual Orientation), final decisions on their content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation. I am committed to working with all my Ministerial colleagues to ensure that the Social Inclusion Strategies are taken forward in the most effective and expeditious way possible, taking account of departmental budget allocations and the need to develop prioritised, sustainable and deliverable Strategy action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to support community pharmacy in North Down in the last three years.
answer_text: The level of government funding for community pharmacies in NI compares favourably to other parts of the UK. Against the backdrop of the current severe budgetary pressures and the multiple demands for additional funding across all parts of health and social care, additional investment continues to be provided to the community pharmacy sector. Funding for community pharmacies in NI has increased in recent years. In relation to the last 3 years, the opening position for 22/23 was £117m. The opening position in 2023/24 was £128m. There was additional funding invested in 2023/24 linked with service pressures and additional services. Therefore, by year-end, over £140m was invested in community pharmacy services in 2023/24. The additional recurrent funding invested in 2023/24 has brought the recurrent funding to over £134m at the start of 2024/25. Prior to the allocation of a budget for the 2024/25 year, my Department committed to providing additional funding to this opening position in recognition of the service pressures that this sector is facing and within what is affordable. The opening annual budget for the community pharmacy sector is £147m, a rise of £19m in 2024 compared to April 2023.This includes committing an additional £2m per quarter in 2024/25 in respect of ongoing service pressures. Whether this can be made recurrent will be dependent upon the delivery of efficiencies and the budget allocation.SPPG does not hold sub-regional funding budgets for community pharmacy services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what discussions she has had with the UK Government regarding the UK Shared Prosperity Fund beyond March 2025.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the timeline of the development proposal for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College to change to Integrated status.
answer_text: On 8 February 2024, the Education Authority (EA) published Development Proposal 727 at the request of the Board of Governors (BoGs) of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College proposing that:Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter.The statutory two-month objection period in relation to this Development Proposal ended on 22 April 2024 and I am informed that a draft report is currently undergoing final review and will shortly be passed to me to inform my decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 11723/22-27, to detail the results of the post project evaluation of the Skill Up programme.
answer_text: The post project evaluation of the Skill Up programme (2021/2024) has recently been completed. It is now being reviewed by Officials in my Department. The final evaluation report will be shared with the Economy Committee and placed in the public domain in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timeline for the publication of his Department's review report on residents' parking schemes.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and I have had the opportunity to consider the draft review report.  I have asked for a small number of amendments to be made to the report and plan to issue the final review report in August.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has fulfilled the funding requirements outlined in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 Funding Plan since its publication.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 was published alongside a 10-year Funding Plan. The Funding Plan outlined that £1.2bn is needed to fully implement the Strategy over the 10-year period. This investment required to deliver the Strategy is in addition to existing expenditure in mental health services.To date, in the absence of additional funding, the Department has been using its existing funding to progress implementation of the Strategy. While this funding has allowed the commencement of enabling actions and scope to take forward work on some other priorities, it does not match the levels of funding as set out in the Funding Plan. A comparison between what has been allocated to the Strategy against the requirement outlined within the funding plan is set out below.YearFunding Allocation(£m)Requirement within Funding Plan (£m)2021/220.650.652022/234.19.592023/245.524.38As a result, delivery plans to date have prioritised those actions which will have a direct service user impact, such as those aimed at alleviating pressures and improving outcomes, as well as those that will continue to enable longer term reform of our mental health provision.The allocation for 2024/25 has not yet been finalised but it is expected that the gap between what is allocated and what is set out in the Funding Plan will continue to grow in the absence of additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the funding provided to projects in North Down from the Mental Health Support Fund, for each year since the inception of the fund.
answer_text: The funding provided to projects in North Down from the Mental Health Support Fund, for each year since the inception of the fund is as follows:OrganisationTotal Funding AwardedAmount received in year 21/22Amount received in year 22/23Amount received in year 23/24Amount received in year 24/25ACET (Northern Ireland) Limited£101,000.00£47,000.00£47,000.00£7,000.00£0.00Find Help NI£259,022.00£63,270.50£84,360.64£109,390.86£2,000.00Holywood Family Trust£249,270.00£62,317.50£62,317.50£124,635.00£0.00North Down Community Network£256,900.00£62,475.00£68,425.00£98,950.00£27,050.00Barnardo's Northern Ireland£94,915.00£0.00£47,457.50£47,457.50£0.00AGE North Down & Ards (AGENDA)£46,238.00£34,584.00£0.00£0.00£11,654.00North Down Community Network and AGENDA are still pending uplift payments of £2,000 and £3,008, respectively, both of which should be processed in the coming months. These figures have been included in this table for these organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the interim Public Service Transformation Board.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan for 2023/24.
answer_text: Delivery of the Mental Health Strategy has been taking place within an extremely challenging financial context using existing, limited resources to take forward actions. As a result of this, the Strategy is being implemented through Annual Delivery Plans which set year-on-year priorities and actions. In 2023/24, progress was made in taking forward a number of actions, including:Implementation of the community perinatal mental health service across the region, with teams now in place in all Trusts.Co-design of a three-year implementation plan for the establishment of the Regional Mental Health Service (RMHS). Completion of a three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Action Plan, with progress made on implementation of actions.Completion and publication of an independent Mental Health workforce review, with work underway to begin planning implementation of key recommendations.   Additional investment in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) to enhance existing provision and alleviate pressures.Roll-out of the emotional health and wellbeing teams in schools, with initial cohort of schools identified and engaged.Establishment of the Digital Mental Health Board and appointment of project leads to develop a digital mental health service action plan.  Completion of a review exploring the optimal approach to engaging the community and voluntary sector in the development of mental health policy and provision.The Annual Delivery Plan for 2024/25 is currently being finalised and will set out a fuller update on progress made against agreed deliverables in 2023/24. This Plan will also set out an agreed set of actions which will be taken forward in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider reviewing the protocol for Gateway Service referrals from the PSNI to ensure that vulnerable children receive the protection and support needed.
answer_text: Ensuring the right response at the right time for children and young people who are referred to Children’s Gateway teams is of the upmost importance. There is not a separate protocol for Gateway Service referrals from the PSNI.Instead, Children’s Gateway teams consider referrals from all organisations, including the PSNI, utilising the regional ‘Understanding the Needs of Children In Northern Ireland’ guidance and under the statutory responsibility set out in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 to safeguard and promote the welfare of children within its area who are in need. Gateway teams screen and threshold referrals received to determine the most appropriate response, either through initial assessment by a Social Worker, onward sign posting or no further action. PSNI referrals for alleged or suspected child abuse are considered under the ‘Protocol for Joint Investigation by Social Workers and Police Officer of Alleged or Suspected Cases of Child Abuse, April 2021’. Following a referral under this protocol Gateway teams and PSNI will jointly consider relevant information known and make an assessment of the information, allegation and context before a decision regarding a joint investigation is made. There is currently ongoing liaison between Department of Health officials and Health and Social Care Trusts to review the referrals that are being made to Children’s Gateway teams by the PSNI. This work aims to ensure that referrals made are appropriate and receive a timely and proportionate response.Department of Health officials also continue to liaise with PSNI colleagues in respect of the referral information that is being sent to Children’s Gateway teams in respect of the quality and timeliness of information. This will support Gateway services to screen and threshold referrals accurately, and ensure no Child in Need is failed to be noticed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to address obstructive pavement parking.
answer_text: The recent introduction of the Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 allows the Department to enforce against vehicles found parked on footways alongside bus lanes, bus stops/stands, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory ‘School Keep Clear’ markings.  The legislation adds to list of specific areas where footway parking is prohibited, and I believe it is a positive step forward in deterring inconsiderate pavement parking. The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’ and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.  Where we cannot act, the police can enforce against vehicles found to be obstructing a footway.On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking. This can be accessed using this link;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/inconsiderate-pavement-parking-findings-report I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have had the opportunity to consider the Findings Report.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Joint Flags Protocol 2005.
answer_text: At a community level we have seen examples of where the Joint Flags Protocol has worked well, with community organisations working with political representatives and other agencies to find solutions to issues through consensus and discussion. We recognise, though, that work around flags continues to be one of our society’s challenging issues. Finding consensus on a way forward on such issues can be challenging. However, recent Assembly debates confirm there is shared opposition to offensive displays such as paramilitary flags.Issues related to flags were considered most recently by the Commission for Flags, Identity, Culture and Traditions (FICT) Commission. We recently agreed that progressing the recommendations of the FICT report would be best placed within the current review of the T:BUC Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide a school crossing patrol on Abbey Road outside Millisle Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol Scheme, which includes undertaking assessments of potential locations in line with Road Safety GB School Crossing Patrol Service Guidelines to determine if a crossing patrol should be introduced.  The EA has advised me that an assessment has been carried out at Abbey Road, outside Millisle Primary School, and that a review of that assessment was also undertaken and the scoring adjusted to take account of further information provided by the school in relation to a traffic incident which occurred in June. I am informed that the site at Abbey Road does not meet the minimum threshold criteria for the introduction of a School Crossing Patrol.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children deemed homeless in North Down in the last three years.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024, published on 13 June, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024. 	Please refer to Section 2 in the accompanying tables for detail on Homelessness acceptances.  Table 2.7 details the number of children from households accepted as homeless by age bracket, however we do not hold this information specific to North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with the PSNI in relation to flags on street furniture since coming into office.
answer_text: I can advise the member that I have not had any direct engagement with PSNI in relation to flags on street furniture since coming into office.However, upon receiving a complaint, or a request for removal of an attachment, and the location having been established, my officials contact the PSNI to determine if removal is likely to increase the risk of local community tension.Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will include paid carer's leave in the proposed employment bill.
answer_text: Question 7 - AQO 646/22-27I intend to bring forward an Employment Rights Bill within the current mandate to ensure our employment law framework operates effectively for employees and businesses. I am aware there have been calls for the leave to be a paid right which go beyond the provisions in Britain, the South of Ireland and the EU. The cost of providing paid carer’s leave would be considerable and would have to be fully funded by the Executive. In addition to the costs of providing paid carer’s leave there are other possible implications such as the availability of funding for any HMRC system changes that would be required. These changes would require considerable set up costs and the ongoing annual costs would be substantial.Carer’s leave is one of the issues within an Employment Rights Bill that I intend to consult upon and any policy proposals for new legislation will be informed by the outcome of the consultation process. These in turn will also need the necessary approvals from the Executive and the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has issued guidance to schools in relation to hosting political events during the pre-election period.
answer_text: DE Circular 2024/09 “Use of school premises for political events – Advice” was issued to all schools on 24 May 2024. Details of the circular contents can be found here.DE Circular 2024/09 - Use of School Premises for Political Events - Advice | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the safe staffing levels in the Ulster Hospital; and (ii) the number of days the Ulster Hospital dropped below the safe staffing levels in the last three years.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the impact the Neurology Active Referral Management system has had on providing timely appointments for the management of headache and migraine since its introduction in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The introduction of the Neurology Acute referral Management system (NARMS)in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has led to headache referrals being streamlined to advice, investigation or face to face clinic review pathways. The advice pathway (25-47% of referrals) had a written response with guidance within 48 hours (pre-encompass), investigations (17-20% of referrals) were largely radiological red flag CT performed within a 2-6 week timeframe and clinic appointments (33-47% of referrals) were seen within maximum 12 weeks. The service has experienced some staffing pressures which has seen an increase in waiting times but these should be addressed later in the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with (i) Artemis Technologies; and (ii) Condor Ferries in relation to the electric passenger ferry pilot between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: I met with Artemis in August 2022 when I was previously in this post.My Department has met Artemis Technologies on its proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route on a number of occasions. At the most recent meeting, on 16 May 2024, which was also attended by a representative from Condor Ferries, officials were provided with an update on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11969/22-27, to detail his Department’s road defect intervention criteria, in accordance with the limited service maintenance policy.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service which means my Department can only repair the highest priority defects across the network. Defects greater than 50mm depth are being repaired across all roads. On high traffic roads, defects greater than 20mm are also being repaired. Pursuant to AQW 11969/22-27, Castle Park Road is a low traffic road and therefore, in accordance with the limited service maintenance policy, defects greater than 50mm will be repaired.A copy of these standards can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have been able to access calcitonin gene-related peptide medications in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland. This process is relevant to the commissioning of treatments for migraines including calcitonin gene-related peptide medications.In the last five years NICE have recommended a number of treatments for migraine which have been endorsed for use in Northern Ireland. It should be noted that most of these treatments are predominately provided within primary care and the SPPG does not hold prescribing data for SEHSCT.However, the SPPG is able to provide the number of successful Cost-per-Case applications submitted by a secondary care clinician and approved by SPPG. These figures are not likely to represent the total usage of these treatments and it should be noted that a successful CPC application does not guarantee that an individual began treatment.TATreatmentNo. in SEHSCTTA906 (July 2023)Rimegepant0TA871(March 2023)Epitinezumab0TA764(February 2022)Fremanezumab0TA682(March 2021)Erenumab0TA659(November 2020)Galcanezumab0TA631(July 2020)Fremanezumab10

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made to deliver a number of Cancer Strategy actions, for example:Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) delivering a vague symptoms pathway for people with vague but worrying symptoms which may be cancer, for patients across the region.Haematology pathway reviews have been completed. Officials are currently implementing the recommendations to help improve service delivery and sustainability.A review of cancer support services for Adolescents and Young Adults has also been completed, with work progressing to implement the recommendations.Breast cancer services in Northern Ireland are being reviewed, with view to developing a more resilient and equitable service model. A directory of cancer support services has been developed to help signpost those impacted by a cancer diagnosis to support services in their area. Work to develop a Cancer Research Strategy is progressing, with plans to publish during the current financial year. Northern Ireland was the first part of the UK to commence work to implement an optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer patients. Whilst there has been progress, it will not be possible to fully implement the cancer strategy without significant additional investment over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has made any representation to the Electoral Commission in relation to the use of yLink Smartcards as an acceptable form of voter identification.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has not made any representation to the Electoral Commission in relation to the use of yLink Smartcards as an acceptable form of voter identification.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to reduce waiting times for cancer treatment in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for cancer treatments.  In the first quarter of 2024/25 non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity.  In addition, the Department continues to facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times through the inter-Trust transfer of patients. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group of DOH meet quarterly with trust colleagues to review performance against 14-day breast, 31 and 62 targets/pathways to maximise on efficiencies within current service provision.  Trusts also provide Cancer Performance Optimisation Plans.  These plans are intended to reduce variation in practice, reduce variation in waiting times, maximising workforce capacity and skill mix, thereby optimising capacity and productivity within core service provision.  In 23/24, the South Easter Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) experienced a 10% increase in referrals compared to 2022/23.  SEHSCT continues to work with Primary Care to ensure that patients are referred appropriately.  Recently, GP events have been delivered and were well attended and well received. SEHSCT recognises the absolute priority of reducing cancer waiting times and works continuously to improve these using all parts of its multi-professional team.  The following list is by no means exhaustive but highlights some of the many examples of actions that SEHSCT is currently taking to reduce the waiting times for patients: Bi-weekly Director-led cancer-focused improvement group established in 2024 to develop and monitor action plans and mitigations specifically in regard to reducing the waiting time between referral and first outpatient appointment. Working with specialties to implement enhanced triage to reduce the demand for red flag services. Review of specialty capacity to ensure maximisation of red flag slots in clinic, including utilising virtual clinics where appropriate. Implementation of seasonal templates to manage predicted increases in demand.Utilising non-recurrent funding to provide additional outpatient and diagnostic capacity via Waiting List Initiatives and Independent Sector (IS) clinics. Trust inclusion in the Vague Symptom Pathway to commence in summer 2024 as part of the NI Cancer Strategy programme. Where appropriate, instigating direct to test pathways to accelerate decision making relating to diagnosis of cancerThe Trust has also implemented a number of innovative service developments focused on improving diagnosis and treatment pathways: Transurethral laser ablation (TULA) has recently been set up.  The mole mapping service has been running for 18 months and is diagnosing patients earlier. Further development of advanced endoscopic techniques for polyp excision, including full-thickness endoscopic resection. Photo triage implemented in dermatology. Importantly, once diagnosed, 95% of patients

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why lockers are not available at the entrance to the Public Gallery of the Assembly Chamber to enable visitors to store bags.
answer_text: Due to the limited space that is available at the entrance to the Public Gallery of the Assembly Chamber storage boxes have been provided for small items such as mobile phones and wallets.  As this area is an evacuation route in the event of an emergency evacuation it is important that it is kept clear.The Assembly Commission has however provided larger lockers that visitors are able to store bags in at the ramped entrance to the Great Hall.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has made any representation to the Electoral Commission in relation to the use of student cards as an acceptable form of voter identification.
answer_text: I have not made representations to the Electoral Commission in relation to the use of student cards as an acceptable form of voter identification.This is not a matter for the Department for the Economy as voter identification is not within my Department’s remit.The Electoral Office for Northern Ireland provides a list of appropriate identification documents for voters. This can be found at https://www.eoni.org.uk/Vote/Voting-at-a-polling-place

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 12219/22-27, to detail (i) the expenditure of the Executive Insight and Strategy Unit; and (ii) the areas of research which have been explored.
answer_text: In the 2023/24 financial year the Executive Insight and Strategy Unit (EISU) spent approximately £215k on staffing (one Director, 1 DP Economist and 0.5 FTE admin support), including employee costs and travel and subsistence.The areas of research that have been explored include:Measuring the Missions (People, Planet, Prosperity & Peace);NI Economic progress; and  Other ad hoc research to support Ministerial and Head of Civil service engagements, such as Venture Capital & Private Equity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the announcement on interim change to eligibility criteria, for his assessment of how many additional children in North Down will avail of free school meals and uniform grants.
answer_text: The 2023/24 school census shows 2,976 pupils attending a school in the North Down constituency are entitled to free school meals. While the Department is not able to model the impact of the interim rise in the income threshold for free school meals and uniform grant applications at constituency level, this interim rise seeks to broadly maintain the same number of children and young people able to access these supports in the 24/25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number of claims in North Down to the Road Claims Unit; and (ii) amount paid out in those claims, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department maintains its claims records by Section Office area which broadly align with Council areas.  The number of claims received for accidents that occurred in the Ards and North Down Section Office area for the last five full financial years are detailed in the table below. 2019/20202020/20212021/20222022/20232023/2024Ards & North Down           312            280            247            371            686 These figures include claims for vehicle damage, personal injury and property damage.The compensation paid on claims for accidents that occurred in the Ards and North Down Section Office area for the last five full financial years are detailed in the table below. 2019/20202020/20212021/20222022/20232023/2024Ards & North Down£244,566.27£280,957.61£314,190.14£316,770.16£425,081.19Again, these figures include claims for vehicle damage, personal injury and property damage.In addition, note that claims received in a financial year are not always concluded in the same financial year. Therefore, the claims expenditure figures for a given financial year will usually include claims that were received in prior financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to install any permanent speed indicator devices in North Down during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Speed Indicator Devices (SIDs) are not considered to be traffic signs and do not fall under the remit of my department as they fall under the remit of Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) and are funded by the Department of Justice through the Northern Ireland Policing Board.I recognise the road safety benefits SID signs can bring and my department works in partnership with the above mentioned bodies to ensure their safe and managed placement through developed policy  Speed Indicator Devices DEM 191/23 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk). My department is aware of 6 locations identified by the PCSP for SID signs in the North Down and Ards area but would suggest that you make contact with your local PCSP for details of their specific locations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the closure of the public consultation on the new Strategy for Victims and Survivors, to detail the timeframe for the publication of the strategy.
answer_text: Officials are currently reviewing responses to the public consultation which closed on 29 March, after which we will receive an updated Strategy for our consideration. We expect that we will receive this in the coming weeks, and it is our intention to publish after the summer recess. The updated Strategy will be presented to the TEO Committee in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the redevelopment of Whitespots Country Park, Newtownards.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Whitespots Country Park is being delivered by Ards and North Down Borough Council with my Department acting as the ‘Accountable Department’ under the Complementary Funds.  The Complementary Fund is contributing £7.4m to the project with a further £1.1m from the DAERA Environment Fund and a remaining Council contribution of £0.8m. The Outline Business Case has received final approval, and the Letter of Offer will be issued in next few weeks. The project has a completion date of spring 2029.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure on what date the Event Duration Monitor was installed at Brompton Road wastewater pumping station.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater pumping stations is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that the event duration monitor at Brompton Road wastewater pumping station was installed and in effective use from 9 February 2024.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12383/22-27, whether he will amend policy arrangements set out in DE Circular 1996/41, to ensure pupils in statutory preschool settings can access Education Authority transport services.
answer_text: A change to the Home to School Transport policy would require a full review of the current policy.  My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and work is progressing on an end-to-end review of SEN.  Work is also underway on an action plan arising from the EA Landscape Review, to include detailed consideration of the operational delivery of services such as school transport.It is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the Home to School Transport Policy and the associated policy options. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department, I need to give careful consideration to the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the services available to (i) diagnose; and (ii) treat Ehlers-Danlos syndromes, in all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Ehlers-Danlos syndromes (EDS) are a group of rare inherited conditions that affect connective tissue and range in severity. The route to diagnosis will likely initially occur with the patient’s GP who may make a referral to secondary care for assessment by a specialist.  The specialist will assess the indications for EDS and agree the appropriate clinical care plan.  This may include referral for genetic testing (for very rare presentations) and/or referral to a specialist provider in GB consistent with the patient’s clinical needs.Although there is no specific treatment for EDS, it is possible to manage many of the symptoms, with support and advice from a range of healthcare professionals such as physiotherapy and occupational therapy. My Department is committed to improving services available for patients living with rare conditions as evidenced by the UK Rare Diseases Framework which was jointly published by the four UK Health Ministers on 9 January 2021 and the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan, published in 2022.  The work of the Action Plan is being advanced under the leadership of my Department through the NI Rare Disease Implementation Group. Areas of work in progress include the development of clinical pathways for rare diseases to support and inform health professionals, patients and their families on how conditions are identified, diagnosed and managed including the input and role of wider HSC highly specialised service providers in their care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in Northern Ireland currently diagnosed with Ehlers-Danlos syndromes.
answer_text: Information on the number of people in Northern Ireland that are living with a diagnosis of Ehlers-Danlos syndromes (EDS) is not held centrally. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) was, therefore, asked to provide a response.While Belfast, Northern and Western HSCTs responded that their systems do not record the required information, the answers and other information that they provided now follow. The Southern and South Eastern HSCTs were unable to provide a response within the required time frame.Belfast HSCTBelfast HSCT responded that ‘Ehlers-Danlos syndromes’ is a non-specific term used for a collection of quite distinct conditions. Clinical Genetics do not keep a population register of patients with any of the various EDS conditions, but the Genetics database flags 87 people in Northern Ireland with a confirmed diagnosis. This is a mixed dataset based purely on diagnostic coding and has not been validated. Belfast HSCT recommended the patient support group ‘Ehlers-Danlos Support UK’ (https://www.ehlers-danlos.org/) for information about this patient group.Northern HSCTNorthern HSCT responded that prevalence in the community is estimated to be 1 in 500 by some studies, depending on the definition of EDS. Apart from rare genetic forms there are no specific diagnostic tests, and it has no way to estimate current numbers attending.Western HSCTWestern HSCT responded that there are no waiting lists or specialties that would identify these patients easily and it does not hold an active list of patients with this condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the recently announced capital funding for Crawfordsburn Primary School will include a new building for Bright Sparks Preschool.
answer_text: Funding of accommodation for Bright Sparks Pre-school will not be provided as part of the Crawfordsburn Primary School new build project. As previously advised, in my response to AQW 12306/22-27, the Education Authority is currently investigating options to resolve the accommodation issues at Bright Sparks Pre-School.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the advice provided by his Department to pharmacies in relation to the Ask for ANI scheme; and (ii) the number of pharmacies engaging in the scheme.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the pilot of the Ask for ANI scheme across Jobs and Benefits offices.
answer_text: The Home Office Ask for ANI pilot scheme commenced on 20 February 2023 in four Jobs & Benefit offices located in Andersonstown, Armagh, Larne, and Lisnagelvin, however the Home Office evaluation of these pilots remains outstanding. In recognition of the help and support required for victims of domestic violence and abuse, staff from each of the Department’s 35 Jobs and Benefits offices attended specialist domestic violence and abuse training delivered by Onus in November 2023.  Each office has been successfully accredited with a Workplace Safe Place Charter and has a dedicated Safe Space where victims can be sensitively guided to. This will enable them to contact friends or family, the PSNI or specialist domestic abuse services for support and advice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to give an update on the appointment of a Permanent Secretary to the Executive Office.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the resurfacing of the Groomsport Road roundabout has not been completed by the announced timeframe of 29 March 2024; and (ii) when he anticipates the work will be completed.
answer_text: The scheme commenced on 19 February 2024 and, with the exception of the final surface, was substantially complete on 19 April 2024. The final resurfacing was completed on 27 May 2024 with only a few minor items such as topsoiling of verges and setting of ironwork completed on 31 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) consultation; and (ii) communication his Department has undertaken with local residents in relation to the road closures to enable works to the Groomsport Road roundabout.
answer_text: The Groomsport Road roundabout scheme commenced on 19 February 2024 and was substantially complete by 19 April 2024 with only the final surfacing to be completed over two consecutive weekends in May. Formal public consultation is not required when carrying out resurfacing and other relatively minor road maintenance activities, however discussions are held with external stakeholders such as the PSNI and directly adjacent property owners as required.In the case of the Groomsport Roundabout scheme, careful planning and consideration was given to the delivery of this scheme to help minimise traffic disruption and details of the closures were communicated to the public via Variable Message and Black on Yellow advisory signs, strategically placed at all approaches to the works. Details were also posted on my Department’s TrafficWatchNI website. During the works traffic ‘Marshalls’ were provided by the contractor to help minimise disruption. In advance of the scheme commencing, a press release was also published providing general information to the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for a colposcopy, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether Health and Social Care Trusts notify a patient when they become aware of a data breach concerning personal information; and (ii) whether his Department holds any monitoring data in relation to this.
answer_text: Under Data Protection legislation each Health and Social Care organisation is designated an independent ‘data controller’. The Information Commissioner’s guidance, states that they should have policies and procedures in place to manage potential data breaches. These will include consideration of notification of breaches to the Information Commissioner’s Office and to data subjects involved.The Department does not monitor information in relation to such matters as this is the responsibility of the regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department issues guidance to GPs in relation to delivering test results which may cause distress to patients; and (ii) for his assessment of using a text message to deliver such results.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department's active travel delivery plan will be published.
answer_text: My Department is developing an Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes a full update of our design guidance for active travel and active travel network plans for the largest towns and cities in the North. We are aiming to consult on draft proposals for the Active Travel Delivery Plan later this year and officials are currently working to finalise the various workstreams over the coming months. Once adopted, the plan will provide our teams with a firm basis for the prioritisation of active travel investment within our towns and cities for the next ten years and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his plans to commission research into bullying in schools.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether independent celebrants will be included in the Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill.
