question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why statutory nursery schools are unable to access Education Authority bus services.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is required to provide transport assistance to eligible pupils at grant aided schools under Article 52 (in conjunction with Article 44) of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 (as substituted), in accordance with arrangements approved by the Department of Education (DE). Eligibility for transport assistance is determined by the EA in line with the policy arrangements set out in the DE Circular 1996/41. Under this policy, pupils below compulsory school age, including those in reception classes, are not eligible for assistance with transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the advice his Department provides to housing associations in relation to housing people with a history of anti-social behaviour and sex offences in the same vicinity as vulnerable adults.
answer_text: My Department provides the Housing Executive with detailed ASB guidance which covers a number of different scenarios including guidance on when someone is ineligible for an allocation as a result of unacceptable behaviour including serious Anti-Social Behaviour.  This is incorporated into Chapter 2 of the Housing Selection Scheme Guidance Manual. The Criminal Justice Order (NI) 2008 created the Public Protection Arrangements for Northern Ireland (PPANI) which brings together a number of agencies and departments including the Police, Probation, Prison Service, Health & Social Care Boards and Health & Social Care Trusts, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, NSPCC to work together to provide effective assessment and management of the risks posed by certain sexual and violent offenders, including individuals who have committed violent offences within a domestic setting.The Chief Executive of the Housing Executive has advised of the following:“In respect of housing individuals with relevant sex offences within PPANI the decision as to whether an offer of accommodation is suitable is a matter for the relevant PPANI agency, primarily the Police Service of Northern Ireland or Probation Board for Northern Ireland. In such circumstances any housing association making an offer of accommodation to a person within PPANI should in the first instance contact the Housing Executive who will liaise with the relevant PPANI agency in respect of the suitability or otherwise of any offer. The Housing Executive and other PPANI agencies have sought to promote the PPANI arrangements by means of a number of workshops with Housing Associations whereby each PPANI organisation outlined the support they offer in respect of risk management. Attendance at such sessions was at the discretion of housing associations.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number; and (ii) percentage of inpatients in the Ulster Hospital who are unable to be discharged due to the lack of availability of domiciliary care and suitable care home places.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development proposal for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College to transform to Controlled Integrated status.
answer_text: On 8 February 2024, the Education Authority (EA) published DP 727 at the request of the Board of Governors (BoGs) of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College proposing that:		Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter.	The statutory two-month objection period in relation to this DP ended on 22 April 2024 and I am informed that my officials are preparing a submission for my consideration.  The assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires careful consideration of all evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all children and to ensure they have access to high quality education that is viable and sustainable in the long term.I have not yet received a submission for my consideration and am therefore unable to provide a precise date by which a decision will be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of domiciliary care packages in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Unfortunately, it remains the position that the demand for a home care service outweighs capacity alongside the challenges to retain and recruit home care workers across the region. However, South Eastern H&SC Trust has made a marked improvement with a reduction of 47% in the unmet need as at end of March 2024. There are currently 5530 packages of care being actively delivered in the South Eastern H&SC Trust settlement areas, Belfast, Down and Lisburn, North Down and Ards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11363/22-27, for an update on a resolution for the accommodation of Bright Sparks Preschool, Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: Bright Sparks Pre-School continues to operate from Crawfordsburn Primary School whilst the Education Authority (EA) investigates options to resolve the current accommodation issues. A further meeting took place with Bright Sparks representatives on 30 May 2024.Proposals for remedial works have been sent to the South-Eastern and Social Care Trust for consideration, thereafter a meeting is to be set up with the EA, when a final decision will be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans for active travel in North Down for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Department is currently developing an Active Travel Network Delivery Plan for the North, which includes a full update of our design guidance and proposed active travel network plans for our larger towns and cities, including Bangor, Holywood, and Donaghadee. We plan to consult on draft proposals for the Active Travel Network Plan later in 2024.I would also refer the Member to the current council reports which provide details of schemes in delivery in the Ards and North Down Council area - https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/list-council-reports-202324The Department also previously issued letters of offer to the Ards and North Down Council for the delivery of two greenways, namely: Comber to Newtownards Greenway and Newtownards to Green Road Bangor Greenway. The Department is continuing to engage with the Council on these projects.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of their Department's Economic Policy Unit.
answer_text: The Executive Insight and Strategy Unit (formerly known as the Economic Policy Unit) was formed in 2023 to develop a stronger understanding of our performance and international competitiveness, and to support policy development. The unit continues to develop the relevant socio-economic research, data, and emerging trends at regional, national, European, and global level. Identifying gaps in data and capability and working with colleagues across the Executive and external stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether the decision to reject the proposal to close Queen Elizabeth II Primary School, Kilskeery, was consistent with his Department's decision to close St Anne’s Primary School, Donaghadee; and (ii) to detail the rationale for the decisions in relation to both schools.
answer_text: On 13 May 2024 I took the decision not to approve Development Proposal (DP) 720 - Queen Elizabeth II Primary School will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2024, or as soon as possible thereafter.The reason for my decision (which is accessible on the Department’s website at the following link: Minister's Decision.pdf (education-ni.gov.uk)) is: “Having carefully considered all of the evidence and analysis with regards to this proposal against the Sustainable Schools Policy and also carefully considered those comments received during the statutory objection period, I have decided not to approve the proposal for closure.  I would of course expect the Board of Governors to monitor the school closely against SSP criteria and ongoing support to be provided by the EA as managing authority”.The decision in relation to DP 673 (closure of St Anne’s Primary School, Donaghadee) was taken by the Permanent Secretary of the Department of Education and is accessible at this link: Department’s website

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will conduct a review of safety on the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and it is committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Officials will review the sites where there are clusters of collisions on the A2 to identify if there are any common causation factors for these collisions which would allow an effective road safety measure to be introduced.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  If driver attitudes towards speeding and careless driving do not change, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to rise

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps her Department is taking to promote tourism in North Down.
answer_text: Tourism NI has regularly promoted the North Down Area through its seasonal marketing campaigns which are targeted to specific market segments based on extensive research. One of the most recent pieces of promotional activity was the launch of a major Spring advertising campaign on 22 January 2024 which ran until 31 March.  The campaign featured TV, radio, press, press supplements, outdoor, social and digital advertising, all underpinned by a comprehensive PR strategy. A double page spread dedicated to the Mournes and Strangford including North Down was contained within a 24-page Tourism NI consumer supplement alongside full page press advertorials have featured in national press titles North and South.Radio stings dedicated to North Down were broadcast across North of Ireland radio stations featuring North Down Museum, the Starfish Cafe, Mount Stewart and Tracey’s Farmhouse Kitchen.Planning is in train for the next campaign due to take place in autumn, and with regional balance one of my key priorities, North Down will again be featured.Tourism NI also works with its partner Tourism Ireland to host international media, with North Down featuring regularly in many media itineraries. The Crawfordsburn Inn recently featured in a piece on ‘Yonder’fr’ by French Journalist, Alain Maurice following his trip to the North of Ireland hosted by Tourism NI. In addition, the North Down Coastal path was featured in the itinerary of a recent media trip in February 2024, undertaken by the ‘Curious Pixie’, a travel influencer whose website and content are viewed in over 120 countries.  Tourism NI has an ongoing programme of PR activity North and South, with tourism businesses and visitor experiences from North Down regularly featuring in PR content, media trips, and a commissioned TV programme on Virgin Media TV. A media trip in August 2023 included Noble restaurant and Bangor resulting in coverage in the Irish Times and Irish Examiner. Another example from Spring 2024 is a piece in the Irish Times commissioned by Tourism NI, called ‘Leap into a romantic getaway in Northern Ireland’; The North Down Coastal Path was specifically mentioned as a ‘must see’ place to visit.North Down regularly features across the Discover Northern Ireland social media pages. For example, The Culloden Hotel and Spa was featured in Instagram and Facebook posts in both January and February 2024 highlighting it as the perfect hotel for a romantic getaway and spa break. Also, Donaghadee was the focus of a Facebook post in November 2023 which showcased the filming locations of the BBC's Hope Street. Tourism NI also works closely with the tourism industry in North Down and across the North to support the development of engaging and immersive tourism experiences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether information in relation to incidences of sexual violence and harassment which have occurred in further education colleges between students is recorded by her Department.
answer_text: The Department does not record incidences of sexual violence and harassment which have occurred in Further Education colleges between students. Where such allegations are made, they are recorded and investigated by individual Further Education colleges under Safeguarding, Care and Welfare policies and procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what (i) financial resources; (ii) access to support services; and (iii) guidance is available to schools to educate about sexual misconduct.
answer_text: My department provides funding to a range of arms-length and non-departmental public bodies, including the Education Authority (EA) and Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA), to provide support and resources for schools in relation to child protection, safeguarding and the preventative curriculum.  There are no financial resources dedicated specifically to the issue of sexual misconduct, as this sits within the wider context of safeguarding, child protection and the curriculum.The EA’s Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) provides safeguarding support, advice and guidance and training, which includes harmful sexual behaviour, to education settings as well as to the EA Youth Service.  The CPSS also operates a support helpline in relation to safeguarding concerns.  Regional CPSS offices may also direct schools to relevant localised supports.  My department issues statutory guidance to schools and other educational settings to support them in safeguarding and protecting children and young people.  Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools aims to assist the school Board of Governors and school staff in fulfilling their safeguarding and child protection responsibilities.  This guidance includes information relating to harmful sexual behaviours and signposts other agencies and support services, including Gateway Services, Social Services and the PSNI. Circular - 2022/02 - Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour provides specific guidance for schools and other educational settings about identification and management of harmful sexual behaviour displayed by children and young people, including supports in place as part of any Safety and Support Plans and Risk Assessment and Management Plans.  This Circular also signposts appropriate support services and resources including the CPSS, Social Services, the Youth Justice Agency and PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether information in relation to incidences of sexual violence and harassment which have occurred in schools between pupils is recorded by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) do not record information in relation to all incidences of sexual violence and harassment in schools.  Record keeping in relation to safeguarding and child protection matters is the responsibility of individual schools and their Boards of Governors (BoGs).  BoGs should ensure that the arrangements operated by the school for the retention, management and release of records comply with statutory requirements, including data protection legislation. The Department. Circular 2020/07 - Child Protection: Record Keeping in Schools provides a framework for managing child protection records in order to ensure schools create and retain reliable records to demonstrate accountability for decisions and actions taken.  The Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools provides guidance on wider safeguarding and child protection, including harmful sexual behaviour and record keeping in schools where there is a child protection concern. Circular 2022/02 - Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour provides specific guidance for schools and other educational settings about identification and management of harmful sexual behaviour displayed by children and young people.The EA’s Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) provides training to designated safeguarding roles within schools and advice on managing a range of child protection concerns, including harmful sexual behaviour, via its helpline. CPSS record details of the types of advice and support sought by schools and provide updates on any areas of concern to inform policy, training needs etc.  CPSS also maintain records on the number of Risk Assessment Management Plans developed under the DE circular 2022/02, given their role in supporting the school Principal and Designated Teacher for Child Protection in managing this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce a part-time 20mph speed limit outside Grange Park Primary School, Bangor, in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding provided by his Department for (i) rape crisis referral centres; and (ii) sexual assault referral centres in each of the last three years.
answer_text: In NI, we have only one Sexual Assault Referral Centre, which is funded by Department of Health.  The Rape Crisis NI is a voluntary sector organisation which is funded by the Joseph Rountree Foundation and the Justice and Equality Fund.Sexual assault referral centres (SARCs) offer medical, practical and emotional support to anyone who has been raped, sexually assaulted or abused. The cost of the Sexual Assault Referral Centre, operated by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT), was:2020/2021 £1,035,0452021/2022 £1,067,132 2022/2023 £1,612,204 Please note: 2023/24 costs are not yet availableThe figures above are sourced from Trust Cost Submissions.These figures represent actual expenditure including any non-recurrent expenditure by Trusts.Expenditure is quoted as fully absorbed costs; this means figures include direct, indirect, overheads and capital charges costs.From 2020/21 to 2021/22 per Ministerial agreement, the annual costing returns were temporarily suspended during the Covid Pandemic and to allow a regional costing system to be implemented. During this period, it was agreed that the 2019/20 costs would be uplifted by 2.5% for 2020/21 and a further 3.1% for 2021/22. Costs produced for 2022/23 are the first through the new regional system for 2022-23 and therefore are subject to further review and refinement.The reporting period is financial year April-March; i.e. '2022/2023' is financial year ending March 2023.In addition to the expenditure identified clearly above as relating to sexual assault, there may be other relevant support services being provided for which the expenditure is not necessarily labelled as for sexual assault victims, as costs are not recorded by reason for referral.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for their assessment of the Women Against Violence Europe handbook, Prevention and Support Standards for Women Survivors of Violence; and (ii) whether recommendations on budgeting and costing the prevention of violence against women and support services for survivors will be a consideration when allocating funding for the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Women Against Violence Europe (WAVE) Handbook: Prevention and Support Standards for Women Survivors of Violence sets out an overview of the key legal developments and practical advances enabled by the Istanbul Convention. WAVE is a network of over 170 European women’s NGOs working towards prevention and protection of women and children from violence. The draft Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework has been informed by international standards and a range of best practice research. We will consider new research as relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of road safety on the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Officials will review the sites where there are clusters of collisions on the A2 to identify if there are any common causation factors for these collisions which would allow an effective road safety measure to be introduced.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  If driver attitudes towards speeding and careless driving do not change, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to rise

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to introduce a third-party right of appeal in planning decisions.
answer_text: I remain committed to ensuring that our planning system continues to work in an efficient and effective way and will continue to review the progress that has been made to date. Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system. I have no current plans to introduce a third -party right of appeal.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for its assessment of the affordability of the provision of catering for voluntary and charity groups visiting Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: When providing catering services for voluntary and charity groups, the event organiser, during the planning of their event, discusses their requirements and associated budget with our contractor’s representative on site at Parliament Buildings.  Typically, catering is then provided within the budget envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a feasibility study for Grey Point Fort, Helen’s Bay, will be included in his Department's budget for 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department has bid for monies for a feasibility study for the State Care Monument of Grey Point Fort in 2024/25.  However, budgets remain undecided.  At this time, I can only assure you of our commitment to progress plans for the fort once funding becomes available.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 7281/22-27; and (ii) AQW 8508/22-27.
answer_text: (i) AQW 7281/22-27 was answered on 14 June 2024.(ii) AQW 8508/22-27 was answered on 6 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the (i) condition; and (ii) structural integrity of Castle Park Road, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to austerity by the British Government which has meant that it has had to introduce a limited service maintenance policy, meaning that only the highest priority road defects are repaired. This underfunding has unfortunately resulted in a deteriorating road network. Castle Park Road was last inspected on 9 April 2024 and is considered to be structurally sound. There were no defects that met the intervention criteria in accordance with the current limited-service maintenance policy. My Department will continue to monitor this area and repair defects in accordance with the current policy.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the report A Workforce Fit for the Future: RCGPNI Retention Strategy; and (ii) whether he will implement the recommendations arising from the report.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department is progressing work in a number of the key areas outlined in the Retention Strategy.  This includes the recently agreed 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract which is also an important step forward to achieving greater stability in General Practice.  The contract makes significant progress against key aims identified by GPs in their negotiations, including provision of £5million dedicated to GP indemnity, as an interim measure, pending identification of the long-term model for future provision.  We are focussing on building the GP workforce, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.  We are also taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs) and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists.In terms of the GP Fellowship Scheme, my Department is considering how the Scheme might be further developed, subject to available funding, to extend the range and scope of Fellowship placement opportunities and provide for Educational Bursaries to be available for all Fellows. 	My Department has also developed guidance which will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care and is engaging with relevant NICS Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unnecessary directing of patients to GPs.My Department is committed to working to identify solutions to the issues facing General Practice, including further roll out of MDTs, exploring digital solutions and working with GPs and HSC Trusts to promote stability in General Medical Services.  However, progress on initiatives will be constrained by the availability of the necessary funding to support this. Regrettably, the 2024-25 Budget outcome for my Department was significantly less than what we required and has resulted in 2.3% less funding in 2024-25 than we spent in 2023-24.  Health will be facing significant budgetary pressures in 2024-25 with some extremely difficult decisions required to manage within the budget allocated.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the work being carried out to ensure completion of the statutory duty to promote equality of opportunity for all visa holders.
answer_text: Immigration status is not a devolved matter. There are however extensive legal protections in place in respect of equality in this jurisdiction.  Section 75(1) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 requires departments and other public authorities when carrying out functions to have due regard to:the need to promote equality of opportunity across protected characteristics of religious belief, political opinion, gender, race, disability, age, marital status, dependants, and sexual orientation ; and regard to the desirability of promoting good relations between persons of different political opinion, religious belief, and racial group.These obligations, and the commitments in our Equality Scheme, are at the core of policy and decision making in The Executive Office. Consultation papers on new policy proposals can be made available in a range of formats and variety of languages. Our Department takes a leading role in seeking to embed the section 75 duties across government here. We also sponsor the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, which has a responsibility to promote equality of opportunity and good relations, and to provide equality rights advice to employers and members of the public.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for their assessment of the reason the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership closed; and (ii) whether their Department has carried out any formal evaluation of the partnership.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership (NISMP) was hosted by the Northern Ireland Local Government Association (NILGA) between 2011 and 2022. One member of staff was employed, and when the post became vacant in April 2022, NILGA advised that they believed they were not the correct organisation to host the NISMP.The Executive Office agreed with Home Office to formally take on some of the SMP functions, primarily those focusing on communication and engagement, to co-ordinate and encourage multi-agency support for asylum seekers and their integration into our society.  This fits with our co-ordination responsibilities.As TEO was not responsible for the establishment of the NISMP, the Department has not carried out any formal evaluation. We will continue to review the new arrangements for the communication and engagement responsibilities we have adopted, with Home Office, to ensure they are fit for purpose and meet local needs going forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Dispersal Fund, including a breakdown of its allocation by local council area.
answer_text: Since 2022/23, Full Dispersal funding has been provided by Home Office to enhance the capacity and infrastructure to support the wider dispersal of asylum seekers here. We were pleased to have been able to allocate £1.54million of this funding to councils to develop local support over the past two financial years, as follows:Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council£88,478Ards and North Down Borough Council£152,939Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council£84,218Belfast City Council£406,228Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council£162,095Derry City and Strabane District Council£59,448Fermanagh and Omagh District Council£79,262Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council£173,390Mid and East Antrim Borough Council£50,000Mid Ulster District Council£143,175Newry, Mourne and Down District Council£147,695TOTAL£1,546,928In the absence of confirmed funding by Home Office for 2024/25, unfortunately we are not in a position to determine further allocations. We continue to press Home Office for confirmation of this year’s funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the (i) financial; and (ii) transition support provided to asylum seekers who enter roles on the Shortage Occupation List.
answer_text: Immigration policy is an excepted matter. Decisions in respect of financial and transition support for those who are permitted to enter roles on the now Immigration Salary List (previously the Shortage Occupation List), whilst awaiting an asylum claim, therefore remain the responsibility of the Home Office. Once granted refugee status, support services provided in the devolved sphere would not be impacted if an individual had previously been given permission to work, by Home Office, whilst awaiting their asylum claim decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether funding for the Skill Up Programme will be made available in the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) for an update on the evaluation the 2023-24 Skill Up Programme.
answer_text: Long term strategic investment in our skills system will enable a flourishing economy and I am considering the best means of closing the skills gap, including which associated programmes and funding will best address this. The Post Project Evaluation of the three-year Skill Up programme will be completed by 30th June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of wheelchair accessible social houses in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Work is ongoing to survey all NIHE properties across NI to classify them on an Accessible Housing Register. Regrettably in the absence of the full picture NIHE are not in a position at this time to provide the requested data.The Housing Executive does not hold Housing Association stock figures”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when (i) the Ending Violence against Women and Girls strategic framework will be approved; and (ii) the associated implementation plan will commence.
answer_text: We remain committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls and associated action plan for consideration by the Executive Committee as soon as possible. Options are currently being considered and it is anticipated the updated Framework and Action Plan will be brought to the Executive shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6866/22-27, why work to install a new pedestrian crossing on Bloomfield Road, Bangor, has not commenced.
answer_text: The installation of a new pedestrian crossing on the Bloomfield Road, Bangor is listed within the Ards and North Down Borough Council Annual Report 2023/24 and the work was initially anticipated to commence on-site before the end of March 2024. However, there was a slight delay but I am pleased to advise that work has started on site to facilitate NIE works and the remaining works will be scheduled for completion during the school holidays, taking into account the availability of resources and capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps being taken to reduce the waiting list of large families seeking long-term social housing in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There were 122 large families on the waiting list for the North Down Parliamentary Constituency, 91 of whom were deemed to be in housing stress at the end of December 2023. There were 20 allocations of social homes to large families on the waiting list over the preceding 12 months.In terms of housing supply there are currently 98 Housing Executive properties with four or more bedrooms in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency.There are currently no 4-bedroom properties included within the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) in the North Down Constituency. The proportion of applicants from single person, elderly and small family households is significantly higher; however, NIHE continue to engage with Housing Associations to identify and progress schemes which will deliver a sustainable mix of house types to meet current and future needs.There have been 151 social homes delivered in North Down Parliamentary Constituency in the last 5 years which have included 31 no. 1 bed homes, 94 no. 2 bed homes, 25 no. 3 bed homes and 1 no. 4 bed home. This aligns to those profile of applicants in acute housing need and is kept under annual review”.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date he became aware of the outcome of a building condition survey carried out in June 2023 by the Education Authority into Bright Sparks Preschool, Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: I accepted an invitation to visit Bright Sparks pre-school on Wednesday 1 May 2024.During this visit, the Chairperson of Bright Sparks Management Committee referenced the condition survey which had been carried out on the modular unit where the pre-school provider was operating from.However, I was also made aware that discussions had taken place with Education Authority representatives on Monday 29 April regarding the accommodation provision.I understand that a further survey on the modular unit is to be undertaken this week by the Education Authority to identify urgent statutory remedial works required on the modular unit and that a further meeting is planned for 16 May to try and agree a resolution of the ongoing accommodation issues.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 9717/22-27, whether the recipients of the fund have received confirmation from the Department that the funding is secure until March 2025.
answer_text: Funding availability for the first progress period from April to July 2024 has been confirmed.  Funded organisations received notification of this on 3 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the requirements in the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Clinical Guideline CG174 have been implemented in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations.  NICE Clinical Guideline CG174 - Intravenous fluid therapy in adults in hospital was endorsed by my Department for implementation in July 2014.The former HSCB, in collaboration with Trusts established a regional Task and Finish Group which identified the recommendations which would have the greatest impact on patient care and subsequently prioritised these for implementation. The following priorities were identified and individual subgroups were established to take forward implementation:Education and training – medical, nursing and pharmacyDevelopment of a Fluid Management ChartDevelopment of an Audit ToolThe work of these groups has now been completed.  However, the agreed action plan has resource implications which has not yet been secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the (i) condition; and (ii) structural integrity of Moss Road, Millisle.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the condition of Moss Road, Millisle. Verge strengthening / stabilisation works are required at several locations along Moss Road in advance of resurfacing. The design of the proposed works is well advanced and currently going through the road safety audit process. It is currently envisaged that verge strengthening work will commence during the current financial year subject to acquiring the necessary lands and budget availability.In addition, a number of large-scale repairs, remote from the verge strengthening work, have been programmed for Moss Road. These are planned to commence in the coming months. The full programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will resurface Clandeboye Road, Bangor.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce measures to prevent overtaking on Moss Road, Millisle.
answer_text: All requests for improvements to the road network are assessed in line with my Department’s current policies and guidance and all works are subject to prioritisation within the funding available.Hazard warning road markings are currently provided as centre lines on the Moss Road at locations where it is necessary to highlight to road users the presence of a road junction, central refuge or other hazard.  It is not proposed to introduce any further measures to prevent overtaking on the Moss Road at this time.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will resurface Groomsport Road, Bangor.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the implementation of the framework Developing a more strategic approach to 14-19 Education and Training.
answer_text: Oral Question No 13 & 14 – AQO 365 & AQO 366/22-27I recently met with my Ministerial colleague, the Minister for Education to discuss the 14-19 Framework and other shared priorities. During this meeting we reaffirmed our commitment to our departments working collaboratively to support the educational opportunities for all our young people.Given the importance of this work, Minister Givan and I jointly tasked officials with preparing proposals for our consideration to further enhance delivery of the Framework and joint working across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will resurface the A2 road between Donaghadee and Millisle.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme. Regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the modular buildings planned for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College.
answer_text: The uplift to admissions at Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College brings the school’s approved numbers into line with the level at which the school has already been operating.  I am aware that the school requires additional accommodation to meet the needs of its increased enrolment and it had been anticipated that a scheme to deliver this additional accommodation would be completed for September 2024 however, due to complications arising from the Public Private Partnership contract this is no longer possible.  The Education Authority is proactively pursuing alternative proposals to deliver the accommodation, but it is anticipated that there could be a delay of up to 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the announcement of the uplift of admissions for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College, for his assessment of the capacity of the school to accommodate the number of pupils approved for enrolment.
answer_text: The uplift to admissions at Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College brings the school’s approved numbers into line with the level at which the school has already been operating.  I am aware that the school requires additional accommodation to meet the needs of its increased enrolment and it had been anticipated that a scheme to deliver this additional accommodation would be completed for September 2024 however, due to complications arising from the Public Private Partnership contract this is no longer possible.  The Education Authority is proactively pursuing alternative proposals to deliver the accommodation, but it is anticipated that there could be a delay of up to 12 months.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount spent on the Communities in Transition Project since its introduction, broken down by focus area.
answer_text: As of 31 March 2024, Communities in Transition (CIT) has awarded over £19 million to projects across the eight CIT geographical areas. The breakdown is as follows: Carrickfergus and Larne 	£2,088,013.Derry/Londonderry	£1,884,606.North Down		£1,540,709.Lurgan/Craigavon           £1,146,861.West Belfast                    £2,630,702.North Belfast                   £1,363,507.Shankill                           £1,705,975.East Belfast                     £1,778,823.Regional Projects            £5,183,813.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of determinations made by the Victims’ Payment Board which have been (i) appealed; and (ii) overturned following an appeal, since the introduction of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of 15 April 2024, 1,251 determinations have been made by the Victims’ Payments Board since the Scheme opened to applications.Of these, 162 determinations have been appealed with 46 successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial support will be available for people from Northern Ireland competing in the Paralympic Games.
To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will be launching a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme, in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2023.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023, with a view to publishing in Summer 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on progress in relation to the provision of financial support schemes for the installation of (i) domestic heat pumps; and (i) any other domestic renewable energy sources.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023.  The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources in the north of Ireland by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity. Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects ensuring consumers pay a fair price for electricity produced locally.Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.Independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters is available from organisations such as the Energy Saving Trust. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to businesses without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre, Millisle.
answer_text: Officials from the Historic Environment Division are opening the heritage buildings to the public, free of charge, Thursday to Monday. Officials are continuing to meet potential operators with the objective of establishing a sustainable commercial model for the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council for the 2024-25 financial year will be published.
answer_text: The 2024-25 work programmes for the Ards and North Down area, as with all other Council areas, are currently being developed in advance of budgets being confirmed.  It will take several weeks after budgets are confirmed to finalise and prepare the report and agree a date to present to each Council.Following the presentation to Council, the Report will be published on the Department’s website and issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Minority Ethnic Development Fund.
answer_text: Since its establishment in 2002 the Minority Ethnic Development Fund (MEDF) has enabled hundreds of projects and groups to support minority ethnic people.  In line with a review of the Fund, a 3-year funding scheme is currently in place from April 2022 – March 2025 and there are 31 successful applications currently being supported. More information on the Fund and organisations currently receiving funding can be found at.	www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/articles/racial-equality#toc-6	www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/minority-ethnic-development-fund-successful-organisations

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Communities in Transition Project.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what plans they have to establish a Citizen’s Assembly, as recommended in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: We recognise the role that society plays in supporting policy making and we will be  examining all approaches to civic participation which are effective and value for cost.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a Programme for Government.
answer_text: Work on the Programme for Government continues at pace and we aim to provide a draft Programme for Government for further consideration in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why up-to-date statistics on terminations of pregnancy are not published on his Department's website.
answer_text: The Department publishes an annual report on terminations of pregnancy carried out in Northern Ireland.  The most recent report was published in January 2023 and covered the period 1 April 2021 – 31 March 2022.   The data contained within this report, however, only considers those terminations carried out in HSC Hospitals in Northern Ireland as an inpatient or day case, in line with the definitions and scope of previous iterations of this publication. Following the commissioning of abortion services in Northern Ireland, as instructed by the Secretary of State on 2 December 2022, work is ongoing to ensure that future annual publications include statistics on all terminations of pregnancy in Northern Ireland.  As a result, the next annual publication which has been delayed until June 2024 will include statistics from the introduction of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020 on 31 March 2020 to March 2023 respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why the resurfacing of the Groomsport Road roundabout was prioritised before other outstanding maintenance projects in North Down.
answer_text: There are a number of factors used when determining the need for road resurfacing, including road usage, general surface condition, structural deformation, public enquiries and public liability claims.Groomsport Road Roundabout forms part of the busy A2 corridor and had started to show signs of structural failure so was included in the 2021/22 resurfacing programme. However, it could not be completed due to contractor capacity issues prior to the expiry of the contract in November 2021.The scheme was planned for inclusion in the 2024/25 resurfacing programme subject to available resources. However, following an in-year reallocation of capital funding the scheme has been brought forward into this 2023/24 year.All the schemes in the North Down Area which are listed in the 2023/24 Council Report have been delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to address flooding in North Down.
answer_text: My Department has responded to a number of flooding incidents over recent months in North Down, most notably within the Bangor area. Flooding in the Bangor Area will be examined as part of a wider ‘Bangor Significant Flood Risk Area’ study, which is already on my Department’s Rivers Directorate 10-year capital works programme. The study will consider the recent flood events in Autumn 2023 and seek to identify if there are any viable flood alleviation options to mitigate flood risk in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department's spending on active travel in North Down in the last three years, including details of the projects in which the spend occurred.
answer_text: Monitoring of Active Travel spend within the Department is not broken down by parliamentary constituency.  The North Down constituency falls within the Ards and North Down Borough Council area which forms part of DfI Southern Division.  The following figures were recorded against Southern Division’s specific capital Active Travel projects for the current financial year to 31 January 2024 and the three previous financial years:Financial Year£m2020/210.42021/221.92022/230.92023/24 (to January 2024)1.0The Department also issued letters of offer to the Ards and North Down Council for the delivery of two greenways, namely: Comber to Newtownards Greenway (£2.4m) and Newtownards to Green Road Bangor Greenway (£2.3m). Projects relating to Active Travel in Ards and North Down Council area can be found in each of the Department’s Roads Reports to Council; Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2020/2021 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2021/2022 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2022/2023 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2023/2024 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many Penalty Charge Notices have been issued by his Department in Crawfordsburn in each of the last three years.
answer_text: 7 Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) have been issued in Crawfordsburn in the last 3 years as follows: 13th August 2022 – 3 PCNs26th August 2023 – 3 PCNs5th September 2023 – 1 PCN

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action he is taking to ensure NI Water responds to planning consultations in a timely manner.
answer_text: My department works with statutory planning consultees to help speed up responses to planning applications through the ongoing work of the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which links statutory consultees with local planning authorities.  NI Water is represented on, and participates in, this forum. NI Water has returned 85% or more of their statutory responses within the 21-day target in four of the last five years and reported their best performance to date (97% of responses on time) in 2021-22 (the most recent available annual statistics).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to address traffic congestion on Main Street, Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: I can advise there are extensive existing waiting restrictions in the form of double yellow lines to facilitate traffic progression throughout Crawfordsburn Village.  Crawfordsburn Village has numerous residential properties, and it is not considered appropriate to restrict the parking further at this time.It is recognised that Crawfordsburn Village will have some delays during peak periods.  However, there are concerns that addressing these peak period delays could inappropriately attract further commuting traffic through the Village rather than using the A2 Belfast Road.Concerns about vehicles which are parked inappropriately which cause an obstruction or danger to any other road users should be reported to the to the police who is the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has any plans to align pay for further education lecturers with school teachers.
answer_text: Further Education lecturers’ pay, as well as the terms and conditions of employment, are matters for the Further Education colleges as employers. However, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers and regard this as a priority issue. 		Following the concerns raised by Further Education lecturers regarding the announcement of the teachers’ pay agreement, I have asked my officials to explore the options for aligning further education lecturers pay with teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has concluded work on the review of the feasibility study pertaining to flood risk management in Omagh town; and (ii) to provide a timeline for the publication of potential options to reduce flood risk in Omagh town.
answer_text: A review of the feasibility study for Omagh is ongoing and it is expected to be completed by the end of the summer. Once the review has been completed my Department will consider how best to communicate any viable proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will resurface Crawfordsburn Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered as a result of years of Tory austerity and cuts. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council once future budgets are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many Penalty Charge Notices have been issued to vehicles parked in a cycle lane, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 gives my Department the power to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) when a vehicle is detected parked on a legislated Mandatory Cycle Lane. These are cycle lanes which are part of the carriageway (road) and are identified by a solid white line or by a raised traffic island. 53 PCNs have been issued to vehicles parked on a legislated mandatory cycle lane between 15th March 2021 and 14th March 2024.Advisory Cycle Lanes, which are identified by a broken white line, cycle tracks on footways or those separate from the carriageway, are not generally enforceable by a Traffic Attendant unless there are parking restrictions marked on the carriageway, for example yellow lines, as these would also apply to the cycle lane, track, or footway. As PCNs do not record the actual location of the vehicle, it is not possible to detail the total number of PCNs issued to vehicles parked on cycle lanes, cycle tracks or on footways separate from the carriageway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the Healthy Happy Minds pilot, what steps he will take to ensure the provision of counselling services in primary schools.
answer_text: An independent post project evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and is available on the Department’s website.  I will assess the findings of the evaluation and take the time to consider this along with wider research / feedback from stakeholders in developing the Department’s longer-term approach.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Board criteria including that a spouse, partner and child is eligible to apply on behalf of a deceased person, but not a sibling.
answer_text: Under the direction of the former Head of the Civil Service (HOCS), draft legislation based wholly on the Hart Report recommendations was developed in 2018.  Following a full 16-week consultation exercise, which received 526 responses, a consultation report, policy position paper and draft legislation were sent to the then Secretary of State.  Thereafter, at the request of the Secretary of State, the former HOCS led a series of discussions with local political parties to agree their position on those policy issues.  The draft Bill to establish the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA) and the HIA Redress Board reflected that political consensus and subsequently the legislation was enacted on 5 November 2019.The Hart Report recognised that not all redress schemes allowed surviving spouses or children to receive part or all of the compensation that an abused person would have received before they died.  However, the Hart Report noted that the adverse experiences of victims and survivors had an impact throughout their adult lives when it came to being a parent or spouse, and that their families suffered as a result of that childhood experience. Hart therefore recommended that it would be just and humane that only those directly affected, namely the spouse or children of a deceased individual should be able to claim 75% of the compensation that would have been awarded to their spouse or parent. However, following responses to the consultation and engagement with the local political parties, it was agreed that no reduction would be made to any application made in respect of a deceased individual.Section 6 of the Historical Institutional Abuse (Northern Ireland) Act 2019 provides for applications in respect of entitlement to claim compensation in respect of a deceased person.
