question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to review the definition of socially disadvantaged, in relation to the statutory admissions criteria for pre-schools.
answer_text: The definition of “socially disadvantaged circumstances” has had to be extended over time to ‘read in’ new welfare payments which have subsumed those named in the legislation. I am aware that this legislation needs to be reviewed. A consultation to seek views on the matter ran from 19 April to 16 July 2021 and confirmed that there is no longer support for the indefinite retention of this statutory priority criterion but its removal is only likely to receive the necessary support when all children have access to 22.5 hours of pre-school per week – a level of provision currently available to just 40% of children across Northern Ireland.   It is my intention to move to a position where 22.5 hours is the standard offer and the socially disadvantaged criterion can be removed completely.  This will take some time to achieve and will be subject to agreement and significant investment by the Assembly.In the meantime, I can reassure you that there are sufficient places in every area across Northern Ireland to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target aged child whose parents want it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why uniform grants are not available for pupils in statutory pre-school settings.
answer_text: Uniform grants are available as a contribution towards the costs of school uniform for low-income families with children of compulsory school-age. It is not compulsory for children in Northern Ireland to attend pre-school and therefore in keeping with other parts of the UK, uniform grants are not available to the families of children attending pre-school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether all proposed schemes contained in the Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2023-24 will be completed by the end of the financial year.
answer_text: The programming of delivery of the schemes proposed within the Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2023-24 can be impacted by a range of factors including availability of resources and capacity, both Departmental and contractor, and also other events such as severe adverse weather.  My officials are striving to advance the completion of those schemes which were identified for delivery in the Report during the 2023-24 financial year.  Any of these schemes that are not completed during this financial year will be prioritised for completion early in the 2024-25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any representations they have made to the Secretary of State regarding the review of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: Ministers’ have written to the Secretary of State to support the extension of the backdating deadline, which will allow more time for eligible applicants to avail of backdated payments. TEO officials have met with NIO officials to discuss the review of the Scheme.  The conversation focused on the Scheme Principles, as set out in Regulation 4 of the Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020 and TEO’s view of progress in relation to the Principles through the implementation and delivery of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the draft Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan will be published.
answer_text: The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan.  At this stage, I am not in a position to advise on a publication date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to reopening Craigavad Railway Station.
answer_text: My Department is working towards finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review which considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. The Review includes a recommendation to explore the case for developing new stations, however, being at a strategic level, the Review has not considered the location of these stations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the development of any new rail halts along the existing network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with (i) the Education Authority; and (ii) teaching trade unions in relation to the recruitment and retention of teaching staff.
answer_text: I have had no direct engagement with the Education Authority in relation to the recruitment and retention of teaching staff however I have met with the Northern Ireland Teaching Council (NITC) and the issue of some post-primary schools experiencing difficulties in recruiting for certain subject areas has been raised. The unions also indicated that their opinion is that teachers in Northern Ireland not receiving a pay award for 2021, 2022 and 2023 has impacted on the recruitment and retention of teaching staff.   Intensive engagement has been ongoing between both sides of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) to resolve the ongoing teachers’ pay dispute. TNC includes representatives from teachers’ employers, sectoral interests and the Department as well as representatives from the five main teachers’ unions.A significant formal pay offer has now been made to NITC which, if accepted, will bring the salary of a beginning teacher in Northern Ireland to £30,000 which is the same as a beginning teacher in England. This is a 24.3% pay rise for beginning teachers.  The pay offer equates to a cumulative total of 10.4% plus £1000 being applied to the other teachers’ and leadership pay scales.It is expected that the acceptance of the pay offer will address many of the recruitment and retention issues expressed to me. Engagement will continue through TNC to address any outstanding factors which may be impacting on the recruitment and retention of teaching staff.My Department recognises the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic teacher workforce planning, and plans to engage with key stakeholders to get a better understanding of any identified shortfall areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department has taken to improve road safety on Belfast Road, Holywood.
answer_text: In November 2022 and September 2023, the Divisional team completed reviews of the existing engineering measures on the Belfast Road, Holywood which concluded that the existing arrangements on this location complied with current policy guidelines and did not identify any further road safety improvements that would bring further road safety benefits to the area.All requests for improvements to the road network, including road improvement schemes, are assessed in line with my Department’s current policies and guidance and all works are subject to prioritisation within the funding available.Details of the planned list of local transport and safety measures for the 2023/24 financial year are provided in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf    Local transport and safety measures programmed for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.Issues relating to inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI which has responsibility for enforcement relating to both matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce parking restrictions on Belfast Road, Holywood.
answer_text: Waiting restrictions such as double yellow lines, may be introduced on the road network where parking is causing an obstruction to other traffic, resulting in progression issues, in the form of queues, tailbacks or difficulties making turning movements.  Officials have previously assessed the Belfast Road and have not identified a traffic progression issue at this location when cars are parked (on the road) as the road is sufficiently wide to enables two vehicles to pass safely. It is therefore considered that permanent waiting restrictions in the form of double yellow lines would not be appropriate at this location.I appreciate that there have been requests for waiting restrictions to prevent footway parking.  During November 2023, the Department made legislation that added to the list of locations where parking wholly or partly on a footway is prohibited to now include beside bus lanes, bus stops, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory school keep clears.The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’, and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.  The PSNI has the authority to take immediate action if a vehicle parked on the footway obstructs the free passage for pedestrians.On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking.  I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have considered the Findings Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will introduce measures on the Bryansburn Road roundabout, Bangor, to improve road safety.
answer_text: All requests for improvements to the road network, including road improvement schemes, are assessed in line with my Department’s current policies and guidance and all works are subject to prioritisation within the funding available.A review of Bryansburn Road roundabout, Bangor indicates that measures have previously been implemented at this location and the roundabout currently has a good road safety record.  There are no plans to implement any additional measures at this time.Details of the planned list of local transport and safety measures for the 2023/24 financial year are provided in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf   Local transport and safety measures programmed for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale for a redress scheme for victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses, as recommended by the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report.
answer_text: Work on a draft Bill, covering both the establishment of the Public Inquiry and the Financial Redress Scheme, is well advanced but there are some key policy decisions which the Executive will need to consider before it is ready to go out to public consultation. The consultation period is likely to be 12 weeks and, once responses are analysed and any amendments are made, the draft Bill will then be introduced in the Assembly. Therefore, it is not possible to provide a definitive time period for the financial redress scheme opening, but we are committed to progressing as quickly as possible. Regarding non-financial redress – there is already important support services which has supported over 300 victim and survivors to date. Other non-financial redress elements like apology and memorialisation are critical parts of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6994/22-27, why pedestrian safety was not a consideration in his Department’s decision.
answer_text: Road safety is one of my highest priorities and I am committed to reduce and remove, where possible, those risks through engineering measures that fall within the remit of the Department.To this end my officials have reviewed the junction of Belfast Road and Abbey Ring, Holywood in relation to pedestrian safety and concluded that the existing engineering measures are appropriate and comply with the Department’s current policy guidelines.I am aware that there are limited parking opportunities in this area when events are being held and I would reiterate that concerns about vehicles which are parked inappropriately which cause an obstruction or danger to any other road users should be reported to the to the police who is the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the availability of NHS dental services for children in North Down.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and are not required to register new patients. Neither does the Department commission Health Service places in practices, for adults or children.There are nineteen practices in the North Down Assembly Area who have made arrangements with the Strategic Performance and Planning Department to provide Health Service dentistry. The Department does not hold any information as to whether these practices currently have room to accept new registrations of children.General Dental Practices in the North Down Assembly Area at 31st December 2023Practice1, 2Practice AddressAbbey Dental Surgery32 ABBEY STREET, BANGOR, BT20 4JABallyholme Dental Practice22A GROOMSPORT ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 5LNBangor Orthodontics2 BINGHAM LANE, BANGOR, BT20 5DRBrunswick Dental Practice38 BRUNSWICK ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 3DUBUPA Dental Care Bangor SpringhillBANGOR SPRINGHILL, 4A KILLEEN AVENUE, BT19 1NBCastlebawn Dental Practice32-34 Victoria Road, Bangor, BT20 5EXClandeboye Dental Clinic109G CLANDEBOYE ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 3JWDonaghadee Dental Surgery55/57 HIGH STREET, DONAGHADEE, BT21 0AQHamilton Road Dental42 HAMILTON ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 4LEHarbour Dental Practice13 THE PARADE, DONAGHADEE, BT21 0AEHazeldene Dental Practice64 DONAGHADEE RD, BANGOR, BT20 4QXHelens Bay Dental Practice1A STATION ROAD, HELENS BAY, BT19 1TNHigh Street Dental Practice116 HIGH STREET, HOLYWOOD, BT18 9HWHollywood Dental Care128 HIGH STREET, HOLYWOOD, BT18 9HWLivingston Dental Healthcare38 HAMILTON ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 4LEMercer Dental Care97 BRUNSWICK ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 3DWNigel Smith Dental Care107 HAMILTON ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 4LNRichard Loane & Associates4 HAMILTON ROAD, BANGOR, BT20 4LEShore Road Dental & Implant Clinic32 SHORE ROAD, HOLYWOOD, BT18 9HXActive dental surgeries at the 31st December 2023. List may include a number of Orthodontic and Specialist Practices. These practices do not typically have registered patient lists, with instead patients normally seen on referral from a GDS dental practice. A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment. The absence of Executive Ministers has delayed the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  In recent days I have considered proposals for a pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24 that is in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report. My intentions were communicated to the British Dental Association (Northern Ireland) on 5 March 2024 and the Department awaits a response. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I am currently considering investment proposals to further support General Dental Services into 2024/25, which will be shared with the British Dental Association in due course. A consultation on both Older Persons’ and Children’s Oral Health Improvem

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the long-term impact of the lack of access to an NHS dentist for children.
answer_text: There is increasing evidence that children who develop problems in their primary teeth may be at increased risk of disease and poor oral health in adolescence and adulthood. Therefore, it is important to identify children who are at risk of tooth decay at an early stage to implement preventative interventions to protect young teeth and establish good habits. Dental attendance can be an important predictor of oral health across the life course.In addition, untreated tooth decay can cause pain and infections (often requiring the use of multiple courses of antibiotics) that may lead to problems with sleeping, eating, speaking, playing, and learning. Children who have poor oral health often miss more school and this can also impact on work attendance for parents and carers. Dental attendance in line with NICE guidance regarding risk-based recall intervals can help to support child oral health and reduce the need for more invasive dental interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to ensure patients in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust with a diagnosis of ADHD can access lisdexamphetamine.
answer_text: Patients in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, and others across Northern Ireland who are affected by the intermittent supply of lisdexamfetamine (Elvanse®) products should contact their prescriber or relevant healthcare professional for advice on treatment options.As seen in my response to AQW 8194/22-27, communication has been issued to healthcare professionals providing advice and action to take during this period of supply disruption to help minimise the impact on patients. My Department is also continuing to monitor the supply situation with relevant stakeholders, including DHSC and the other devolved governments, and where necessary national and locally tried and tested mitigation arrangements can be enacted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all new school buildings will include air filtration units in classrooms.
answer_text: All classrooms in new school builds are ventilated in line with BB101:2018 and will be served via Mechanical Ventilation units with Heat Recovery (MVHR), which is now standard practice.The MVHR filtration will be compliant with EN 13779 'Ventilation and room-conditioning systems in non-residential buildings' as set out in BB101:2018, and with World Health Organisation (2005) air quality guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funding is available to schools to install air filtration units in classrooms.
answer_text: My Department does not provide specific funding for air filtration units in classrooms. Where natural ventilation is proven to be deficient in a school the issue should be reported to the Education Authority’s Maintenance Helpdesk.Remedial works will be undertaken to rectify the situation which may include repairs or replacement of windows or the installation of an air ventilation system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will take to address the shortage of lisdexamphetamine.
answer_text: There are national and locally tried and tested contingency arrangements in place for dealing with any medicine supply disruption, which includes providing communications to healthcare professionals to help mitigate the impact a supply disruption may have on patients ensuring they can continue to access treatments. As a consequence of the ongoing supply disruption to lisdexamfetamine (Elvanse®), my Department issued an updated Medicine Supply Notification (MPS 3159) to Health and Social Care (HSC) providers on 13 February 2024. This guidance provides healthcare professionals with an update on the current supply disruption, with intermittent supplies expected to continue until April 2024 for these products. The update also provides advice and actions for healthcare professionals to help mitigate the impact an intermittent supply disruption will have on an affected patient, which reflects messaging agreed by all the UK administrations.Healthcare professionals can also access the Department of Health and Social Care’s (DHSC) Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) website to receive up-to-date shortage information and the anticipated resupply dates for those lisdexamfetamine (Elvanse®) products affected. As this is a UK wide supply disruption and DHSC leads on the arrangements for maintaining medicines supply chains to the UK, my Department will continue to work closely with DHSC and other relevant partners, including the other devolved governments on this matter to minimise the impact these supply disruptions will have on patients Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Commissioner for Public Appointments.
answer_text: In May 2021 a public appointment competition was launched for the role of Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPANI), although an appointment decision was not reached prior to the end of the last mandate.In the absence of an Executive, CPANI was added to the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 list of specified offices to allow the appointment function to be exercised by the Secretary of State. However, this did not happen prior to the recent return of the Executive.The appointing authority for CPANI has therefore now reverted to the First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly. Given the passage of time, officials are currently in the process of exploring appropriate next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 38/22-27, to detail the business case for Ballycopeland Windmill, Millisle.
answer_text: The Business Case is still being finalised. Officials in Historic Environment Division (HED) have gathered data to test options:Engage USEL to operate the site;Contract with an alternative operator; orClose and repurpose the facility for alternative use.  The Business Case is taking into account the previous investment in the Windmill and, alongside the heritage merits, the wider DFC objectives involved in the promotion of and support for employment for those with disabilities. The preferred option has yet to be established. A decision may remain subject to confirmation of budget for 24/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department’s No More Silos action plan was in line with recommendations contained in the Bengoa report.
answer_text: The No More Silos (NMS) Action Plan was published in October 2020 in response to the COVD-19 pandemic.  The aim of the plan was to implement quickly some of the emerging findings from the then ongoing Urgent and Emergency Care Review, to ensure that urgent and emergency care services across primary and secondary care could be maintained and improved in an environment that was safe for patients and staff. The NMS Action Plan was developed in a way that was consistent with the recommendations made in the Bengoa Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reinstate funding for specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse.
answer_text: On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse would not be withdrawn. I provided reassurance regarding my ongoing commitment to the importance of the provision of specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse.I acknowledged that the current contract with Nexus was due to end automatically on 31 March 2024 and that there had been ongoing under performance issues. However, in order to avoid a gap in provision in services for new people coming forward, I approved a further extension of 12 months to this contract to allow further discussions to occur to map out future sustainable arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the regeneration of Queen’s Parade, Bangor.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the pay and grading review for non-teaching staff.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with the Education Authority to urgently address queries in relation to the Pay & Grading review business case.  I have asked them to complete this work as quickly as possible as I am committed to ensuring that support staff in the education sector are paid at a fair level for the important work they do. There are significant costs associated with the Pay & Grading review and my Department will need additional funding from the Executive in order to implement its recommendations.  While funding for the review was not provided as part of the recent financial package from HM Treasury, the Finance Minister has advised that this issue can be revisited as part of the Budget 2024-25 process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will conduct a pay review for interpreters employed by Health and Social Care NI.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will however be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  There have been a number of correspondences to my office regarding this issue and as such I have asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review this matter.I will endeavour to do the best I can to come to a suitable resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact the new Minor Injuries Unit at the Ulster Hospital has had on waiting times in the Emergency Department.
answer_text: Since opening in September 2023, the Minor Injury Unit in the Ulster has seen over 10,000 patients, with only 5 complaints.  The Trust has had 9 formally logged compliments and the patient experience surveys have delivered excellent feedback, from those who have used the service, with over 97% recommending it.As part of the consultation process, the Trust will be engaging in a formal assessment of the changes to its Minor Injury Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many complaints the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has received in relation to the Minor Injuries Unit at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The new enhanced Minor Injury Unit opened on the Ulster hospital site on 6 September 2023.Since then the Trust has treated 10,000 patients there and received only 5 complaints. In the same period, the Trust has received 9 formal compliments concerning the Enhanced Minor Injury Service.Patient surveys have received excellent feedback on the service with over 97% of patients, who have used the Minor Injury Unit, recommending it.  Patients have an opportunity to share their experiences of health and care via the Care Opinion website and the Trust welcomes those views so that it can further develop its service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timescale for the delivery of a new building for the Urgent Care Centre at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The new Urgent Care Centre (UCC)  and associated new X Ray Department are to be co-located underneath the Ulster Hospital Acute Services Block Emergency Department, within existing vacant shell space.  Works have commenced on site and the UCC and X-Ray Department are to be completed in late 2024/2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he intends deliver a new school building for Priory Integrated College, Holywood, given that Fresh Start funding is no longer available to them.
answer_text: I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when planning is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support this bid for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he intends deliver a new school building for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School given that Fresh Start funding is no longer available to them.
answer_text: I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when planning is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support this bid for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent investment announcement, to detail the road maintenance schemes which will be taken forward in North Down.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development and delivery of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact bursaries provided for courses that lead to qualified teacher status in England, have on enrolments in Northern Ireland, where such bursaries are not available.
answer_text: My Department does not currently operate any bursary schemes for ITE programmes in Northern Ireland as (unlike other parts of the United Kingdom); there has traditionally been an oversupply in the overall number of teachers in Northern Ireland.We are, however, aware of recent concerns about teacher supply issues, particularly in some post-primary subjects. We recognise the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic teacher workforce planning. My Department therefore plans to engage with key stakeholders to understand better any workforce shortages and gaps. While consideration may be given to the introduction of ITE bursary schemes to address specific teacher shortages, such schemes would have to be tightly targeted and would remain subject to available funding.It is recognised that the existence of bursaries in England could attract individuals who might otherwise have considered undertaking their ITE programme in Northern Ireland. However, teacher supply issues are multifaceted, and it is essential to note that there are many factors which determine students’ decisions on whether to apply for an ITE course in Northern Ireland.  It should also be noted that the ITE courses provided in Northern Ireland do not lead to Qualified Teacher Status, rather they lead to qualifications which meet the requirements of the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland’s qualification rules (as set out in GTCNI Guidance 01/2015 (Revised March 2017)).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why bursaries are not provided for courses that lead to qualified teacher status in Northern Ireland, as is the case in England.
answer_text: My Department does not currently operate any bursary schemes for ITE programmes in Northern Ireland as (unlike other parts of the United Kingdom); there has traditionally been an oversupply in the overall number of teachers in Northern Ireland.We are, however, aware of recent concerns about teacher supply issues, particularly in some post-primary subjects. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to introduce bursaries for courses that lead to qualified teacher status, as is the case in England.
