question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide a school crossing patrol urgently for Grange Park Primary School, Bangor, following a recent road traffic collision involving a pupil.
answer_text: I was concerned to hear about this incident and I hope the pupil involved is recovering well. The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol Scheme (SCP) Scheme and it has advised me that it has been in contact with Grange Park Primary School in relation to this incident. The EA must undertake an assessment of any potential SCP locations in line with Road Safety GB ROSPA, School Crossing Patrol Services Guidelines. I am informed that the EA will liaise with the school, and required statutory bodies, in undertaking an assessment and if the location meets the requirements, it will progress to recruitment of an SCP as matter of urgency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has made deputations to HM Treasury regarding reducing the VAT rate for hospitality businesses in Northern Ireland, given the increase in running costs.
answer_text: VAT is an Excepted Matter and responsibility for any reduction in VAT is an issue for the British Treasury to decide upon. Over recent years, my Department has repeatedly raised with the British Treasury, the negative impact the higher level of VAT on tourism & hospitality is having locally compared to the lower rate that applies in the South.  My officials continue to highlight the adverse impacts of the taxation system locally in their regular meetings with Treasury counterparts.  At our first meeting, I pressed the Chief Secretary to the Treasury that a new Fiscal Framework, which provides funding based on need and a basis for increased fiscal powers for the Executive, should be an immediate priority. This will better enable the Executive to sustainably fund public services and provide support for both citizens and businesses during the ongoing cost of living / business crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what financial support he intends to provide to hospitality businesses who are struggling with increased running costs.
answer_text: My department is taking steps to support businesses facing high energy costs through delivery of the Executive’s ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ Strategy.  I also intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to take this agenda forward. The level of VAT is also an issue for hospitality businesses and the Department of Finance has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impacts on hospitality of high levels of VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to installing traffic lights at the junction of Seapark Road, Holywood, and the A2.
answer_text: The A2 is a strategic trunk road route that carries some 38,000 vehicles per day and is a priority for traffic progression.  I am advised that there are currently no safety concerns at the junction of Seapark Road, Holywood with the A2.  Due to these factors there are no current plans to install traffic signals at this junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has plans to introduce further Residents' Parking Schemes.
answer_text: A review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the review report, which will establish the policy for such schemes going forward, will be published when I have considered its findings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale on the erection of a memorial to victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse.
answer_text: The issue of a memorial is sensitive for victims and survivors. Many of whom have different views how this should be progressed.  The late Sir Anthony Hart recognised this in the Inquiry report.Our officials have engaged with victims’ and survivors’ group representatives since March 2022 on this matter and have considered advice from the Commissioner. A cross-departmental sub-group was established to examine ways of meeting as many different views as possible. Ministers will shortly be meeting victims’ and survivors’ representative groups, and will consider all proposals to implement this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale of the appointment of a Northern Ireland Climate Commissioner.
answer_text: The NI Climate Commissioner will play a prominent role in overseeing and reporting on the commitments made of all Departments in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.TEO officials have taken forward significant policy development work on options for establishing the office of the Climate Commissioner.We will consider these options in the weeks ahead to determine the most appropriate way to progress with the establishment of the office, and the appointment of an NI Climate Commissioner.The timescale for the appointment of a Commissioner will be established once the necessary duties and powers of the office have been agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work of his Department to progress the Bangor Waterfront Development within the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: My Officials are working in collaboration with colleagues in the Ards and North Down Borough Council to deliver the Bangor Waterfront Belfast Region City Deal project. My Department  has approved the Outline Business Case, and issued the Contract for Funding for the project in January 2023. Work is currently progressing well to appoint design teams and further develop the proposals to inform a Final Business Case. My Officials continue to work on the various City Deal Project-and-Programme Board governance structures to support and oversee the delivery of this transformational project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the street light repair at Twisel Bridge, Holywood.
answer_text: My officials advise me that the street light in question is mounted on an NIE owned pole and can only be accessed and repaired by an NIE engineer.  Due to the storm damage caused by the recent inclement weather, NIE engineers have been prioritising the repair of the electrical network.  However, I can advise that NIE have repaired the fault and the light is now operational.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce parking restrictions at the junction of Belfast Road and Abbey Ring, Holywood.
answer_text: I can advise that waiting restrictions such as double yellow lines, may be introduced on the road network where parking is causing an obstruction to other traffic, resulting in progression issues, in the form of queues, tailbacks or difficulties making turning movements.My Department has previously assesses the Belfast Road / Abbey Ring junction for 15m corner restrictions in the form of double yellow lines and given the road width at the location and no history of traffic progression issues, it is not considered appropriate to introduce permanent restrictions at this junction.Concerns about vehicles which are parked inappropriately which cause an obstruction or danger to any other road users should be reported to the to the police who is the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reverse his Department's decision to close St. Anne’s Primary School, Donaghadee.
answer_text: St Anne’s Primary School closed with effect from 31 October 2023, and I do not intend to reverse the decision.  I understand that the Member will be aware that the decisions to approve closure and not to approve the transformation of the school to controlled integrated status were the subject of legal challenge.  Leave for Judicial Review was refused.  In his rationale for refusing leave, the presiding judge concluded that there were no arguable grounds to the challenge which had a realistic prospect of success.I and my Department will continue to encourage, facilitate and support the development of integrated education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail all Housing Executive refurbishment schemes planned in North Down over the next three years.
answer_text: Housing Executive refurbishment schemes planned in North Down over the next three years are an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his correspondence with Ards and North Down Borough Council regarding the revitalisation of the steps on Bangor seafront, between Queens Parade and Princetown Road.
answer_text: Ards and North Down Borough Council contacted my Department in October 2022 and March 2023 seeking agreement to the Council’s proposal to undertake work to the steps on Bangor seafront which the Department accepted.  My Department has no record of any more recent correspondence or discussion on the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce parking restrictions on (i) Seapark Road; and (ii) Ballymenoch Park, Holywood.
answer_text: Officials will assess the need for additional restrictions within this area from May to September during the period when the Department is aware of reports of inconsiderate parking. If a change is proposed it will be considered for inclusion in the work programme for the next financial year, which is presented to council each year and then published online.Concerns about vehicles which are parked inappropriately which cause an obstruction or danger to any other road users should be reported to the to the police who is the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for the timescale to install a pedestrian crossing on Bloomfield Road, Bangor.
answer_text: The installation of a new pedestrian crossing on the Bloomfield Road, Bangor is listed within the Ards and North Down Borough Council Annual Report 2023/24 and the work is anticipated to commence on-site before the end of March 2024. My officials are working to confirm the exact timing for the delivery of this scheme which will be agreed in consultation with the Department’s contractor, taking into account the availability of resources and capacity.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the work of their departmental Children’s Champion in the last three years.
answer_text: Key work undertaken by the departmental Children’s Champion over the last three years includes:Initial engagement with the NI Youth Forum and the NI Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY) on issues such as establishing a regular mechanism for youth engagement on policy areas within the remit of TEO. Supporting implementation of, and promoting the use of, Child Rights Impact Assessments (CRIAs) in the development of any new policy.Development of Child Friendly questionnaires for gathering outcomes data on Good Relations Programmes.Championing the participation of young people in policy development. Across TEO, youth consultation has played a key role in a number of policy developments including the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy, the Covid Recovery Plan, the Refugee Integration Strategy, and the current review of the T:BUC Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many prosecutions his Department has pursued for the unauthorised erection of flags on street furniture, in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not have the powers to progress prosecutions, this would be for PSNI to progress.My Department has not made any complaints to PSNI against individuals in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Reawakening the Ulster Folk Museum project.
answer_text: The Reawakening the Ulster Folk Museum project is an operational matter for National Museums NI.		You should, therefore, direct your question to National Museums NI at info@nationalmuseumsni.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many flags his Department has removed from street furniture in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.With the exception of a few banners which presented danger to road users, my Department has not removed any flags from street furniture in the last five years.Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many Penalty Charge Notices have been issued by his Department in Helen’s Bay, Co. Down, over the last five years.
answer_text: There were 560 Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued in Helen’s Bay (Church Road, Fort Road, and Grey Point) between 18 February 2021 and 19 February 2024.As per the Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule, older PCN data is not retained.Helen’s Bay falls under the Bangor area for the reporting of PCNs.  This data is regularly published through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@department-for-infrastructure/penalty-charge-notice-data

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people are on the social housing waiting list in North Down; and (ii) how many on the list are considered homeless.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is best placed to directly answer questions, which relate to wholly operational matters under its remit. The Housing Executive has a dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk in relation to your specific query.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
To ask the Minister of Health for his plans to address the funding shortfall faced by community pharmacies.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population.I have received representation from pharmacy contractors and from their representative body, Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI).An urgent meeting was held this week to with representatives of CPNI to consider the funding issues. I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the UK Government's financial settlement, published on 13 February 2024, will have any impact on previous funding commitments arising from the Fresh Start Agreement for shared and integrated education.
answer_text: £150 million of the Fresh Start funding package for shared education and shared housing has been re-packaged by the British Government into the funding accompanying the restoration of the Executive.Some shared and integrated education projects that were initially being considered to progress using Fresh Start funding have not yet become contractually committed. Fresh Start funding will no longer be available to take forward these projects.It will now a matter for the Executive to determine allocations for departments, including Capital allocations to the Department of Education in its Budget for 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans for new build social housing in North Down in the next five years.
answer_text: The information you have requested regarding plans for new build social housing in North Down over the next five years, is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to Northern Ireland Housing Executive at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many social houses in North Down have been purchased through the House Sales Scheme in the last three years.
answer_text: Unfortunately, this information is not available in the format requested, details are not recorded by Council area. Please see figures below listing the number of Housing Association properties sold under the House Sales Scheme for the requested years.YEARHOUSE SALES BY HOUSING ASSOCIATIONS2019/2020972020/2021892021/20221602022/2023Not yet availableThe Housing Executive is best placed to directly answer questions, which relate to wholly operational matters under its remit. The Housing Executive has a dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk in relation to your specific query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) date and time; and (ii) volume of untreated sewage discharge from Brompton Road Wastewater Pumping Station into Belfast Lough, over the last three years.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress for a new building for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: This project is in the final stage of design with a planning application submitted in July 2022.  Procurement of a contractor is programmed to commence following receipt of planning permission, subject to capital funding being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement he has had with the Crown Estate in relation to permissions needed to progress the redevelopment of Queens Parade, Bangor.
answer_text: My officials, in the Department’s Regional Development Office, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work collaboratively with the developer, Bangor Marine, to progress the regeneration of Queen’s Parade. Bangor Marine and Ards and North Down Borough Council are working together to secure the approvals required for the developer’s proposals that fall on the section of the scheme belonging to the Crown Estate, currently the Marine Gardens car park. Given that the negotiations between the parties are of a commercial nature, I have not had any engagement with the Crown Estate. I am hopeful that agreement will be reached in the near future and work can proceed later this year to unlock the regeneration potential of this key City Centre site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce a part-time 20mph speed limit zone outside Grange Park Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with the Department’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070The program to date has been rolled out in two tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for the timescale to install the pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, between the Groomsport Road roundabout and the Donaghadee Road roundabout.
answer_text: The installation of a new Puffin Crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor is identified in the Ards and North Down Borough Council Annual Report 2023/24 for the work to be completed during the 2024/25 financial year. Link to the report; Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2023/2024 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The exact timing for the delivery of this scheme will be agreed in consultation with the Department’s contractor, taking into account the availability of resources and capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the works to address the recurrent flooding on Bridge Road, Helen’s Bay.
answer_text: Following extensive investigation works at Bridge Road, Helen’s Bay, officials have determined that the recent flooding was attributed to damaged drainage within private property. A temporary solution was installed in December 2023 which has been successful in preventing a reoccurrence of flooding during recent prolonged periods of rain. My Department is currently taking forward a design of a new drainage system to substantively resolve the matter and it is hoped this will be delivered in the 2024/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to address the recurrent flooding on Rugby Avenue, Bangor.
answer_text: Officials in my department have reviewed the recent flooding at Rugby Avenue, Bangor and consider the cause to be a build-up of leaves on gully gratings that prevented the surface water from entering the drainage system. When removed, the drainage system worked as designed and the flooding cleared.  My officials will continue to monitor the system and take action as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to address the recurrent flooding at Ballysallagh Road Bridge, Bangor.
answer_text: The recent flooding at Ballysallagh Road Bridge, Bangor has been attributed to a damaged section of drainage that is remote from the road.  Repair work is currently being undertaken and is nearing completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5482/22-27, when the new provider for the Promote Day Opportunities Centre will be confirmed.
answer_text: Action Mental Health informed the South Eastern HSC Trust (SEHSCT) in November 2023 that it would not be renewing its contract to deliver the Promote Day Opportunity Service in Bangor after 31st March 2024. Aware of the impact this decision by Action Mental Health would have on the service users and their families, SEHSCT immediately began to find an alternative provider. A new provider has been secured and the Trust are hopeful that transition arrangements will progress imminently.  A plan is in place to ensure that this transition will be as seamless as possible.SEHSCT continues to meet with Action Mental Health as they are responsible for engaging with their current staff to keep them updated on the position.  A further meeting between SEHSCT and AMH Promote families has been arranged for week commencing 23 February 2024 to provide an update on the current position.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale on the appointment of the (i) Irish Language Commissioner; and (ii) Commissioner for the Ulster-Scots and Ulster British Tradition.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of this Act for which it is responsible.This will be in-line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure his Department publishes up to date waiting times for red-flagged breast clinic referrals.
answer_text: As part of the Cancer Waiting Times publications which are released quarterly by the Department, the length of wait from an urgent GP referral for suspect breast cancer to first appointment with a breast cancer specialist is monitored and reported on in figure 3 and table 5.  The most recent publication was published on 11th January 2024.  This can be found at the following link:Northern Ireland waiting time statistics: cancer waiting times July - September 2023 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).The number of referrals received by HSC Trusts for suspect breast cancer has been temporarily removed from this publication due to data quality issues currently under review and it is hoped that this data will be available in the next release of this publication on Thursday 28th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for red flag breast clinic referrals, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As part of the Cancer Waiting Times publications which are released quarterly by the Department, the length of wait from an urgent GP referral for suspect breast cancer to first appointment with a breast cancer specialist is monitored and reported on.  The most recent publication was published on 11th January 2024.  This can be found at the following link:Northern Ireland waiting time statistics: cancer waiting times July - September 2023 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).The next publication will be issued on Thursday 28th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Commissioner for Victims and Survivors advice to government regarding the needs of those bereaved due to Troubles-related incidents.
answer_text: We are extremely sympathetic to the issues affecting bereaved victims and survivors, and we are keen to address their needs and acknowledge the ongoing loss felt by many.We have not yet had an opportunity to consider the Commissioner’s advice paper, but we are committed to doing so as part of the development of the new Strategy for Victims and Survivors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to ensure red-flagged breast clinic referrals are meeting their target of receiving an appointment within two weeks.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for breast assessments. Non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts in 2023/24 to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity.  In addition, the Department continues to facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times through the inter-Trust transfer of patients. Bringing our waiting lists to an acceptable level across a range of specialties, including breast services, is a long term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment and additional staffing. The scale at which I will be able to tackle the unacceptable waiting lists will be greatly influenced by the outcome of the current budgetary discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work will commence to repair structural defects on Moss Road, Millisle.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.  Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his plans for the future of Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre and ability café.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of my Department’s state care monuments to local communities and their role in attracting visitors. I am equally aware of the importance of supporting people with disabilities to move closer, into and sustain employment through a range or pre-employment and in-work support provision.Both of these elements of my Department’s responsibilities come together with regard to Ballycopeland Windmill, a state care monument where Ulster Supported Employment Limited (USEL) manage the facilities including the Ability Cafe.The challenge of finding a viable operating model for the site, within a difficult budgetary environment, is one that I have instructed my officials to examine further. They are assembling a business case to assess a number of options.I anticipate that this work will be complete by the end of February. Decisions following the review may at that stage remain subject to confirmation of budget for 24/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when Grey Point Fort, Helen’s Bay, will reopen to the public.
answer_text: It is with regret that, due to DfC’s currently constrained ability as a result of budgetary pressures to invest in its State Care Monuments, I am not in a position at the present time to advise when Grey Point Fort will be reopened. I appreciate the closure of Grey Point Fort has been disappointing for many in the local community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure the Domestic and Sexual Abuse helpline and counselling services for children and young people are adequately funded.
answer_text: My Department jointly funds the 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline with the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities.  The Helpline is a confidential, Freephone service available 24/7.My Department jointly with the PSNI, also funds the Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre, which delivers 24/7 support and services to children, young people, women and men who have been sexually abused, assaulted or raped, whether this has happened in the past or more recently.  The Rowan can provide information and advice on accessing other specialist support services and counselling which are available from a range of statutory, voluntary and community organisations.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust provides, on a regional basis, investigation and therapeutic support for children suspected or confirmed to be victims of sexual abuse through the Child Care Centre. In addition, there are a range of therapeutic services in all five HSC Trusts aimed at specific groups of children and young people. My Department is continuing to invest in these services in the context of the roll-out of the Framework for Integrated Therapeutic Care.Finally, my Department contracts Barnardo’s NI to provide therapeutic support for children and young people who are potentially vulnerable to child sexual exploitation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timescale on the revitalisation of Grey Point Fort, Helen’s Bay.
answer_text: Our plans to revitalise the Fort are currently on hold due to my Department’s significantly constrained ability, in light of budgetary pressures, to invest in our State Care Monuments. A site conservation management plan has been completed and included feedback from a public consultation forum in 2022. A feasibility study will be the next step to inform the future development of the site - which will include an assessment of potential operating models.I appreciate the closure of Grey Point Fort has been disappointing for many in the local community. At this time, I can only assure you of our commitment to progress planning for the fort once funding becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support his Department is providing to Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College to transform to Integrated status.
answer_text: The statutory process for any eligible school to transform to integrated status begins with the parental ballot.  My Department paid for the parental ballot for Rathmore Primary School and Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College.  Following a successful ballot, work continues to enable a case for change and Development Proposal to be published, which is the current stage for both schools.  My Department has produced guidance, Integration Works, setting out the steps to be taken before and after the statutory process begins – early community and parental engagement underpinning successful transformations.  My Department funds both the Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) and the Education Authority (EA) to provide on the ground support to schools considering transformation – both organisations are sources of operational expertise and can therefore provide such support throughout the transformation process.   NICIE has advised that, in the case of Rathmore Primary School, two Senior Development Officers and two Development Officers have worked, and continue to work, with the school.  In the case of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College, two Senior Development Officers and three Development Officers are providing this support.  This is in addition to EA officers in the Shared Education and Sectoral Support Team, and Area Planning providing support e.g. for Transformation Action Plans (mechanism by which a school designs their vision for the transformed school, involving a self-evaluation process and a roadmap for the way ahead) and in preparing the cases for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support his Department is providing to Rathmore Primary School, Bangor, to transform to Integrated status.
answer_text: The statutory process for any eligible school to transform to integrated status begins with the parental ballot.  My Department paid for the parental ballot for Rathmore Primary School and Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College.  Following a successful ballot, work continues to enable a case for change and Development Proposal to be published, which is the current stage for both schools.  My Department has produced guidance, Integration Works, setting out the steps to be taken before and after the statutory process begins – early community and parental engagement underpinning successful transformations.  My Department funds both the Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) and the Education Authority (EA) to provide on the ground support to schools considering transformation – both organisations are sources of operational expertise and can therefore provide such support throughout the transformation process.   NICIE has advised that, in the case of Rathmore Primary School, two Senior Development Officers and two Development Officers have worked, and continue to work, with the school.  In the case of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College, two Senior Development Officers and three Development Officers are providing this support.  This is in addition to EA officers in the Shared Education and Sectoral Support Team, and Area Planning providing support e.g. for Transformation Action Plans (mechanism by which a school designs their vision for the transformed school, involving a self-evaluation process and a roadmap for the way ahead) and in preparing the cases for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reinstate core grant funding for Women’s Aid Federation Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 5744/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide funding required to secure the future of Ballycopeland Windmill Visitor Centre and Ability Cafe.
answer_text: I have directed my officials to bring forward recommendations on how best to maintain access to this important heritage asset and the café. I understand the significance of the Windmill and café to the local community and its importance in attracting visitors to the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the rationale behind the decision to close the Ballycopeland Windmill Visitor Centre and Ability Cafe.
answer_text: My Department has not taken a decision to close the Ballycopeland Windmill Visitor Centre and Ability Café. Ulster Supported Employment Limited (USEL) currently manages the site, and the Department has worked during 2023/24 to attempt to address a subsidy request to support USEL’s continued operation from 2024/25 to 2026/27. To this point, officials in the Department have not been able to confirm this subsidy due to budget uncertainty beyond this financial year. Unfortunately, this has meant that USEL cannot renew their Service Level Agreement with the Department to continue their Ballycopeland operation beyond 31 March.I have directed my officials as soon as possible to bring forward recommendations on how best to maintain access to this important heritage asset and the café. I understand the significance of the Windmill and café to the local community and its importance in attracting visitors to the area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.
answer_text: TEO officials are currently working on the administrative preparations and the documentation required to begin the recruitment process for a new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.The Commission for Victims and Survivors is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI) and, as such, the appointment process must comply with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments. We hope to launch the appointment competition soon. It is estimated that it could take up to 6 months to complete the recruitment competition.As a body corporate the Commission continues in legal existence even in the absence of a Commissioner. The Chief Executive Officer of CVS will now be responsible for the day-to-day business of the Commission. CVS will continue to operate on the basis of the work programme which was agreed by the previous Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will reinstate the double yellow lines on Brompton Road, Bangor, which were removed during resurfacing works in 2023.
answer_text: I am advised there was no legislation for the double yellow line road markings which were on Brompton Road, Bangor prior to it being resurfaced, therefore new legislation will need to be created before the road markings can be reinstated. I can confirm that the Department is content to progress with a ‘No Waiting’ restriction at this location and that the legislative process has commenced.  However, it must be noted that this legislative process will require public consultation and can take some time.   Details of this and other planned legislation for the introduction of Waiting Restrictions for the area can be found in the Ards and North Down Borough Council Annual Report 2023/24 in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will resume the Happy Healthy Minds programme.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will direct NI Water to remove the fence erected at Seacourt Pumping Station, Bangor.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timeline for the implementation of the draft Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: We are committed to bringing forward the draft Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls for consideration by the Executive Committee as soon as possible. Our officials are liaising with Departmental colleagues and members of the Co-design group regarding the outcome of the public consultation, with a view to submitting the draft Strategic Framework to the Executive in coming weeks. Preparatory work to enable effective delivery is continuing, as outlined in the Foundational Action Plan, and a draft Implementation Plan (for the first two years of what is a proposed seven-year Strategy) is being developed for consideration by Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to address the waiting time for results of cervical screening tests in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Since primary HPV (human papillomavirus) testing was fully introduced into the cervical screening pathway on 11th December 2023 the turnaround time for the issuing of results has improved. The results of cervical screening samples which test negative for HPV are being issued to the woman’s GP within 2 weeks. Where screening samples test positive for HPV, cytology assessment is also carried out, so the issuing of results will take a few weeks longer.For those women whose tests were taken prior to 11th December 2023, plans are now in place to clear the backlog and release results over the following weeks.  It should be noted that the slides in the backlog had already been tested for HPV and those slides that tested positive were prioritised for cytology assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure that Promote Day Opportunities Centre in Bangor has sufficient funding to deliver services.
answer_text: I am aware that Action Mental Health (AMH) Promote provides a valuable service to enable people with learning disabilities to access diverse day activities and develop greater levels of independence. AMH informed SEHSCT in November 2023 that it would not be renewing its contract to deliver the Promote Day Opportunity Service in Bangor after 31 March 2024, due to wider financial pressures.SEHSCT have worked closely with the provider since June 2023 to better understand the challenges faced and to identify options that would enable continued delivery of services. Unfortunately, it is not feasible to continue the service with the current operating model.SEHSCT have identified an alternative provider to continue delivery of the service at the current site in Bangor. Throughout this process there has been and will continue to be engagement with service users, families and the providers.The Department remains committed to the expansion of day opportunities for people with learning disabilities. Throughout 2023, the Department has led on an exercise to finalise a draft service model for learning disability, which includes several outcomes and actions to expand day services and short breaks in Northern Ireland. The current draft has been shared with key stakeholders to inform the next phase of this work and wider consultation.In parallel to this work, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group are working closely with Trusts to undertake a financial review of adult learning disability services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the 16 new Nurture Groups being established in primary schools under the Fair Start Action Plan.
answer_text: All 16 new Nurture Groups established under the Fair Start Action Plan are operational. In terms of the related capital works at each of these Primary Schools, nine have been completed in full, two are due for completion by the end of this month and five are waiting planning permission for modular accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether teachers still have to pay an annual registration fee to the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland, despite the Council’s dissolution.
answer_text: GTCNI has yet to be dissolved and continues to exist as a Non Departmental Public Body (NDPB) of DE, currently operating under direct Departmental oversight. Its staff continue to deliver their statutory function of registering new teachers entering the profession and maintaining the registration of the existing teaching workforce. Registration is seen as an important safeguard, helping to protect the quality of the teaching workforce in our schools.The NDPB continues to be funded through the annual fee paid by all registered teachers and these fees will continue to be collected until legislation to dissolve and replace GTCNI can be brought through the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will remove restrictions on the type of apprenticeship with funding available, for people aged 25 and over.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will allocate additional money to the Mental Health Support Fund given the high levels of interest and over subscription.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland has informed teachers that their contact details have potentially been leaked following a reported data breach.
answer_text: The data breach which was reported in the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland’s (GTCNI) 2020-21 Annual Report and Accounts occurred in March 2021.It did not involve the GTCNI’s teacher registration database and was restricted to the release of information on a very small number of teachers (<5) all of whom were immediately informed.The GTCNI also informed the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) who investigated the breach.  The ICO concluded that the remedial actions taken by the GTCNI were appropriate and robust and it did not need to take any further action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why there is no principal release time for teaching pre-school principals.
answer_text: While nursery schools do not currently receive a specific principal release time allocation, these schools do receive additional funding which can be used to facilitate release time.  Nursery schools currently receive a higher 'per pupil' funding rate as a result of the higher Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) weighting factor of 1.452 as compared to 1.08 for a primary school pupil.  The additional funding provides flexibility for nursery principals to use as required, including engaging substitute teaching cover to release time for the principal to carry out administrative duties.  The issue of nursery principal release time has been raised through the Review of Workload Impact on School Leaders and DE officials are engaging with the teachers’ unions regarding this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the public consultation on the functions delivered by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The public consultation ran from 13 June to 30 September 2022 and a detailed analysis of the responses received was published in October 2022. A link to the analysis can be found here. Consultation on Functions Delivered by GTCNI - Analysis of ResponsesA majority of those who responded agreed that all of GTCNI's current functions should be maintained and that the establishment of a new regulatory body for the profession should be the preferred way forward. This will require new primary legislation. Since then, the Department has undertaken further engagement with the profession, with other UK and ROI teaching regulators and with other stakeholders. These discussions have allowed us to refine our thinking on the form a replacement for GTCNI should take. An update on our current thinking was shared with every teacher on 05 February 2024. As part of this process, we are seeking further feedback from the profession, prior to officials presenting options and recommendations for a GTCNI replacement Bill for Ministerial consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why pre-school educational settings cannot access the C2K network.
answer_text: The Education Authority signed a contract in December 2023 with a new Strategic Partner and School Management System provider that will transform the Educational Technology provision in all schools and include, for the first time, provision for all statutory nursery schools. This system will be rolled out under the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) programme.  It should be noted that all statutory nursery teachers have received Microsoft Surface Pro 7 Plus devices in line with what their colleagues have received in Primary, Post Primary, Special and Education other than at school (EOTAS) centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 4308/22-27, (i) what plans his Department has to engage with the community and voluntary sector regarding the priorities contained within the draft sexual health improvement action plan; and (ii) whether a budget has been identified to implement the new plan.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what assistance his Department is providing to businesses for (i) energy bills; and (ii) rates, in the context of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will commission an independent review of the Rating System in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are no current plans for an independent review of the rating system. It would be a matter for any incoming Executive to decide if there was merit in any such review, or if this would constitute best use of resources in light of past work in this area, in particular the 2019 Business Rates Review which has already resulted in several policy changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why Health Visitors cannot share information with primary and pre-schools to assist with identifying Special Educational Needs; and (ii) what work is being undertaken by his Department to allow this.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering how information sharing between health and education professionals can be improved to deliver better outcomes for children as part of our response to the “A Fair Start” report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why Child Development Services cannot share information with primary and pre-schools to assist with identifying Special Educational Needs; and (ii) what work is being undertaken by his Department to allow this.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response to AQW 5150/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact repeated flooding of Newtownards Road, Bangor has on the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service to deliver its services, in the context of the flooding occurring adjacent to their premises.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the enrolment figures for Further Education Colleges in the last five years.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for a timescale for her Department’s implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act; and (ii) what preparation is being undertaken to ensure the successful implementation of the Act.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Bill completed its passage through the Assembly on 24 March 2022 and received Royal Assent on 12 May 2022.  Preparations are currently being made for the enactment of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Northern Ireland Act from May 2024.A cross-sector working group, led by the EA, has been established to scope the operational impact of this change in legislation.  The group will ensure that the employing authorities across the education sector are prepared for full compliance with the legal requirements of the Equality Commission Northern Ireland’s Fair Employment Code of Practice.  The Equality Commission has published guidance for schools which may be downloaded from the Commission’s website: School Teachers – A Guide on Recruitment and Selection and a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance will be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the recommendations from the Review of the Use of Restraint and Seclusion in Educational Settings.
answer_text: The Review of the Use of Restraint and Seclusion in Educational Settings made six recommendations.In terms of recommendation 1, I plan to consider this matter further with my officials before determining the appropriate next steps. Recommendations 2, 3 and 5 relate to the publication of statutory guidance on the use of restrictive and supportive practices in educational settings. Department of Education officials engaged with a wide range of stakeholders to co-design draft statutory guidance in accordance with the report recommendations, and a public consultation on the draft guidance ran from 5th September to 10th November 2023. The responses to the consultation are currently being analysed.  Subject to my approval, the consultation report will be published on the Departmental website in due course and I will consider the next steps to be taken thereafter.On recommendation 4, officials continue to work with Health colleagues to develop regional guidance on pupils’ medication and healthcare needs including the use of equipment in educational settings. It is anticipated that guidance in relation to medication needs will be ready for publication during summer term.  Recommendation 6, which relates to the Department commissioning a periodic review and publishing a report on the use of restrictive and supportive practices in educational settings, can only be progressed once the statutory guidance is in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why recommendations 5, 18, 21, 29, 30, 31, 32, 34, 35, 36, and 39 of the Independent Review of Integrated Education have not been approved and actioned by her Department.
answer_text: Further to the then Minister’s response to AQW 4074/22-27, it was a Ministerial decision that these recommendations were not approved therefore they would not be actioned by my Department.In relation to the reasons the specific recommendations were not approved:5 - there are already a range of surveys undertaken about the experiences of children and young people, including attitudinal information, and this is capable of being analysed by school type;  18 - the Education and Training Inspectorate self-evaluation framework for schools is not intended to be sector specific;21 - the Association of Principals and Teachers in Integrated Schools – APTIS – is an established cross-phase association that meets regularly;29, 30, 31, 32, and 39 – Fresh Start capital funding has specific and set parameters and the funding is fully committed;34, 35, 36 – the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) runs an Excellence in Integrated Education Award for integrated schools; additionally, when its views were sought in 2020 by the then Minister, NICIE advised it did not consider the kitemark to be the most effective means of celebrating integrated success.  These views formed part of the overall consideration of the recommendations contained in the Independent Review of Integrated Education report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether students registered at Further Education Colleges will receive a hardship payment to help with the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a Covid-19 booster vaccination will be offered to all Health and Social Care staff, including Care Home and Pharmacy staff.
answer_text: The COVID-19 vaccination programme has followed the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccinations and Immunisations (JCVI) and for the autumn programme, JCVI have recommended frontline health and social care workers should be offered a booster vaccination.  Therefore all frontline staff in Trusts as well as frontline staff in independent contractors such as GPs, dentists and community pharmacists are eligible to receive a booster dose via a Trust led clinic or at a participating community pharmacy.  In addition, all care home staff will be offered a booster vaccination while community pharmacy teams visit every care home in Northern Ireland to offer vaccination to all residents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the rollout of the Covid-19 booster vaccination programme this winter; and (ii) which groups can avail of a Covid-19 booster vaccination.
answer_text: The autumn COVID-19 booster vaccination programme was launched in September and remains on course for completion in December, in line with advice from the Joint Committee on Vaccinations and Immunisations (JCVI).  The number of doses given to date can viewed on the vaccination dashboard - NI Covid-19 Vaccinations Dashboard - COVID-19 (Coronavirus) Northern Ireland.   Just over one million people in NI are eligible and the programme is being implemented using a combination of GPs, community pharmacies and Trust vaccination clinics with GPs expected to administer the majority of vaccinations by inviting their eligible patients to attend a vaccination clinic.JCVI recommended that for the 2022 autumn booster programme the following groups should be offered vaccination:Residents in care homes and staff working in care homes.Frontline Health and Social Care Workers All adults aged 50 years and over (this includes those turning 50 years of age by 31 March 2023)Persons aged 5 to 49 years in a clinical risk group - including pregnant women Persons aged 5 to 49 years who are household contacts of people with immunosuppression or carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why some substitute teachers have not received payment in October 2022 for work completed in September 2022; and (ii) what steps she is taking to address this situation.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my answer to AQW 4860/22-27 submitted on 21 October, which set out the reasons why some supply teachers did not receive timely payment for some or all of their salary.  It also provided the position in respect of my direction for interim emergency payments to be processed for all affected teachers.  In respect of addressing outstanding issues with the new NISTR system, work continues to make further improvements and changes to system logic based on the payment difficulties encountered and on other user feedback.  This includes block bookings for individual recurrent days of the week, improvements to the sign-off process and critically, the generation of an automated payroll file.  Planned additional features will continue to be added throughout the developmental phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many substitute teachers have not had payments processed by the Northern Ireland Substitute Teacher Register and are subsequently overdue payments.
answer_text: The Department funds the Education Authority to administer the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) which is effectively a booking system enabling schools to source and engage professionally qualified teachers who have undergone a range of pre-employment checks.  On the third working day of each month, a file of approved (signed-off) bookings is extracted from the NISTR and sent to the Department for processing of salary to temporary teachers on schools’ behalf.There have been a number of issues regarding the implementation of a new NISTR system and the timely payment of temporary teachers. The Department and the EA continue to work together intensively, alongside stakeholder groups, to ensure that immediate steps are taken to ensure staff are paid for their valuable service.  In the current exceptional circumstances, particularly in light of the cost-of-living crisis, the Department has asked the EA to process interim emergency payments to all teachers impacted as a matter of urgency.Due to administrative errors in extracting the file and uploading it to the payroll system; 309 temporary and supply teachers received only partial salary on 18 October for bookings that were approved by schools and should have been paid under normal circumstances.  The EA has sincerely apologised for this error and is processing interim emergency payments to reach these teachers by 25 October.The other factor that has caused problems in the timely payment of temporary teachers relates to the fact that the appropriate approval processes at school level were not completed in line with the payroll deadline.  This prevented payment being made on the planned date for an estimated 300 teachers.  Incorrect data input in respect of hours worked also appears to have featured in the sign-off process.  The EA is contacting all relevant schools to seek confirmation of attendance, check period of service and obtain other necessary sign-off information to enable further interim emergency payments to pursued.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to increase the number and connectivity of cycle lanes in North Down.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department takes active travel very seriously and recognise the benefits of increasing the number of and connectivity of cycle lanes across the north including in North Down and while there are no immediate plans for works in the North Down area, officials will, over the coming months and years, be developing proposals across all areas and in conjunction with local councils to improve the walking, cycling and wheeling network.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether all registered University students in Northern Ireland will receive a hardship payment to help with the cost-of-living crisis.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what power his Department has to address parking on, and obstruction of, cycle lanes.
answer_text: The Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 gives my Department the power to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) when a vehicle is detected parked on a legislated Mandatory Cycle Lane. These are cycle lanes which are part of the carriageway (road) and are identified by a solid white line or by a raised traffic island.Advisory Cycle Lanes, which are identified by a broken white line, cycle tracks on footways or those separate from the carriageway, are not generally enforceable by a Traffic Attendant unless there are parking restrictions marked on the carriageway, for example yellow lines, as these would also apply to the cycle lane, track or footway.Vehicles parked causing an obstruction, where no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway, are a matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to (i) address Long Covid; and (ii) support people living with Long Covid.
answer_text: Knowledge of Long Covid/Post-COVID-19 Syndrome (PCS) is still evolving.  The condition is complex with more than one body system affected.  Individuals can have unique combinations of fluctuating symptoms.   There is no specific diagnostic test for PCS and the published evidence base for interventions specifically aimed at a curative treatment is sparse at present.  High quality research is needed to inform future treatments and models of care.   Until then, the current service is focussed on holistic assessment, exclusion of other significant conditions, identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and those who need onward referral to secondary or community services.  This is complemented by signposting to self-management resources.  As new evidence becomes available my Department will continue to review and update Long Covid services in Northern Ireland.  This will inform the service model moving forward.(ii) 	The following services to support and assist people who are living with this condition have been established in all 5 HSC Trusts.Strand 1 -  Post-COVID-19 syndrome (PCS) patients referred by primary or secondary care to a one-stop-shop MDT assessment servicePatients can be referred to the MDT assessment clinic from primary and secondary care.  Patients receive an holistic assessment to include:identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation; and, those who may need onward referral to secondary or community services and/or signposting to self-management resources.Strand 2: - Bespoke pulmonary rehabilitation / dysfunctional breathing service for patients with significant respiratory symptoms post-COVID-19The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective arrangements for outpatient rehabilitation following COVID-19 resulting in significant respiratory symptoms.  Strand 3 - Patients discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19)The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective  arrangements for the follow-up and assessment of people discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19), reflecting the known risk of long-term sequelae following a long stay in critical care.Strand 4 - Strengthening psychology support to all TrustsThe psychological impact of COVID-19 (depression, anxiety disorders, PTSD, traumatic bereavement, psychosis, risk to self and/or others) is recognised in extant national guidance.  Each Trust has been supported to recruit an additional senior psychologist, with their main role supporting strand 3, but also providing advice to staff working in strands 1 and 2.Strand 5 - Regional MDT clinical physiology, SLT and dietetic support Selected patients may need diagnostic tests by respiratory clinical physiologists, onward referral for dietetic and speech and language therapy (SLT) from the one-stop-shop MDT assessment service, the critical care follow-up and pulmonary rehabilitation strands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will amend The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 to include Relationship and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: These are historical questions from 2022 and the answer below is therefore based on the current position in respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE). Relationships and Sexuality education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age as set out in The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007. The statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and the Personal Development strand of Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level, prescribes the high-level minimum content RSE at each key stage. This is the minimum entitlement that all young people must receive.  The minimum content provides flexibility and schools have a high degree of autonomy over what is taught.On 5 June 2023 the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland brought forward Regulations under his powers in section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 to ensure that the recommendations in paragraphs 85 and 86 of the 2018 report of the United Nations Committee on the Ending of Discrimination Against Women (“the CEDAW Report”) were implemented in respect of Northern Ireland.  Specific to education, paragraph 86(d) required the United Kingdom Government to “Make age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents, covering early pregnancy prevention and access to abortion, and monitor its implementation”.  The Department is required to report to the Assembly by September 2026 on the implementation.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work to ensure pupils at key stages 3 and 4 (aged 11-16 in years 8-12) receive age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion. On 1 January 2024 my Department issued RSE guidance to schools to support the changes brought about by the Secretary of State. Whilst the Board of Governors and the principal have statutory responsibility to deliver the minimum curriculum content, they also have autonomy over when, how and who is involved in curriculum delivery, aligned with the ethos of the school.  This includes the change to the curriculum made by the Secretary of State with regards to RSE.In relation to the Gillen Review, my officials continue to engage with the Department of Justice (DoJ) led Gillen Implementation Team and with the Executive Office to tackle the issue and end violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work she is undertaking to implement the recommendations of the Gillen Review that are relevant to her Department.
answer_text: These are historical questions from 2022 and the answer below is therefore based on the current position in respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE). Relationships and Sexuality education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age as set out in The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007. The statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and the Personal Development strand of Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level, prescribes the high-level minimum content RSE at each key stage. Specific to education, paragraph 86(d) required the United Kingdom Government to “Make age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents, covering early pregnancy prevention and access to abortion, and monitor its implementation”.  The Department is required to report to the Assembly by September 2026 on the implementation.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work to ensure pupils at key stages 3 and 4 (aged 11-16 in years 8-12) receive age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion.
