question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Regional Review of Neurology Services.
answer_text: The Regional Review of Neurology Services is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years. A number of workstreams have been established in priority areas including workforce, long term and complex conditions and care coordination. Completion of the review is subject to available clinical capacity and while workstreams have continued to make progress, this has been at a slower pace than anticipated due to ongoing capacity issues. In this context the Department has agreed a revised timescale which will see the development of a Final Report together with an implementation and investment plan by March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department will take to address traffic congestion, road safety and speeding on the Brunswick Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I can advise that Brunswick Road in Bangor is 870m long and has street lighting along its entire length and footways on both sides. It is subject to a 30mph speed limit and the double yellow lines marking the corner restrictions for the four side roads and at either end are intended to enhance road safety and facilitate traffic progression. A central white line highlights generous lane widths for an urban road and red patches on the road with zig zag markings and associated traffic signs highlight the presence of the school and nursery.  Given the measures already in place on Brunswick Road and the fact it has a good road safety record, I can advise my Department has no plans to implement any additional measures at this time.Issues relating to inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI which has responsibility for enforcement relating to both matters.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 4905/17-22, for an update on the installation of a Clinical Lead for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis after the recruitment for this post was paused in response to the Covid-19 Pandemic.
answer_text: A key constraint in the establishment a consultant-led Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (ME)/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS) service is that there is no specific training programme within the medical colleges for this. In this context, and despite several recruitment competitions, it has not been possible to identify a suitable candidate.   My officials are continuing to engage with the Patient and Client Council and service user representatives on ME/CFS services.  While a number of offers have been rejected by service user representatives, my officials will continue to explore other options to provide a medically-led service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions her Department is taking to progress Section 6.4.5 of the Education Authority Operational Plan 1: 2022-24 and establish a new Integrated primary school to meet the needs of pupils in the East Belfast, South East Belfast and Castlereagh area.
answer_text: The workstream contained within the first Operational Plan (OP1), published by the Education Authority (EA) on 1 September 2022, is a medium-term workstream. OP1 states that “medium term workstreams are exploratory in nature and may require collaborative and innovative work to provide sustainable education provision for a geographical/school cluster area.  Medium term workstreams may require a longer timeframe and therefore may span a number of Operational Plans”.The specific workstream given at Section 6.4.5 of OP1 provides the Background/Engagement as “Establish a new Integrated primary school to meet the needs of pupils in the East Belfast, South Belfast, South East Belfast and Castlereagh area. The Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education [NICIE] is working with the Integrated Education Fund [IEF] to bring forward further integrated provision for the area”.The establishment of a new school requires the publication of a Development Proposal (DP) and currently it is only at this is the point at which my Department becomes engaged.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why Groomsport Road, Bangor, was not included for resurfacing in his Department's Annual Report 2022/23 to Ards and North Down Borough Council.
answer_text: The asphalt resurfacing contract used by my Department within the Ards and North Down (A&ND) Council area expired in November 2021 and due to lack of contract, it was not possible to complete the proposed programme for 2021/22. A replacement contract is programmed to be awarded in early 2023. This situation limits the amount of resurfacing possible during the current financial year and this is reflected in the limited number of schemes included within the Council report.In determining future programmes, my Department will consider factors including road usage, general surface condition and structural deformation. These factors will be considered when preparing the 2023/24 A&ND resurfacing programme and Groomsport Road will be considered for inclusion in the programme the extent of which will be influenced by budget availability and successful contract award. Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions are being taken by her Department to support and enable the integration of Ukrainian refugees in society.
answer_text: Responsibility for all matters relating to the resettlement of Ukrainian nationals displaced by war transferred to The Executive Office from 1 April 2024.  The Executive Office (TEO) holds strategic responsibility for refugees and chairs the Strategic Planning Group on Refugees and Asylum Seekers. The transfer of responsibilities to one Department, will allow a more cohesive approach to the vital work of resettling and integrating refugees.  Up to 31 March 2024, my Department provided support to Ukrainian Nationals under the UK Homes for Ukraine Scheme. This sponsorship scheme allowed Ukrainian nationals displaced by war to temporarily live in NI and this arrangement also helped them integrate within communities. Officials from the NI Housing Executive provide support and advice on housing options where, over time, sponsorship arrangements have broken down.  Ukrainian nationals temporarily resettled under the Homes for Ukraine scheme have the same access to public services, including Health & Social Care and benefits as NI citizens.  They also have the right to work here for the period of their temporary Visa.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how the number of Network Maintenance Schemes in the Ards and North Down Council area compares against the other council areas for (i) completed works; and (ii) planned works.
answer_text: The number of schemes either undertaken or planned within a Council area is not a relevant comparator of investment across Council areas as costs can vary significantly from scheme to scheme and from year to year for a wide variety of reasons including scheme length, complexity, type of material used etc. Ards and North Down (A&ND) Borough Council area is allocated funding for Capital Structural Maintenance on the same basis as all other council areas using established criteria including total road length and structural condition.  However the primary factor governing the number of resurfacing schemes in A&ND for the current year is the current lack of a resurfacing contract which is planned for award in early 2023.  Full details of the Interim Term Contract Asphalt Resurfacing (ITCAR) strategy which affects all council areas can be found at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/transportni-procurement-guidance-documentsI have also included a link to my Department’s publications page where all Roads Reports to Councils are published from which a comparison can be made against completed and planned Network Maintenance schemes in other council areas; Publications | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline the criteria for resurfacing roads; (ii) how his Department determines whether a road meets this criteria; and (iii) how readily a road requiring resurfacing may move up a priority list if its condition suddenly deteriorates.
answer_text: Under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, my Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition and to seek to fulfil this duty, an annual programme of resurfacing and reconstruction works is undertaken, the extent of which is dictated by the availability of funding.On trunk roads, information is routinely gathered and analysed from a variety of specialist machine surveys and visual inspections and this information is used in order to help inform decisions regarding our annual maintenance programmes. On other roads Divisional programmes are prepared on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage and general surface condition surveys as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims. Programmes are subject to continual review and take into account a wide range of factors such as worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, contractor capacity etc. In the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of the indicators outlined above as well as on the professional engineering judgements of local engineers. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Development Proposal for St Anne’s Primary School, Donaghadee; (ii) when her Department will publish their decision on the Development Proposal; and (iii) why a decision on the Development Proposal is overdue.
answer_text: There are two published Development Proposals (DPs) currently under consideration by my Department in respect of St Anne’s Primary School (PS). DP 673 was published by the Education Authority (EA) on 22 January 2022 and proposes the discontinuance of the school and DP 683 was published by the EA on 16 June 2022 and proposes that the school will transform to Controlled Integrated status.  The statutory objection period in respect of DPs 673 and 683 concluded on 21 March 2022 and 14 October 2022 respectively.My officials are currently assessing both proposals and collating all pertinent information to provide me with submissions to assist me in reaching my decisions.  A considerable amount of correspondence has been received by my Department during the statutory consultation period; accordingly, time is required to assess this representation fully before my officials can provide me with recommendations.I am unable to provide a precise timescale as to when a decision will be taken in respect of the proposals; however, I am acutely aware of the need for a timely resolution in the interest of children, parents, and staff.  When a proposal for discontinuance has been brought forward under Article 14 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, alongside a proposal for Transformation to Controlled Integrated status, in accordance with Article 94 (4) of the Education Reform (NI) Order 1989, the legislation requires that ‘the Department shall consider both proposals together but shall not determine the proposal under Article 14 (i.e. DP 673) until it has made its determination with respect to the proposal for acquisition of controlled integrated status’.  Put simply, a school that has a proposal approved for discontinuance cannot transform and, therefore, procedurally the decision on the Transformation must precede the decision on the discontinuance.As the statutory objection period in respect of DP 683 has only concluded on 14 October 2022, I do not consider my decisions to be overdue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Development Proposals for (a) Enniskillen Integrated School; (b) Rowandale Integrated Primary School, Moira; (c) Drumlins Integrated Primary School, Ballynahinch; and (d) Spires Integrated Primary School, Magherafelt; and (ii) why the decisions on the respective Development Proposals are overdue.
answer_text: Development Proposals (DPs) for pre-school provision at Enniskillen Integrated Primary School (IPS), Rowandale IPS, Drumlins IPS and Spires IPS have all been published by the Education Authority (EA) and the statutory two-month objection period on each proposal has now expired.My officials have been gathering and analysing all pertinent information in relation to the proposals and this will be set out in submissions for my consideration and ultimate decision.Where DPs are brought forward for provision in the Integrated sector, they are assessed in line with the statutory duty, considered on a case-by-case basis and balanced against other relevant statutory and policy requirements.I am acutely aware of the need for certainty for the interested parties and I will not delay my decisions any longer than necessary as soon as proposals are brought to me for my decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Development Proposal for Strangford Integrated College, Carrowdore; (ii) when her Department will publish their decision on the Development Proposal; and (iii) why a decision on the Development Proposal is overdue.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 675 was published by the Education Authority (EA) on behalf of the Board of Governors (BoG) of Strangford Integrated College (IC) on 14 April 2022.  The statutory consultation period in respect of the proposal concluded on 28 June 2022.My officials are currently assessing the proposal and collating all pertinent information to provide me with a submission to assist me in taking my decision.I am unable to provide a precise timescale as to when a decision will be taken in respect of the proposals; however, I am acutely aware of the need for a certainty in the interest of children, parents and staff.  Assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires careful consideration of all evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all learners.  In the circumstances, I do not consider that a decision is overdue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Development Proposal for the Causeway Area to establish a new co-educational, 11-18, non-selective controlled integrated post-primary school in Coleraine; (ii) when her Department will publish their decision on the Development Proposal; and (iii) why a decision on the Development Proposal is overdue.
answer_text: On 9 March 2022, the Education Authority (EA) published a series of Development Proposals (DPs) in relation to the Causeway Area.  DPs 676–680 propose the closure of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College (IC) and the establishment of a new Controlled IC which will include Autism specific and Learning Support Classes.  The statutory two-month objection period concluded on 23 May 2022.My officials are currently collating all responses received by the Department, along with all other pertinent information and will provide me with a detailed submission to assist me in taking decisions that is in the best interest of all learners.Unfortunately, I am unable to offer a specific date for a decision.  I appreciate the importance of a timely decision regarding the proposals and I am acutely aware of the need for certainty for the interested parties.  I will not delay a decision any longer than necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what engagement her Department has had with the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee regarding pay settlement for teachers’ salary for (i) the 2021- 2022 academic year; and (ii) the 2022 – 2023 academic year.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC). Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and DE.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) comprising the five recognised teachers’ unions. My Department is not responsible for the management of the TNC although it is represented on it.Engagement has been ongoing over the last number of months between Management Side and the NITC to explore options for a teachers’ pay settlement for 2021-22.  Following receipt of the NITC pay claim for 2022-23, these discussions are now considering the options for a multi-year pay deal, which will be dependent on the Education budget and Public Sector Pay Policy for 2022-23. Management Side remains committed to resolving issues regarding teachers’ pay through discussion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of how the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has reduced violence against women and girls.
answer_text: During my tenure, I have brought forward a number of changes to provide better protections for victims of violent and abusive behaviours, including the new domestic abuse offence, a new stalking offence and protections under the recent Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  Collectively, these added protections and new offences provide an important response to a wide range of violent and abusive behaviours that predominantly affect women and girls.  These measures will also contribute positively to our wider agenda of creating safer communities and protecting those that are affected. While the offence only came into operation in April, I am encouraged by the number of changes that have been made in this short time. I am also pleased that a number of cases are due to be heard before the courts in the coming weeks and that one case has already resulted in a conviction and sentence of imprisonment for the offender involved.In line with the reporting requirements in the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, my Department must prepare a report on the operation of the stalking offence, the threatening or abusive offence, and Stalking Protection Orders, within two to three years of the new offences and Orders have coming into operation.  This will allow for a more meaningful assessment of the effectiveness of the Act to be carried out in due course.I also hope to consult on a new seven year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy later this year.  While this will apply to all, regardless of gender, we know that it will predominantly benefit women who are most affected by these types of crimes and behaviours.  My Department is also engaging with the Executive Office in relation to taking forward their Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the Independent Review of Education Interim Report.
answer_text: I welcome the Independent Review of Education panel’s Interim Report and am pleased that the panel has made progress. I am aware they have carried out extensive engagement with stakeholders and I look forward to considering the Final Report in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of how the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 has reduced violence against women and girls.
answer_text: During my tenure, I have brought forward a number of changes to provide better protections for victims of violent and abusive behaviours, including the new domestic abuse offence, a new stalking offence and protections under the recent Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  Collectively, these added protections and new offences provide an important response to a wide range of violent and abusive behaviours that predominantly affect women and girls.  These measures will also contribute positively to our wider agenda of creating safer communities and protecting those that are affected. In line with the reporting requirements in the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, my Department must prepare a report on the operation of the domestic abuse offence, child aggravators and generic aggravators within two to three years of the new offence have coming into operation in February this year.  This will allow for a more meaningful assessment of the effectiveness of the Act to be carried out in due course.I also hope to consult on a new seven year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy later this year.  While this will apply to all, regardless of gender, we know that it will predominantly benefit women who are most affected by these types of crimes and behaviours.  My Department is also engaging with the Executive Office in relation to taking forward their Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the degradation of teachers’ pay due to rate of inflation over the last 10 years.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC). Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and DE.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) comprising the five recognised teachers’ unions. My Department is not responsible for the management of the TNC although it is represented on it. Pay awards for teachers must comply with the parameters laid out in NI Executive’s public sector pay policy.  Given the pressures on Northern Ireland public services and associated budgets in recent years, this policy has dictated that pay awards must be affordable and justified by a pressing labour market case.In this context, the annual awards for teachers are detailed below.  Please note, these awards are in addition to incremental progression for eligible teachers, estimated at 0.8% of the pay bill per annum.0%		-	from 1st September 2012*  (NI Executive imposed pay freeze)1%		-	from 1st September 20131%		-	from 1st September 20140%		-	from 1st September 2015*  (Unions rejected pay offer)1% 		- 	from 1st September 20162.25%	-	from 1st September 20172%		-	from 1st September 20182%		-	from 1st September 20192%		-	from 1st September 2020*incremental progression was paid to eligible teachers in these yearsI hope you find this information useful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the impact that the lack of an Executive is having on the Education sector, including what planned work that her Department has had to put on hold due to budgetary uncertainty.
answer_text: Education is facing extensive unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations. In advance of a Final Budget being agreed by a new Executive, I have made a number of 2022-23 risk-based expenditure planning decisions to ensure minimal impact on children and young people.  This includes maintaining the Aggregated Schools Budget at the same level as in 2021-22, providing funding to continue the Healthy Happy Minds pilot and Engage II programme until December 2022, extending the School Holiday Food Grant payments to cover the full financial year, and the funding of a number of other time sensitive critical areas.My Department started the year with inescapable or pre-committed pressures estimated at a minimum of c£409m against the Department of Finance advised indicative baseline, and this figure has now risen to c£530m (of which c£122m relates to Special Educational Needs). Even against the Finance Minister’s 2022-25 Draft Budget proposals, the Department is heading towards a very significant overspend of up c£370m. This means that it will be extremely challenging to implement a range of things, including the education actions in the Northern Ireland COVID Recovery Plan and any recommendations that will emanate from the Independent Review of Education.My Department is extremely restricted in its ability to reprioritise or reduce spend due to the vast majority of its budget being required to address statutory and demand led obligations, and indeed about 80% of the total Education budget is subsumed by staff costs. Whilst work is ongoing to identify programmes of spend that could be scaled back or stopped, this will be very limited; and obviously any reduction in spend would invariably require cuts to essential frontline services that support the most vulnerable within our society, as well as risking breaching statutory obligations.My hope would be that a new Executive will re-consider the Finance Minister’s previous 2022-25 Draft Budget proposals, with the aim of agreeing a multi-year budget in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what representations he has had from the Minister of Education in relation to funding for the Engage Programme.
answer_text: On 22 June 2022, the Department of Education’s interim Accounting Officer at that time wrote to my Permanent Secretary to seek the Department of Finance’s approval for a Direction, given by the Education Minister, to incur expenditure in delivery of the Engage III programme (£10 million) for the period 1 September to 31 December 2022.  Following an assurance which the Education Minister provided to me that this expenditure would be prioritised from the funding envelope ultimately allocated to her Department, I provided approval for the Ministerial Direction on 6 July 2022.On 11 October 2022, the Department of Education’s Accounting Officer wrote to me seeking approval for a second Ministerial Direction which he has been given by the Education Minister, to continue the Engage III programme for the period 1 January to 31 March 2023 at a cost of £6.9 million.  As departments are now collectively projecting a significant overspend against our Treasury control totals for 2022-23, and the Department of Education specifically is anticipating significant inescapable pressures beyond its baseline, I have responded to the Education Minister to explain that if I am to provide my approval for this further expenditure to be incurred, I would need a firm assurance that her department will be able to take action to ensure this additional expenditure can be funded without overspending against its final budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Engage Programme.
answer_text: In an effort to offset the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic, the Engage Programme was launched in September 2020 using Covid-19 funding provided by DoF and continued in 2021-22 and 2022-23. Over £57m in funding was allocated to settings across the three years of the programme.The Engage programme aimed to limit any long-term adverse impact of COVID-19 by supporting pupils’ learning and engagement on their return to school/setting through provision of high quality one to one, small group or team teaching support in every school/setting in Northern Ireland. The Programme provided the flexibility and autonomy settings needed to meet the diverse needs of their pupils and allow them to address the effects of Covid-19.  One of the key strengths of the programme is that was shaped by those delivering it on the ground, which allowed for the delivery of a range of interventions and approaches which resulted in increased confidence and motivation of thousands of pupils.The 2023/24 budget allocation was extremely challenging for education and as a result the Department had to make significant savings from 1 April 2023. In this context the Engage programme was assessed as not realistically affordable and came to an end on 31 March 2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support her Department has made available to assist people working from home in the context of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My officials have worked collaboratively across departments to provide information on all current support available in relation to the cost of living. This information is available on NI Direct’s Cost of Living webpage: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-living. My Department will continue to do all it can to support those experiencing hardship.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what consultation his Department conducted regarding the decision to close the Sydenham Bypass on 30 September to 3 October 2022, 7 to 10 October 2022, 14 to 17 October 2022 and 21 to 24 October 2022; and (ii) what were the reasons for the closure taking place at these times.
answer_text: (i) My Department carefully planned these road works and associated traffic management arrangements in order to minimise inconvenience to the public.  Prior to the awarding of the contract, officials consulted with both the George Best City Airport and Belfast Harbour Estate.  Further meetings were held before commencement of the works and all traffic management arrangements were publicised on social media and through partner organisations such as Belfast City Airport.(ii)  There is no ideal time for works of this scale and disruption is inevitable.  The works were scheduled for the weekend and overnight because this is when the traffic flow is at its lightest compared to during the week.  Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work his Department is doing, in conjunction with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, to address coastal erosion.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum meets 1-2 times per year and brings together relevant government Departments, local Councils with a coastline and other interested parties to discuss coastal change issues in the North and seeks to adopt a more strategic approach to managing those issues. A joint DfI / DAERA Coastal Forum Working Group has also been created to support the work of the Coastal Forum and progress actions to improve coastal management. DAERA has invested over £1.5M on evidence gathering with the primary aim of acquiring a baseline dataset for the coastline. DfI, DAERA and Irish Government Departments are seeking to secure funding under Investment Area 5.2 of PEACE PLUS programme which the Departments expect to enable a cross border approach to the protection of the marine and coastal environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of asylum seeker and refugee children currently attending school who (i) live in MEARS accommodation; and (ii) are housed through the Homes for Ukraine scheme.
answer_text: The Department does not hold data on the number of children living in contingency MEARS accommodation as it is not a signatory to the data-sharing agreement in place between the Home Office and MEARS.Data on the number of children arriving from Ukraine and enrolled in schools here will be available when the Department’s October census is published.Moving forward my officials continue to engage with the Education Authority and other NICS departments to develop and implement proposals to support the increasing number of ASR families arriving in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures his Department is taking to mitigate against further traffic congestion caused by closing the Sydenham Bypass on 30 September to 3 October 2022, 7 to 10 October 2022, 14 to 17 October 2022 and 21 to 24 October 2022.
answer_text: Local officials have met with the contractors to review the arrangements over the first weekend to seek to identify improvements aimed at reducing congestion over the weekends of 30 September - 3 October, 7- 10 October, 14-17 October and 21- 24 October when further works were planned. As well as providing increased signage, additional cones were deployed to encourage vehicles to use the planned diversion along the Sydenham Road.  My Department has also been working with our contractor to open access to the M3 bridge from the Westlink to the Sydenham Road for the remainder of the works and this should also help alleviate congestion.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what provisions her Department has in place to assist refugee and asylum seeker children (i) in accessing schools in an accessible location to their accommodation; and (ii) to ensure that they are supported in the continuation of their education.
answer_text: The Department works in partnership with schools and the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES) to help families find a suitable school place, preferably as close to their accommodation as possible.  Schools receive advice, guidance and support from IES to help children move from early induction to early language acquisition and on to sustained integration within the school community.  IES also provides families with advice on access to free school meals, school uniform grants, school transport assistance and if required additional English classes.The Department has provided additional funding for temporary primary and post-primary Welcome Hubs at two schools in the Greater Belfast area to enable children who are accommodated in hotels to access education until they are housed and have a permanent school place in proximity to their eventual location.The Department continues to engage with the EA and other departments to develop and implement proposals to support the increasing number of Asylum Seeking and Refugee (ASR) families arriving in Northern Ireland.  DE is represented on the Strategic Planning Group (SPG) for ASR which is chaired by The Executive Office and meets fortnightly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the reasons for the delays in the NI Water Bangor Sewerage Improvement Project at Brompton Road and Stricklands Glen Wastewater Pumping Stations.
answer_text: The status of work at wastewater infrastructure across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education on how many occasions have children with special educational needs been left on transport arranged by the Education Authority and not taken to school (i) since the beginning of 2022-23 academic year; and (ii) in the previous five academic years.
answer_text: The number of occasions that children attending a special school have been left on transport arranged by the Education Authority is detailed in the table below:Academic yearNumber of incidents involving pupils attending a Special School*2022/2332021/2212020/2122019/2012018/1952017/184* Figures include incidents on EA bus services and Private Hire services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Northern Ireland is the only region in the UK that does not have a sexual health strategy.
answer_text: No decision has been taken to develop a new Sexual Health Strategy. The previous Sexual Health Promotion Strategy lapsed in 2015, following a two year addendum. Since then, a sexual health improvement action plan has set the direction for progress on sexual health awareness and promotion. The latest draft action plan was in its final stages of agreement at the end of 2019, but due to the pandemic, work on this ceased. Officials are now refreshing this action plan. The need for a new strategy will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will produce a coastal management strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department already has a coastal management strategy for Northern Ireland.  This was launched in June 2006 by the then Department of the Environment and is entitled “Towards an Integrated Coastal Zone Management Strategy for Northern Ireland 2006 – 2026”.  This Strategy sets out the long-term objectives for achieving sustainable coastal management, through improvements to existing management systems, the development of new management systems and identifying and dealing with potential areas of conflict. With regards to future policy, both my Department and the Department for Infrastructure have agreed a series of policy workshops to explore future coastal policy options through a Coastal Forum which will enable climate change adaptation and increase resilience to coastal change.  One workshop was held back in December 2021 and another is being arranged for November 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is planning to introduce legislation to address coastal erosion.
answer_text: Officials in my Department and DfI have been tasked to look at policy options around coastal erosion risk management.Currently there is no specific legislation in Northern Ireland to address coastal change, resulting in a lack of targeted resources and scientific data to assist in coastal management.  This gap has been recognised for some time and my Department is working collaboratively with DfI, coastal councils and the National Trust in a Coastal Forum.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will extend the 30mph speed limit on the B21 to the junction of High Bangor Road and Rocklyn Crescent, given the high density of residential homes in this area.
answer_text: I am advised that my Department has previously completed a speed limit review of the B21 Donaghadee Road and High Bangor Road in accordance with current policy which indicated that the existing speed limit is appropriate, given the level of development and the nature of the road.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads -Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a circular has been sent to school authorities advising them of the passage of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 and the impact of section 9 (1) of this legislation with regards to the provision of Relationships and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: These are historical questions from 2022 and the answer below is therefore based on the current position in respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE). Relationships and Sexuality education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age as set out in The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007. The statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and the Personal Development strand of Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level, prescribes the high-level minimum content RSE at each key stage. This is the minimum entitlement that all young people must receive.  The minimum content provides flexibility and schools have a high degree of autonomy over what is taught.On 5 June 2023 the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland brought forward Regulations under his powers in section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 to ensure that the recommendations in paragraphs 85 and 86 of the 2018 report of the United Nations Committee on the Ending of Discrimination Against Women (“the CEDAW Report”) were implemented in respect of Northern Ireland.  Specific to education, paragraph 86(d) required the United Kingdom Government to “Make age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents, covering early pregnancy prevention and access to abortion, and monitor its implementation”.  The Department is required to report to the Assembly by September 2026 on the implementation.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work to ensure pupils at key stages 3 and 4 (aged 11-16 in years 8-12) receive age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion. On 1 January 2024 my Department issued RSE guidance to schools to support the changes brought about by the Secretary of State. Whilst the Board of Governors and the principal have statutory responsibility to deliver the minimum curriculum content, they also have autonomy over when, how and who is involved in curriculum delivery, aligned with the ethos of the school.  This includes the change to the curriculum made by the Secretary of State with regards to RSE.In relation to the Gillen Review, my officials continue to engage with the Department of Justice (DoJ) led Gillen Implementation Team and with the Executive Office to tackle the issue and end violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what actions are being taken to ensure that comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights is made a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents; and (ii) how will its implementation be monitored.
answer_text: These are historical questions from 2022 and the answer below is therefore based on the current position in respect of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE). Relationships and Sexuality education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age as set out in The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007. The statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and the Personal Development strand of Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level, prescribes the high-level minimum content RSE at each key stage. This is the minimum entitlement that all young people must receive.  The minimum content provides flexibility and schools have a high degree of autonomy over what is taught.On 5 June 2023 the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland brought forward Regulations under his powers in section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 to ensure that the recommendations in paragraphs 85 and 86 of the 2018 report of the United Nations Committee on the Ending of Discrimination Against Women (“the CEDAW Report”) were implemented in respect of Northern Ireland.  Specific to education, paragraph 86(d) required the United Kingdom Government to “Make age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents, covering early pregnancy prevention and access to abortion, and monitor its implementation”.  The Department is required to report to the Assembly by September 2026 on the implementation.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work to ensure pupils at key stages 3 and 4 (aged 11-16 in years 8-12) receive age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion. On 1 January 2024 my Department issued RSE guidance to schools to support the changes brought about by the Secretary of State. Whilst the Board of Governors and the principal have statutory responsibility to deliver the minimum curriculum content, they also have autonomy over when, how and who is involved in curriculum delivery, aligned with the ethos of the school.  This includes the change to the curriculum made by the Secretary of State with regards to RSE.In relation to the Gillen Review, my officials continue to engage with the Department of Justice (DoJ) led Gillen Implementation Team and with the Executive Office to tackle the issue and end violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when his Department will publish the findings of the Review of Bathing Waters in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is undertaking a Review of Bathing Waters during 2022 and 2023. There has been a significant public response to the Review, and officials have been assessing the viability of nominated sites over the summer, to be followed by a programme of water quality testing of viable sites.I expect to be in a position to publish the outcome of the review in summer of next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what representations he has had from the (i) Education Authority; and (ii) Department of Education, on the £268 million deficit in the Education Authority budget.
answer_text: As an Arm’s Length Body, the Education Authority does not engage directly with my Department; rather, engagement is between the Department of Education, as the sponsor department, and the Department of Finance.In the absence of an agreed Budget, my Department provided all departments with contingency planning envelopes at the start of the 2022-23 financial year to assist with financial planning. The Education Minister has discretion to prioritise her Department’s expenditure in line with her priorities. I understand that the deficit you have referred to is against the planning envelope that the Education Minister has provided to the Education Authority.In June 2022, the Department of Education provided my Department with a list of its anticipated pressures, including pressures within the Education Authority.The Department of Education provides my Department with monthly Outturn & Forecast Outturn returns, which has also highlighted financial pressures for 2022-23 for the Department of Education, including within the Education Authority, and which my Department continues to keep under review.  My officials also hold regular meetings with DE Finance officials, at which pressures have been advised and discussed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement he has had with Ards and North Down Borough Council in relation to byelaws in Crawfordsburn Country Park.
answer_text: I can confirm that there has been substantial and ongoing engagement between my Department and Ards and North Down Borough Council in relation to byelaws applicable to Crawfordsburn Country Park.This has included a multi-agency meeting in September 2021 involving various locally elected representatives, the chief executive of the council along with other relevant stakeholders. This has been followed up with ongoing written correspondence between the Council and my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) the mechanism for the Education Training Inspectorate to inspect religious education in schools; and (ii) how this differs from other subjects on the curriculum.
answer_text: Article 102 of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 provides for inspection of schools, and other relevant establishments. Inspections under this Article must be conducted by inspectors appointed by the Department to monitor, inspect and report on the quality of education. The functions conferred on inspectors by this legislation are not exercisable in relation to any provision for religious education included in the curriculum of a school except with the agreement of the Board of Governors of the school. The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) does not inspect the provision of religious education, however at the request of the Chair of the Board of Governors, inspectors will include religious education lessons as part of the evidence base when evaluating the quality of provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work of her Department to deliver on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: The Department have been taking work forward in partnership with the IFA, NIFL and the Sports Ground Safety Authority (SGSA) to ensure final proposals are informed by an up-to-date understanding of needs.However, the challenges created by the continued absence of a functioning Executive mean I cannot bring new proposals for endorsement and agreement as is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will produce a women’s employment strategy.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is leading on the development of the Executive's new Gender Equality Strategy.  Employment issues affecting women have been raised by stakeholders as part of the co-design process.  Subject to Executive approval, it is anticipated that the Strategy will include actions targeted at removing barriers faced by women in accessing the labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce a 20mph speed limit on Brunswick Road, Bangor outside St Comgall's Primary School, St Comgall's Pre-School and Trinity Nursery School.
answer_text: I can confirm that these schools were assessed along with all of the other schools within the Ards & North Down area using the assessment framework. The highest scoring schools were included in tranche one with the next highest ranking schools included in tranche two.  Based on the assessment score, these schools did not rank highly enough to warrant inclusion in either tranche one or two.The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is being delivered this year and upon completion, over 215 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative.  Once these sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department will be in a better position in order to progress a review to determine if a further roll out of 20mph speed limits at schools will be taken forward.  Therefore, regrettably, I am unable at this stage to confirm if these measures will be introduced at St Comgall's Primary School, St Comgall's Pre-School and Trinity Nursery School.  However, I can assure you that it will be included in the assessment process for any future roll-out along with all the other schools that do not currently have such measures.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads -Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will resurface Bridge Road South, Helen’s Bay.
answer_text: As previously confirmed in responses to AQW 2726/22-27 and AQW 3707/22-27, details of resurfacing programmes are to be included in my Department’s 2022-23 Annual Report for Ards and North Down Borough Council.I can confirm that the Report was presented to Council on 11 October 2022 and is now published on the Department’s website and has been placed in the Assembly library. A web-link to the published Report is included below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-20222023Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work her Department is undertaking to introduce a legislative ban on conversion therapy.
answer_text: Working closely with representatives of those most impacted is ongoing.The issue was discussed with the LGBTQI+ Co-design Group as part of the work on development of the Strategy. 	Policy work is continuing to inform the drafting of legislation. It is essential that legislation is robust and fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will carry out a feasibility study for a cycle lane along the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to carry out a feasibility study for a cycle lane along the A2 between Bangor and Belfast.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education which recommendations from the Fair Start Report (i) have been fully implemented; (ii) are in the process of being implemented; and (iii) have not been implemented by her Department.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that excellent progress is being made against all of the actions within the report "A Fair Start". Forty eight actions for change are being taken forward this year. Forty from the original report plus eight additional actions. The report identified forty seven actions in total. These were split between Short term (ST) actions (Years 1 and 2), Medium Term (Years 3 and 4) and Long Term (Year 5 and beyond). Given that the plan was published during the 2021/22 financial year, 2022/23 is considered to be Year 1 of the Action Plan.The latest progress report can be found at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/progress-reports-fair-start-report-and-action-plans. The next progress report will be published in December 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education which recommendations from the Independent Review of Integrated Education (i) have been fully implemented; (ii) are in the process of being implemented; and (iii) have not been implemented by her Department.
answer_text: The Independent Review of Integrated Education report was published by Peter Weir, then Minister of Education, on 2 March 2017 for information purposes and without any endorsement in advance of considering the recommendations.  When the Executive resumed in January 2020 Minister Weir considered the 39 recommendations contained in the report.Recommendations 12, 15, 17, 19, 20, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27 and 28 have been actioned or progressed as far as possible e.g. within the rules of Fresh Start capital funding.  Recommendation 22 is within the remit of the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) to take forward.  My Department funds NICIE to encourage and promote integrated education and to provide support to all integrated schools.  Additionally, support services are provided for grant aided schools by the Education Authority.  Grant Maintained Integrated Schools can avail of these should they wish to – an additional Common Funding Formula factor has been provided to them as a contribution to the costs of such services.Recommendations 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 13, 14, 16, 33, 37 and 38 are being considered as part of the Terms of Reference of the Independent Review of Education given the fact that they relate primarily to statutory requirements, or wider Departmental policy, which is being considered through the work of the Independent Panel.  Recommendations 5, 18, 21, 29, 30, 31, 32, 34, 35, 36 and 39 are not approved and will not be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the work of the Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group; and (ii) whether his Department intends to publish the recommendations of the Review Group.
answer_text: (i) In 2019, my Department asked the then Health and Social Care Board to undertake a review of gender identity services and bring forward proposals for a service model which is, safe, accessible, sustainable and meets my Department’s waiting times targets. The Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group has produced their draft report and implementation plan and submitted it to my Department for consideration.    (ii) My officials are currently considering the Review Group’s draft report and implementation plan and they will shortly draft advice for my consideration. My final decision will be informed by a public consultation on the report including the Review Group’s recommendations along with other relevant documents, which will be published at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how long is the waiting list for Regional Gender Identity Service at the Brackenburn Clinic; and (ii) what his Department is doing to address this waiting list.
To ask the Minister of Health to list the organisations he has engaged with regarding the implementation of Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2022.

To ask the Minister of Health what community engagement his Department has had with patients of Priory surgery in North Down, in the context of his Department advertising the General Medical Services contract for the practice and the freeze on the movement of GP patients in practices in North Down, Ards and East Belfast.
answer_text: GP Partners at Priory & Springhill Surgery (Holywood/Bangor), have given notice to resign their GMS contract from 1st February 2023.  Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) Officers are currently advertising the Priory/Springhill GMS Contract with a view to finding a new contract holder to take over the practice on 1st February 2023.The SPPG has written to all registered patients at Priory & Springhill Surgery, aged 16 and above, to confirm that the Surgery will remain open until 31st January 2023 whilst the SPPG advertise to find new doctors to take over the practice from 1st February 2023.  Patients were advised that there is no need to register with an alternative GP practice.  A dedicated SPPG telephone number was provided for patient enquiriesSPPG Communications Officers continue to respond to media enquiries and have issued an initial statement to local MLAs, MPs and councilors as well as update statements to the local press.Patients and wider public will be kept updated regarding developments in process of finding replacement contractor to take over the care of patients at Priory & Springhill Surgery from 1st February.A temporary freeze on the free movement of patients in the Holywood, Bangor and East Belfast areas was introduced on 11th August 2022 and remained in place until 31st August 2022, as a temporary short-term measure in response to large numbers of patients from Priory and Springhill Surgery seeking registration at alternative GP practices in these areas.SPPG Officers engaged with patients during this time to provide advice and support as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why AQW 2616/22-27, AQW 2617/22-27 and AQW 2725/22-27 have not been answered; and (ii) whether the Minister will meet with me to discuss the Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2022.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to undertake weed management and resurface the roads and pavements in the (i) Belmont Road; (ii) Belmont Avenue; (iii) Belmont Park; (iv) Belmont Crescent; (v) Killeen Drive; and (vi) Killeen Close, areas of Bangor.
answer_text: My Department completed its first scheduled weed control treatment within the Ards and North Down Council Area earlier this year and a second treatment is now at an advanced stage with the timescale for completion largely dependent on weather conditions until the end of the treatment season.As previously confirmed in AQW 2726/22-27, details of resurfacing programmes will be included in my Department’s 2022-23 Annual Report for Ards and North Down Borough Council which is due to be presented on 11 October 2022.  The Report will be published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library shortly after the Council meeting. A web-link to the published Report will also be issued to all MLAs in the Council area.Should you have any further questions on these matters, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department ensures that Translink conducts meaningful engagement regarding significant projects in local communities.
answer_text: I can assure you that both my Department and Translink places considerable importance on engagement with a broad range of stakeholders in relation to all infrastructure projects and works in partnership with a number of external agencies to help deliver bespoke engagement campaigns aimed to specifically maximise engagement and awareness, while carefully considering the responsibilities Translink has to deliver safe, effective public transport. Where there are instances that Translink could improve in this area, constructive feedback is always welcomed to ensure we can continue to deliver the best services possible to our citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will meet with Hope 4 Life NI to discuss the educational programmes it plans deliver in schools.
answer_text: As Minister of Education, I and my officials regularly meet with a wide range of stakeholders and Hope4Life is welcome to contact my Department in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of Translink’s plans to fell 141 trees in the vicinity of Carnalea Station, Bangor.
answer_text: Details on projects in relation to the safety and maintenance of the rail network such as those regarding the felling of trees at Carnalea Station are an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many pavements in North Down are paved with flag stones; and (ii) the number of people who have been injured on uneven pavements in North Down, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain details of the number of pavements in North Down which are paved with flag stones, however, I can advise that there are approximately 277,000 square metres of flagged footway within the Ards and North Down Council area.My Department also does not maintain details of the number of people injured on the road network, however, it does maintain information on public liability claims by Roads Section Office area which broadly align with Council areas. The Ards and North Down Section Office area covers the Ards and North Down Council area.  The table below provides details of the number of public liability personal injury claims received in each of the last three full financial years (as at 30 September 2022) for which the Department’s Claims Management System has recorded the accident as occurring on a public footway.  Please note that as cases progress updated information is often obtained on defect type, incident location and other matters pertaining to a case, therefore, data in the Claim Management System may be subject to change:Financial Year Number of Personal Injury Claims 2019-2020 232020-2021 242021-2022 42It should be noted that these figures relate to the numbers of claims received and do not provide any indication of subsequent liability on the Department. In all cases the Department will investigate and defend public liability claims, with every case turning on its own facts, however it may ultimately up to the courts to decide if the Department has any liability. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what provisions her Department has in place to mitigate the rising global costs of materials necessary for essential school maintenance and repairs.
answer_text: As part of the Term Service Contracts (TSCs) contract extensions, and as a mitigating action against the rising global costs of materials, TSC contractors have accepted the Education Authority’s (EA) offer of price uplifts to reflect the inflationary price increase in materials based on the Building Cost Information Service (BCIS) Measured Term Contract updating percentages relevant for each schedule of rates for the TSCs.In addition, the EA have agreed certain prices in the price lists of the TSCs for specific maintenance elements of which the impact of inflation is higher than what could have been reasonably and diligently anticipated, and in excess of the adjustments following application of the relevant BCIS MTC updating percentages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the amount of money provided to pupils receiving the Free School Meal allowance will increase with the rate of inflation.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for determining the monetary value of a free school meal for pupils using a cafeteria system in all schools with the exception of voluntary grammar and grant-maintained integrated schools where this is determined by the Department. In order to ensure equality between pupils entitled to free school meals attending different settings, the monetary value determined by the Department is currently the same as the value set by the EA. The EA has advised that it has no current plans to raise this monetary value as it recognises the impact that an equivalent price increase could have on paying pupils, particularly in light of the current cost of living pressures being experienced by families.  The EA will continue to ensure this price remains under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether financial pressures due to the rising global costs of materials will prohibit planned maintenance work or new school buildings from progressing.
answer_text: Maintenance work will continue to be prioritised within available budgets.  My response to your question AQW 3484/22-27 outlines action being taken by the Education Authority to mitigate against the rising global costs of materials.In relation to new builds, the Department of Finance introduced measures to assist contractors that have been subject to significant and unforeseen cost increases and this has been applied to projects across the capital programme, where applicable. Going forward, a specific clause will be included on new contracts, which will permit inflationary pressures to be taken into account and business case addendums will continue to be prepared to seek approval for increased rates for labour and materials during the life cycle of capital projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the work currently being undertaken by her Department to produce legislation on sign language.
answer_text: My officials are finalising work on proposals for a Sign Language Bill through continued engagement with Deaf groups. However, given the cross cutting nature of such proposals, legislation cannot proceed without Executive approval. I remain committed to bringing forward Sign Language legislation built on the principles of equality and social inclusion in ensuring that the Deaf community here have the same rights and opportunities as those in the hearing community and are able to access services in their own language.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the arrangements being implemented to ensure the continuation of primary school counselling services while the independent evaluation of the Happy Healthy Minds Project is ongoing.
answer_text: The independent evaluation of the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Services pilot which has been commissioned by my Department is currently ongoing. To ensure the continuation of services pending the evaluation report, the pilot has been further extended until the end of December 2022. The evaluation will help to inform future provision subject to securing the necessary resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on negotiations with Trade Unions regarding the advancement of pay policy for 2022-23 in the NI Civil Service.
answer_text: I have been liaising with officials about how far public sector pay can be progressed in the absence of an agreed budget. I met recognised Civil Service trade unions on 28 September 2022 to discuss the very significant budgetary and political issues that we face at this time. Civil Servants due to receive an increase under the terms of any pay award for 2022 will receive any arrears backdated to 1 August 2022 when negotiations are completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why some substitute teachers pay for September 2022 will be delayed until October 2022; and (ii) what steps she is taking to mitigate this situation.
answer_text: There are several reasons why a supply teacher who worked days in August may not have received payment.  My Department does not employ teachers.  The Education Authority (EA) administers the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) on the Department’s behalf.  All grant-aided schools can offer bookings to available teachers registered on the system and latterly approve if the teacher accepts and attends.  All bookings approved prior to payroll cut-off are transferred from NISTR to the Department for salary processing.A short delay in the go-live date of the new NISTR system from 22 to 29 August and performance issues experienced in the first few days of operation resulted in schools being unable to book teachers for August days on the new system in the normal manner.  The EA put extensive additional resources in place to manually input thousands of retrospective bookings on schools’ behalf and to provide assistance to those experiencing difficulties in using the new solution. The Department also extended the August payroll deadline by two days to provide additional time for bookings to be placed, accepted and approved on the system. Nearly 3,500 temporary teachers received salary payment on the normal payroll date (16 September) however it is regrettable that a number of teachers who worked in August did not receive payment for various reasons for example:Retrospective bookings placed on the NISTR system were not confirmed and/or signed-off prior to payroll cut-off;Some teachers and schools experienced technical access issues in respect of the new system and were unable to confirm or sign-off bookings;Requests for retrospective bookings from schools were received after the extended payroll cut-off;Salary could not be processed as bank details were not provided; andErrors in the payroll file received from NISTR such as duplicate bookings that could not be investigated and resolved within timescales.Every potential avenue was explored to provide those affected with an interim payment option, however given the range and complexity of issues, no viable option could be identified.  The impacted teachers will receive payment as part of the next monthly payroll run.  The NISTR Support Team and the system suppliers and developers are working diligently to resolve any known issues and to make ongoing improvements in line with feedback received from users to ensure that the new system provides the envisaged intuitive booking system that enables schools to make contact with available supply teachers with ease and provides a platform for equitable employment opportunities for this vital part of our workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will produce a women’s health strategy.
To ask the Minister of Education to list the stakeholders she has invited to sit on the Stakeholder Engagement Forum since 7 September 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm that the following external stakeholders have been invited to join the Stakeholder Engagement Forum which will inform the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  Invitations to other Departmental representatives and public sector bodies will be issued over the coming days.  Further organisations and individuals may be asked to contribute as the work evolves.OrganisationEarly Years Organisation Sólás MENCAP Save the Children Barnardos Employers for Childcare PlayBoard NI NICMA Unite Union – Childminding Branch Parenting NI NICCY NCB  Northern Ireland Daycare Owners Forum  NICVA Ashton Centre Childcare Employers Forum NI Rural Women’s Network Women’s Resource and Development Agency Altram FSB Equality Commission for NIStranmillis University CollegeIndependent Early Years SpecialistParentkindChildren in NI (CiNI)Autism NIThe Welcome Project/ Migrant Centre NIEarly Years Daycare Forum

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will ensure the provision of grants to those businesses which are most exposed to food and fuel pressures, such as the hospitality sector.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including food and other commodities.As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level. In that context, I welcome the announcement by the Prime Minister of an Energy Price Guarantee and her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their energy bills over the coming months.My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the reports by (i) Ulster University Economic Policy Centre, Cost of Division, (2016); and (ii) Deloitte, Research into the Financial Cost of the NI Divide (2007).
answer_text: The 2016 UUEPC report, which included an empirical update of the 2007 Deloitte report, indicated that across most areas our costs typically fall within the range of costs identified in other regions.  The one exception is policing, where average costs are significantly greater than all other comparable police forces across these islands, accounting for over half the estimated additional cost. The report analysis identifies areas where division is potentially contributing to a higher cost of service delivery, but states it is only one of many factors and it should not be concluded that the additional costs identified represent potential savings which could be achieved. In some instances that may be the case, but in others instances the costs are unavoidable or would require significant investment to address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the reports by (i) Ulster University Economic Policy Centre, Cost of Division, (2016); and (ii) Deloitte, Research into the Financial Cost of the NI Divide (2007).
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided for AQW 2985/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the social housing waiting list in North Down for the last three years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below detailing the social housing waiting list in North Down as at June of each of the last three years.ApplicantsApplicants in Housing Stress(30 points or more)Full Duty ApplicantsJune 20221,8871,308994June 20211,9421,359961June 20201,7071,229880 The applicant Information is as at end of June of the relevant year.  For full Duty Applicants, these are applicants accepted as being homeless, to whom the Housing Executive owes a duty under the Homelessness legislation, Article 10(2) of the Housing Order, 1988 to “secure that accommodation becomes available for his/her occupation”.

answer_text: The Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) is currently formulated on a 3-year basis. The information below is therefore provided on that basis.
