question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to ensure road safety on the Belfast Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I am advised that local officials have carried out an assessment of the Belfast Road in Bangor and as explained in the response to your previous AQW 531/22-27, whilst the demand for a pedestrian crossing is acknowledged, regrettably for wider safety reasons there are no plans to install a crossing at this location.  I am sure you will agree that pedestrian crossings must be located in such a manner to ensure they provide adequate space for pedestrians and vulnerable users to safely cross the road and are also sufficiently visible to drivers thus affording them sufficient time to make adjustments so the safety of road users is not compromised. Given that these key criteria cannot be met, regrettably my Department is unable to provide a pedestrian crossing at this location.As is the case across the entire road network, it is incumbent on all road users to be mindful of the safety of themselves and of others, especially vulnerable road users such as cyclists or pedestrians and those with mobility issues or a visual impairment.In light of the above and a review of the safety record along this stretch of road, there are no plans to provide additional road safety measures at this time. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a list of roads due to be resurfaced in the North Down area.
answer_text: Resurfacing programmes within the Ards & North Down area are now being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council which will in due course be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) timeline of when her Department's Childcare Stakeholder Engagement Forum will be established; and (ii) Terms of Reference for the group.
answer_text: I will shortly be inviting a range of organisations and individuals to join the Childcare Stakeholder Engagement Forum, with a view to convening the first meeting in the autumn.  Draft Terms of Reference for the Forum are being developed and will be considered by the group at its first meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 2031/22-27, to detail how many reviews, or other pieces of work, her Department has put on hold pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The Department of Education has two reviews which have been put on hold pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 772/22-27; (ii) AQW 2617/22-27; (iii) AQW 2616/22-27; and (iv) AQW 2615/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Education how many children with special educational needs do not have a school place for September 2022, broken down by (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary.
answer_text: As at 22 August 2022 there are 33 children with statements of special educational needs (SEN) identified as requiring either a new or change of placement for September 2022 who are waiting to receive confirmation. The Primary/Post Primary Split of the 33 children is27 - Primary School6 - Post Primary Consultations with these schools and parents are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will introduce a register of all home educated children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Article 45 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 (as amended) places a duty on parents to ensure that their child receives an ‘efficient full-time education suitable to his age, ability and aptitude and to any special educational needs he may have, either by regular attendance at school or otherwise’. This enables parents to secure their child’s education either at school or outside of the formal school environment.The Education Authority (EA) holds a voluntary register of Electively Home Educated (EHE) children in Northern Ireland, but this does not include all EHE children.  While the EA has a record of children who have attended a school and subsequently been de-registered to be home educated, it does not hold information on EHE children who have never been registered at school unless their parents make contact with the EA. This is the case in other jurisdictions within the UK.If a child has never attended a school, there is no legal requirement on parents to register their child with the EA, but the EA does invite parents to make contact with the EHE team for advice on matters such as the curriculum, public examinations and future enrolment at school.The Department for Education in England has been preparing ‘The Schools Bill’ which has passed the first and second readings and the Committee stage in the House of Lords.  Among other things, this bill proposes that The Education Act 1996 is amended to include a duty for local authorities to register children not in school in England, which would include home educated children never registered at a school. There are no plans at present to introduce a compulsory register of all home educated children in Northern Ireland, however I shall follow the progress of the Schools Bill with interest and consider if it would be appropriate to introduce similar legislation and a duty to register all EHE children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1661/22-27, when he requested legal advice on the implications of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2022 and updated Directions; and when he expects to receive a legal opinion.
answer_text: My Department requested legal advice regarding the implications of the Abortion Regulations 2022 and the Abortion Services Directions 2022 between 20 May 2022 and 7 June 2022.  My Department subsequently received advice which was under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what services his Department currently funds for post-pregnancy counselling; and what the waiting times are to access these services.
answer_text: Post - pregnancy counselling services are delivered in a range of circumstances. Women who have had a pregnancy loss, stillbirth or neonatal death will be able to avail of support and guidance from bereavement midwives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made any additional funding available for pregnancy choices or post-pregnancy counselling support since the Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2020 commenced on 31 March 2020.
answer_text: Since before the introduction of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No.2) Regulations 2020, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have contributed grant funding in the region of £137k per annum to Informing Choices NI (ICNI, formerly the Family Planning Association NI) to support the provision of certain sexual and reproductive health advice services, including with regard to pregnancy choices and counselling, as well as contraception, STIs, and sexual health and relationships education for people with learning disabilities.  This includes a telephone helpline, web-based advice, information leaflets to GPs, GUM clinics, maternity units and community/voluntary groups and a three-option counselling service and post pregnancy counselling service.I awarded a non-recurrent uplift in grant funding to all of the Department’s core-funded bodies in 2020, in recognition of the financial challenges faced by the voluntary and community sector during the COVID-19 pandemic, through which ICNI received a further £15k, although this was not specifically for pregnancy choices or counselling. The advice and counselling service currently provided as part of the central access point operated for NI by the British Pregnancy Advice Service (BPAS) is funded by the Department of Health and Social Care in England. The provision of advice, guidance and counselling will form a key component of an approved and commissioned abortion service, subject to available funding and business case approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of why the number of children being home educated has risen significantly over the last five years.
answer_text: While the Education Authority can quantify the number of children deregistering from school to be Electively Home Educated, it does not hold sufficient detail on the reasons for their deregistration so as to ascertain why the numbers have  increased.  Most schools indicate a ‘parental request’ as the reason a child is being deregistered from school to be educated at home. Other reasons provided for deregistration have included home educating whilst awaiting a different school placement, concerns about COVID and health conditions.The responsibility for a child’s education rests with the child’s parents and parents may secure their child’s efficient full-time education either at school or outside of the formal school environment. Article 45 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 (as amended) places a duty on parents to ensure that their child receives an ‘efficient full-time education suitable to his age, ability and aptitude and to any special educational needs he may have, either by regular attendance at school or otherwise’.If parents wish to deregister their child from school for the purposes of Elective Home Education, they are required to do so in writing, informing the school Principal of their intention to home educate and that the child can be removed from the school register from a specific date. A reason for deregistration, however, is not required.Guidelines on Elective Home Education published by the EA states that parents choose to home educate for a wide variety of reasons which may include, but are not limited to:• Religious/Spiritual beliefs• Philosophical reasons• Dissatisfaction with the system• Bullying• Special Educational Needs• Mental Health Issues• As a short term measure for a specific reason

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action she is taking to ensure all children with special educational needs have a school place in September 2022.
answer_text: Securing appropriate provision for children with statements of Special Educational Needs (SEN) remains a key priority for me and is being taken forward by the Education Authority (EA) supported by the Department and key partners, to ensure children are placed appropriately and in line with their needs.  The EA established a cross-directorate planning group tasked to ensure that there is an appropriate place in a special school or specialist provision in a mainstream school, for every child with a statement who needs one.  Senior officials in my Department attend these meetings and hold regular oversight meetings with senior EA staff to monitor progress and provide me with regular updates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will review the criteria for pupils to qualify for the free Home to School Transport scheme.
answer_text: The existing home to school transport policy set out in Department of Education Circular 1996/41 (updated September 2009) was in the process of being reviewed when the pandemic hit and was subsequently put on hold.  The review is now paused pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education. Any proposed changes to the structure of the Education system would have a fundamental impact on the home to school transport policy which would need to be considered before progressing any further with a review of transport.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why the Wi-Fi in Parliament Buildings has limited speed and connectivity.
answer_text: Due in part to the greater demand for video conferencing by Members and staff as a result of the Covid-19 pandemic, Information Systems (IS) Office has identified deficiencies in the current Wi-Fi service, both in terms of signal availability in certain areas of Parliament Buildings and in the reliability of Wi-Fi connections to sustain high bandwidth applications. IS Office accepts that the system cannot cope reliably with these increased demands.The Assembly Commission has agreed to replace the Wi-Fi network infrastructure, which will include a dedicated internet connection. This project will be completed in the current financial year.Pending the replacement of the Wi-Fi network, the IS Office has reissued written advice to Members on how best to manage Wi-Fi connections on mobile devices when in Parliament Buildings. Should Members experience any difficulty with Wi-Fi access or performance in Parliament Buildings they should contact the IS Office Service Desk at the relevant time. The IS Office will continue to monitor the current Wi-Fi service in Parliament Buildings and will respond appropriately to any specific issues as they arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of Mother and Baby Units, and other work on perinatal mental health.
answer_text: Implementation of the specialist perinatal mental health services is being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams across the region.  Services in the Belfast Trust and the South Eastern Trust are operational and accepting referrals. The three remaining trusts are completing recruitment processes.In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region, including engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will implement the New Decade, New Approach commitment to fund three cycles of IVF treatment.
answer_text: It is not yet possible for me to provide an implementation date for the increase in IVF cycles.  I have always been clear that providing up to three cycles of IVF treatment for those who are eligible will require sustained investment to enable the Regional Fertility Centre to deliver greater volumes of treatment.  Whilst work is ongoing to develop the necessary business case and implementation plan for this, the member will be aware that long term affordability remains a major issue in the absence of an agreed Executive budget for the next three years. Once funding is in place, it is likely that a phased approach to firstly two, and then three cycles will be adopted.  This is to ensure that the service is not overwhelmed by demand and that waiting lists do not increase significantly, as well as the need to ensure there is adequate capacity to deliver, including within the independent sector initially. Once there is clarity on future funding and implementation, a Frequently Asked Questions guide will be developed to assist patients to understand the new arrangements, including eligibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will publish the findings of the Welfare Mitigation Review.
answer_text: The independent review of welfare mitigations has been completed and a final report has been submitted to the Department. This will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action her Department is taking to implement the recommendations of the Independent Review of Discretionary Support.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of Discretionary Support has been published on my Department’s website.  I hope to make an announcement soon on how I propose to improve Discretionary Support by making it more accessible and ensuring that emergency financial support is there for people when they need it most.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will engage with the women’s sector in Northern Ireland to produce a women’s employment strategy.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has policy lead on employability and would be responsible for developing such a strategy.  However, my Department makes a key contribution, particularly through our skills and education remit. Education, training and skills development opportunities, at all levels, are available at our Higher Education Institutions, Further Education Colleges and through a range of other training providers.  In addition, our recently published Skills Strategy for Northern Ireland - Skills for a 10X Economy - has led to the establishment of the Northern Ireland Skills Council and a Skills Equality, Diversity and Inclusion Sub-Committee.  The sub-committee considers access and participation in skills and education and aims to address barriers or issues that contribute to underrepresentation from those, for example, with a disability or of a particular gender or age.   Membership of the sub-committee includes representation from the women’s sector as well Union representation, the Equality Commission for NI and other Government Departments.  The Women in STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) Action Plan, co-designed by government and industry was published in March this year.  The activities underpinning the action plan are creating an environment that encourages and supports more women to reach their full STEM potential in Northern Ireland.My Department also continues to participate in the structures established by DfC to develop four social inclusion strategies, which includes one on gender.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what he is doing to ensure transgender people can access hysterectomies as part of gender affirming surgery.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, which operates the Regional Gender Identity Service at the Brackenburn Clinic, is not commissioned for this procedure as part of gender affirming surgery. However, if transgender people have gynaecological issues they will be assessed for hysterectomy and surgery will be performed if clinically appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Minor Injuries Unit in Bangor Hospital will reopen.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 57/22-27 for the answer to this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce a 20mph speed limit zone outside Grange Park Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is being delivered this year and upon completion, 215 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative. When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out I am not in a position to commit to install part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Service will be made available to all schools on a permanent basis.
answer_text: Following conclusion of the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Service Pilot in June 2022, an independent evaluation of the pilot will commence in July 2022 and is expected to report by late Autumn 2022. Once received, this evaluation will inform future provision of therapeutic and counselling support at primary school level and at that stage consideration will be given to the funding position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will include Brompton Bay, Bangor in his Department's published Bathing Water Profiles.
answer_text: I recognise the significant increase there has been in open water swimming, and my Department is currently undertaking a review of Bathing Waters.  A pre-consultation with councils and a public consultation have both been carried out, and my Department is now considering the proposals for new sites including Brompton Bay. Bathing water profiles will be produced and published for all bathing waters that are formally identified through the ongoing review process.My Department publishes bathing water profiles for all 26 identified bathing waters in Northern Ireland. These profiles are reviewed annually.A list of current bathing water profiles can be found here https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/2022-bathing-water-profiles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to install a pedestrian crossing on the Belfast Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I am advised that local officials have previously explained that while a demand for a pedestrian crossing is acknowledged, for wider safety reasons there are no plans to install a crossing at this location. Pedestrian crossings must be safe and ensure they can provide enough space for pedestrians and vulnerable users to safely cross the road and must also be located in such a way that they are clearly visible to drivers affording them sufficient time to make adjustments in order to ensure the safety of all road users. Given that these key criteria cannot be met, my Department unfortunately cannot safely provide a pedestrian crossing at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has funding available for all schools to install HEPA air filtration units to mitigate against the spread of COVID-19.
answer_text: The most effective mitigating measure in relation to Covid-19 is natural ventilation and my Department and the Education Authority are supporting schools in prioritising the effective implementation of this measure. Air cleaners were reviewed in detail by SAGE and its Environment and Modelling Group concluded that there is currently very little evidence that air cleaners are an effective control to prevent COVID-19, however the principles of air cleaning suggests that they may be useful in some cases and may be suitable for spaces where there is insufficient ventilation and the ventilation can’t be improved. All schools have received guidance in relation to ventilation, and supplied with CO2 monitors to assist in identifying areas where ventilation needs to be improved.  Schools with concerns about ventilation have been advised to contact the EA maintenance helpline for advice/support on the best approach for their individual circumstances.  The Education Authority Maintenance Service have received requests from 59 schools for air filtration units. All 59 schools were assessed on site by maintenance staff, resulting in 46 schools requiring air filtration units. 200 air filtration units have been distributed to date across the Education estate. There is a sufficient stock of air filtration units to meet the current demands and additional units will be purchased if there is a requirement for them.
